E_{total} = \vert E_{noise} \vert + \vert E_{missing} \vert
$$

$$
E_{total} = 2 \text{ GB} + 1.5 \text{ GB} = 3.5 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 2 — Veracity Score

$$
Q = 1 - \frac{E_{total}}{D_{total}}
$$

$$
Q = 1 - \frac{3.5}{50} = 1 - 0.07 = 0.93
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{Q = 0.93 \;\; (93\% \text{ data quality})}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Worked Example | Key Concept Demonstrated | Big Data V Illustrated |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial Sensor Volume | Storage scaling with replication + compression | **Volume** |
| Smart Traffic Velocity | Real-time streaming requirement | **Velocity** |
| Weather Sensor Veracity | Data quality and trustworthiness | **Veracity** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End IoT-Big Data Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[IoT Sensor Layer] --> A2[IoT Gateway]
    A3[RFID and Beacons] --> A2
    A4[Smart Wearables] --> A2
    A5[Connected Vehicles] --> A2
    A2 --> B1[Edge Computing Filter]
    B1 --> B2[Message Broker Kafka]
    B2 --> C1[Big Data Storage HDFS]
    B2 --> C2[NoSQL Database Cassandra]
    C1 --> D1[Batch Processing Hadoop MapReduce]
    C2 --> D2[Stream Processing Spark Streaming]
    D1 --> E1[Analytics Engine ML and AI]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Actionable Insights]
    E1 --> F2[Predictive Models]
    F1 --> G1[IoT Actuator Feedback Loop]
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> A1
```

**Architecture Walkthrough:**

- **A1–A5 (IoT Sources):** Heterogeneous devices generate raw telemetry.
- **A2 (Gateway):** Aggregates and protocol-translates incoming data.
- **B1 (Edge Filter):** Performs local noise reduction to lower upstream load.
- **B2 (Kafka Broker):** High-throughput message queue for decoupled ingestion.
- **C1, C2 (Storage):** HDFS for batch workloads, Cassandra for low-latency reads.
- **D1, D2 (Processing):** MapReduce for batch, Spark Streaming for real-time.
- **E1 (Analytics):** Machine learning and AI derive insights.
- **F1, F2 (Outputs):** Insights and predictions are operationalized.
- **G1 (Feedback Loop):** Decisions are sent back to IoT actuators — **closing the loop**.

---

## 4.2 The 5 V's of Big Data in IoT — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[Volume] --> V1d[Massive Sensor Scale]
    V2[Velocity] --> V2d[Real Time Streams]
    V3[Variety] --> V3d[Heterogeneous Formats]
    V4[Veracity] --> V4d[Sensor Noise and Drift]
    V5[Value] --> V5d[Actionable Intelligence]
    V1d --> IOTBD[IoT Big Data Convergence]
    V2d --> IOTBD
    V3d --> IOTBD
    V4d --> IOTBD
    V5d --> IOTBD
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Industry-Standard Reference Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Perception Layer Sensors and Actuators] --> P2[Network Layer WiFi 5G LoRa]
    P2 --> P3[Processing Layer Big Data Engines]
    P3 --> P4[Application Layer Industry Specific Insights]
    P4 --> P5[Business Layer Strategic Decision Making]
    P5 -.Feedback.-> P1
```

**Industry Reference:** This 5-layer model is the de-facto standard used in KTU-recommended textbooks and aligns with the **IEEE IoT Reference Architecture**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define the Internet of Things. Explain how IoT acts as a primary source of Big Data.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of interconnected physical devices embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies that collect and exchange data over the internet with minimal human intervention.

IoT acts as a **primary source of Big Data** because:
1. **Massive Volume:** Billions of connected devices (estimated 75+ billion by 2025) generate petabytes of data daily — for example, a single smart factory produces ~100 GB/day.
2. **High Velocity:** Sensors stream data in real time (millisecond granularity), demanding stream processing engines.
3. **Heterogeneous Variety:** IoT data includes time-series, video, audio, geolocation, and binary telemetry — all simultaneously.
4. **Continuous Generation:** Unlike traditional batch data, IoT never stops producing data, making it the **largest contributor** to the global data explosion.

---

### Question 2
**Q: List and briefly explain any three characteristics (V's) of Big Data in the context of IoT.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Volume:** Refers to the enormous scale of data generated by IoT devices. A single autonomous vehicle generates ~4 TB/day, and a connected jet engine produces ~10 TB per 30-minute flight.

2. **Velocity:** Describes the speed at which IoT data is generated and must be processed. Sensor data arrives continuously in real time, requiring stream processing frameworks like Apache Flink or Spark Streaming.

3. **Veracity:** Addresses the trustworthiness and quality of IoT data. Sensors deployed in harsh environments (heat, vibration, humidity) suffer from **noise, drift, and missing values**, requiring Big Data cleaning pipelines.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the relationship between IoT and Big Data. Discuss the role of Hadoop and Spark in processing IoT-generated Big Data.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (7 + 7 = 14 marks):**

#### Part (a) — Relationship Between IoT and Big Data (7 marks)

The relationship between IoT and Big Data is **symbiotic and bidirectional**, forming a closed feedback loop:

1. **IoT as Data Generator for Big Data:**
   - IoT devices (sensors, actuators, RFID, wearables) continuously produce massive volumes of heterogeneous data at high velocity.
   - This data contributes to the "Volume, Velocity, Variety" dimensions of Big Data.

2. **Big Data as Intelligence Enabler for IoT:**
   - Big Data technologies (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL) provide the storage, processing, and analytics infrastructure to make sense of IoT data.
   - Analytics results (predictions, anomalies, recommendations) are fed back to IoT systems for **automation and smart actuation**.

3. **Closed-Loop Architecture:**
   - Sensors → Ingestion (Kafka) → Big Data Storage (HDFS) → Processing (Spark) → Analytics (ML) → Insights → Actuators → Real-world action.

#### Part (b) — Role of Hadoop and Spark in IoT Big Data Processing (7 marks)

**Apache Hadoop:**
- **HDFS (Storage):** Distributes IoT data across commodity clusters with built-in fault tolerance (3× replication), making it ideal for the **Volume** characteristic.
- **MapReduce (Processing):** Handles batch processing of historical IoT data (e.g., daily sensor log analysis) using parallel map and reduce phases.

**Apache Spark:**
- **In-Memory Processing:** 100× faster than MapReduce for iterative ML algorithms running on IoT data.
- **Spark Streaming:** Enables real-time processing of IoT streams (Velocity), supporting use cases like live anomaly detection.
- **MLlib:** Provides pre-built machine learning algorithms for predictive maintenance, pattern recognition, and classification of IoT data.

**Combined Power:** Hadoop provides durable, cost-effective storage for massive IoT archives, while Spark delivers high-speed analytics on top — together forming the **standard Big Data stack for IoT pipelines**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**Q: Explain the 5 V's of Big Data with specific IoT examples. Calculate the daily storage requirement for a network of 1000 IoT sensors, each generating 5 KB/s, with HDFS replication factor 3 and compression ratio 4.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (7 + 7 = 14 marks):**

#### Part (a) — 5 V's of Big Data with IoT Examples (7 marks)

1. **Volume:** A smart city with 1 million sensors, each generating 1 KB/s, produces ~86 TB/day.
2. **Velocity:** A connected car's anti-collision radar processes data at 100 Hz (every 10 ms).
3. **Variety:** Smart home generates time-series (temperature), audio (voice commands), video (cameras), and binary (door sensors) simultaneously.
4. **Veracity:** Industrial sensors in high-vibration environments produce ~5–10% noisy readings requiring Big Data cleaning.
5. **Value:** Predictive maintenance analytics on jet engine IoT data can save airlines **millions in unplanned downtime**.

#### Part (b) — Storage Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $N_d = 1000$, $R_s = 5$ KB/s, $r = 3$, $C_r = 4$

**Step 1 — Daily volume per sensor:**

$$
D_{daily} = 5 \text{ KB/s} \times 86400 \text{ s} = 432000 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 2 — Total daily volume:**

$$
D_{total} = 1000 \times 432000 = 432{,}000{,}000 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply replication:**

$$
S_{req} = 432{,}000{,}000 \times 3 = 1{,}296{,}000{,}000 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply compression:**

$$
S_{eff} = \frac{1{,}296{,}000{,}000}{4} = 324{,}000{,}000 \text{ KB} \approx 308.99 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{S_{eff} \approx 309 \text{ GB/day}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting replication factor:** Many students compute $D_{total}$ directly without multiplying by $r = 3$. **Always state HDFS replication explicitly.**
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes:** 1 GB = 1,073,741,824 bytes (binary) ≠ 1,000,000,000 bytes (decimal). KTU expects the **binary (1024-based) conversion**.
> 3. **Mixing up compression direction:** A compression ratio of 4 means the file becomes **1/4th** the original size, so you **divide**, not multiply.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram:** For 7-mark questions on "relationship", a **neat labeled block diagram is compulsory** — failing to draw it costs at least 2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring Velocity:** When asked about IoT-Big Data relationship, students often discuss only Volume. Always mention **real-time streaming and velocity**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Relationship Between IoT and Big Data**

- **IoT = Data Generator; Big Data = Data Processor.** The relationship is **bidirectional and symbiotic**.
- **The 5 V's** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) are the standard framework for characterizing Big Data in IoT.
- **IoT contributes ~30–40% of all global Big Data** — it is the single largest data-generating paradigm in 2024+.
- **A single autonomous vehicle = ~4 TB/day; a jet engine = ~10 TB/30 min** — memorize these flagship statistics.
- **Key Big Data tools for IoT:** HDFS (storage), Kafka (ingestion), Spark (processing), MLlib (analytics), NoSQL (real-time queries).
- **Closed-loop architecture:** Sensors → Ingestion → Storage → Analytics → Actuation → Sensors (feedback).
- **HDFS default replication factor = 3.** Always apply this in storage calculations.
- **Compression ratio $C_r$ = original size / compressed size.** Divide the storage by $C_r$ to get effective storage.
- **Edge computing** reduces upstream load by filtering noise at the source before Big Data ingestion.
- **Use cases to remember:** Smart cities, Industrial IoT (IIoT), connected vehicles, smart healthcare wearables, precision agriculture, smart grids.
- **Veracity score formula:** $Q = 1 - \frac{\vert E_{noise} \vert + \vert E_{missing} \vert}{D_{total}}$, with $Q \in [0,1]$.
- **KTU exam tip:** Whenever the question asks to "explain the relationship", **always include a labeled block diagram** — this is the most common 7-mark question pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Center
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Center

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Data Center** is a dedicated, secured physical facility — comprising a controlled environment, redundant power supplies, high-bandwidth networking, fire suppression, and cooling infrastructure — designed to host **compute, storage, and networking resources** that support the ingestion, processing, storage, retrieval, and distribution of enterprise and analytical data at scale. In the context of **Big Data Analytics (PECPT755)**, a data center provides the physical and logical substrate on which **distributed data-processing frameworks** (such as Hadoop, Spark, and NoSQL clusters) operate, enabling horizontal scalability, fault tolerance, and high-throughput parallel computation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** A data center is **NOT** a single server or a small server room. It is an *engineered facility* purpose-built to deliver **five nines (99.999%) availability** for mission-critical workloads, with measurable power, cooling, and security Service Level Agreements (SLAs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Data Center = City Power Grid" Analogy

Imagine your **home inverter** versus the **Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) grid**. Your home inverter can light a few bulbs for a few hours. The grid, by contrast, is a massive, interconnected system of power plants, substations, transformers, redundant transmission lines, control rooms, and cooling towers — all engineered so that **millions of homes never experience a blackout**, and when one plant fails, the load is seamlessly transferred to another.

A **data center is the KSEB grid for digital workloads**:

| Home Inverter (Naïve Analogy) | Data Center (Formal Equivalence) |
|---|---|
| Battery | UPS + Diesel Generator |
| Inverter circuitry | Power Distribution Units (PDUs) |
| House wiring | Structured cabling & switches |
| Room temperature | Hot/Cold aisle containment |
| 1 light bulb | 1 virtual machine / container |
| Family usage pattern | Tenant workload orchestration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Big-Data Context:** When we say "Hadoop runs on a data center," we mean that the HDFS NameNode, DataNodes, YARN ResourceManagers, and NodeManagers all live on physical hardware that is housed, powered, cooled, and networked **inside a data center facility**. The data center is the *substrate*; Hadoop is the *workload*.

## 1.3 Why Data Centers Matter in Big Data Analytics

Big Data characteristics — the **5 V's** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value) — demand infrastructure that:

- **Scales horizontally** (add more commodity servers rather than upgrade one).
- **Tolerates failures** (commodity hardware fails; data must survive).
- **Moves data efficiently** (East-West traffic inside the data center often exceeds North-South traffic).
- **Co-locates compute and storage** to minimize I/O latency.

All of these requirements can ONLY be met by a purpose-built **data center**, not a desktop PC or a generic server closet.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted for KTU Board Exam)

- **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** benchmark target: **1.0 (theoretical ideal)**, industry average ≈ **1.58** (Uptime Institute 2023).
- **Standard rack power density**: **5–10 kW per rack** (legacy), **20–40 kW per rack** (modern AI/ML racks).
- **Tier IV availability**: **99.995%** uptime ⇒ maximum **26.3 minutes downtime per year**.
- **Standard rack unit (U)**: **1U = 1.75 inches = 44.45 mm**.
- **Typical humidity range**: **40–55% relative humidity** (ASHRAE A1 class).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a Data Center

A data center is **NOT a single box**. It is a layered system. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize the following **logical layers**:

### Layer 1 — Physical Facility Layer
- Land/building shell, raised flooring, fire suppression (FM-200, Novec 1230), structural cabling trays, biometric access control, CCTV.

### Layer 2 — Power Infrastructure Layer
- Utility grid feeds → **Transformers** → **UPS systems (N+1 or 2N redundancy)** → **Diesel Rotary UPS (DRUPS)** or **Battery UPS** → **PDUs** → **Rack-level power strips**.

### Layer 3 — Cooling Infrastructure Layer
- **CRAC/CRAH units** (Computer Room Air Conditioning / Air Handler).
- Hot-aisle / cold-aisle containment.
- Free cooling / economizers.
- Liquid cooling for high-density racks.

### Layer 4 — IT Compute & Storage Layer
- **Compute:** x86/ARM servers, GPUs, FPGAs, ASICs.
- **Storage:** SAN (Storage Area Network), NAS (Network Attached Storage), object stores (S3-compatible), direct-attached storage.
- **Hypervisors / Container Runtimes:** VMware ESXi, KVM, Docker, Kubernetes.

### Layer 5 — Network Fabric Layer
- **Spine-Leaf architecture** (modern data centers).
- **Top-of-Rack (ToR) switches**, **End-of-Row (EoR) switches**.
- **Border Leaf / Edge Routers** connecting to the Internet.

### Layer 6 — Software & Orchestration Layer
- Cluster managers: YARN, Kubernetes, Mesos.
- Monitoring: Prometheus, Grafana, Zabbix.
- Configuration: Ansible, Terraform.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Mapping:** When asked *"How does a data center support Hadoop?"* — answer: *Compute/Storage layers host the cluster nodes; Network layer carries HDFS block transfer traffic (default 128 MB blocks) and YARN shuffle traffic; Orchestration layer manages YARN's container placement.*

## 2.2 Data Center Tier Classification (Uptime Institute)

This is a **favourite KTU question** — frequently asked for **3 marks or as a sub-part (7 marks)**.

| Tier | Availability | Downtime/Year | Redundancy | Concurrent Maintenance | Fault Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tier I** | 99.671% | ~28.8 hrs | N | No | No |
| **Tier II** | 99.741% | ~22.0 hrs | N+1 | No | No |
| **Tier III** | 99.982% | ~1.6 hrs | N+1 | **Yes** | Partial |
| **Tier IV** | 99.995% | ~26.3 min | **2N (or 2N+1)** | Yes | **Full** |

> [!NOTE]
> **N+1** = One extra component for every N needed. **2N** = Fully mirrored duplicate system. **2N+1** = Mirrored + one extra. Tier IV is mandatory for financial and government big-data workloads.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | Purpose in KTU Exam |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** | $\text{PUE} = \dfrac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}$ | Dimensionless | Measures data center energy efficiency. **Ideal = 1.0** |
| 2 | **Data Center Infrastructure Efficiency (DCiE)** | $\text{DCiE} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Reciprocal of PUE. |
| 3 | **Carbon Usage Effectiveness (CUE)** | $\text{CUE} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions (kg)}}{\text{IT Energy (kWh)}}$ | kgCO₂/kWh | Green data center metric. |
| 4 | **Water Usage Effectiveness (WUE)** | $\text{WUE} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Water Use (L)}}{\text{IT Energy (kWh)}}$ | L/kWh | Cooling-water sustainability. |
| 5 | **Annual Availability (Tier n)** | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{8760 - \text{Downtime}_{hrs}}{8760} \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Used to compute Tier ratings. |
| 6 | **Rack Density** | $\text{Density} = \dfrac{\text{Rack Power (W)}}{\text{U units occupied}}$ | W/U | Measures compute intensity per U. |
| 7 | **Heat Dissipation** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Watts (W) | $m$ = airflow kg/s, $c_p$ ≈ 1.005 kJ/(kg·K) for air. |
| 8 | **Storage Capacity (HDFS example)** | $C_{total} = N_{nodes} \times D_{disks} \times S_{disk}$ | TB / PB | Used in Hadoop capacity planning. |
| 9 | **Replication Factor Overhead** | $C_{effective} = \dfrac{C_{raw}}{r}$ | TB / PB | $r$ = HDFS replication factor (default 3). |
| 10 | **Shuffle Network Bandwidth Requirement** | $B = \dfrac{D_{shuffle} \cdot N_{mappers}}{T_{deadline}}$ | Gbps | Used in YARN/Spark capacity planning. |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Rule:** I have used `\dfrac`, `\cdot`, and `\Delta` instead of `|` (pipe) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. In your own exam sheets, prefer `\dfrac{a}{b}` over `\vert a/b \vert`.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why this Matters in the Real World

- **Hyperscale Operators** (AWS, Google, Microsoft, Meta) run data centers in the **hundreds of MW** range. A single 100 MW data center can power ~80,000 homes.
- **Indian Context:** Mumbai, Chennai, Hyderabad, and Bengaluru host India's largest hyperscale data centers. The **STL/Adani–Google $1.2 B Vizag data center** is India's largest.
- **Big Data Use Case:** Training a single large language model (LLM) can require **~10 GWh** of energy — roughly the daily consumption of a small city. This is only feasible inside purpose-built, PUE-optimized data centers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Tier IV Downtime (0.4 hours/year)

The Uptime Institute defines Tier IV availability as **99.995%**. We must convert this into maximum allowable annual downtime.

**Step 1:** Write the availability equation.

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{8760 - \text{Downtime}_{hrs}}{8760}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute availability = 99.995% = 0.99995.

$$
0.99995 = \frac{8760 - D}{8760}
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by 8760.

$$
0.99995 \times 8760 = 8760 - D
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the LHS.

$$
8753.622 = 8760 - D
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for D.

$$
D = 8760 - 8753.622 = 6.378 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction — KTU Board Exam Note:** The widely-quoted "26.3 minutes" figure is actually the **unavailability budget per year** (i.e., 0.005% × 525,600 minutes = **26.28 minutes**), not hours. The two metrics — *hours* vs *minutes* — are often confused in textbooks. Always express Tier IV downtime as **26.3 minutes/year** for full marks.

**Step 6 (Corrected):**

$$
D_{minutes} = (1 - 0.99995) \times 525{,}600 = 0.00005 \times 525{,}600 = 26.28 \text{ minutes}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing PUE for a Sample Data Center

**Problem Statement (KTU-style 7-mark question):**
A data center has a total facility power draw of **12 MW**. The IT equipment (servers, storage, network) consumes **8 MW**. Compute the **PUE** and the **DCiE**, and comment on whether the data center is energy-efficient.

**Step 1:** State the PUE formula.

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{\text{Total Facility Power}}{\text{IT Equipment Power}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values.

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{12 \text{ MW}}{8 \text{ MW}} = 1.5
$$

**Step 3:** Compute DCiE.

$$
\text{DCiE} = \frac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1.5} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
A PUE of 1.5 means that for every **1 W** consumed by IT equipment, an additional **0.5 W** is consumed by cooling, lighting, and power distribution losses. The Uptime Institute 2023 industry average is **1.58**, so this data center is **above-average efficient** but still wastes 33.3% of input energy on overhead.

**Step 5: Overhead power calculation.**

$$
P_{overhead} = P_{total} - P_{IT} = 12 - 8 = 4 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 6: Annual energy cost (assuming ₹7/kWh).**

$$
E_{annual} = P_{total} \times 8760 = 12 \times 10^3 \times 8760 = 1.0512 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{annual} = 1.0512 \times 10^8 \times 7 = \text{₹ } 7.36 \text{ Crore}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HDFS Capacity Planning for a Big Data Cluster

**Problem:** A Hadoop cluster has **20 DataNodes**. Each DataNode has **4 disks** of **2 TB** each. The HDFS replication factor is **3**. Compute the raw and effective storage capacity.

**Step 1:** Compute per-node raw capacity.

$$
C_{node} = D_{disks} \times S_{disk} = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ TB per node}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute cluster-wide raw capacity.

$$
C_{raw} = N_{nodes} \times C_{node} = 20 \times 8 = 160 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the replication-factor overhead.

$$
C_{effective} = \frac{C_{raw}}{r} = \frac{160}{3} \approx 53.33 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4: Comment.**
Storing 160 TB of raw blocks allows only ~53 TB of unique logical data due to HDFS's 3× replication. The remaining 106.67 TB is **redundancy overhead**, which is the price paid for fault tolerance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Data Center vs Server Room vs Cloud

| Parameter | Server Room | On-Premise Data Center | Cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scale** | 1–10 racks | 10–1000+ racks | Virtually unlimited |
| **Uptime SLA** | Best-effort | 99.9%–99.995% (Tier III/IV) | 99.95%–99.99% per region |
| **CapEx** | Low | Very High | Zero (OpEx model) |
| **Cooling** | Window AC | CRAC + Hot/Cold aisle | Provider-managed (often free cooling) |
| **Big-data Suitability** | Poor (no HA) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Data Sovereignty** | Full | Full | Depends on region/law |
| **PUE** | 2.0–3.0+ | 1.2–1.6 | 1.1–1.3 (hyperscale) |
| **Time to Deploy** | Days | 6–24 months | Minutes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
**Define a data center. List any four components of a data center infrastructure.**

**Model Answer:**
A data center is a centralized, secured physical facility that houses networked computer servers, storage systems, and supporting infrastructure (power, cooling, networking) used for the **storage, processing, management, and dissemination** of large volumes of data.

Four components:
1. **Compute resources** — Servers, blade chassis.
2. **Storage systems** — SAN, NAS, object stores.
3. **Network fabric** — Switches, routers, firewalls.
4. **Power infrastructure** — UPS, diesel generators, PDUs.
*(Any 4 of: Cooling, Fire suppression, Physical security, Software orchestration)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
**What is PUE? State its ideal value and the formula.**

**Model Answer:**
**Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** is a metric defined by *The Green Grid* to measure the energy efficiency of a data center. It is the ratio of **total facility power** to **IT equipment power**.

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{\text{Total Facility Power (W)}}{\text{IT Equipment Power (W)}}
$$

**Ideal value = 1.0** (all input power is consumed by IT). Typical industry average is **1.5–1.6**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture, Tiers & PUE

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1 Pattern, 2024 Scheme]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Explain the **Uptime Institute Tier Classification** of data centers. Compare Tier III and Tier IV in terms of availability, redundancy, and fault tolerance.

**Model Answer:**

The **Uptime Institute** classifies data centers into **four tiers** (I, II, III, IV) based on their **availability, redundancy, and fault-tolerance capabilities**. This classification is globally accepted and is a **direct syllabus item** under KTU PECPT755 Module 1.

**Comparison Table — Tier III vs Tier IV:**

| Parameter | **Tier III** | **Tier IV** |
|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | 99.982% | 99.995% |
| **Annual Downtime** | ~1.6 hours | ~26.3 minutes |
| **Power Redundancy** | N+1 | 2N or 2N+1 |
| **Cooling Redundancy** | N+1 | 2N |
| **Concurrent Maintenance** | Supported | Supported |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Partial (one failure domain) | Full (multiple failure domains) |
| **Single point of failure** | Can exist (but with maintenance path) | None |
| **Cost (relative)** | Moderate | Very High (2–3× Tier III) |
| **Big-Data Use Case** | Production Hadoop cluster | Mission-critical, financial, real-time stream processing |

**Conclusion:** Tier IV is required only when **zero data loss** and **continuous operation** are mandatory (e.g., stock exchanges, real-time fraud detection in banking). For most big-data batch analytics, **Tier III** offers the best cost-to-availability trade-off.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — A data center draws **20 MW** of total facility power, of which **12 MW** is consumed by IT equipment. Compute (i) PUE, (ii) DCiE, (iii) overhead power in kW, and (iv) annual energy cost at ₹6.5/kWh. Comment on the efficiency.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $P_{total} = 20$ MW, $P_{IT} = 12$ MW, Rate = ₹6.5/kWh.

**(i) PUE:**

$$
\text{PUE} = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}} = \frac{20}{12} = 1.667
$$

**(ii) DCiE:**

$$
\text{DCiE} = \frac{1}{\text{PUE}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{1.667} \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

**(iii) Overhead Power:**

$$
P_{overhead} = P_{total} - P_{IT} = 20 - 12 = 8 \text{ MW} = 8000 \text{ kW}
$$

**(iv) Annual Energy Cost:**

$$
E_{annual} = 20{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ hr} = 1.752 \times 10^8 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Cost} = 1.752 \times 10^8 \times 6.5 = ₹ 113.88 \text{ Crore/year}
$$

**Comment on Efficiency:**
A PUE of **1.667** is **close to the Uptime Institute 2023 industry average (1.58)** but still leaves room for improvement. **40% of the input energy is wasted** on cooling and power distribution. Adopting **free cooling, hot-aisle containment, and 2N power architecture** can bring PUE below 1.3.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Components, Big-Data Role & HDFS Capacity

**[Internal Choice Alternative — 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

#### (a) [7 Marks] — Describe the **role of a data center in Big Data Analytics**. Explain with a diagram how an HDFS cluster is hosted inside a data center.

**Model Answer:**

A data center provides the **physical infrastructure** on which big-data frameworks operate. Without a data center, scaling Hadoop/Spark to petabyte-level workloads is infeasible.

**Roles of a Data Center in Big Data Analytics:**

1. **Hosting distributed compute nodes** — Each Hadoop DataNode/Spark Worker is a physical or virtual server inside a rack.
2. **High-bandwidth internal network** — East-West traffic (HDFS block transfer, Spark shuffle) demands **10–100 Gbps** spine-leaf fabric.
3. **Co-located storage** — Avoids moving petabytes over WAN; enables data-locality computation (HDFS principle).
4. **Fault-tolerant power & cooling** — 24×7 operation is mandatory because big-data jobs can run for hours.
5. **Physical security & compliance** — Required for GDPR, HIPAA, RBI data-localization norms.

**Diagram — HDFS Cluster in a Data Center (described verbally for KTU sheets):**

```
[Data Center Rack 1]──┐
[Data Center Rack 2]──┤── ToR Switch ── Spine ── Border ── Internet
[Data Center Rack 3]──┘
   │  │  │
   ▼  ▼  ▼
 NameNode, DataNode1, DataNode2 ... DataNode20
   (YARN ResourceManager, NodeManagers)
```

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] — A Hadoop cluster has **15 DataNodes**, each with **6 disks of 1 TB** capacity. The replication factor is set to **3**, but the cluster uses an **Erasure Coding (EC) policy of RS(6,3)** for cold data. Compute the (i) raw cluster capacity, (ii) replicated-data effective capacity, (iii) erasure-coded effective capacity, and (iv) % saving due to EC.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 15$ nodes, $D = 6$ disks/node, $S = 1$ TB/disk, $r = 3$, EC scheme = **Reed-Solomon(6,3)** (i.e., 6 data blocks + 3 parity blocks).

**(i) Raw Capacity:**

$$
C_{raw} = N \times D \times S = 15 \times 6 \times 1 = 90 \text{ TB}
$$

**(ii) Replicated Effective Capacity:**

$$
C_{replicated} = \frac{C_{raw}}{r} = \frac{90}{3} = 30 \text{ TB}
$$

**(iii) Erasure-Coded Effective Capacity:**
RS(6,3) means 9 blocks stored for every 6 data blocks. Storage overhead = 9/6 = 1.5×.

$$
C_{EC} = \frac{C_{raw}}{1.5} = \frac{90}{1.5} = 60 \text{ TB}
$$

**(iv) % Saving with EC:**

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{C_{EC} - C_{replicated}}{C_{replicated}} \times 100\% = \frac{60 - 30}{30} \times 100\% = 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Comment:** Erasure Coding (RS 6,3) saves 50% storage compared to 3× replication. The above calculation shows the effective storage doubles from 30 TB to 60 TB — this is **why modern data centers use EC for cold archival big-data tiers**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Data Center Questions:**
> 1. **PUE Confusion:** Many students write $\text{PUE} = \frac{\text{IT Power}}{\text{Total Power}}$ — this is **WRONG**. The denominator must be **Total Facility Power**, not the other way around. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Tier Downtime Unit Mix-up:** Do not state "Tier IV has 26.3 **hours** of downtime." It is **26.3 minutes**. The 99.995% figure is per year. (-1 mark)
> 3. **HDFS Replication Factor:** When asked for *effective* capacity, always **divide** by replication factor. Writing 90 TB instead of 30 TB will lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Erasure Coding vs Replication:** If the question explicitly mentions EC, do not default to 3× replication in your answer.
> 5. **Confusing Rack Unit (U) and Server Height:** A "2U server" means it occupies 2 rack units (≈ 8.9 cm), NOT that it is 2 cm or 2 inches tall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Data center = engineered facility for IT compute/storage/networking with redundant power, cooling, and security.
- **Five nines (99.999%)** availability = **Tier IV** benchmark.
- **4 Tiers:** I (99.671%), II (99.741%), III (99.982%), IV (99.995%).
- **PUE** = Total Facility Power ÷ IT Power. **Ideal = 1.0.** Lower is better.
- **DCiE** = 1/PUE × 100%. Industry avg ≈ 60–70%.
- **Rack Density:** Legacy 5–10 kW; modern AI racks 20–40 kW; future ≥ 100 kW.
- **Spine-Leaf** is the **modern** data center network topology (replaces legacy 3-tier).
- **HDFS + Data Center:** DataNodes are physical servers inside racks; NameNode on dedicated master hardware.
- **Replication default = 3×** in HDFS. **Erasure Coding (e.g., RS 6,3)** is used for cold data to save storage.
- **Big-Data Workload Sizing:** Use $C_{effective} = C_{raw} / r$ for HDFS planning.
- **Power Redundancy Codes:** N (no redundancy), N+1, 2N, 2N+1.
- **Green Data Center Metrics:** PUE, DCiE, CUE, WUE — all are part of the **sustainability triad** of modern data centers.
- **Indian Data Center Hubs:** Mumbai, Chennai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad — major hyperscale zones.
- **KTU 2024 Weightage:** Expect 1 direct 3-mark definition + 1 sub-part (7 marks) from Tier/PUE per exam cycle.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do not confuse "server room" with "data center" — a server room is **not** Tier-classified; a data center **is**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hadoop Preliminaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hadoop Preliminaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based, distributed storage and computational framework designed to store and process **extremely large datasets** across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is engineered to scale up from a single server to **thousands of machines**, each offering local computation and storage, rather than relying on high-end hardware.

The **Apache Hadoop** framework consists of four key modules:
1. **Hadoop Common** – The shared utilities and libraries that support other Hadoop modules.
2. **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** – A distributed, scalable, and fault-tolerant file system that stores data across machines.
3. **Hadoop YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** – A cluster resource management layer responsible for scheduling and managing compute resources.
4. **Hadoop MapReduce** – A programming model for parallel, distributed processing of large data sets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Genesis of Hadoop:** Hadoop was inspired by Google's publications on **Google File System (GFS, 2003)** and **MapReduce (2004)**. Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella created Hadoop in 2005, originally to support the **Nutch** open-source web crawler. It was named after Doug Cutting's son's toy elephant.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a **library of 1 million books** and need to count how many times the word "elephant" appears across all of them.

- **Traditional Approach (Single Computer):** Hire one super-librarian. He has to read every book one after another. It takes **weeks** and if he gets sick (fails), the entire project stops.
- **Hadoop Approach (Distributed):** Hire **1,000 regular librarians** and split the 1 million books into 1,000 piles of 1,000 books each. Each librarian counts "elephant" in their pile. A head librarian (the **Master**) collects and sums up the counts. If one librarian falls sick, the Master simply reassigns his pile to someone else.

This is **exactly** what Hadoop does: **divide the giant task, distribute it to many workers, handle failures gracefully, and aggregate the final result.**

## 1.3 The 4 V's of Big Data Addressed by Hadoop

Hadoop is built to handle the famous **"4 V's of Big Data"**:

| V Property | Meaning | How Hadoop Handles It |
|------------|---------|----------------------|
| **Volume** | Petabytes to Exabytes of data | HDFS distributes storage across thousands of nodes |
| **Velocity** | High speed of data generation/ingestion | YARN + MapReduce process data in parallel in real-time or batch |
| **Variety** | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured | Schema-on-read allows any data format (text, JSON, images, logs) |
| **Veracity** | Uncertainty and inconsistency of data | Distributed processing cleans and validates across nodes |

> [!NOTE]
> Hadoop is **NOT a database** — it is a **data operating system** that sits beneath databases, warehouses, and analytics engines.

## 1.4 Key Terminology for KTU

- **Node** – A single machine (server) in a Hadoop cluster.
- **Cluster** – A collection of nodes working together.
- **Rack** – A physical grouping of nodes (typically 30–40 nodes per rack).
- **Master Node** (NameNode) – Manages metadata of HDFS.
- **Slave Node** (DataNode) – Stores actual data blocks.
- **Job** – A complete MapReduce program submitted to Hadoop.
- **Block** – The smallest unit of data that HDFS reads/writes (default **128 MB** in HDFS 2.x, **64 MB** in HDFS 1.x).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Traditional Systems Fail (The Big Data Bottleneck)

Traditional **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)** and **Enterprise Data Warehouses** hit fundamental walls when data scales up. The reasons are:

1. **Vertical Scalability Ceiling** – RDBMS scale by adding more CPU/RAM/disk to a *single* machine. There is a physical and economic ceiling.
2. **ACID Transaction Overhead** – Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability guarantees become prohibitively slow on petabyte-scale data.
3. **Structured Schema Rigidity** – RDBMS demand schema-on-write; you must define the table structure *before* loading data.
4. **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** – Centralized storage and processing create a critical risk.
5. **Cost of Proprietary Hardware** – Enterprise-grade servers are expensive and not linearly scalable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop's Revolutionary Insight:** Instead of moving **data** to the **computation**, Hadoop moves the **computation** to the **data**. This is called **Data Locality Optimization**.

## 2.2 Hadoop's Core Design Principles

- **Scale-Out (Horizontal) Architecture** – Add more commodity nodes linearly; capacity and throughput grow in proportion.
- **Fault Tolerance by Design** – Hardware failure is *assumed*, not exceptional. Data is replicated (default **3 copies**) so failure is invisible.
- **Write Once, Read Many Times (WORM)** – HDFS optimizes for high-throughput batch reads, not random writes.
- **Schema on Read, not Schema on Write** – Data is loaded raw; structure is applied at query time.
- **Bring Code to Data** – Computation is shipped to the node where the data block resides.

## 2.3 The Hadoop Architecture Layers

Hadoop has a clean three-layer architecture:

### Layer 1: **Storage Layer – HDFS**
- Stores files across multiple machines.
- Splits large files into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB**).
- Replicates each block across **3 different nodes** (default replication factor = 3).
- A **NameNode** stores metadata (file → block mapping).
- **DataNodes** store the actual blocks.

### Layer 2: **Resource Management Layer – YARN**
- YARN = **Y**et **A**nother **R**esource **N**egotiator.
- **ResourceManager (RM)** – Master daemon, allocates cluster resources.
- **NodeManager (NM)** – Per-node slave, monitors containers and resource usage.
- **ApplicationMaster (AM)** – Per-application, negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs.
- **Container** – A bundle of resources (CPU + RAM) allocated to run a task.

### Layer 3: **Processing Layer – MapReduce**
- Splits job into **Map** phase and **Reduce** phase.
- Performs **Shuffling** and **Sorting** between them automatically.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | HDFS Block Size (default) | $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ | Configurable per file |
| 2 | Number of Blocks | $N_b = \lceil \dfrac{F}{B} \rceil$ | $F$ = file size, $B$ = block size |
| 3 | Total Storage per File | $S_{total} = F \times r$ | $r$ = replication factor |
| 4 | Number of DataNodes needed | $D_{min} = r$ | Minimum to fit one file |
| 5 | Replication Factor | $r \in \{1, 2, 3, \dots\}$ | Default $r = 3$ |
| 6 | Cluster Capacity | $C = n \times D \times B$ | $n$ = DataNodes, $D$ = disks/node |
| 7 | MapReduce Speedup (ideal) | $S_{ideal} = N$ | $N$ = number of mappers/reducers |
| 8 | Amdahl's Law (realistic) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = nodes |
| 9 | Storage Overhead Ratio | $R_{overhead} = r$ | For replication |
| 10 | I/O Throughput | $T = \dfrac{F \times r}{t}$ | $t$ = time to read/write |

> [!NOTE]
> **Amdahl's Law is a KTU favorite.** It tells us that even with infinite nodes, the speedup is bounded by the sequential (non-parallelizable) portion of the code. If only 90% of a job is parallelizable, the **maximum speedup is 10x**, no matter how many machines you add.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

Hadoop is the backbone of modern data engineering:

- **Facebook** – Stores over **300 PB** of data in HDFS, processes logs, recommendations, and ad analytics daily.
- **Yahoo** – Pioneer; runs the **world's largest Hadoop cluster** (over 40,000 nodes historically).
- **Netflix** – Uses Hadoop to power its recommendation engine that processes viewing data.
- **Twitter** – Stores 500+ million tweets per day in HDFS for trend analysis.
- **Banking & Healthcare** – Fraud detection, risk modeling, genomics, and clinical analytics.

## 2.6 Hadoop vs RDBMS (KTU-Favorite Comparison)

| Parameter | RDBMS | Hadoop |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| Data Volume | Gigabytes | Petabytes–Exabytes |
| Data Type | Structured | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
| Schema | Schema-on-Write | Schema-on-Read |
| Transactions | ACID compliant | Batch-oriented, BASE |
| Scaling | Vertical (Scale-Up) | Horizontal (Scale-Out) |
| Hardware | High-end servers | Commodity hardware |
| Cost | High (license + hardware) | Low (open-source) |
| Speed (small data) | Faster | Slower (overhead) |
| Speed (big data) | Impractical | Extremely fast |
| Query Language | SQL | HiveQL, Pig Latin, Spark SQL |
| Integrity | Strong (constraints) | Weak (eventual consistency) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: HDFS Block Count Derivation

**Problem:** A research lab stores a **1.2 TB** climate dataset in HDFS with the default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**.
**Find:** (a) Number of blocks, (b) Total storage consumption, (c) Number of distinct DataNodes required.

### Step 1 — Convert to common units

$$
1.2 \text{ TB} = 1.2 \times 1024 \text{ GB} = 1228.8 \text{ GB} = 1258.2912 \text{ MB}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute number of blocks

The number of blocks is the ceiling of (file size ÷ block size):

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1258.2912 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil = \lceil 9.8304 \rceil = 10 \text{ blocks}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **ceiling** (not floor) is mandatory because the last block is *always* allowed to be smaller than $B$ but a new partial block is still created.

### Step 3 — Total storage consumed (with replication)

$$
S_{total} = F \times r = 1258.2912 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 3774.8736 \text{ MB} \approx 3.69 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 4 — Minimum distinct DataNodes

A single block can be stored on multiple nodes, but each block needs at least $r$ distinct nodes for full fault tolerance. The minimum DataNodes in the cluster must be at least equal to the replication factor:

$$
D_{min} = r = 3
$$

### Step 5 — HDFS rack-awareness constraint

Hadoop's default rack-awareness policy places **one replica on a local rack, one on a different rack, and a third on the same different rack**. So the cluster should have **at least 2 racks** for proper replication.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Amdahl's Law for Hadoop Speedup

**Problem:** A MapReduce job has **20%** non-parallelizable code (driver setup, file I/O, final aggregation). The cluster has **10 nodes**.
**Find:** Maximum achievable speedup compared to single-node execution.

### Step 1 — Identify the parameters

- Parallel fraction: $P = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$
- Sequential fraction: $(1 - P) = 0.20$
- Number of nodes: $N = 10$

### Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{10}}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the denominator

$$
\frac{0.80}{10} = 0.08
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.28}
$$

### Step 5 — Final result

$$
S = 3.571 \text{ times faster}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even with **10 nodes**, we only get a **3.57x speedup** because 20% of the job is sequential. This is why optimizing the sequential portion of MapReduce code is critical in real-world systems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: MapReduce Word Count (Conceptual Derivation)

**Input Document:**
> "Hadoop is great. Hadoop is fast. Hadoop is distributed."

**Phase 1 — Split & Input**

HDFS splits the document into lines. The input format converts each line to a $(\text{key}, \text{value})$ pair where key = byte offset, value = line text.

**Phase 2 — Map Function**
The mapper emits a $(\text{word}, 1)$ pair for every word encountered.

$$
\text{Map Output} = \{ (Hadoop, 1), (is, 1), (great, 1), (Hadoop, 1), (is, 1), \dots \}
$$

**Phase 3 — Shuffle & Sort**
Hadoop groups all values for the same key:

$$
\{ Hadoop: [1,1,1], \; is: [1,1,1], \; great: [1], \; fast: [1], \; distributed: [1] \}
$$

**Phase 4 — Reduce Function**
The reducer sums all values for each key:

$$
\text{Output} = \{ (Hadoop, 3), \; (is, 3), \; (great, 1), \; (fast, 1), \; (distributed, 1) \}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: MapReduce Terminology

| Phase | Input | Output | Count |
|-------|-------|--------|-------|
| **InputFormat** | Raw file on HDFS | $(\text{key}, \text{value})$ pairs | Many per file |
| **Map** | $(\text{key}, \text{value})$ | List of $(\text{intermediate key}, \text{value})$ | 1 per record |
| **Combine (Optional)** | Mapper output | Aggregated local output | 1 per mapper |
| **Shuffle & Sort** | All mapper outputs | Grouped by key | 1 per unique key |
| **Reduce** | Grouped values | $(\text{output key}, \text{value})$ | 1 per key |
| **OutputFormat** | Reduce output | Final file on HDFS | 1 (or partitioned) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hadoop Core Architecture (Three-Layer View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientA[Client Application]
    hadoopCore[Hadoop Framework Core]
    storageL[Storage Layer HDFS]
    resourceL[Resource Management Layer YARN]
    processL[Processing Layer MapReduce]
    commonU[Hadoop Common Utilities]

    clientA --> hadoopCore
    hadoopCore --> storageL
    hadoopCore --> resourceL
    hadoopCore --> processL
    storageL --> commonU
    resourceL --> commonU
    processL --> commonU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Hadoop Common** is the bedrock. It contains the Java libraries, scripts, and configurations that all other modules depend on. Without it, HDFS, YARN, and MapReduce cannot start.

## 4.2 HDFS Architecture (Master–Slave)

```mermaid
graph TB
    hdfsClient[HDFS Client]
    nameNode[NameNode Master]
    secondaryNN[Secondary NameNode]
    dataNode1[DataNode 1 Slave]
    dataNode2[DataNode 2 Slave]
    dataNode3[DataNode 3 Slave]
    dataNode4[DataNode 4 Slave]
    block1[Block A Replica 1]
    block2[Block A Replica 2]
    block3[Block A Replica 3]
    block4[Block B Replica 1]
    block5[Block B Replica 2]
    block6[Block B Replica 3]

    hdfsClient --> nameNode
    nameNode --> secondaryNN
    nameNode --> dataNode1
    nameNode --> dataNode2
    nameNode --> dataNode3
    nameNode --> dataNode4
    dataNode1 --> block1
    dataNode2 --> block2
    dataNode3 --> block3
    dataNode1 --> block4
    dataNode2 --> block5
    dataNode4 --> block6
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NameNode** stores the **file-to-block mapping** in memory (fsimage + edit logs). **Secondary NameNode** is *not* a backup NameNode — it periodically merges edit logs into a new fsimage to prevent the NameNode's memory from overflowing. It is a **checkpointing helper**, not a failover node.

## 4.3 YARN Resource Management Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientApp[Client App]
    resMgr[ResourceManager Master]
    nodeMgr1[NodeManager Slave Node 1]
    nodeMgr2[NodeManager Slave Node 2]
    appMaster[ApplicationMaster]
    container1[Container 1 CPU RAM]
    container2[Container 2 CPU RAM]
    container3[Container 3 CPU RAM]
    container4[Container 4 CPU RAM]

    clientApp -->|submit job| resMgr
    resMgr -->|allocate container| appMaster
    resMgr --> nodeMgr1
    resMgr --> nodeMgr2
    appMaster -->|request containers| resMgr
    nodeMgr1 --> container1
    nodeMgr1 --> container2
    nodeMgr2 --> container3
    nodeMgr2 --> container4
    appMaster -->|execute task| container1
    appMaster -->|execute task| container2
    appMaster -->|execute task| container3
    appMaster -->|execute task| container4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **ApplicationMaster** is a per-application negotiator. It is *not* a global master. Each job (e.g., each MapReduce job or Spark application) gets its own ApplicationMaster. This decoupling is the genius of YARN: it allows **multiple applications to share the same cluster simultaneously**.

## 4.4 MapReduce Data Flow (Word Count Walkthrough)

```mermaid
graph LR
    inputFile[Input File on HDFS]
    split1[Split 1]
    split2[Split 2]
    split3[Split 3]
    map1[Mapper 1]
    map2[Mapper 2]
    map3[Mapper 3]
    shufSort[Shuffle and Sort Phase]
    red1[Reducer 1]
    red2[Reducer 2]
    out1[Output Part 1]
    out2[Output Part 2]

    inputFile --> split1
    inputFile --> split2
    inputFile --> split3
    split1 --> map1
    split2 --> map2
    split3 --> map3
    map1 --> shufSort
    map2 --> shufSort
    map3 --> shufSort
    shufSort --> red1
    shufSort --> red2
    red1 --> out1
    red2 --> out2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Between **Mapper** and **Reducer** lies the **Shuffle & Sort** phase, which is **the most network-intensive part of MapReduce**. Hadoop spills mapper output to local disk, partitions it by key, and pulls it across the network to the correct reducer. This is why **data locality** and **combiner functions** are critical optimizations.

## 4.5 Hadoop Ecosystem (Surrounding Tools)

```mermaid
graph TB
    hadoopCore2[Hadoop Core]
    sqoop[Sqoop Data Import Export]
    flume[Flume Log Aggregation]
    kafka[Kafka Streaming]
    hive[Hive SQL on Hadoop]
    pig[Pig Latin Scripting]
    spark[Apache Spark In Memory]
    hbase[HBase NoSQL]
    impala[Impala Real Time Query]
    oozie[Oozie Workflow Scheduler]
    zookeeper[ZooKeeper Coordination]
    mahout[Mahout Machine Learning]
    drill[Apache Drill]

    hadoopCore2 --> sqoop
    hadoopCore2 --> flume
    hadoopCore2 --> kafka
    hadoopCore2 --> hive
    hadoopCore2 --> pig
    hadoopCore2 --> spark
    hadoopCore2 --> hbase
    hadoopCore2 --> impala
    hadoopCore2 --> oozie
    hadoopCore2 --> zookeeper
    hadoopCore2 --> mahout
    hadoopCore2 --> drill
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Hadoop. List any four of its salient features.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Apache Hadoop is an open-source, Java-based framework that enables distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is designed to scale up horizontally from a single server to thousands of machines.

**Salient Features (1.5 marks — any four):**
1. **Scalability** – Scales horizontally by adding more nodes linearly.
2. **Fault Tolerance** – Handles hardware failures automatically via data replication (default 3x).
3. **Cost Effective** – Uses commodity hardware; no need for expensive enterprise servers.
4. **Flexibility** – Handles structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data.
5. **Data Locality** – Computation is moved to data, reducing network traffic.

---

### **Q2. What are the four V's of Big Data? Briefly explain how Hadoop addresses them.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four V's are: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, and Veracity** (1 mark).

| V | Hadoop's Approach (0.5 marks each) |
|---|-------------------------------------|
| **Volume** | HDFS distributes data across thousands of nodes, scaling to petabytes. |
| **Velocity** | YARN + MapReduce enable parallel batch and near-real-time processing. |
| **Variety** | Schema-on-read accepts text, JSON, logs, images, and any format. |
| **Veracity** | Distributed processing cleans, validates, and reconciles data inconsistencies. |

---

## 📗 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Module 1 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the Hadoop architecture in detail. Describe the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode in HDFS. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Hadoop Three-Layer Architecture (2 marks):**
- **Storage Layer (HDFS):** Provides distributed, fault-tolerant storage.
- **Resource Management Layer (YARN):** Manages cluster resources and job scheduling.
- **Processing Layer (MapReduce):** Performs parallel data processing.

**2. HDFS Components (5 marks):**

- **NameNode (Master Daemon)** – Runs on the master node. Stores **metadata** such as the file-to-block mapping, block locations, replication details, and permissions. This metadata is held in RAM for fast access. The NameNode is the single point of coordination for all HDFS operations. If it fails, the entire cluster becomes unavailable.

- **DataNode (Slave Daemon)** – Runs on every slave node. Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk. DataNodes periodically send **heartbeat signals** (every 3 seconds by default) and **block reports** (every 6 hours) to the NameNode to confirm they are alive and to update block location information.

- **Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)** – Does **not** act as a hot standby. It periodically (default every 1 hour, or when 1 million transactions have occurred) **merges the edit logs with the fsimage** to create a new checkpoint. This prevents the NameNode's edit log from growing unbounded and provides a recovery point in case of NameNode failure.

#### **(b) Compare Hadoop with traditional RDBMS across at least 8 parameters. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A detailed comparison across 8 key parameters (1 mark for table quality, 6 marks for proper parameter explanation):

| Parameter | RDBMS | Hadoop |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| **Data Volume** | Gigabytes | Petabytes to Exabytes |
| **Data Variety** | Structured only | Structured, semi-structured, unstructured |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-Write (fixed before loading) | Schema-on-Read (applied at query time) |
| **Scaling** | Vertical (scale-up, expensive) | Horizontal (scale-out, cheap) |
| **Transactions** | ACID compliant | BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) |
| **Query Speed (small data)** | Faster | Slower (overhead) |
| **Query Speed (big data)** | Impractical / fails | Very fast due to parallelism |
| **Cost** | High (proprietary) | Low (open-source, commodity HW) |
| **Hardware** | High-end servers | Commodity hardware |
| **Integrity** | Strong (constraints, joins) | Weak (eventual consistency) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Do not write a one-line description for each parameter. For full marks, **explain each parameter in 1–2 sentences** with at least one real-world example. For instance, when discussing "scaling," mention that Facebook added 30 PB of storage annually using Hadoop's horizontal scaling, which would have been economically impossible with vertical RDBMS scaling.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) Explain the Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) architecture. Discuss the block storage mechanism with a suitable example. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. HDFS Architecture (3 marks):**
HDFS follows a **master–slave architecture** with one **NameNode** (master) and multiple **DataNodes** (slaves). Files are split into fixed-size blocks (default **128 MB**), distributed across DataNodes, and replicated (default **3 copies**) for fault tolerance. The NameNode manages all metadata, while DataNodes handle actual block storage and serve read/write requests.

**2. Block Storage Mechanism (4 marks):**

**Example:** Suppose we upload a **512 MB** file to HDFS.

- **Step 1:** File is split into blocks of 128 MB each.

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{512}{128} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ blocks}
$$

- **Step 2:** Each block is replicated **3 times** by default.
- **Step 3:** Replicas are placed across different DataNodes following the **rack-awareness policy**: 1 replica on the local rack, 1 on a different rack, 1 on the same different rack.
- **Step 4:** The NameNode records the mapping (file → [block1, block2, block3, block4]) and (block → [DataNode1, DataNode5, DataNode7]) in metadata.

- **Step 5:** When a client requests the file, the NameNode returns the block locations, and the client reads directly from the nearest DataNode.

**Total storage consumed:** $512 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 1536 \text{ MB} = 1.5 \text{ GB}$

#### **(b) What is YARN? Explain the responsibilities of ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. YARN Definition (1 mark):**
YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) is the **cluster resource management layer** in Hadoop 2.x and above. It decouples resource management from data processing, enabling multiple data processing engines (MapReduce, Spark, Tez) to share the same cluster.

**2. YARN Components (6 marks):**

- **ResourceManager (RM) — The Global Master (2 marks):**
  - Runs on the master node.
  - Allocates cluster-wide resources (CPU, RAM) to applications.
  - Comprises two sub-components: **Scheduler** (performs allocation based on capacity/fairness rules) and **ApplicationsManager (AsM)** (accepts job submissions and launches the ApplicationMaster).
  - Maintains a global view of available resources.

- **NodeManager (NM) — The Per-Node Slave (2 marks):**
  - Runs on every worker node in the cluster.
  - Manages **containers** (resource bundles) on its node.
  - Monitors resource usage (CPU, memory, disk, network) of each container.
  - Sends periodic heartbeats to the ResourceManager reporting node health and available resources.

- **ApplicationMaster (AM) — The Per-Application Negotiator (2 marks):**
  - One per application; negotiates resources with the ResourceManager.
  - Works with NodeManagers to execute and monitor tasks.
  - Handles task failures and re-execution if a task fails.
  - Reports progress back to the client.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students confuse **ApplicationMaster** with the **NameNode**. They are completely different. NameNode is for **storage metadata** (HDFS). ApplicationMaster is for **compute negotiation** (YARN). Examiners specifically check if you can clearly distinguish these in your answer. Also, never claim the **Secondary NameNode** is a backup of the NameNode — it is **not**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this common misconception.

---

## 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hadoop** = Open-source distributed storage + processing framework, written in Java.
- [x] Inspired by **Google's GFS (2003)** and **MapReduce (2004)** papers; created by **Doug Cutting** in 2005.
- [x] **4 V's of Big Data:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity.
- [x] Hadoop's three layers: **HDFS (Storage), YARN (Resource Mgmt), MapReduce (Processing)**.
- [x] HDFS splits files into **128 MB blocks** (default), replicated **3 times** by default.
- [x] **NameNode** = master (metadata); **DataNode** = slave (actual data); **Secondary NameNode** = checkpoint helper (NOT a backup).
- [x] **Rack-Awareness Policy:** 1 replica local rack, 1 on different rack, 1 on same different rack.
- [x] **YARN components:** ResourceManager (global master), NodeManager (per-node slave), ApplicationMaster (per-job negotiator), Container (resource bundle).
- [x] **MapReduce phases:** InputFormat → Map → Combine (optional) → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → OutputFormat.
- [x] **Block count formula:** $N_b = \lceil F / B \rceil$.
- [x] **Total storage with replication:** $S_{total} = F \times r$.
- [x] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + P/N}$ — the most important speedup formula for KTU.
- [x] **Data Locality:** Hadoop brings computation to data, not vice versa.
- [x] **Schema-on-Read** vs RDBMS's **Schema-on-Write** — major paradigm shift.
- [x] Hadoop is **not a database**; it is a **data operating system** that supports many engines (Hive, Pig, Spark, HBase).
- [x] Hadoop assumes **hardware failure is the norm**, not an exception — fault tolerance is built in.
- [x] Hadoop's I/O philosophy: **Write Once, Read Many Times (WORM)**.
- [x] Default **HDFS block size = 128 MB** (HDFS 2.x+); **64 MB** in HDFS 1.x.
- [x] **Heartbeat interval** = 3 seconds; **Block report interval** = 6 hours.
- [x] **Edit log + fsimage** = NameNode persistence mechanism; Secondary NameNode merges them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain Hadoop architecture" or "explain HDFS," always draw a **labeled block diagram** in your answer sheet. KTU examiners award up to **2 bonus marks** for a clear, well-labeled diagram that shows NameNode, DataNodes, blocks, and replication. The diagrams in SECTION_4 of these notes are the exact style KTU expects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship Between Hadoop and Big Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Relationship Between Hadoop and Big Data # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Big Data > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > **Big Data** is a term that describes the **massive volume** of **structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data** that is so large and complex that it becomes difficult to process using traditional database management systems or conventional data processing applications. Big Data is universally characterized by the **"3Vs" framework**, formally extended to the **"6Vs"** in modern KTU-aligned curricula: | Dimension | Formal Meaning | Engineering Significance | |-----------|---------------|--------------------------| | **Volume** | Terabytes to Zettabytes of data | Scale of storage infrastructure required | | **Velocity** | Speed of data generation and processing | Real-time vs batch processing demand | | **Variety** | Different data formats (text, image, video, logs, sensor) | Need for flexible schema-less storage | | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness and quality of data | Data cleansing and provenance | | **Value** | Business insight extracted from data | ROI of analytics pipelines | | **Variability** | Inconsistency in data flow rates | Dynamic resource allocation | ## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition of Hadoop > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > **Apache Hadoop** is an **open-source, distributed, fault-tolerant framework** that enables the **storage, processing, and analysis of Big Data** across clusters of commodity hardware using simple programming models. It is licensed under the **Apache License 2.0** and was inspired by Google's **GFS (Google File System)** and **MapReduce** papers (2003–2004). ## 1.3 The Core Relationship: Problem ⇄ Solution > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Relationship (KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept):** > - **Big Data** is the **PROBLEM** domain — characterized by data too large, too fast, or too varied for traditional systems. > - **Hadoop** is the **SOLUTION ecosystem** — a software framework purpose-built to address those exact challenges. > - The relationship is **symbiotic**: Hadoop exists *because* of Big Data, and Big Data becomes *manageable* *because* of Hadoop. ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy: "The Mega-Library" Imagine a **single librarian** (traditional RDBMS) trying to manage a **library of 1 billion books** (Big Data). She can only read one book at a time, has limited shelf space, and if her desk collapses (server failure), records are lost. The library becomes unmanageable. **Enter Hadoop:** Now imagine **1,000 librarians**, each responsible for a small section of the library, all working in parallel following a shared rulebook (MapReduce). The library's index is replicated in multiple buildings (HDFS replication), and if one librarian is sick (node failure), others seamlessly cover. **This is precisely how Hadoop relates to Big Data** — by distributing the burden across many commodity nodes, each contributing a small, parallel effort. ## 1.5 Key Engineering Metrics The following constants and standard configurations are essential for KTU numericals: - **Default HDFS Block Size:** **64 MB** (Hadoop 1.x) or **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and 3.x) - **Default Replication Factor:** **3** (i.e., each block stored on 3 different DataNodes) - **Heartbeat Interval (DataNode → NameNode):** **3 seconds** - **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours** (default) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Traditional Systems Fail at Big Data Traditional **RDBMS (Relational Database Management Systems)** and vertical scaling architectures hit fundamental walls when faced with Big Data. The failure points are: 1. **Storage Ceiling** — A single server has a finite disk capacity (typically **1 TB – 100 TB** for enterprise SSDs). Beyond this, vertical scaling becomes economically infeasible. 2. **Processing Bottleneck** — Single-CPU or single-node execution is bound by **Moore's Law limits**; sequential scans of petabyte-scale datasets may take **days**. 3. **Fault Intolerance** — Traditional RAID is designed for small clusters; probability of node failure in a **1000-node cluster** is extremely high (MTBF drops drastically). 4. **Schema Rigidity** — RDBMS enforces **ACID** properties with strict schemas, ill-suited for **semi-structured or unstructured** Big Data. ## 2.2 How Hadoop Solves Each Big Data "V" | Big Data Challenge | Hadoop Component | Mechanism of Solution | |--------------------|------------------|----------------------| | **Volume** | **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) | Splits files into **blocks** (128 MB default) and distributes across cluster | | **Velocity** | **MapReduce / YARN** | Parallel processing on multiple nodes simultaneously | | **Variety** | **Hadoop Ecosystem** (Hive, Pig, Sqoop, Flume) | Schema-on-read, supports all data formats | | **Veracity** | **HDFS Checksums** | Data integrity verified via **CRC32** checksums on every block | | **Value** | **MapReduce + Analytics Tools** | Distributed analytics pipelines extract insights | | **Variability** | **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) | Dynamic resource allocation based on workload | ## 2.3 Hadoop Core Architecture — The Three Pillars > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Hadoop Framework is Built on Three Foundational Layers:** ### Layer 1: Hadoop Common - Provides **utilities and libraries** that support other Hadoop modules. - Includes **Java RPC**, **file system abstractions**, and **serialization mechanisms**. ### Layer 2: HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) - **Master-Slave Architecture:** - **NameNode (Master):** Stores **metadata** (file names, permissions, block locations). - **DataNode (Slave):** Stores **actual data blocks**. - **Key Operations:** Read, Write, Replicate, Delete. - **Block-based storage** with automatic replication. ### Layer 3: MapReduce (Processing Engine) + YARN - **MapReduce:** Distributed programming model with two phases — `map()` and `reduce()`. - **YARN:** Resource management layer (introduced in Hadoop 2.x). - **ResourceManager (Master):** Schedules jobs cluster-wide. - **NodeManager (Slave):** Executes tasks on individual nodes. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Essential Formulas for KTU 2024 Numerical & Theoretical Questions:** | # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Description | |---|------------------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | **Number of Blocks** | $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ | Always round up (ceiling function) | | 2 | **Total HDFS Storage** | $S_{total} = \text{File Size} \times R_f$ | $R_f$ = Replication Factor (default **3**) | | 3 | **Per-Node Storage** | $S_{node} = \dfrac{S_{total}}{N_{nodes}}$ | Evenly distributed across DataNodes | | 4 | **Read Time (Sequential)** | $T_{read} = \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{B_w}$ | $B_w$ = Bandwidth (MB/s) | | 5 | **MapReduce Speedup (Ideal)** | $S_{ideal} = N$ | Linear scaling with $N$ mappers | | 6 | **Amdahl's Law (Practical)** | $S_{actual} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p) + \dfrac{p}{N}}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = nodes | | 7 | **Fault Tolerance Cost** | $S_{overhead} = \dfrac{R_f - 1}{R_f} \times 100\%$ | Wasted storage from replication | | 8 | **HDFS Write Throughput** | $\theta = \dfrac{B_w}{N_{replicas}}$ | Affected by replication pipeline | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Hadoop-Big Data relationship powers critical industries: - **Healthcare:** Analyzing petabytes of genomic data (e.g., **1000 Genomes Project**). - **Finance:** Real-time fraud detection on transaction streams. - **Social Media:** Facebook generates **>4 PB of data per day** (stored and processed using HDFS-based systems). - **IoT:** Sensor data from smart cities, processed via Hadoop's distributed batch layer. - **E-commerce:** Recommendation engines using MapReduce for collaborative filtering. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: HDFS Block Calculation for a Given File Size > **Problem Statement:** A file of size **1.2 TB** is uploaded into an HDFS cluster with a default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Compute: > 1. The total number of blocks. > 2. The total storage consumed (including replication). > 3. The storage overhead percentage. ### Step 1: Convert File Size to a Common Unit We convert **1.2 TB** into **MB** for consistent unit handling. $$ 1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB} = 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB} $$ Therefore, the file size is: $$ \text{File Size} = 1.2 \times 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB} = 1{,}258{,}291.2 \text{ MB} $$ ### Step 2: Apply the Block Count Formula Using the **ceiling** operation (since a partial block still occupies a full block): $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}258{,}291.2}{128} \right\rceil $$ Evaluating the division: $$ \frac{1{,}258{,}291.2}{128} = 9{,}830.4 $$ Applying the ceiling function: $$ N_{blocks} = \lceil 9{,}830.4 \rceil = 9{,}831 \text{ blocks} $$ ### Step 3: Compute Total Storage Consumed $$ S_{total} = \text{File Size} \times R_f = 1{,}258{,}291.2 \text{ MB} \times 3 $$ $$ S_{total} = 3{,}774{,}873.6 \text{ MB} \approx 3.6 \text{ TB} $$ ### Step 4: Compute Storage Overhead Percentage The **overhead** is the storage used *beyond* the raw file size: $$ \text{Overhead} = \frac{S_{total} - \text{Raw Size}}{\text{Raw Size}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Overhead} = \frac{3{,}774{,}873.6 - 1{,}258{,}291.2}{1{,}258{,}291.2} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Overhead} = \frac{2{,}516{,}582.4}{1{,}258{,}291.2} \times 100\% = 200\% $$ > **Interpretation:** The replication factor of 3 means the cluster uses **3× the original storage**, of which **200% is overhead** — this is the price paid for **fault tolerance**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Hadoop Speedup > **Problem Statement:** A MapReduce job has a **non-parallelizable initialization phase** that takes 20% of the total runtime. If the cluster has **10 nodes**, what is the practical speedup achievable? ### Step 1: Identify Parameters - Parallel fraction: $p = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$ - Number of nodes: $N = 10$ ### Step 2: Apply Amdahl's Law $$ S_{actual} = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \dfrac{p}{N}} = \frac{1}{0.20 + \dfrac{0.80}{10}} $$ $$ S_{actual} = \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.08} = \frac{1}{0.28} $$ $$ S_{actual} \approx 3.57 $$ ### Step 3: Compare to Ideal Speedup $$ S_{ideal} = N = 10 $$ ### Step 4: Compute Efficiency $$ \eta = \frac{S_{actual}}{S_{ideal}} \times 100\% = \frac{3.57}{10} \times 100\% = 35.7\% $$ > **Conclusion:** Even with 10 nodes, the **20% serial bottleneck** limits speedup to just **3.57×**, and efficiency drops to **35.7%**. This is why Hadoop engineers minimize non-parallelizable code (e.g., driver setup, output aggregation). ## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Traditional Systems vs. Hadoop | Parameter | Traditional RDBMS / Vertical Scaling | Hadoop Ecosystem | |-----------|--------------------------------------|------------------| | **Data Scale** | **Gigabytes to low TB** | **Petabytes to Exabytes** | | **Architecture** | Centralized (single server) | Distributed (cluster of commodity nodes) | | **Scaling Type** | **Vertical** (scale up: bigger CPU/RAM) | **Horizontal** (scale out: more nodes) | | **Cost per TB** | Very high (enterprise hardware) | Low (commodity hardware) | | **Fault Tolerance** | RAID-based (limited) | **Replication + Re-execution** | | **Data Model** | Schema-on-write (rigid) | **Schema-on-read** (flexible) | | **Processing Model** | SQL queries, ACID transactions | **MapReduce, Spark, Tez** | | **Latency** | Low (milliseconds) | Higher (seconds to minutes) | | **Data Type Support** | Structured only | **Structured + Semi + Unstructured** | | **Best Use Case** | OLTP, financial records | **Batch analytics, log processing, ETL** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Relationship Mapping: Big Data Challenges ⇄ Hadoop Solutions ```mermaid flowchart TD A[BIG DATA CHALLENGES] --> A1[VOLUME
Petabytes to Zettabytes] A --> A2[VELOCITY
Real Time Streams] A --> A3[VARIETY
Structured and Unstructured] A --> A4[VERACITY
Data Quality Issues] A1 --> B1[HDFS
Distributed Storage] A2 --> B2[YARN and MapReduce
Parallel Processing] A3 --> B3[Hadoop Ecosystem
Hive Pig Sqoop] A4 --> B4[HDFS Checksums
CRC32 Verification] B1 --> C[HADOOP FRAMEWORK] B2 --> C B3 --> C B4 --> C C --> D[SCALABLE
FAULT TOLERANT
COST EFFECTIVE
BIG DATA SOLUTION] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Hadoop Core Architecture Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1[CLIENT LAYER] C1[User Application] C2[Hadoop CLI] end subgraph L2[PROCESSING LAYER YARN plus MAPREDUCE] P1[ResourceManager] P2[NodeManager] P3[MapReduce Engine] end subgraph L3[STORAGE LAYER HDFS] S1[NameNode Master] S2[DataNode 1] S3[DataNode 2] S4[DataNode N] end subgraph L4[FOUNDATION LAYER] F1[Hadoop Common
Java RPC and Utilities] F2[Commodity Hardware Cluster] end C1 --> P1 C2 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S1 --> S3 S1 --> S4 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 F1 --> P1 F1 --> S1 F2 --> F1 style L1 fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 style L2 fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 style L3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 style L4 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Data Flow Schematic: File Upload to HDFS with Replication ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client participant NameNode as NameNode Master participant DN1 as DataNode 1 participant DN2 as DataNode 2 participant DN3 as DataNode 3 Client->>NameNode: 1 Request to write file NameNode->>NameNode: 2 Compute block locations and assign DataNodes NameNode->>Client: 3 Return DataNode list DN1 DN2 DN3 Client->>DN1: 4 Send Block 1 data DN1->>DN2: 5 Replicate to DN2 DN2->>DN3: 6 Replicate to DN3 DN3->>NameNode: 7 Block report and ack DN2->>NameNode: 8 Block report and ack DN1->>NameNode: 9 Block report and ack NameNode->>Client: 10 Write successful confirmation ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between Big Data and traditional data. List the characteristics of Big Data.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Traditional Data | Big Data | |--------|------------------|----------| | Volume | GB to low TB | TB to ZB | | Structure | Strictly structured | Structured, semi, unstructured | | Storage | Single RDBMS server | Distributed cluster (HDFS) | | Processing | SQL, ACID transactions | MapReduce, Spark, batch analytics | | Schema | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read | **Big Data Characteristics (3Vs):** 1. **Volume** — Massive data scale 2. **Velocity** — High generation/processing speed 3. **Variety** — Multiple data formats --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What is Hadoop? Explain its two core components.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Hadoop** is an open-source distributed framework for storing and processing Big Data across clusters of commodity computers using a simple programming model. **Two Core Components:** 1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Provides **distributed storage** by splitting files into blocks (128 MB default) and replicating them across multiple DataNodes. Managed by a **NameNode** (metadata) and **DataNodes** (actual storage). 2. **MapReduce** — A **distributed programming model** for parallel data processing, consisting of two phases: - **Map phase** — Transforms input data into key-value pairs. - **Reduce phase** — Aggregates and processes the mapped output. *(Optional 4th mark may include Hadoop Common/YARN mention.)* --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **ONE** full question from each pair. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. --- ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the relationship between Hadoop and Big Data. How does Hadoop address the 3Vs of Big Data?** **Model Answer:** The relationship between Hadoop and Big Data is fundamentally **problem-solution oriented**.
