Big Data represents the **challenges** posed by the explosion of data in terms of volume, velocity, and variety, while Hadoop is the **technology framework** specifically engineered to manage these challenges at scale. **How Hadoop Addresses the 3Vs:** 1. **Volume → HDFS** - HDFS splits large files into **128 MB blocks** and distributes them across a cluster of commodity nodes. - Storage scales **horizontally** — adding more nodes increases capacity linearly. - Default **replication factor of 3** ensures fault tolerance. 2. **Velocity → MapReduce + YARN** - MapReduce parallelizes processing across multiple nodes simultaneously. - **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** dynamically allocates resources for fast job execution. - Streaming frameworks (Kafka, Flume) integrated with HDFS enable near real-time ingestion. 3. **Variety → Hadoop Ecosystem** - **Schema-on-read** approach allows processing of structured (CSV, RDBMS), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (logs, images, video) data. - Tools like **Hive** (SQL-like queries), **Pig** (data flow scripts), and **Sqoop** (RDBMS import/export) handle diverse formats. **Conclusion:** Hadoop's distributed architecture directly counteracts each "V" of Big Data, making it the de-facto solution for **batch-oriented Big Data analytics**. --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: With a neat diagram, explain the Hadoop architecture. Mention the role of NameNode and DataNode.** **Model Answer:** Hadoop follows a **Master-Slave architecture** with two primary layers: ``` ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ HADOOP ARCHITECTURE │ ├──────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ MASTER NODE │ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ NameNode │ ← Metadata │ │ │ (HDFS Master) │ + FS Image │ │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ │ │ Heartbeat (3 sec) │ ├───────────────┼──────────────────────────┤ │ SLAVE NODES │ │ │ ┌───────────▼──────────┐ │ │ │ DataNode 1 │ ← Blocks │ │ │ (HDFS Slave) │ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ DataNode 2 │ ← Blocks │ │ │ (HDFS Slave) │ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ DataNode N │ ← Blocks │ │ │ (HDFS Slave) │ │ │ └──────────────────────┘ │ └──────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` **Role of NameNode (Master):** - Maintains **metadata** (file names, directory tree, block-to-DataNode mapping). - Receives **heartbeats** every **3 seconds** from all DataNodes. - Manages **file system namespace** and client access permissions. - Stores **FsImage** (file system snapshot) and **EditLog** (transaction log). **Role of DataNode (Slave):** - Stores **actual data blocks** (default 128 MB each). - Sends **block reports** to NameNode every **6 hours** (default). - Performs **block creation, deletion, and replication** upon NameNode instructions. - Verifies data integrity using **CRC32 checksums**. **Workflow:** 1. Client requests file access → NameNode responds with block locations. 2. Client directly communicates with respective DataNodes to read/write data. 3. DataNodes periodically report status to NameNode. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Compare Hadoop with traditional RDBMS. List the advantages of Hadoop over RDBMS.** **Model Answer:** | Comparison Parameter | Traditional RDBMS | Hadoop | |---------------------|-------------------|--------| | **Data Volume** | GB to low TB | TB to PB+ | | **Data Variety** | Structured only | Structured, semi, unstructured | | **Scaling** | Vertical (scale up) | Horizontal (scale out) | | **Schema** | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read | | **Transactions** | ACID compliant | BASE (eventual consistency) | | **Processing** | Real-time SQL | Batch-oriented MapReduce | | **Hardware** | High-end servers | Commodity hardware | | **Cost** | Expensive | Cost-effective | | **Fault Tolerance** | RAID, limited | Replication + re-execution | | **Query Language** | SQL | HQL (Hive), Pig Latin | **Advantages of Hadoop over RDBMS:** 1. **Scalability** — Easily scales to thousands of nodes. 2. **Cost-effective** — Uses cheap commodity hardware. 3. **Flexibility** — Handles any data format. 4. **Fault Tolerance** — Replication factor of 3 ensures data availability. 5. **Parallel Processing** — MapReduce enables distributed computation. 6. **Open Source** — No licensing fees (Apache License 2.0). 7. **Ecosystem** — Rich set of tools (Hive, Pig, HBase, Spark, Sqoop, Flume). --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: A 2 TB file is stored in HDFS with block size 128 MB and replication factor 3. Calculate the total number of blocks and total storage consumed.** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Convert File Size to MB** $$ \text{File Size} = 2 \text{ TB} = 2 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ MB} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Number of Blocks** $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{128} \right\rceil $$ $$ N_{blocks} = \left\lceil 16{,}384 \right\rceil = 16{,}384 \text{ blocks} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Total Storage (with replication)** $$ S_{total} = \text{File Size} \times R_f = 2{,}097{,}152 \times 3 = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ MB} $$ Converting back to TB: $$ S_{total} = \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{1024 \times 1024} = 6 \text{ TB} $$ **Step 4: Storage Overhead** $$ \text{Overhead} = (3 - 1) \times 100\% = 200\% $$ **Summary:** - **Number of blocks:** **16,384** - **Total storage:** **6 TB** - **Overhead:** **200%** (extra storage used for fault tolerance) --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap** — Converting between **GB, TB, and MB** incorrectly. Always show explicit unit conversions. > 2. **Ceiling Function Ignorance** — Forgetting to apply $\lceil \rceil$. A file of **128.1 MB** still requires **2 blocks**, not 1. > 3. **Replication Confusion** — The default is **3** unless specified; do not assume 2. > 4. **Block Size Default Trap** — Hadoop 1.x uses **64 MB**, Hadoop 2.x+ uses **128 MB** (most questions assume 128 MB). > 5. **NameNode vs DataNode Mix-up** — NameNode stores **metadata only**, never the actual data. > 6. **ACID vs BASE Confusion** — Hadoop is **NOT ACID-compliant**; it follows the **BASE** model (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency). --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU):** - ✅ **Big Data = Problem**, **Hadoop = Solution** — the core relationship. - ✅ **3Vs of Big Data:** **Volume, Velocity, Variety** (extended to 6Vs: + **Veracity, Value, Variability**). - ✅ **Hadoop's 3 Layers:** **Hadoop Common, HDFS, MapReduce (+ YARN)**. - ✅ **Default HDFS Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+). - ✅ **Default Replication Factor:** **3**. - ✅ **Heartbeat Interval:** **3 seconds**; **Block Report Interval:** **6 hours**. - ✅ **NameNode = Metadata**; **DataNode = Actual Data Blocks**. - ✅ **Master-Slave Architecture** applies to both HDFS and YARN. - ✅ **MapReduce has 2 phases:** **Map (transformation) + Reduce (aggregation)**. - ✅ **Horizontal scaling** (add more nodes) vs **vertical scaling** (bigger machine). - ✅ Hadoop is **BASE**, not **ACID**. - ✅ Hadoop is **schema-on-read**, RDBMS is **schema-on-write**. - ✅ Block count formula: $N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size}}{\text{Block Size}} \right\rceil$ - ✅ Total storage: $S_{total} = \text{File Size} \times R_f$ - ✅ **Amdahl's Law** governs practical MapReduce speedup. - ✅ Real-world users: **Yahoo, Facebook, Twitter, Netflix** — all run Hadoop. - ✅ Hadoop is **inspired by Google's GFS + MapReduce papers (2003–2004)**. - ✅ **Open source** under **Apache License 2.0**.

topics.title: Big Data Generation - Enterprise Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Big Data Generation - Enterprise Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enterprise Data** refers to the vast, structured, semi-structured, and unstructured information generated, collected, and processed by an organization through its daily operations, transactions, business processes, customer interactions, and internal/external communications.

**Big Data Generation in the Enterprise Context** is the continuous, high-velocity, high-volume production of digital information originating from internal organizational systems (ERP, CRM, SCM), transactional databases, sensor networks, web logs, social media, and external partner ecosystems. In KTU 2024 terminology, this is the *first stage* of the Big Data lifecycle — **Data Inception** — where raw signals are born.

### Categories of Enterprise Data (KTU Classification)

| # | Data Type | Description | Example |
|---|-----------|-------------|---------|
| 1 | **Transactional Data** | Records of business events/operations | POS sales, ATM withdrawals, orders |
| 2 | **Master Data** | Core business entities shared across systems | Customer IDs, product catalogs, vendors |
| 3 | **Reference Data** | Data used to categorize or classify | Country codes, currency tables |
| 4 | **Metadata** | Data about data | Schema, indexes, file timestamps |
| 5 | **Log Data** | System/application event records | Server logs, audit trails |
| 6 | **Sensor / IoT Data** | Machine-generated telemetry | RFID, smart meters, GPS |
| 7 | **Social / Unstructured Data** | Human-generated content | Emails, tweets, reviews |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Enterprise Data is the *primary* contributor to the **Three V's of Big Data** — Volume, Velocity, and Variety. A modern enterprise can generate **Terabytes to Petabytes per day**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Factory"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a multinational company as a **Digital Factory**.

Imagine a factory producing physical goods:
- **Assembly lines** (transactional systems) produce cars (transactions) every second.
- **Quality control logs** (audit logs) record every defect.
- **Supplier records** (master data) catalog every part.
- **CCTV footage** (sensor data) runs 24/7.
- **Customer complaint letters** (unstructured data) pour in from email and social media.

Each of these "outputs" is **Enterprise Data**. A factory's output is measured in units/hour; a modern enterprise's data output is measured in **GB/sec**. Just as a factory manager aggregates outputs to optimize production, a data engineer aggregates enterprise data to derive **business intelligence**.

---

## 1.3 Why Enterprise Data Matters in Big Data Analytics

1. **Single Largest Source of Structured Data** — Most enterprise data arrives in tabular form, ideal for analytics.
2. **High Signal-to-Noise Ratio** — Unlike social media, enterprise data is purposeful and traceable.
3. **Compliance & Governance** — Subject to regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, SOX), making data lineage critical.
4. **Direct Revenue Impact** — Patterns in enterprise data directly influence profit, loss, and forecasting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (KTU 2024):** A medium-scale enterprise generates approximately **1 TB of new data per day**, with **80%** being unstructured (emails, logs, media) and **20%** structured (databases).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Enterprise Data Generation Pipeline

Enterprise data does not "appear" — it follows a **deterministic lifecycle**. Understanding this pipeline is a high-weightage KTU topic.

### Stage-by-Stage Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Data Inception (Source)**
- Data is *born* at operational touchpoints: cash registers, web servers, IoT sensors, employee endpoints.
- Each event is timestamped and assigned a unique identifier.

**Stage 2 — Data Capture (Ingestion)**
- Events are captured via **APIs, message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ), change-data-capture (CDC) streams**, or batch uploads.
- Capture must preserve **atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability (ACID)**.

**Stage 3 — Data Storage (Persistence)**
- Stored in **relational databases (RDBMS), data warehouses, data lakes, or distributed file systems (HDFS)**.
- Modern enterprises use **polyglot persistence** — multiple storage types for different data kinds.

**Stage 4 — Data Processing (Transformation)**
- **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** or **ELT (Extract, Load, Transform)** pipelines.
- Operations include cleaning, normalization, enrichment, aggregation.

**Stage 5 — Data Consumption (Analytics)**
- BI dashboards, ML models, real-time alerts, regulatory reports.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Distinguish between **OLTP (Online Transaction Processing)** and **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** systems.

| Feature | OLTP | OLAP |
|---------|------|------|
| **Purpose** | Day-to-day operations | Strategic analysis |
| **Queries** | Simple, fast, frequent | Complex, aggregative |
| **Data Volume** | GBs | TBs to PBs |
| **Schema** | Normalized (3NF) | Denormalized (Star/Snowflake) |
| **Users** | Clerks, front-line staff | Analysts, executives |
| **Examples** | Banking systems, POS | Data warehouses, BI tools |

---

## 2.2 Characteristics of Enterprise Data (The Three V's Applied)

**1. Volume**
Enterprise data scales with business size. Formula for estimating enterprise data growth:

$$
V_{n} = V_0 \times (1 + r)^{n}
$$

Where:
- $V_{n}$ = Data volume after $n$ years
- $V_0$ = Initial data volume
- $r$ = Annual growth rate (typically **0.25 to 0.60** for modern enterprises)
- $n$ = Number of years

**2. Velocity**
Data generation rate. Modern enterprises generate data in:
- **Batch mode** (daily/weekly dumps)
- **Near-real-time** (minute-level)
- **Real-time streaming** (millisecond-level, e.g., stock trading, ride-sharing)

$$
\text{Velocity} = \frac{\Delta \text{Data Volume}}{\Delta t} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{GB}}{\text{sec}} \right]
$$

**3. Variety**
Enterprise data exists in:
- **Structured** (relational tables, CSV, XML schemas)
- **Semi-structured** (JSON, YAML, NoSQL documents)
- **Unstructured** (images, video, free-text logs)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Metric |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Data Volume Growth | $V_n = V_0 (1 + r)^n$ | TB / PB |
| 2 | Data Velocity | $V = \Delta D / \Delta t$ | GB/sec |
| 3 | Structured Data Ratio | $S_r = \frac{D_{\text{struct}}}{D_{\text{total}}}$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | Unstructured Data Ratio | $U_r = 1 - S_r$ | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Storage Cost | $C_s = V_n \times c_{\text{per\_TB}}$ | Currency |
| 6 | Daily Data Generation | $D_d = N_t \times \bar{s}$ | GB/day |
| 7 | Annual Data Growth Rate | $r = \left( \frac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}} \right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Throughput Requirement | $T_p = \frac{D_{\text{peak}}}{\Delta t_{\text{allowed}}}$ | Records/sec |

> Where: $N_t$ = number of transactions/day, $\bar{s}$ = average size per record, $c_{\text{per\_TB}}$ = cost per terabyte.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **capacity planning, infrastructure budgeting, SLA design, and disaster-recovery sizing** for enterprise data centers.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Banking:** Enterprises use real-time enterprise data for **fraud detection** (velocity-driven analytics).
- **Retail:** OLAP cubes over enterprise sales data enable **demand forecasting**.
- **Healthcare:** Enterprise EHR (Electronic Health Records) systems feed Big Data platforms for **predictive diagnostics**.
- **Manufacturing:** IoT sensor data combined with ERP records enables **predictive maintenance**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Enterprise Data Volume Growth Projection

### Problem Statement
A retail enterprise starts with **$V_0 = 2$ TB** of data in 2024. The annual growth rate is **$r = 0.40$** (40% per year). Compute the data volume after **5 years** and identify the storage tier required.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

$$
V_{n} = V_0 \times (1 + r)^{n}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known parameters.**

Given: $V_0 = 2$ TB, $r = 0.40$, $n = 5$ years.

$$
V_{5} = 2 \times (1 + 0.40)^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the base of the exponent.**

$$
1 + 0.40 = 1.40
$$

**Step 4 — Raise to the 5th power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.40)^{5} &= 1.40 \times 1.40 \times 1.40 \times 1.40 \times 1.40 \\
&= 1.96 \times 1.40 \times 1.40 \times 1.40 \\
&= 2.744 \times 1.40 \times 1.40 \\
&= 3.8416 \times 1.40 \\
&= 5.37824
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by initial volume.**

$$
V_{5} = 2 \times 5.37824 = 10.75648 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 6 — Round and interpret.**

$$
V_{5} \approx 10.76 \text{ TB}
$$

> **Interpretation:** In 5 years, the enterprise will need storage exceeding **10 TB** — sufficient to require a **mid-tier data warehouse** (Hadoop cluster with at least **3 data nodes**).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Daily Data Generation Rate

### Problem Statement
An e-commerce platform processes **$N_t = 500{,}000$** transactions per day. Each transaction record averages **$\bar{s} = 4$ KB** in size. Compute the daily data generation, annual data generation, and required I/O throughput.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Use the daily data generation formula.**

$$
D_d = N_t \times \bar{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (ensure unit consistency).**

$$
D_d = 500{,}000 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GB.**

$$
D_d = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 1.907 \text{ GB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute annual data generation.**

$$
D_{\text{year}} = 1.907 \times 365 \approx 696.06 \text{ GB/year} \approx 0.68 \text{ TB/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute peak I/O throughput (assume 8-hour business window).**

$$
T_p = \frac{D_d \times 1024 \text{ MB}}{8 \times 3600 \text{ sec}} = \frac{1953.13 \text{ MB}}{28800 \text{ sec}} \approx 0.068 \text{ MB/sec}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The system requires approximately **68 KB/sec sustained throughput** — well within a single modern SSD's capability, but the analytics layer may need 10–100× more bandwidth.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — OLTP vs OLAP Trade-off

| Parameter | OLTP (Enterprise Source) | OLAP (Enterprise Analytics) |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Workload Type** | Write-heavy, read-light | Read-heavy, write-light |
| **Data Freshness** | Real-time (milliseconds) | Near-real-time (minutes–hours) |
| **Query Complexity** | < 10 joins | 10–50 joins, aggregations |
| **Concurrency** | Thousands of users | Tens of analysts |
| **Storage Cost** | Low (normalized, compressed) | High (denormalized, indexed) |
| **KTU Weightage** | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Use Case** | Order entry, ATM | Sales trend, KPI dashboard |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Structured vs Unstructured Data Ratio

### Problem Statement
An enterprise generates **15 GB/day** of total data. Of this, **JSON logs (semi-structured)** comprise 5 GB and **emails (unstructured)** comprise 7 GB. The remaining is structured. Compute the structured ratio $S_r$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute structured data volume.**

$$
D_{\text{struct}} = D_{\text{total}} - D_{\text{semi}} - D_{\text{unstruct}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
D_{\text{struct}} = 15 - 5 - 7 = 3 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio.**

$$
S_r = \frac{3}{15} = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

$$
S_r = 20\%, \quad U_r = 80\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** This enterprise is **unstructured-dominant** (80%), indicating the need for a **data lake** (HDFS + object storage) rather than a traditional RDBMS-only warehouse.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Enterprise Data Generation Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC1[Transactional Systems OLTP] --> CAP[Data Capture Layer]
    SRC2[ERP and CRM Platforms] --> CAP
    SRC3[IoT and Sensor Networks] --> CAP
    SRC4[Web and Mobile Apps] --> CAP
    SRC5[Social Media and Email] --> CAP
    CAP --> VAL[Validation and Cleansing]
    VAL --> STR[Structured Storage RDBMS]
    VAL --> SEM[Semi-Structured Storage NoSQL]
    VAL => UNS[Unstructured Storage Data Lake]
    STR --> ETL[ETL ELT Pipelines]
    SEM --> ETL
    UNS --> ETL
    ETL => DWH[Data Warehouse]
    ETL => DL[Data Lake]
    DWH --> BI[BI Dashboards]
    DL --> ML[Machine Learning Models]
    DWH --> REG[Regulatory Reports]
    DL --> ML
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The leftmost nodes are **data sources**; the rightmost nodes are **consumers**. The central pipeline (Capture → Cleanse → Store → Transform → Consume) is the canonical **Enterprise Data Lifecycle**.

---

## 4.2 The Three V's of Big Data in Enterprise Context

```mermaid
graph LR
    V[Enterprise Data Generation] --> V1[VOLUME Terabytes to Petabytes]
    V --> V2[VELOCITY Real time to Batch]
    V --> V3[VARIETY Structured Semi Unstructured]
    V1 --> EX1[Example Walmart 2.5 PB of customer data]
    V2 --> EX2[Example Stock trading microsecond latency]
    V3 --> EX3[Example JSON logs CSV tables video files]
```

---

## 4.3 OLTP to OLAP Transition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OLTP Operational DB] --> B[CDC Change Data Capture]
    B --> C[Staging Area]
    C --> D[ETL Engine]
    D --> E[OLAP Data Warehouse]
    E --> F[BI Reports]
    E --> G[Predictive Analytics]
    E --> H[Ad hoc SQL Queries]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** The **CDC (Change Data Capture)** stage is critical — it enables **near-real-time data synchronization** between transactional systems (sources) and analytical systems (targets) without disrupting the OLTP workload.

---

## 4.4 Enterprise Data Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TB
    ROOT[Enterprise Data Universe] --> T1[Structured Data]
    ROOT --> T2[Semi Structured Data]
    ROOT --> T3[Unstructured Data]
    T1 --> S1[RDBMS Tables]
    T1 --> S2[Spreadsheets]
    T1 --> S3[XML Files with Schema]
    T2 --> S4[JSON Documents]
    T2 --> S5[NoSQL Key Value Stores]
    T2 --> S6[Log Files]
    T3 --> S7[Images and Video]
    T3 --> S8[Audio Recordings]
    T3 --> S9[Free Text Emails]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Enterprise Data. List any four sources of enterprise data in a modern organization.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Enterprise Data is the collective information generated, stored, and managed by an organization through its business operations, transactions, and interactions with customers, employees, and systems.

**Four Sources of Enterprise Data:**

1. **Transactional Systems (OLTP):** Sales records, banking transactions, order entries.
2. **Customer Relationship Management (CRM):** Customer profiles, interaction history, support tickets.
3. **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP):** Inventory, procurement, HR records, finance ledgers.
4. **IoT / Sensor Networks:** Machine telemetry, RFID scans, smart-meter readings.

> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for sources with examples)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between OLTP and OLAP systems. Give one example use case for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | OLTP | OLAP |
|---------|------|------|
| **Purpose** | Process daily business transactions | Perform complex analytical queries |
| **Data Type** | Current, operational | Historical, aggregated |
| **Schema** | Highly normalized (3NF) | Denormalized (Star/Snowflake) |
| **Workload** | Read + Write intensive | Read-dominant |
| **Example** | ATM cash withdrawal processing | Quarterly sales trend analysis by region |

**Example Use Cases:**
- **OLTP:** Processing a credit card payment at a retail POS terminal.
- **OLAP:** Generating a year-over-year revenue dashboard for the CFO.

> *(3 marks: 2 for table, 1 for examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Enterprise Data** in the context of Big Data Analytics. Discuss the **Three V's (Volume, Velocity, Variety)** as applied to enterprise-generated data, with one industry example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An enterprise starts with **$V_0 = 5$ TB** of data. The data grows at **$r = 50\%$ per year**. Calculate the data volume after **4 years**. If the cost of storage is **$c = ₹8{,}000$ per TB per year**, what is the annual storage cost at year 4? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a)

**Enterprise Data Definition:** Enterprise Data refers to all structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data created, collected, and managed within an organization's operational and analytical systems.

**The Three V's:**

**1. Volume — Size of Data Generated**
- Enterprise data scales with business activity.
- *Example:* **Walmart** handles over **2.5 PB** of customer transaction data.

**2. Velocity — Speed of Data Generation**
- Modern enterprises generate data in real-time (streaming) or near-real-time.
- *Example:* **Stock exchanges** generate **microsecond-level** trade data requiring real-time analytics.

**3. Variety — Diversity of Data Types**
- Includes structured (databases), semi-structured (JSON logs), and unstructured (video, email).
- *Example:* **Healthcare enterprises** store X-ray images (unstructured), patient records (structured), and HL7 messages (semi-structured) in the same ecosystem.

> **Conclusion:** Understanding the Three V's helps enterprises architect the right storage, processing, and analytics infrastructure.

> *(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for V's with examples, 2 for conclusion)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Given:** $V_0 = 5$ TB, $r = 0.50$, $n = 4$ years, $c = ₹8{,}000$/TB/year.

**Step 1 — Apply the volume growth formula.**

$$
V_n = V_0 \times (1 + r)^n
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
V_4 = 5 \times (1.50)^4
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1.50)^4 &= 1.50 \times 1.50 \times 1.50 \times 1.50 \\
&= 2.25 \times 2.25 \\
&= 5.0625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by initial volume.**

$$
V_4 = 5 \times 5.0625 = 25.3125 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute annual storage cost.**

$$
C_s = V_4 \times c = 25.3125 \times 8{,}000
$$

$$
C_s = ₹2{,}02{,}500 \text{ per year}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The enterprise will require approximately **25.31 TB** of storage by year 4, costing **₹2,02,500 per year**.

> *(7 marks: 2 for formula, 3 for computation, 2 for cost calculation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **Enterprise Data Generation Pipeline** with a neat block diagram. Explain each stage in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **OLTP and OLAP** systems across **six parameters**. An ATM processes **$N_t = 300$ transactions/hour**, each producing a log of **$\bar{s} = 2$ KB**. Calculate the **daily data generation in GB** assuming 12 working hours. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Enterprise Data Generation Pipeline — Five Stages:**

1. **Data Inception (Source Layer):** Data is *born* at operational touchpoints — POS systems, web servers, IoT sensors, employee endpoints. Each event is time-stamped.

2. **Data Capture (Ingestion Layer):** Events are captured using **message queues (Apache Kafka), APIs, change-data-capture (CDC) tools**. ACID properties are preserved.

3. **Data Storage (Persistence Layer):** Data is stored in **RDBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle), data warehouses (Snowflake, Redshift), or data lakes (HDFS, S3)**.

4. **Data Processing (Transformation Layer):** **ETL/ELT pipelines** perform cleaning, normalization, deduplication, and enrichment.

5. **Data Consumption (Analytics Layer):** Outputs feed **BI dashboards (Tableau, Power BI), ML models, and regulatory reports**.

> **Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 above.)*

> *(7 marks: 1 for diagram, 5 for stage explanations, 1 for conclusion)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Six-Parameter Comparison: OLTP vs OLAP**

| # | Parameter | OLTP | OLAP |
|---|-----------|------|------|
| 1 | Purpose | Operational processing | Analytical processing |
| 2 | Schema | Normalized (3NF) | Star / Snowflake |
| 3 | Query Type | Simple, indexed SELECTs | Complex aggregations, GROUP BY |
| 4 | Data Volume | MBs–GBs | TBs–PBs |
| 5 | Users | Thousands of front-line staff | Tens of analysts / executives |
| 6 | Latency | Milliseconds | Seconds to minutes |

**Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:** $N_t = 300$ transactions/hour, $\bar{s} = 2$ KB, working hours = 12.

**Step 1 — Compute total transactions per day.**

$$
N_{\text{daily}} = 300 \times 12 = 3{,}600 \text{ transactions}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute daily data volume in KB.**

$$
D_d = 3{,}600 \times 2 = 7{,}200 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to GB.**

$$
D_d = \frac{7{,}200}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 0.00687 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in MB for clarity.**

$$
D_d = \frac{7{,}200}{1024} \approx 7.03 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** The ATM generates approximately **7.03 MB (~0.00687 GB)** of log data per day.

> *(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 3 for calculation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing definitions:** "Enterprise Data" ≠ "Big Data" — Enterprise Data is a *source* of Big Data, not a synonym.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram:** In 7-mark questions, **no diagram = 1–2 mark deduction** if explicitly asked.
> 3. **Unit errors:** Forgetting to convert KB → MB → GB in numerical problems costs 1 full mark.
> 4. **Confusing OLTP/OLAP:** Students often swap their *examples* (e.g., citing a data warehouse as OLTP). The examiner strictly checks.
> 5. **Formula without substitution:** Writing $V_n = V_0(1+r)^n$ without plugging in values = partial credit only.
> 6. **Vague Three V's explanation:** Always pair each V with a **quantified industry example** to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Big Data Generation: Enterprise Data**

- ✅ **Enterprise Data** = data generated by an organization's operations, transactions, and interactions.
- ✅ **Three V's:** Volume (size), Velocity (speed), Variety (type).
- ✅ **OLTP** = transactional, normalized, write-heavy, real-time.
- ✅ **OLAP** = analytical, denormalized, read-heavy, batch/near-real-time.
- ✅ **Pipeline Stages:** Inception → Capture → Storage → Processing → Consumption.
- ✅ **Data Ratio Rule:** Typically **80% unstructured, 20% structured** in modern enterprises.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $V_n = V_0 \times (1 + r)^n$ for data growth projection.
- ✅ **Velocity Formula:** $V = \Delta D / \Delta t$ measured in GB/sec.
- ✅ **Data Storage Cost:** $C_s = V_n \times c_{\text{per\_TB}}$.
- ✅ **CDC (Change Data Capture):** Critical bridge between OLTP and OLAP systems.
- ✅ **Polyglot Persistence:** Modern enterprises use multiple storage types for different data kinds.
- ✅ **Regulatory Drivers:** GDPR, HIPAA, SOX mandate enterprise data governance and lineage.
- ✅ **Daily Data Metric:** A medium enterprise generates ~1 TB/day; large enterprise >100 TB/day.
- ✅ **Diagram Rule:** Always include a labeled block diagram in 7-mark theory questions.
- ✅ **Unit Conversion:** 1 TB = 1024 GB = $1024^2$ MB = $1024^3$ KB.
- ✅ **Examples to Memorize:** Walmart (Volume), Stock Exchange (Velocity), Healthcare (Variety).

---

**End of Module 2 — Big Data Generation: Enterprise Data | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: IoT Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoT Data** refers to the large volumes of heterogeneous, time-series, and high-velocity information generated by physical devices, sensors, actuators, and embedded systems that are interconnected through the Internet of Things (IoT). In the context of the **KTU 2024 Big Data Analytics syllabus**, IoT data represents one of the most prominent *real-world sources* of Big Data, characterized by continuous streaming behavior, machine-generated origin, and a need for real-time (or near-real-time) analytics.

From a rigorous standpoint, IoT Data can be formally defined as:

> **IoT Data = f(Sensor Streams, Time-Stamped Events, Environmental Readings, Machine-to-Machine (M2M) Transmissions)**

where the function $f$ represents the ingestion, normalization, and contextualization of raw sensor observations into analytically meaningful datasets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT755 – Module 2):**
> IoT Data is a *primary data source* that demonstrates the **5 V's of Big Data** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value. Students must be able to identify how each "V" manifests in IoT ecosystems.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **smart city hospital**. Thousands of wearable health devices monitor patients continuously — each one records heart rate, oxygen saturation, body temperature, and blood pressure every second. All these numbers are *IoT Data*. They pour in like a never-ending river of measurements.

Now, this data has three distinct features that distinguish it from ordinary business data:

1. **It is produced by machines, not humans** (so it never sleeps, never stops, and arrives in a constant stream).
2. **It is time-sensitive** (an abnormal heart rate detected *now* matters far more than the same reading stored for weeks).
3. **It is structurally diverse** (a temperature sensor sends a single float value, a camera sends a video frame, a smart meter sends a JSON payload).

In the Big Data Analytics course, IoT Data is studied because it is the *practical testbed* where streaming, real-time, and distributed analytics techniques (covered across Modules 1–5) are actually deployed in the industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world scale benchmark:** A single modern commercial jet engine generates **~10 TB of data per hour**. A smart factory can generate **>1 PB of IoT data per day**. These numbers are essential for KTU numerical/estimation questions.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical/Engineering Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sensor sampling rate | **1 Hz – 100 kHz** | samples/sec |
| IoT data growth (global) | **~79.4 ZB by 2025** | Zettabytes |
| Latency tolerance (healthcare) | **< 100 ms** | milliseconds |
| Edge device data buffer | **8 MB – 2 GB** | memory |
| MQTT message size | **< 128 B** | bytes |
| CoAP payload size | **< 1 KB** | bytes |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, the **Nyquist sampling rate** ($f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$) is the most frequently used formula when estimating IoT data volume from sensor frequency.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five V's of IoT Data — Structured Logic Breakdown

### V-1. Volume (Sheer Scale)

- **Why:** IoT deployments often involve *thousands to millions* of sensors deployed geographically.
- **How:** Each device pushes readings at fixed intervals, multiplying into massive aggregate volume.
- **Example:** 1 million sensors × 100 bytes/reading × 1 reading/sec ≈ **28 GB/sec**, ≈ **2.4 TB/day** (excluding redundancy).

### V-2. Velocity (Speed of Arrival)

- **Why:** Many IoT use-cases (autonomous vehicles, industrial control, healthcare) demand *real-time* response.
- **How:** Stream processing engines (Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Spark Streaming) handle continuous inflows.
- **Bursty behavior:** Peaks can be **10×–100×** the average rate.

### V-3. Variety (Heterogeneity)

- **Why:** IoT spans *cross-domain* devices — temperature, image, audio, vibration, GPS, video.
- **How:** Schema-on-read approaches (NoSQL, data lakes) accommodate unstructured/semi-structured payloads.

### V-4. Veracity (Trustworthiness)

- **Why:** Sensors drift, fail, get tampered with, and produce noisy/erroneous data.
- **How:** Statistical outlier detection, Kalman filtering, and digital-twin cross-validation are applied.

### V-5. Value (Actionable Insight)

- **Why:** Raw bytes are useless — only *derived insights* (predictive maintenance alerts, anomaly flags) yield ROI.
- **How:** ML/AI models (classification, clustering, forecasting) run on cleansed IoT streams.

---

## 2.2 Architecture of IoT Data — Layered View

The standard **four-layer IoT data architecture** used in KTU textbooks:

| Layer | Function | Typical Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Physical sensors & actuators collect data | MEMS sensors, RFID, GPS |
| **Network Layer** | Data transmission | Wi-Fi, 5G, LoRa, MQTT, CoAP |
| **Processing Layer** | Ingestion, cleaning, aggregation | Kafka, Flink, Spark Streaming |
| **Application Layer** | Analytics & decision-making | Dashboards, ML models, alerts |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and form the *core estimation toolkit* for KTU 2024 numericals in Module 2.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = N \times R \times S \times T$ | Total IoT data volume (N devices, R readings/sec, S bytes/reading, T seconds) | bytes |
| 2 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling theorem | Hz |
| 3 | $D = \frac{1}{f_s}$ | Time between consecutive samples | seconds |
| 4 | $\lambda = \frac{\text{events}}{\text{time window}}$ | Arrival rate (Poisson model) | events/sec |
| 5 | $B = R \times S$ | Bandwidth requirement per device | bps |
| 6 | $B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i$ | Aggregate bandwidth across N devices | bps |
| 7 | $L = t_{process} - t_{arrive}$ | End-to-end processing latency | seconds |
| 8 | $T_{p} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | M/M/1 queue processing time (Little's Law) | seconds |
| 9 | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \frac{S_{original}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Storage efficiency factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Storage} = V \times \text{retention days}$ | Total storage required | bytes |

**Important constraints / boundary conditions:**

- For real-time IoT: $L \leq 1$ second (typical KTU benchmark).
- MQTT QoS levels: 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once).
- Edge vs Cloud split is determined by the **latency budget** $L_{budget}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

IoT data analytics is the cornerstone of:
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Predictive maintenance reduces downtime by 30–50%.
- **Smart Healthcare:** Continuous patient monitoring cuts ICU readmission rates.
- **Smart Grids:** Real-time load balancing prevents blackouts.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Lidar + camera fusion streams require < 100 ms latency.
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil-moisture and drone imagery drive irrigation optimization.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — IoT Data Volume Estimation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A smart factory deploys **5,000 vibration sensors**. Each sensor samples at **2 kHz**, with each sample being a **4-byte float**. The factory runs **24/7**. Calculate:
1. Total data volume produced per day.
2. Aggregate bandwidth required.
3. Required storage for **30 days** of retention (assume no compression).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $N = 5000$ sensors
- $f_s = 2 \text{ kHz} = 2000$ samples/sec
- $S = 4$ bytes/sample
- $T = 24 \times 3600 = 86400$ seconds/day

**Step 1 — Verify Nyquist Compliance:**
The highest frequency component $f_{max}$ in industrial vibration is typically < 1 kHz.

$$
f_s = 2000 \text{ Hz} \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 1000 = 2000 \text{ Hz}
$$

Nyquist condition is *just satisfied* (borderline). In practice, the engineer would raise $f_s$ to 4 kHz.

**Step 2 — Daily Volume per Sensor:**

$$
V_{sensor} = R \times S \times T = 2000 \times 4 \times 86400
$$

$$
V_{sensor} = 2000 \times 345600 = 691{,}200{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to MB:

$$
V_{sensor} = \frac{691{,}200{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 659.18 \text{ MB/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate Daily Volume:**

$$
V_{total} = N \times V_{sensor} = 5000 \times 659.18 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
V_{total} \approx 3{,}295{,}900 \text{ MB/day} \approx 3.14 \text{ TB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Aggregate Bandwidth:**

$$
B_{total} = N \times R \times S = 5000 \times 2000 \times 4
$$

$$
B_{total} = 40{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/sec} = 40 \text{ MB/sec} = 320 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Storage for 30 Days:**

$$
\text{Storage} = V_{total} \times 30 = 3.14 \times 30 \approx 94.2 \text{ TB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> 1. Daily Volume ≈ **3.14 TB/day**
> 2. Bandwidth ≈ **320 Mbps**
> 3. 30-day Storage ≈ **94.2 TB**

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Latency & Queueing (M/M/1 Model)

**Problem Statement:**
An IoT gateway processes IoT events at a service rate $\mu = 200$ events/sec. The arrival rate is $\lambda = 150$ events/sec. Calculate the average time spent in the system using Little's Law / M/M/1 model.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 150$ events/sec
- $\mu = 200$ events/sec
- System stability requires $\mu > \lambda$ (✓ satisfied).

**Step 1 — Utilization Factor:**

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75
$$

**Step 2 — Average Time in System:**

$$
T_p = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{200 - 150} = \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
T_p = 0.02 \text{ seconds} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This **20 ms** figure is well within the real-time IoT budget (< 100 ms). If $\lambda$ rose to **190 events/sec**, then $T_p = 0.1$ sec, dangerously close to the edge of acceptability.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Edge vs Cloud

| Parameter | Edge Processing | Cloud Processing |
|---|---|---|
| Latency requirement | **< 10 ms** (critical) | > 100 ms acceptable |
| Data volume per device | Small (< 1 MB/s) | Large (> 10 MB/s) |
| Connectivity | Intermittent/poor | Stable broadband |
| Privacy sensitivity | High (healthcare, defense) | Low (public data) |
| Computational complexity | Lightweight inference (TinyML) | Heavy training (deep learning) |
| Cost model | CapEx on edge hardware | OpEx on cloud VMs |

---

## 3.4 Compressed Storage Estimation

**Problem:**
The 3.14 TB/day from Example 1 is compressed using **z-standard (zstd)** with a compression ratio of **3.5×**. Find the new daily storage.

$$
V_{compressed} = \frac{V_{total}}{CR} = \frac{3.14}{3.5} \approx 0.897 \text{ TB/day}
$$

For 30 days:

$$
\text{Storage}_{compressed} = 0.897 \times 30 \approx 26.9 \text{ TB}
$$

This represents a **~71% storage saving** — a standard KTU optimization scenario.
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – July 2023]
**Q: List and briefly explain the characteristic features (5 V's) of IoT Data with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| V | Feature | IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| Volume | Massive scale of data | 1 million smart meters in a city |
| Velocity | Real-time high-speed arrival | Lidar in autonomous car at 10 Hz |
| Variety | Multiple data formats | Temperature (float) + camera (image) + GPS (string) |
| Veracity | Noise, sensor errors | Faulty vibration sensor reporting constant 0 |
| Value | Deriving actionable insight | Predicting machine failure 7 days in advance |

*(1 mark for listing the V's, 1 mark for correct examples, 1 mark for crisp explanation.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Q: Differentiate between Edge Computing and Cloud Computing in the context of IoT data analytics. Mention any two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Near the data source | Centralized data center |
| Latency | Very low (< 10 ms) | Higher (50–500 ms) |
| Bandwidth use | Low (local processing) | High (raw data upload) |
| Cost | CapEx (hardware) | OpEx (subscription) |

**Two advantages of Edge:** (1) Reduced latency, (2) Lower bandwidth consumption. *(1 mark)*
**Two advantages of Cloud:** (1) Scalable storage, (2) Heavy ML training capability. *(1 mark)*
**Correct comparison with examples:** *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations for PECPT755, Module 2 carries one compulsory 14-mark question with internal choice. **Both alternatives are provided below** as Question A and Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the architecture of IoT Data Analytics with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart agricultural IoT system has **2,000 soil-moisture sensors**. Each sensor sends a reading of **8 bytes** every **30 seconds**. Compute:
1. The total daily data volume generated. **(2 marks)**
2. The aggregate bandwidth required. **(2 marks)**
3. The storage needed for **90 days** of retention, with a compression ratio of **4:1**. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The **four-layer IoT data analytics architecture** is:

1. **Perception Layer (1 mark):** Contains physical sensors (soil moisture, temperature, humidity) and actuators. *Role:* Convert physical phenomena into electrical signals/digital readings.

2. **Network Layer (2 marks):** Handles transmission using protocols like **MQTT** (lightweight pub/sub for low-bandwidth), **CoAP** (RESTful for constrained devices), **HTTP/HTTPS**, **5G/LPWAN**. *Role:* Reliable and secure transport.

3. **Processing Layer (2 marks):** Ingestion via **Apache Kafka**, real-time processing via **Spark Streaming / Flink**, storage in **HDFS, S3, Cassandra, InfluxDB**. *Role:* Cleansing, transformation, windowed aggregation.

4. **Application Layer (2 marks):** Hosts **ML models, dashboards (Grafana, Tableau), alert engines, decision support systems**. *Role:* Convert data into actionable insights (irrigation scheduling, yield prediction).

> [!TIP]
> **Block diagram should be drawn for full 7 marks.** Use the Mermaid architecture in Section 4.2 as reference.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $N = 2000$, $S = 8$ bytes, sampling interval $\Delta t = 30$ s.

**Step 1 — Sampling rate per sensor:**

$$
R = \frac{1}{\Delta t} = \frac{1}{30} \approx 0.0333 \text{ readings/sec}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily data per sensor:**

$$
V_{sensor} = R \times S \times T = 0.0333 \times 8 \times 86400
$$

$$
V_{sensor} \approx 23{,}040 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 22.5 \text{ KB/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate daily volume (Part 1 — 2 marks):**

$$
V_{total} = N \times V_{sensor} = 2000 \times 22{,}500 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
V_{total} \approx 45{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 42.9 \text{ MB/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Bandwidth (Part 2 — 2 marks):**

$$
B_{total} = N \times R \times S = 2000 \times 0.0333 \times 8
$$

$$
B_{total} \approx 533 \text{ bytes/sec} \approx 4.26 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Storage for 90 days (Part 3 — 3 marks):**

Uncompressed storage:

$$
V_{90} = 42.9 \text{ MB/day} \times 90 \approx 3861 \text{ MB} \approx 3.77 \text{ GB}
$$

With compression ratio CR = 4:

$$
V_{compressed} = \frac{3.77}{4} \approx 0.94 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the #1 cause of lost marks. Always convert KB ↔ MB ↔ GB using $1024$, not $1000$ (unless SI decimal is specified).
> 2. **Sampling interval vs sampling rate confusion:** Interval = 30 sec, Rate = 1/30 Hz. Reversing this gives a wrong answer by a factor of 900.
> 3. **Compression ratio direction:** CR = 4 means original is 4× larger. Divide, do not multiply.
> 4. **Diagram quality:** In 7-mark architecture questions, a missing block diagram costs **at least 2 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Discuss the challenges in handling IoT Big Data. Explain at least **six challenges** with brief justifications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of the **MQTT protocol** for IoT data communication. Compare MQTT with **CoAP** on the parameters: (i) Transport, (ii) Message size, (iii) QoS levels, (iv) Power consumption. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Six Key Challenges in IoT Big Data:**

1. **Data Volume Management (1 mark):** Petabyte-scale storage and high-throughput pipelines needed; classical RDBMS cannot scale.

2. **Real-time Processing (1.5 marks):** Latency-critical applications (surgery robots, stock trading) need sub-second response. Batch processing is too slow.

3. **Data Quality & Noise (1 mark):** Sensor drift, missing values, duplicates; requires statistical cleansing and imputation.

4. **Heterogeneity (Schema) (1 mark):** Devices from different vendors use proprietary formats → requires schema-on-read approaches.

5. **Security & Privacy (1.5 marks):** IoT devices are often vulnerable entry points; HIPAA/GDPR compliance for sensitive data.

6. **Network Reliability (1 mark):** Intermittent connectivity (rural, mobile, underground) demands buffering, store-and-forward, and edge caching.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) Working (3 marks):**

```
[Publisher Sensor] → [MQTT Broker] → [Subscriber Analytics App]
        ↑                    ↓
        └── Topic: "factory/temp" ──┘
```

- **Publisher-Subscriber (Pub/Sub)** model.
- Lightweight, header as small as **2 bytes**.
- Runs over **TCP** on port **1883** (or **8883** for TLS).
- Three QoS levels: 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once).

**MQTT vs CoAP Comparison (4 marks — 1 each):**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Message size | Up to 256 MB | < 1 KB (typical) |
| QoS levels | 3 (0, 1, 2) | 2 (Confirmable, Non-confirmable) |
| Power consumption | Moderate | Very low (suitable for constrained nodes) |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Mark-Loss Warning (Module-Wide)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks in IoT Data Questions:**
> 1. ❌ Confusing **MQTT** (TCP) with **CoAP** (UDP) — *a 1-mark killer*.
> 2. ❌ Using $1000$ instead of $1024$ for binary unit conversions (KB/MB/GB).
> 3. ❌ Forgetting the **Nyquist factor of 2** in sampling rate calculations.
> 4. ❌ Writing "real-time" without specifying the **latency threshold** (e.g., < 100 ms).
> 5. ❌ Not drawing a **neat, labeled block diagram** in architecture questions — *costs 2–3 marks*.
> 6. ❌ Omitting **units** in final numerical answers — strict KTU penalization.
> 7. ❌ Mixing up the **M/M/1 formula** $T_p = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ with Little's Law $L = \lambda W$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoT Data (Module 2):**

- ⭐ IoT data is **machine-generated, continuous, time-series, and high-velocity**.
- ⭐ The **5 V's** are Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.
- ⭐ **Four-layer architecture:** Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- ⭐ **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — always check before volume calculations.
- ⭐ **Volume formula:** $V = N \times R \times S \times T$.
- ⭐ **Bandwidth:** $B = R \times S$ per device, summed across N devices.
- ⭐ **M/M/1 latency:** $T_p = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ (stability: $\mu > \lambda$).
- ⭐ **Edge vs Cloud:** Edge = low latency, privacy; Cloud = heavy compute, scale.
- ⭐ **MQTT** = TCP, pub/sub, 3 QoS levels, header 2 bytes.
- ⭐ **CoAP** = UDP, RESTful, designed for constrained nodes.
- ⭐ **Compression** reduces storage linearly by ratio; e.g., CR=4 → storage = original / 4.
- ⭐ **Real-time IoT latency budget** = typically < 100 ms; ultra-critical = < 10 ms.
- ⭐ **Common protocols:** MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP, LoRaWAN.
- ⭐ **Stream processors for IoT:** Apache Kafka, Apache Flink, Spark Streaming, Apache Storm.
- ⭐ **Storage systems:** HDFS (batch), InfluxDB / TimescaleDB (time-series), Cassandra (wide-column), S3 (lake).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internet Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internet Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Internet Data** refers to the vast, heterogeneous, and continuously generated corpus of digital information that flows across, originates from, or is hosted upon the global Internet infrastructure. In the context of Big Data Analytics (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECPT755), Internet Data is formally classified as a primary **unstructured / semi-structured data source** that exhibits all canonical Big Data characteristics — the **5 V's**: **Volume**, **Velocity**, **Variety**, **Veracity**, and **Value**.

From a KTU board-examination perspective, Internet Data encompasses data acquired from:
- **Web pages** (HTML / DOM trees)
- **Web Services & APIs** (REST / SOAP endpoints returning JSON / XML)
- **Social Media Platforms** (tweets, posts, comments, likes, shares)
- **IoT / Sensor Streams** (telemetry over MQTT, CoAP)
- **Logs & Clickstreams** (server logs, browser events)
- **Open Data Repositories** (government, scientific, financial)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECPT755 – Module 2):** Internet Data is treated as the *external ingestion layer* of the Big Data pipeline. Students must be able to *identify the data format*, *select an appropriate acquisition technique*, and *justify the technique using the 5V framework*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the Internet as a **giant, ever-flowing river** (the *Velocity* dimension). On its banks sit **countless fish markets** — each market (Facebook, Twitter, weather sensors, e-commerce sites) stocks a *different kind of fish* in *different-shaped baskets* (the *Variety* dimension). The **total weight of fish** caught in a single day is staggering (the *Volume* dimension). However, **not all fish are fresh or safe to eat** — some are stale, mislabeled, or poisonous (the *Veracity* dimension). A Big Data analyst is essentially a **fisherman** who must build nets (crawlers, scrapers, API connectors), refrigeration trucks (Hadoop, Spark), and processing factories (analytics algorithms) to convert this raw, messy, massive catch into **nutritious, sellable product** (the *Value* dimension).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Scale Reference (2024 benchmarks):**
> - **Google processes ~100,000 GB of data every 4 minutes** (~99,000,000 GB/day).
> - **Twitter/X generates ~500 million tweets per day**.
> - **Facebook ingests ~4 petabytes of new data daily**.
> - **Global IoT devices exceed 18 billion**, each producing continuous telemetry.

## 1.3 The 5V Framework Applied to Internet Data

| Dimension | Definition | Internet Data Manifestation |
|-----------|------------|------------------------------|
| **Volume** | Scale of data (PB–ZB) | Global IP traffic crossed **~3.4 ZB/year** (Cisco, 2023) |
| **Velocity** | Speed of generation/processing | Real-time stock ticks at **microsecond** latency |
| **Variety** | Structural heterogeneity | HTML, JSON, XML, CSV, images, video, audio |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness / noise | Fake news, bots, broken HTML, spoofed IPs |
| **Value** | Business insight density | Sentiment → brand health; clickstream → conversion |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for 5V Examination Recall:** "**V**ery **V**ery **V**ery **V**ery **V**alid" → Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Layers of Internet Data

Internet Data acquisition follows a **four-layer reference architecture** in Big Data systems:

1. **Source Layer** — Where data is born (browsers, mobile apps, sensors, servers).
2. **Transport Layer** — Protocols carrying the data (HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, MQTT, WebSockets, AMQP).
3. **Acquisition Layer** — The "net" (Web Crawlers, API Callers, Scrapers, Message Brokers).
4. **Ingestion Layer** — The "loading dock" (Kafka, Flume, Logstash, NiFi, Sqoop).

### 2.1.1 Data Acquisition Techniques — Comparative Analysis

| Technique | Mechanism | Output Format | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|-----------|------------|
| **Web Crawling** | BFS/DFS traversal of hyperlinks | HTML pages (corpus) | Discovers new pages | Politeness, scale limits |
| **Web Scraping** | DOM/Regex extraction from known pages | Structured records (CSV/JSON) | Targeted, accurate | Brittle to layout changes |
| **REST API Polling** | Periodic HTTP GET to endpoints | JSON / XML | Stable, contractual | Rate limits, latency |
| **Streaming API (Webhooks/WebSockets)** | Persistent connection, push-based | Event stream (JSON) | Real-time, low-latency | Connection management overhead |
| **Message Queue Subscription** | Subscribe to topic (Kafka/MQTT) | Records | Decoupled, scalable | Requires broker infra |
| **RSS / Atom Feed** | Polled XML feed | XML items | Standardized, simple | Limited metadata |

### 2.1.2 HTTP Request–Response Lifecycle (Foundational)

Every internet data exchange is bound by the **HTTP protocol** semantics:

- **HTTP Methods:** `GET` (idempotent retrieval), `POST` (submit data), `PUT` (replace), `DELETE`, `PATCH`.
- **Status Codes:** `2xx` Success, `3xx` Redirection, `4xx` Client Error, `5xx` Server Error.
- **Headers:** `Content-Type`, `Authorization`, `User-Agent`, `Accept`, `RateLimit-Remaining`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **REST API Constraints (Roy Fielding, 2000):** Statelessness, Uniform Interface, Cacheability, Layered System, Code-on-Demand (optional). KTU examiners frequently test the distinction between **REST and SOAP**.

## 2.2 Data Formats on the Internet

| Format | Structure | Parser Cost | Use Case |
|--------|-----------|-------------|----------|
| **HTML** | Semi-structured (DOM) | High (need HTML parser) | Web pages |
| **JSON** | Semi-structured (key-value) | Low | API responses, NoSQL storage |
| **XML** | Semi-structured (tree) | Medium | SOAP, RSS, legacy enterprise |
| **CSV** | Structured (flat) | Very low | Tabular data export |
| **Parquet** | Columnar binary | Low (read-only relevant columns) | Big Data storage (HDFS) |
| **Avro** | Row-binary + schema | Low | Streaming (Kafka) |
| **Protobuf** | Binary, schema-evolution | Very low | gRPC, microservices |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **mathematically verifiable** and appear consistently in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers. **Bookmark this table.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Total Data Volume | $D_{total} = N \times \bar{s}$ | Bytes | $N$ = records, $\bar{s}$ = mean record size |
| 2 | Per-Second Ingestion Rate | $R = \dfrac{D_{total}}{T}$ | Bytes/sec (Bps) | $T$ = duration in seconds |
| 3 | Bandwidth Requirement (Shannon-aligned) | $BW = R \times (1 + \alpha)$ | Bps | $\alpha$ = protocol overhead fraction (typ. **0.10–0.20**) |
| 4 | Required Storage Capacity | $S = D_{total} \times r$ | Bytes | $r$ = replication factor (HDFS default **3**) |
| 5 | Effective Throughput (compressed) | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{R}{c}$ | Bps | $c$ = compression ratio (typ. **2–10×** for text) |
| 6 | API Polling Cadence | $f = \dfrac{Q}{N_{calls}}$ | Hz | $Q$ = quota, $N_{calls}$ = calls/window |
| 7 | Time to Crawl N pages | $T_{crawl} = \dfrac{N}{p \times t_{p}}$ | seconds | $p$ = parallelism, $t_p$ = avg page-fetch time |
| 8 | Crawler Politeness Delay | $d_{min} = \dfrac{1}{r_{req}}$ | seconds | $r_{req}$ = request rate to host (reqs/sec) |
| 9 | Sampling Size (95% CL, 5% error) | $n = \dfrac{1.96^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | records | $p$ = proportion, $e$ = margin of error |
| 10 | Cardinality (HyperLogLog est.) | $E = \alpha_m \cdot m^2 \cdot Z^{-1}$ | unique elements | $m$ = registers, $Z$ = harmonic mean of $2^{-M_j}$ |

### 2.3.1 Worked Example — API Rate-Limit Engineering

A Twitter API allows **900 requests / 15-min window** (per user token). You must scrape **100,000 tweets** with **100 tweets per request**.

$$
N_{calls} = \frac{100{,}000}{100} = 1{,}000 \text{ calls}
$$

$$
\text{Windows required} = \frac{1{,}000}{900} = 1.11 \Rightarrow 2 \text{ full windows}
$$

$$
T_{min} = 2 \times 15 \text{ min} = 30 \text{ min}
$$

Thus, **minimum safe scrape duration is 30 minutes** to remain within rate limits. This is a classic 7-mark KTU-style computation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **E-commerce:** Amazon scrapes competitor prices every **15 min** for dynamic pricing.
- **Healthcare:** Wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch) push **~1 KB/min per device** of vitals to cloud.
- **Finance:** Bloomberg terminals consume **Level-2 market data at ~10 MB/sec** per symbol.
- **Smart Cities:** Barcelona's IoT platform ingests **~120 GB/day** from 1.5M sensors.
- **Social Listening:** Brandwatch processes **~1.2 trillion social posts** historically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Internet Data Volume Over Time

Let us derive the **cumulative data volume** generated by a streaming internet source over a continuous period.

**Given:**
- Constant mean arrival rate: $\lambda$ (records per second)
- Mean record size: $\bar{s}$ (bytes)
- Observation window: $T$ (seconds)

**Step 1 — Number of records in time T.**

$$
N = \lambda \cdot T
$$

*Logic:* Rate $\lambda$ multiplied by duration $T$ gives the count of records (dimensional analysis: $\frac{\text{records}}{\text{sec}} \times \text{sec} = \text{records}$).

**Step 2 — Raw data volume.**

$$
D_{raw} = N \times \bar{s} = \lambda \cdot T \cdot \bar{s}
$$

*Logic:* Each record contributes $\bar{s}$ bytes, so total bytes = count × size.

**Step 3 — Apply protocol overhead factor $\alpha$.**

Transport protocols (HTTP, TLS, TCP/IP) add header bytes. The overhead-adjusted volume is:

$$
D_{tx} = D_{raw} \times (1 + \alpha)
$$

*Logic:* If $\alpha = 0.15$, then 15% extra bytes are headers; the multiplier $(1 + \alpha)$ scales raw payload to transmitted volume.

**Step 4 — Apply compression ratio $c$ for at-rest storage.**

$$
D_{stored} = \frac{D_{tx}}{c}
$$

*Logic:* Compression ratio $c > 1$ means storage is smaller than transmitted; we divide by $c$.

**Step 5 — HDFS replication factor $r$.**

$$
D_{HDFS} = D_{stored} \times r
$$

*Logic:* HDFS stores $r$ replicas (default 3) for fault tolerance; physical disk footprint multiplies by $r$.

**Combined Master Equation:**

$$
\boxed{D_{HDFS} = \frac{\lambda \cdot T \cdot \bar{s} \cdot (1 + \alpha) \cdot r}{c}}
$$

### 3.1.1 Numerical Evaluation — Worked Out

A weather-aggregation platform collects data from **50,000 IoT sensors**, each emitting **one JSON record (256 bytes)** every **10 seconds**, operating **24/7**. Protocol overhead is **15%**, compression ratio is **4×**, and HDFS replication is **3×**. Compute the **annual HDFS storage footprint**.

**Step 1 — Records per sensor per year.**

$$
N_{sensor} = \frac{365 \times 24 \times 3600}{10} = \frac{31{,}536{,}000}{10} = 3{,}153{,}600 \text{ records}
$$

*Logic:* 1 year = 31,536,000 seconds; divide by 10-sec interval.

**Step 2 — Total records across all sensors.**

$$
N_{total} = 50{,}000 \times 3{,}153{,}600 = 1.5768 \times 10^{11} \text{ records}
$$

*Logic:* 5 × 10⁴ × 3.1536 × 10⁶ = 1.5768 × 10¹¹.

**Step 3 — Raw payload volume.**

$$
D_{raw} = 1.5768 \times 10^{11} \times 256 = 4.0366 \times 10^{13} \text{ bytes} \approx 40.37 \text{ TB}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply by 256 bytes/record.

**Step 4 — Transmission volume with overhead.**

$$
D_{tx} = 4.0366 \times 10^{13} \times 1.15 = 4.6421 \times 10^{13} \text{ bytes} \approx 46.42 \text{ TB}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply by $(1 + 0.15) = 1.15$.

**Step 5 — Compressed at-rest volume.**

$$
D_{stored} = \frac{4.6421 \times 10^{13}}{4} = 1.1605 \times 10^{13} \text{ bytes} \approx 11.61 \text{ TB}
$$

*Logic:* Divide by compression ratio $c = 4$.

**Step 6 — HDFS replicated volume.**

$$
D_{HDFS} = 1.1605 \times 10^{13} \times 3 = 3.4815 \times 10^{13} \text{ bytes} \approx 34.82 \text{ TB}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply by replication factor $r = 3$.

**Final Answer:** The system requires **≈ 34.82 TB of physical HDFS storage per year**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Crawler Time-to-Completion Under Politeness Constraints

**Given:**
- Total pages to crawl: $N = 1{,}000{,}000$
- Per-server politeness rate: $r_{req} = 1$ request/second
- Number of unique servers: $H = 50{,}000$
- Average page fetch time: $t_p = 0.5$ seconds
- Number of parallel crawler threads: $p = 100$

**Step 1 — Minimum politeness delay per server.**

$$
d_{min} = \frac{1}{r_{req}} = 1 \text{ second}
$$

*Logic:* If we may send at most 1 req/sec to a single host, we must wait at least 1 sec between successive requests to the *same* host.

**Step 2 — Lower bound on total crawl time (single-threaded, politeness-bound).**

$$
T_{min}^{politeness} = H \times d_{min} = 50{,}000 \times 1 = 50{,}000 \text{ sec} \approx 13.89 \text{ hours}
$$

*Logic:* Each of the 50,000 servers can be hit at most once per second; serializing across all servers gives 50,000 sec minimum.

**Step 3 — Lower bound on total crawl time (parallelism-bound).**

$$
T_{min}^{parallel} = \frac{N \times t_p}{p} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5}{100} = 5{,}000 \text{ sec} \approx 1.39 \text{ hours}
$$

*Logic:* With 100 threads fetching 0.5-sec pages, throughput is $\frac{p}{t_p} = 200$ pages/sec.

**Step 4 — Governing bound (maximum of the two).**

$$
T_{governing} = \max(T_{min}^{politeness},\, T_{min}^{parallel}) = 50{,}000 \text{ sec}
$$

*Logic:* The actual crawl is bounded by the *slower* of the two constraints; here, politeness dominates.

**Final Answer:** The crawl will take **at least 50,000 seconds (≈ 13.89 hours)** regardless of how many threads are added, *because politeness to individual servers is the binding constraint*.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** This is a *classic KTU 14-mark question pattern*. Students who fail to compute BOTH bounds and then take the maximum lose **3 marks** on average.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Format Selection Decision Table

| Scenario | Recommended Format | Justification |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Streaming clickstream into Kafka | **Avro** | Compact binary + schema registry |
| REST API → Data Lake (analytics) | **Parquet** | Columnar → fast aggregate queries |
| Web-scraped product listings | **JSON / CSV** | Human-readable, easy transformation |
| SOAP enterprise B2B exchange | **XML** | Industry standard, XSD validation |
| Mobile app → backend (RPC) | **Protobuf** | Smallest payload, fast serialization |
| Archival / long-term storage | **Parquet + Snappy** | Best $/GB + read performance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow: Choosing an Internet Data Acquisition Technique

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Need Internet Data]):::start
    q1{Is the data on a fixed URL with structured endpoints?}:::q
    q2{Is the data on public web pages without an API?}:::q
    q3{Does the data change in real time?}:::q
    q4{Is the data published as feeds or events?}:::q

    a1[Use REST API Calls]:::ans
    a2[Build a Web Scraper]:::ans
    a3[Build a Web Crawler BFS DFS]:::ans
    a4[Use Streaming API WebSocket]:::ans
    a5[Subscribe to Message Queue Kafka MQTT]:::ans
    a6[Consume RSS Atom Feed]:::ans

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> q3
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q3 -- Real Time --> a4
    q3 -- Not Real Time --> a1
    q2 -- Known Finite Set --> a2
    q2 -- Large Unknown Set --> a3
    q3 -.->|Streaming option| a4
    q3 -.->|Pub Sub option| a5
    q4 -- Yes --> a6
    q4 -- No --> a1

    classDef start fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,color:#000
    classDef q fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,color:#000
    classDef ans fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Architecture Explanation (Block-Level Functional Mapping)

The pipeline above implements the **Lambda-inspired ingestion pattern**:

1. **Source Layer (yellow/peach):** Six classes of internet data sources, each with distinct structural and temporal characteristics.
2. **Transport Layer (blue):** Protocols are chosen based on source requirements — REST APIs use HTTPS polling, IoT uses MQTT, logs use syslog, etc.
3. **Acquisition Layer (green):** Specialized "data collectors" (crawlers, scrapers, connectors) translate raw transport streams into uniform record format.
4. **Ingestion Layer (pink):** Distributed messaging systems (Kafka, Flume, Logstash) decouple *producers* from *consumers* and provide durability + back-pressure handling.
5. **Landing Zone (purple):** Raw data is persisted in HDFS or S3 in original format ("data lake" principle).
6. **Processing & Curated Zone (gold/blue):** Spark/MapReduce jobs transform raw → curated (Parquet/ORC).
7. **Analytics (green):** BI tools, ML models, and dashboards consume curated data.

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is the **standard reference** the KTU 2024 examiner expects students to reproduce in 14-mark answers on "Explain the architecture of an Internet Data ingestion pipeline."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
