# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Internet Data and list any four characteristics that classify it as Big Data.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Internet Data is the large-scale, heterogeneous digital information generated, transmitted, and stored across the global Internet infrastructure — encompassing web pages, API responses, social media posts, IoT telemetry, logs, and open datasets.

**Four Characteristics (1.5 marks, 0.375 each):**
1. **Volume** — measured in petabytes to zettabytes (e.g., global IP traffic ≈ 3.4 ZB/year).
2. **Velocity** — generated and consumed in real time (e.g., stock ticks at microsecond latency).
3. **Variety** — multiple formats coexist: HTML, JSON, XML, CSV, images, video.
4. **Veracity** — high noise, duplication, and trustworthiness concerns (bots, fake news).

*(Value is the optional 5th V — 0.5 bonus credit if mentioned.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Web Crawling and Web Scraping. Give one example use-case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Web Crawling | Web Scraping |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| **Purpose** | Discover and download *unknown* web pages | Extract *specific* data from *known* pages |
| **Traversal** | Follows hyperlinks (BFS/DFS) | Static targeting — no link following |
| **Output** | Raw HTML corpus | Structured records (CSV/JSON) |
| **Example** | **Googlebot** indexing the web | **Price-monitoring bot** extracting iPhone prices from Amazon |

**Crawling (1.5 marks):** Automated traversal of hyperlinks to build an index. *Example:* Googlebot discovering new pages.

**Scraping (1.5 marks):** Targeted extraction of fields from a known page layout using DOM parsing or regex. *Example:* Extracting product prices from Amazon for competitor analysis.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**, OR a single 14-mark descriptive answer. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ✍️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Explain the architecture of an Internet Data ingestion pipeline for Big Data Analytics. With a neat diagram, describe the four functional layers and the role of each component. Also compute the annual storage footprint for the scenario given below.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Architecture Description**

**Model Answer:**

An Internet Data ingestion pipeline consists of **four functional layers** that progressively transform raw external internet signals into analytics-ready data:

1. **Source Layer (1.5 marks):** This is where data originates. It includes:
   - **Web pages** (HTML documents on websites).
   - **APIs** (REST/SOAP endpoints returning JSON/XML).
   - **Social media** platforms (Twitter, Facebook, Instagram).
   - **IoT devices** (sensors, wearables, smart meters).
   - **Server logs and clickstreams** (user interaction traces).
   - **Open data repositories** (government, scientific).

2. **Transport Layer (1.5 marks):** Defines how data is carried across the network.
   - **HTTP/HTTPS** for web pages and REST APIs.
   - **WebSockets** for real-time bidirectional streaming.
   - **MQTT / CoAP** for lightweight IoT telemetry.
   - **FTP / SFTP** for bulk file transfers.
   - **Syslog / TCP tail** for log streaming.

3. **Acquisition Layer (2 marks):** The "data collectors" that interface with sources.
   - **Web Crawlers** (e.g., Apache Nutch, Heritrix) — BFS/DFS traversal.
   - **Scrapers** (e.g., Scrapy, BeautifulSoup) — DOM-based extraction.
   - **API Connectors** (e.g., custom Python/SDK clients) — REST/SOAP.
   - **Log Agents** (e.g., Filebeat, Fluentd) — log shipping.

4. **Ingestion Layer (2 marks):** Buffers, queues, and persists data for downstream processing.
   - **Apache Kafka** — distributed event-streaming platform with topic-based pub/sub.
   - **Apache Flume** — log-aggregation agent (source → channel → sink).
   - **Logstash** — server-side data processing pipeline.
   - **Landing Zone** — HDFS or S3 holding raw data ("data lake").

**Diagram (included in Section 4.1 above).**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Storage Footprint Calculation**

**Given Scenario:**
A social media analytics firm collects data from **200,000 user accounts**. Each account generates an **average of 5 posts/day**, each post (with metadata) averages **512 bytes**. Protocol overhead = **20%**, compression ratio = **5×**, HDFS replication factor = **3**. Compute the **annual HDFS storage footprint**.

**Step 1 — Records per day (1 mark).**

$$
N_{day} = 200{,}000 \times 5 = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ posts/day}
$$

*Logic:* 2 × 10⁵ accounts × 5 posts each.

**Step 2 — Records per year (1 mark).**

$$
N_{year} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 365 = 3.65 \times 10^{8} \text{ posts/year}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply daily count by 365 days.

**Step 3 — Raw payload volume (1 mark).**

$$
D_{raw} = 3.65 \times 10^{8} \times 512 = 1.8688 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \approx 186.88 \text{ GB}
$$

*Logic:* 3.65 × 10⁸ × 512 = 1.8688 × 10¹¹ bytes.

**Step 4 — Transmission volume with overhead (1 mark).**

$$
D_{tx} = 1.8688 \times 10^{11} \times 1.20 = 2.2426 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \approx 224.26 \text{ GB}
$$

*Logic:* Apply $(1 + \alpha) = 1.20$.

**Step 5 — Compressed at-rest volume (1 mark).**

$$
D_{stored} = \frac{2.2426 \times 10^{11}}{5} = 4.4851 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes} \approx 44.85 \text{ GB}
$$

*Logic:* Divide by compression ratio $c = 5$.

**Step 6 — HDFS replicated volume (1 mark).**

$$
D_{HDFS} = 4.4851 \times 10^{10} \times 3 = 1.3455 \times 10^{11} \text{ bytes} \approx 134.55 \text{ GB}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply by replication factor $r = 3$.

**Step 7 — Final statement (1 mark).**

**Final Answer:** The system requires **≈ 134.55 GB of physical HDFS storage per year** (≈ 0.13 TB). This is comfortably within a single commodity server, but the architecture must be designed to **scale horizontally** as user accounts grow.

---

### ✍️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Compare and contrast the various data formats used for Internet Data (HTML, JSON, XML, CSV, Parquet, Avro, Protobuf). For a streaming clickstream analytics use-case, justify your choice of format. Also, derive the minimum crawl time for the given scenario.**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Format Comparison & Justification**

**Model Answer:**

| Format | Structure | Human-Readable | Parser Speed | Schema Evolution | Best Suited For |
|--------|-----------|----------------|--------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **HTML** | DOM tree | Yes | Slow (browser engine) | No | Web pages |
| **JSON** | Key-value, nested | Yes | Fast | No (text-based) | REST APIs, NoSQL |
| **XML** | Tree with tags | Yes | Medium | XSD support | SOAP, RSS, enterprise |
| **CSV** | Flat tabular | Yes | Very fast | No | Tabular exports, logs |
| **Parquet** | Columnar binary | No | Very fast (column-pruned) | Limited | Data lake analytics |
| **Avro** | Row-binary + schema | No | Fast | Yes (schema registry) | Kafka streaming |
| **Protobuf** | Binary + .proto schema | No | Very fast | Yes (backward compat) | gRPC, mobile backends |

**Justification for Clickstream Streaming (3 marks):**

For a **streaming clickstream analytics use-case**, the optimal format is **Apache Avro**:

1. **Compact binary serialization** — minimizes network bandwidth, critical when ingesting millions of click events per second.
2. **Schema evolution support** — clickstream event schemas change frequently (new fields like `session_id`, `experiment_variant`); Avro's schema registry handles backward/forward compatibility automatically.
3. **Native Kafka integration** — Kafka's recommended serializer is Avro, with Confluent Schema Registry providing centralized schema governance.
4. **Language-agnostic** — Avro generates code for Java, Python, Go, C++, matching polyglot microservices.
5. **Self-describing** — schema is embedded in the file header, enabling schema-less reads for downstream consumers.

*(Alternative valid choice: Protobuf, with justification of even smaller payload size and Google ecosystem integration.)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Crawl Time Derivation**

**Given Scenario:**
A startup wants to build a web search index by crawling **2,000,000 pages** distributed across **80,000 unique servers**. Average page fetch time = **0.4 seconds**, politeness rate = **2 requests/second/server**, and parallel threads = **200**. Compute the **minimum crawl time**.

**Step 1 — Politeness-bound lower limit (2 marks).**

$$
d_{min} = \frac{1}{r_{req}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ seconds per server}
$$

*Logic:* At 2 req/s, the gap between consecutive requests to the same host must be ≥ 0.5 s.

$$
T_{politeness} = H \times d_{min} = 80{,}000 \times 0.5 = 40{,}000 \text{ sec}
$$

*Logic:* Each of 80,000 servers contributes 0.5 s of minimum wall-clock.

**Step 2 — Parallelism-bound lower limit (2 marks).**

$$
T_{parallel} = \frac{N \times t_p}{p} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \times 0.4}{200} = \frac{800{,}000}{200} = 4{,}000 \text{ sec}
$$

*Logic:* With 200 threads, each fetching a 0.4-s page, throughput is 200/0.4 = 500 pages/sec.

**Step 3 — Governing bound (1.5 marks).**

$$
T_{governing} = \max(40{,}000,\ 4{,}000) = 40{,}000 \text{ sec}
$$

*Logic:* Politeness dominates; parallelism cannot help us hit a single server faster than 2 req/s.

**Step 4 — Conversion and final statement (1.5 marks).**

$$
T_{governing} = \frac{40{,}000}{3{,}600} \approx 11.11 \text{ hours}
$$

**Final Answer:** The minimum crawl time is **40,000 seconds (≈ 11.11 hours)**, governed by politeness constraints. To reduce this, the firm must either negotiate higher rate limits with target servers (whitelist agreements) or use **distributed crawling with IP rotation** across geographically distributed datacenters.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on Internet Data questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Web Crawling with Web Scraping (–1 mark):** Crawling = *discovery*; Scraping = *extraction*. Examiners deduct a full mark for using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the "governing bound = MAX" rule (–2 marks):** In crawl-time derivations, students often compute only the parallelism bound and ignore politeness. The actual time is the **maximum** of the two lower bounds.
> 3. **Forgetting protocol overhead $\alpha$ (–1 mark):** Real internet data carries HTTP/TLS/IP headers (typically 10–20%). Omitting $(1 + \alpha)$ in volume calculations is a recurring error.
> 4. **Writing "Big Data = 3V" instead of 5V (–1 mark):** KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly adopts the **5V model** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value). The 3V model is outdated in board valuation.
> 5. **Mixing up REST and SOAP (–1 mark):** REST = lightweight, JSON/HTTP, stateless; SOAP = heavy, XML/WSDL, supports WS-Security. Examiners expect the distinction.
> 6. **Skipping unit conversions (–0.5 mark):** Always express final answers in **TB / GB / hours** with explicit conversion steps shown.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Use this as your 2-minute pre-exam revision checklist.**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- **Internet Data** = unstructured / semi-structured digital information from the Internet, exhibiting the **5 V's** of Big Data.
- **Four-layer architecture:** Source → Transport → Acquisition → Ingestion.
- **HTTP semantics:** `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`; status codes `2xx/3xx/4xx/5xx`.
- **REST API constraints:** Stateless, Uniform Interface, Cacheable, Layered (Fielding, 2000).
- **Web Crawling** = link-traversal (BFS/DFS) → builds an index.
- **Web Scraping** = DOM/regex extraction from known pages → structured records.

### 📌 Data Formats
- **JSON** — APIs, semi-structured.
- **XML** — SOAP, RSS, enterprise.
- **CSV** — flat tabular, simple.
- **Parquet** — columnar, analytics-optimized.
- **Avro** — row-binary + schema, Kafka-friendly.
- **Protobuf** — compact binary, gRPC.

### 📌 High-Yield Formulas
- $D_{HDFS} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot T \cdot \bar{s} \cdot (1 + \alpha) \cdot r}{c}$
- $T_{crawl} = \max\!\left(\dfrac{N \cdot t_p}{p},\ H \cdot d_{min}\right)$
- $d_{min} = \dfrac{1}{r_{req}}$ (politeness)
- $n = \dfrac{1.96^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ (sample size, 95% CL)

### 📌 KTU-Favorite Numbers to Memorize
- HDFS default replication: **3**
- HTTP overhead: **10–20%**
- Sampling constant (95% CL): **1.96**
- Twitter API limit: **900 req / 15 min** (user token)
- Compression ratio for text/JSON: **2–10×**

### 📌 Examiner's Pet Topics (predict ~80% chance in ESE)
1. Compare crawling vs. scraping (3 marks).
2. Draw and explain ingestion pipeline diagram (7 marks).
3. Compute crawl time under politeness + parallelism (7 marks).
4. Compute storage footprint using the master equation (7 marks).
5. Justify format choice for a given use-case (7 marks).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** Memorize the **master equation** $D_{HDFS}$ and the **MAX-rule for crawl time**. Together, they cover **~50% of all 14-mark Part B questions** in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomedical Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Biomedical Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Data** refers to the heterogeneous, multi-modal, large-scale, and high-velocity information generated from biological systems, clinical encounters, molecular laboratories, medical imaging devices, wearable sensors, genomic sequencers, and public health surveillance systems. In the context of KTU's Big Data Analytics framework, it is treated as a specialised subclass of **domain-specific big data** that requires dedicated acquisition, storage, transformation, and analytical pipelines because of its strict regulatory, ethical, and structural constraints.

From a Big Data Analytics perspective, biomedical data is the **raw substrate** on which:

1. **Descriptive analytics** is performed (e.g., patient cohort summaries).
2. **Predictive analytics** is executed (e.g., disease onset probability).
3. **Prescriptive analytics** is delivered (e.g., personalised treatment recommendations).
4. **Cognitive/prescriptive analytics** is being researched (e.g., digital twins of organs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Biomedical data is one of the *three* canonical non-commercial verticals (alongside **Smart City Data** and **IoT/Industrial Data**) in Module 2. The university expects students to connect the *generic* 5V/8V Big Data model with the *biomedical-specific* attributes of heterogeneity, sparsity, noise, and regulatory compliance.

## 1.2 The 8 V's of Biomedical Big Data (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Variability, Visualisation, Vulnerability)

| Dimension | Biomedical Manifestation | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Genomic sequences, imaging archives | **~30 GB per whole-genome sequence**, **~1.2 TB per PET-CT scan** |
| **Velocity** | ICU streaming, wearable ECG | **~1000 samples/sec per ICU bed** |
| **Variety** | Clinical notes, images, signals, omics | **≥ 6 distinct formats per patient** |
| **Veracity** | Missing lab values, sensor noise | **15 – 30% missingness** in typical EHRs |
| **Value** | Diagnostic insight, treatment efficacy | Inferred via ROC-AUC, NRI, IDI |
| **Variability** | Inter-clinic protocol differences | Site-specific bias |
| **Visualisation** | 3D MRI, longitudinal heatmaps | High dimensional |
| **Vulnerability** | HIPAA, GDPR-Ayushman Bharat rules | Regulatory ceiling |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multilingual Hospital"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a hospital as a giant **multilingual library** where every floor uses a different language:
> - **Ground floor (EHR/EMR)** stores patient files in free-text English.
> - **First floor (Imaging)** uses DICOM pixel-arrays.
> - **Second floor (Genomics)** stores strings of A, T, G, C.
> - **Third floor (Wearables)** streams time-series voltages.
> - **Basement (Billing/Insurance)** uses alphanumeric ICD-10 codes.
>
> A Big Data system in healthcare is essentially a **universal translator + librarian** that ingests all these languages simultaneously, indexes them with a common spine (e.g., the **FHIR resource model**), and serves them to a clinician in real time. The "librarian" must also obey strict privacy rules (HIPAA / DPDP Act 2023) — you can read the book, but you cannot photocopy a page that identifies the reader.

## 1.4 Sources of Biomedical Data

1. **Electronic Health Records (EHR/EMR)** — structured + unstructured.
2. **Medical Imaging** — CT, MRI, PET, X-ray, ultrasound, mammography.
3. **Genomic & Multi-Omics** — DNA-seq, RNA-seq, proteomics, metabolomics.
4. **Wearable & Ambient Sensors** — smartwatches, ECG patches, smart implants.
5. **Clinical Trials & Registries** — phase-trial data, cohort studies.
6. **Pharmacy & Claims Data** — drug prescriptions, insurance claims.
7. **Public Health Surveillance** — disease incidence dashboards (e.g., IDSP-India).
8. **Patient-Reported Outcomes (PROs)** — surveys, mobile-app logs.
9. **Biobanks & Pathology** — tissue samples with linked metadata.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Taxonomy of Biomedical Data

Biomedical data is classified along **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### Axis A — Degree of Structure

- **Structured Data** — fixed-schema tabular data (lab values, vitals, billing codes). Stored in relational/SQL warehouses.
- **Semi-Structured Data** — has tags/keys but no rigid schema (HL7 v2 messages, JSON-FHIR resources, DICOM headers).
- **Unstructured Data** — free-text clinical notes, pathology images, audio dictations. Requires NLP/CV preprocessing.

### Axis B — Modality of Acquisition

- **Imaging** (radiology, dermatology photos, retinal scans)
- **Signal** (ECG, EEG, EMG, PPG)
- **Molecular** (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics)
- **Tabular/Phenotypic** (demographics, comorbidities, drug exposure)
- **Textual** (discharge summaries, radiology reports)

### Axis C — Temporal Resolution

- **Snapshot** (one-time imaging)
- **Episodic** (visits, admissions)
- **Continuous/Streaming** (ICU monitor, wearable)

## 2.2 The Biomedical Data Lifecycle (BDLC)

The **biomedical analytics pipeline** consists of six stages, each with associated tools and KT 2024-recommended technologies:

1. **Acquisition** → HL7 FHIR APIs, DICOM routers, sequencer basecallers.
2. **Ingestion & Transport** → Apache Kafka, Mirth Connect, AWS HealthLake.
3. **Cleansing & Normalisation** → OMOP CDM, SNOMED CT mapping, LOINC harmonisation.
4. **Storage** → Hadoop HDFS, Delta Lake, AWS S3 + Glacier, genomics-specific (BAM/CRAM).
5. **Analytics** → Spark MLlib, H2O, TensorFlow, Snakemade (genomics), MONAI (imaging).
6. **Visualisation & Decision Support** → Tableau, Power BI, custom clinical dashboards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is biomedical data different from generic big data?**
> 1. **Irreversibility of anonymisation** — genome is itself an identifier.
> 2. **High class-imbalance** — rare diseases affect 1 in 10,000+.
> 3. **Strong causality requirement** — correlation is not enough for treatment decisions.
> 4. **Regulatory non-negotiables** — FDA SaMD, EU MDR, ICMR guidelines.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_{2} p(x_i)$ | $p(x_i)$ = probability of token/event $x_i$ | bits |
| 2 | **Joint Entropy** | $H(X,Y) = -\sum_{i,j} p(x_i,y_j) \log_{2} p(x_i,y_j)$ | Pairwise probability | bits |
| 3 | **Conditional Entropy** | $H(Y \mid X) = H(X,Y) - H(X)$ | Residual uncertainty | bits |
| 4 | **Mutual Information** | $I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$ | Shared information | bits |
| 5 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Reduction in entropy after split on attribute $A$ | bits |
| 6 | **Gini Impurity** | $Gini(p) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{2}$ | Misclassification probability | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Sensitivity (Recall)** | $Sens = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | True positive rate | ratio in [0,1] |
| 8 | **Specificity** | $Spec = \dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | True negative rate | ratio in [0,1] |
| 9 | **Precision (PPV)** | $PPV = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Positive predictive value | ratio in [0,1] |
| 10 | **F1-Score** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{PPV \cdot Sens}{PPV + Sens}$ | Harmonic mean | ratio in [0,1] |
| 11 | **AUC-ROC** | $AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR^{-1}(t)) \, dt$ | Threshold-independent metric | ratio in [0,1] |
| 12 | **Genomic Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{L_{raw}}{L_{compressed}}$ | $L$ = sequence length | ratio |
| 13 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Power ratio | dB |
| 14 | **DICOM File Size (approx)** | $S_{DICOM} \approx w \cdot h \cdot d \cdot b \cdot n$ | $w,h,d$ = dims; $b$ = bits/px; $n$ = slices | bytes |
| 15 | **Cohort Survival Function** | $S(t) = \prod_{t_i \le t}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$ | Kaplan–Meier estimator | probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the KTU board exam, students often write $H(X) = -\sum p \log p$ without specifying the **base** of the logarithm. Always state whether the answer is in **bits (base 2)**, **nats (base $e$)**, or **Hartleys (base 10)**. Default to **base 2 → bits** unless told otherwise.

## 2.4 Why Biomedical Analytics Matters in Engineering

- **Precision Medicine** — drugs tailored to a patient's genome.
- **Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CADx)** — second-opinion AI for radiologists.
- **Drug Repurposing** — graph-mining of molecular interaction networks.
- **Pandemic Forecasting** — SIR/SEIR + ML on transmission data (post-COVID-19 relevance).
- **Hospital Resource Optimisation** — predicting ICU bed demand (Operations Research + ML).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **KTU-favourite** analytical problems. Memorise both the mathematical transitions and the *intuition* behind each step — board examiners award marks for explaining the "why" as much as the "how".

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Information Gain in a Clinical Decision Tree

**Problem Context.** A hospital dataset has 100 patient records, of which **60 are disease-positive** and **40 are disease-negative**. The decision is whether to perform a biomarker test $A$ that partitions the data into two branches: $A =$ `Positive` (30 records → 25 positive, 5 negative) and $A =$ `Negative` (70 records → 35 positive, 35 negative). Compute the **Information Gain** achieved by testing $A$.

### Step 1 — Compute the parent entropy $H(S)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\!\left(p_{+}\log_2 p_{+} + p_{-}\log_2 p_{-}\right) \\
     &= -\!\left(\frac{60}{100}\log_2 \frac{60}{100} + \frac{40}{100}\log_2 \frac{40}{100}\right) \\
     &= -\!\left(0.6 \times \log_2 0.6 + 0.4 \times \log_2 0.4\right) \\
     &= -\!\left(0.6 \times (-0.7370) + 0.4 \times (-1.3219)\right) \\
     &= -\!\left(-0.4422 - 0.5288\right) \\
     &= 0.9710 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Parent entropy is the impurity *before* any split. A value of 0.9710 bits confirms a moderately mixed population.

### Step 2 — Compute the entropy of the "Positive" branch $H(S_{A=+})$

The branch has 30 records, with 25 positive and 5 negative.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{A=+}) &= -\!\left(\tfrac{25}{30}\log_2 \tfrac{25}{30} + \tfrac{5}{30}\log_2 \tfrac{5}{30}\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(0.8333 \times (-0.2630) + 0.1667 \times (-2.5850)\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(-0.2192 - 0.4308\right) \\
           &= 0.6500 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The "Positive" branch is *purer* (25/30 = 83% disease-positive).

### Step 3 — Compute the entropy of the "Negative" branch $H(S_{A=-})$

The branch has 70 records, with 35 positive and 35 negative.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{A=-}) &= -\!\left(\tfrac{35}{70}\log_2 \tfrac{35}{70} + \tfrac{35}{70}\log_2 \tfrac{35}{70}\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(0.5 \times (-1) + 0.5 \times (-1)\right) \\
           &= 1.0000 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The "Negative" branch is maximally impure (50-50 split), contributing the *full* bit of uncertainty.

### Step 4 — Compute the weighted post-split entropy

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid A) &= \frac{\vert S_{A=+} \vert}{\vert S \vert}\, H(S_{A=+}) + \frac{\vert S_{A=-} \vert}{\vert S \vert}\, H(S_{A=-}) \\
            &= \frac{30}{100}\times 0.6500 + \frac{70}{100}\times 1.0000 \\
            &= 0.1950 + 0.7000 \\
            &= 0.8950 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** A *weighted average* of child entropies, weighted by sample count.

### Step 5 — Compute the Information Gain

$$
\begin{aligned}
IG(S, A) &= H(S) - H(S \mid A) \\
         &= 0.9710 - 0.8950 \\
         &= 0.0760 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Verdict:** The biomarker test $A$ reduces uncertainty by only **0.0760 bits** — a *weak* discriminator. In a KTU answer, conclude: *"The test is not very informative on its own; combine it with additional biomarkers for clinically meaningful decisions."*

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Genomic Compression Ratio

**Problem Context.** A raw FASTQ file of a human whole-genome sequencing run has $L_{raw} = 1{,}200{,}000{,}000$ characters (one per base call, with quality scores included). After CRAM compression with reference genome, the file size is $L_{compressed} = 300{,}000{,}000$ characters. Compute (a) the compression ratio, (b) the storage saving percentage, (c) the per-base information content assuming uniform nucleotide distribution.

### (a) Compression Ratio

$$
CR = \frac{L_{raw}}{L_{compressed}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{9}}{3.0 \times 10^{8}} = 4.0
$$

### (b) Storage Saving Percentage

$$
\text{Saving \%} = \left(1 - \frac{1}{CR}\right)\times 100 = \left(1 - 0.25\right)\times 100 = 75\%
$$

### (c) Per-base Information Content

For a uniform alphabet of 4 nucleotides, the maximum entropy per symbol is:

$$
H_{base} = \log_2 4 = 2.0 \; \text{bits per base}
$$

For the *observed* distribution $p = (p_A, p_T, p_G, p_C) = (0.30, 0.30, 0.20, 0.20)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{obs} &= -\!\left(0.30\log_2 0.30 + 0.30\log_2 0.30 + 0.20\log_2 0.20 + 0.20\log_2 0.20\right) \\
        &= -\!\left(2(0.30)(-1.7370) + 2(0.20)(-2.3219)\right) \\
        &= -\!\left(-1.0422 - 0.9288\right) \\
        &= 1.9710 \; \text{bits per base}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Verdict:** Observed entropy $1.9710 < 2.0$ bits — the genome is *slightly* non-uniform (a well-known biological fact: GC content ≠ 50% in many species). The theoretical lower bound for lossless compression is the entropy $H_{obs}$, so the minimum achievable file size is:

$$
L_{min} = 1.9710 \times 1.2 \times 10^{9} / 8 \approx 296 \; \text{MB}
$$

Since CRAM achieves **300 MB**, it operates within 1.4% of the Shannon limit — a remarkable engineering feat.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sensitivity, Specificity, PPV from a Confusion Matrix

**Problem Context.** A radiology AI model for lung-nodule detection produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1,000 CT slices:

| | Predicted Malignant | Predicted Benign |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Actual Malignant** | $TP = 180$ | $FN = 20$ |
| **Actual Benign** | $FP = 50$ | $TN = 750$ |

### (a) Sensitivity

$$
Sens = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{180}{180+20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

### (b) Specificity

$$
Spec = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{750}{750+50} = \frac{750}{800} = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
$$

### (c) Precision (PPV)

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{180}{180+50} = \frac{180}{230} = 0.7826 = 78.26\%
$$

### (d) F1-Score

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Sens}{PPV + Sens} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7826 \times 0.90}{0.7826 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7043}{1.6826} = 0.8372
$$

**Final Verdict:** Strong recall (90%) and specificity (93.75%), but precision drops to 78% because of 50 false positives. In a KTU answer, recommend a **second-reader workflow** or **threshold tuning** to raise PPV without sacrificing recall.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Metric

| Clinical Scenario | Preferred Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Cancer screening** | Sensitivity (Recall) | Missing a cancer is worse than a false alarm |
| **Confirmatory biopsy decision** | Specificity / PPV | Avoid unnecessary invasive procedures |
| **Rare-disease cohort discovery** | F1-Score | Balances both classes under imbalance |
| **Threshold-free model comparison** | AUC-ROC | Independent of operating point |
| **Survival analysis** | C-Index (concordance) | Handles censored clinical data |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams are **KTU-2024-aligned** functional architectures. They do not attempt to render physical anatomy; they depict the *information flow* of a biomedical data analytics platform, which is what the syllabus emphasises.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Biomedical Big Data Analytics Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    src1[EHR and EMR Systems] --> ing[Ingestion Layer]
    src2[Medical Imaging Devices] --> ing
    src3[Genomic Sequencers] --> ing
    src4[Wearables and ICU Monitors] --> ing
    src5[Patient Reported Outcomes] --> ing

    ing --> kafka[Apache Kafka Streaming Bus]
    kafka --> clean[Cleaning and Normalisation Engine]
    clean --> map[Standards Mapper: HL7 FHIR, SNOMED, LOINC, OMOP CDM]

    map --> lake[Data Lake: S3 or HDFS]
    map --> wh[Data Warehouse: OMOP CDM]

    lake --> spark[Apache Spark Analytics Engine]
    wh --> spark
    spark --> ml1[Machine Learning Models]
    spark --> dl1[Deep Learning Models: CNN, RNN, Transformer]
    spark --> stat[Statistical Analysis: Kaplan Meier, Cox Regression]

    ml1 --> dash[Clinical Decision Dashboard]
    dl1 --> dash
    stat --> dash

    dash --> doc[Clinician Workstation]
    dash --> res[Researcher Portal]
    dash --> pat[Patient Mobile App]
```

**Reading the Diagram.** Data flows from left (heterogeneous sources) → middle (Kafka bus, cleansing, standards mapping, lake/warehouse) → right (analytics, dashboards, end-users). The horizontal centre is the **ETL + standardisation core**; without it, the right-hand analytics would be incoherent.

## 4.2 Architecture Diagram — Genomics-Specific Sub-Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sample[Patient DNA Sample] --> ext[DNA Extraction and Library Prep]
    ext --> seq[Sequencer: Illumina NovaSeq or Oxford Nanopore]
    seq --> bcl[Base Call Files: BCL]
    bcl --> fastq[FASTQ Generation]
    fastq --> align[Alignment to Reference Genome: BWA or Bowtie2]
    align --> bam[BAM File]
    bam --> cram[CRAM Compression]
    bam --> var[Variant Calling: GATK or DeepVariant]
    var --> vcf[VCF File]
    vcf --> ann[Annotation: ClinVar, gnomAD, dbSNP]
    ann --> clinical[Clinical Interpretation and Reporting]
    clinical --> pgx[Pharmacogenomics Decision Support]
    clinical --> report[Genetic Counsellor Report]
```

**Reading the Diagram.** This sub-pipeline is invoked when a clinician orders a **genomic test**. The intermediate files (FASTQ → BAM → VCF) are *standardised* by the Global Alliance for Genomics and Health (GA4GH), making cross-hospital analyses possible.

## 4.3 Workflow Diagram — Privacy-Preserving Analytics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Raw PHI Data: Protected Health Information] --> anon[Anonymisation Layer]
    anon --> k1{k-anonymity satisfied?}
    k1 -- No --> supp[Suppression and Generalisation]
    supp --> anon
    k1 -- Yes --> diff[Add Differential Privacy Noise]
    diff --> fed[Federated Learning Node]
    fed --> agg[Secure Aggregation Server]
    agg --> model[Global Model Weights, No Raw Data Shared]
    model --> deploy[Deployed Diagnostic Model]
    deploy --> audit[Audit Log and HIPAA Compliance Trail]
```

**Reading the Diagram.** This pipeline **never moves raw patient data outside the hospital**. Instead, model *gradients* are exchanged under a **Differential Privacy** budget $\epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 1$ for healthcare, lower = more private). It is the **gold standard** for multi-hospital collaboration post-GDPR.

## 4.4 Layered View — The 5-Layer Biomedical Data Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Layer 5: Application Layer\nClinical Dashboards, CDS Hooks, Patient Portals]
    L4[Layer 4: Analytics Layer\nSpark MLlib, H2O, TensorFlow, PyTorch, MONAI]
    L3[Layer 3: Storage Layer\nHDFS, Delta Lake, Genomics BAM/CRAM, DICOM PACS]
    L2[Layer 2: Integration Layer\nHL7 FHIR Servers, DICOM Routers, ETL Jobs, Kafka]
    L1[Layer 1: Source Layer\nEHR, Imaging, Genomics, Wearables, Claims, PROs]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

**Reading the Diagram.** Each layer depends on the one below. The **Integration Layer (L2)** is the most biomedical-specific — generic IT curricula rarely cover HL7 FHIR, but every KTU Big Data student must know it.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following question bank mirrors the **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks are distributed exactly as per the official template: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks each with internal choice**, and every sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any three V's of Big Data with a biomedical example for each.**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

1. **Volume** → A single whole-genome sequence is **~30 GB**; a tertiary-care hospital generates **petabytes/year** of imaging data, demanding HDFS-class storage.
2. **Velocity** → ICU bedside monitors stream **~1,000 samples/sec** of ECG and SpO₂, requiring Kafka-style real-time ingestion.
3. **Veracity** → EHRs contain **15–30% missing lab values**, demanding statistical imputation before ML modelling.

(3 marks: 1 mark per V with a correct biomedical example.)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is HL7 FHIR? How does it differ from traditional HL7 v2?**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

**HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources)** is a modern standard that models healthcare data as **discrete, reusable "Resources"** (e.g., `Patient`, `Observation`, `DiagnosticReport`) exchanged over **RESTful HTTPS** using **JSON or XML**. Unlike **HL7 v2**, which uses pipe-delimited, session-based text messages that are hard to parse, FHIR is **web-native, schema-validated, and human-readable**, enabling seamless big-data ingestion. FHIR also supports **SMART-on-FHIR** OAuth2.0 authorisation.

(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for contrast.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2 Choice Q1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **end-to-end pipeline** for ingesting, storing, and analysing genomic data in a Big Data framework. Mention at least **four file formats** and **two tools** for each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hospital uses a sepsis-prediction model on 1,200 ICU admissions. The confusion matrix is given below. Compute **Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. Comment on which metric is most clinically relevant and why.

| | Predicted Sepsis | Predicted No-Sepsis |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Actual Sepsis** | $TP = 240$ | $FN = 60$ |
| **Actual No-Sepsis** | $FP = 100$ | $TN = 800$ |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Stage 1 — Sample to Sequence Acquisition (1.5 marks)**
DNA is extracted from the patient sample, a sequencing library is prepared, and a sequencer (e.g., **Illumina NovaSeq X**, **Oxford Nanopore PromethION**) generates raw **base-call (BCL)** files.

**Stage 2 — File Format Conversion (1.5 marks)**
The **BCL → FASTQ** conversion is performed by tools such as **bcl2fastq** or **Illumina DRAGEN**. **FASTQ** is a text format containing both nucleotide reads and per-base quality scores (`Phred+33` encoded).

**Stage 3 — Alignment (1.5 marks)**
Reads are aligned to a **reference genome (GRCh38)** using **BWA-MEM**, **Bowtie2**, or **STAR**. The output is a **SAM (Sequence Alignment Map)** file, which is then converted to its binary form **BAM**, and optionally compressed to **CRAM** for storage efficiency.

**Stage 4 — Variant Calling and Annotation (1.5 marks)**
**GATK HaplotypeCaller** or **DeepVariant** (a CNN-based caller) identifies single-nucleotide variants (SNVs) and indels, producing a **VCF (Variant Call Format)** file. Tools such as **SnpEff**, **VEP**, and **ANNOVAR** annotate variants with consequences, allele frequencies (gnomAD), and clinical significance (ClinVar).

**Stage 5 — Storage and Analytics (1 mark)**
Files are stored on **HDFS** or cloud object stores (**AWS S3**). Distributed analytics is performed using **Apache Spark** (`Glow`, `ADAM` projects) and **Snakemake / Nextflow** pipelines. Down-stream ML models (e.g., polygenic risk scores) are computed via **scikit-learn** or **TensorFlow**.

**Block Diagram (any equivalent 6-block pipeline is acceptable):**

```
[Patient Sample] → [Sequencer] → [FASTQ] → [BWA Aligner] → [BAM] → [GATK Caller] → [VCF] → [SnpEff Annotator] → [Annotated VCF] → [Spark ML]
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

Given: $TP=240$, $FN=60$, $FP=100$, $TN=800$, total = 1,200.

**Step 1 — Sensitivity (1.5 marks)**

$$
Sens = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{240}{240+60} = \frac{240}{300} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

**Step 2 — Specificity (1.5 marks)**

$$
Spec = \frac{TN}{TN+FP} = \frac{800}{800+100} = \frac{800}{900} = 0.8889 = 88.89\%
$$

**Step 3 — Precision (1 mark)**

$$
PPV = \frac{TP}{TP+FP} = \frac{240}{240+100} = \frac{240}{340} = 0.7059 = 70.59\%
$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (1.5 marks)**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{PPV \cdot Sens}{PPV + Sens} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7059 \times 0.80}{0.7059 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5647}{1.5059} = 0.7501
$$

**Step 5 — Accuracy (1 mark)**

$$
Acc = \frac{TP+TN}{Total} = \frac{240+800}{1200} = \frac{1040}{1200} = 0.8667 = 86.67\%
$$

**Step 6 — Clinical Commentary (0.5 mark)**
**Sensitivity is the most clinically relevant metric for sepsis prediction** because *missing a sepsis case* leads to rapid multi-organ failure and high mortality, while *false alarms* merely trigger additional lab tests. A sensitivity of 80% is acceptable but not ideal — the KTU key recommends a target $\geq 90\%$ for sepsis screening tools.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2 Choice Q1 — Alternative Set]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **standards and interoperability frameworks** used in biomedical data: HL7 v2, HL7 FHIR, DICOM, SNOMED CT, LOINC, ICD-10/11, and OMOP CDM. For each, state one biomedical use-case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A clinical dataset of 200 patients has 120 diseased and 80 healthy. A decision-tree biomarker $A$ splits them as follows:
- `A = High`: 70 patients → 60 diseased, 10 healthy
- `A = Low`: 130 patients → 60 diseased, 70 healthy

Compute the **Information Gain** of biomarker $A$. Justify whether the biomarker should be adopted in a screening test.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**1. HL7 v2 (1 mark)** — Pipe-delimited text messaging standard for hospital-internal data exchange (e.g., lab orders between LIS and HIS). **Use-case:** ADT (Admit-Discharge-Transfer) messages.

**2. HL7 FHIR (1 mark)** — RESTful JSON/XML resource model with OAuth2.0 security. **Use-case:** Mobile-app patient-portal access to immunization records.

**3. DICOM (Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine) (1 mark)** — Standard for medical imaging, embedding pixel data + patient metadata. **Use-case:** Storing and transmitting CT/MRI scans across PACS.

**4. SNOMED CT (Systematized Nomenclature of Medicine – Clinical Terms) (1 mark)** — The most comprehensive clinical terminology (>350,000 concepts). **Use-case:** Encoding diagnosis in an EHR for semantic interoperability.

**5. LOINC (Logical Observation Identifiers Names and Codes) (1 mark)** — Universal code system for laboratory tests and clinical observations. **Use-case:** Mapping "Hemoglobin" from different hospital labs to a single code `718-7`.

**6. ICD-10 / ICD-11 (International Classification of Diseases) (1 mark)** — WHO's disease classification system used for billing and mortality statistics. **Use-case:** Coding the cause of death on a death certificate.

**7. OMOP CDM (Observational Medical Outcomes Partnership Common Data Model) (1 mark)** — OHDSI's relational schema that normalises data from heterogeneous sources for population-level analytics. **Use-case:** Multi-hospital pharmaco-epidemiology studies.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Parent Entropy $H(S)$ (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\!\left(\tfrac{120}{200}\log_2 \tfrac{120}{200} + \tfrac{80}{200}\log_2 \tfrac{80}{200}\right) \\
     &= -\!\left(0.6 \log_2 0.6 + 0.4 \log_2 0.4\right) \\
     &= -\!\left(0.6 \times (-0.7370) + 0.4 \times (-1.3219)\right) \\
     &= 0.9710 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Entropy of `A = High` (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{A=H}) &= -\!\left(\tfrac{60}{70}\log_2 \tfrac{60}{70} + \tfrac{10}{70}\log_2 \tfrac{10}{70}\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(0.8571 \times (-0.2224) + 0.1429 \times (-2.8074)\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(-0.1906 - 0.4012\right) \\
           &= 0.5918 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Entropy of `A = Low` (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{A=L}) &= -\!\left(\tfrac{60}{130}\log_2 \tfrac{60}{130} + \tfrac{70}{130}\log_2 \tfrac{70}{130}\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(0.4615 \times (-1.1156) + 0.5385 \times (-0.8931)\right) \\
           &= -\!\left(-0.5149 - 0.4809\right) \\
           &= 0.9958 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Weighted Post-Split Entropy (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid A) &= \tfrac{70}{200}\times 0.5918 + \tfrac{130}{200}\times 0.9958 \\
            &= 0.2071 + 0.6473 \\
            &= 0.8544 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Information Gain (1 mark)**

$$
IG(S, A) = 0.9710 - 0.8544 = 0.1166 \; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Verdict (0.5 mark)**
The biomarker yields a gain of **0.1166 bits** — a *weak-to-moderate* discriminator. For a screening test, the KTU examiner would accept it as *borderline*; combine with another biomarker to cross the conventional **IG $\geq$ 0.20 bits** threshold for clinical adoption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Biomedical Data**
> 1. **Confusing HL7 v2 with FHIR** (–2 marks). v2 uses pipes (`|`); FHIR uses JSON/XML over HTTPS.
> 2. **Forgetting the base of the logarithm** (–1 mark). Always default to **base 2 → bits** and state it.
> 3. **Confusing PPV with Sensitivity** (–2 marks). PPV depends on disease *prevalence*; Sensitivity does not.
> 4. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** (–0 mark but it *will* break the table). Use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX contexts.
> 5. **Skipping the clinical commentary** at the end of an analytics problem (–1 mark). KTU 2024 rewards the *engineering judgement*, not just the arithmetic.
> 6. **Ignoring the genomic compression unit** (–1 mark). Always state whether the size is in **bytes, MB, or GB**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Biomedical Data (Module 2)**

- ✅ Biomedical data is **multi-modal** (text + image + signal + molecular + tabular).
- ✅ The **8 V's** are Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value, Variability, Visualisation, Vulnerability.
- ✅ A typical **whole-genome sequence** ≈ **30 GB**; a **PET-CT scan** ≈ **1.2 TB**.
- ✅ ICU streaming velocity ≈ **1,000 samples/sec per bed**.
- ✅ EHR missingness is typically **15–30%**; imputation is mandatory.
- ✅ Key standards: **HL7 v2, HL7 FHIR, DICOM, SNOMED CT, LOINC, ICD-10/11, OMOP CDM**.
- ✅ Genomic file chain: **FASTQ → SAM → BAM → CRAM → VCF**.
- ✅ Always default **entropy to base 2**; remember $\log_2 4 = 2$ bits per nucleotide.
- ✅ **Sensitivity = TP / (TP + FN)** is the metric of choice for **screening**.
- ✅ **Specificity = TN / (TN + FP)** is the metric of choice for **confirmation**.
- ✅ **F1-score** is the harmonic mean of precision and recall.
- ✅ **Differential privacy** with $\epsilon = 1$ is the standard for federated healthcare AI.
- ✅ **HIPAA (US), GDPR (EU), DPDP Act 2023 (India)** are the three primary regulations.
- ✅ Block diagram: **Sources → Kafka → Cleaning → Standards → Lake/WH → Spark → Dashboard**.
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects a **clinical justification** at the end of every analytics problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Generation from Other Fields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Generation from Other Fields # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Generation from Other Fields** refers to the **systematic, continuous, and high-velocity production of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured datasets** from heterogeneous, non-web domains — such as scientific research, healthcare, finance, manufacturing, transportation, energy, and the Internet of Things (IoT) — that contribute to the **5 V's paradigm of Big Data**: **Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value**. In the context of **PECPT755 – Big Data Analytics (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, this topic is positioned within Module 2 to broaden the student's mental model of *where* big data actually originates. While Module 1 typically focuses on web-scale data (search engines, social media, e-commerce clickstreams), this module explicitly addresses **domain-specific data ecosystems** that drive modern analytics pipelines. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Data Rainfall Model"** Imagine the modern world as a giant **mountain range**, and each field of human activity is a different **mountain peak**. Just as each peak receives rainfall differently — one peak catches heavy snow (cold storage of genomic data), another catches drizzle (banking transactions), another catches hailstorms (high-frequency trading) — every domain generates its **own unique "rain of data"** with distinct intensity, drop size, and timing. - **Scientific research** is like a **monsoon** — massive volume of data from telescopes, particle colliders, and genome sequencers, often arriving in **petabyte-scale bursts**. - **Healthcare** is like **fine mist** — patient records, MRI scans, and wearable readings that arrive **continuously but with high sensitivity** (veracity is critical). - **Financial markets** are like a **waterfall** — millions of transactions cascading every second with extreme **velocity**. - **IoT/Sensor networks** are like **drizzle across an entire valley** — small data points from millions of devices, but cumulatively enormous. The **big data engineer** is essentially the **hydrologist** who builds the **reservoirs (databases), aqueducts (pipelines), and treatment plants (analytics)** to capture, channel, and refine this data rainfall into useful insights. ## 1.3 The 5 V's — Standard KTU Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bolded Core Metrics for KTU 2024 Board Examinations:** | Metric | Definition | Example from "Other Fields" | |--------|------------|------------------------------| | **Volume** | Total quantity of data generated | Petabytes/day from **LHC** at CERN | | **Velocity** | Speed at which data is produced and processed | **Sub-millisecond** trades in stock markets | | **Variety** | Different data formats and structures | **FASTA**, **DICOM**, **FIX protocol** | | **Veracity** | Trustworthiness and quality of data | **Noisy sensor readings** in IoT | | **Value** | Business/scientific insight extracted | **Predictive diagnostics** in healthcare | ## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Curriculum KTU examiners expect students to recognize that **big data is NOT limited to social media**. Real-world industry demand (Kerala's IT sector, Technopark, Infopark employers) is increasingly skewed toward **healthcare analytics, financial analytics, smart agriculture, and IoT-driven manufacturing**. Module 2 prepares the engineer to **contextualize the 5 V's within a specific domain** before applying storage and processing tools. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Domain-Wise Breakdown of Data Generation > [!NOTE] > **Logic Flow:** Domain → Data Source → Data Type → 5 V Dominance → Engineering Utility ### 2.1.1 Scientific & Research Data Generation - **Sources:** Particle colliders (CERN's LHC), radio telescopes (SKA, LOFAR), genome sequencers, climate simulators. - **Data Types:** Structured sensor logs, unstructured image data, semi-structured XML/JSON metadata. - **Volume Metric:** LHC produces approximately **1 petabyte/second** of raw collision data, of which only **~1% is stored** after filtering. - **Engineering Utility:** Distributed storage with **HDFS**, processing via **MapReduce** and **Apache Spark**. ### 2.1.2 Healthcare & Medical Data Generation - **Sources:** Electronic Health Records (EHR), Medical Imaging (MRI, CT, X-ray), Wearables (Fitbit, Apple Watch), Genomic sequencing. - **Data Types:** **DICOM** images, HL7 messages, CSV vitals, FASTQ/BAM genomic files. - **Velocity Metric:** A single ICU bed can generate **~1,000 data points/second**. - **Engineering Utility:** Real-time patient monitoring, predictive disease modeling, drug discovery pipelines. ### 2.1.3 Financial & Banking Data - **Sources:** Stock exchanges (NSE, BSE, NYSE), ATM networks, credit card transactions, mobile banking apps. - **Data Types:** Transactional records (structured), news feeds (unstructured), FIX protocol streams. - **Velocity Metric:** The **New York Stock Exchange** handles **~1.5 billion shares/day**, with **algorithmic trading** operating at **microsecond** latency. - **Engineering Utility:** Fraud detection, algorithmic trading, risk modeling, regulatory compliance (RBI norms). ### 2.1.4 Internet of Things (IoT) & Sensor Data - **Sources:** Smart meters, GPS trackers, industrial PLCs, agricultural sensors, connected vehicles. - **Data Types:** Time-series telemetry, log files, M2M messages via **MQTT** protocol. - **Volume Metric:** Cisco's estimates project **~79.4 zettabytes/year** from IoT devices by 2025. - **Engineering Utility:** Smart city planning, predictive maintenance, precision agriculture (relevant to Kerala's spice and rubber industries). ### 2.1.5 Industrial & Manufacturing Data (Industry 4.0) - **Sources:** SCADA systems, CNC machines, robotic arms, supply chain RFID tags. - **Data Types:** Structured operational logs, semi-structured XML-based MES data, video feeds from quality control. - **Engineering Utility:** Predictive maintenance using **Kafka + Spark Streaming**, digital twins, quality control. ### 2.1.6 Energy & Utilities - **Sources:** Smart grids, oil & gas SCADA, wind turbine sensors, solar farm inverters. - **Velocity Metric:** Smart meters record consumption every **15 minutes**, generating **~250 GB/day** for a city of 1 million. - **Engineering Utility:** Load forecasting, renewable integration, outage prediction. ### 2.1.7 Transportation & Smart Mobility - **Sources:** GPS fleet trackers, ride-hailing apps (Uber/Ola), airline telemetry, autonomous vehicle LiDAR. - **Velocity Metric:** A single autonomous test vehicle produces **~4 TB/day** from cameras, radar, and LiDAR. - **Engineering Utility:** Route optimization, traffic management, autonomous driving AI training. ### 2.1.8 Social, Public & Government (Beyond Web 2.0) - **Sources:** Census, Aadhaar/UIDAI records, GSTN filings, satellite imagery (ISRO). - **Engineering Utility:** Policy planning, disaster response, urban governance. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Equations & Metrics for KTU Board Examinations** | # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes | |---|------------------|---------------|----------------| | 1 | **Data Generation Rate** | $R_{gen} = \frac{D_{total}}{T}$ | Petabytes/hour, TB/day | | 2 | **Data Reduction Ratio** | $DRR = \frac{D_{raw} - D_{processed}}{D_{raw}} \times 100\%$ | CERN filters: $\approx 99\%$ discarded | | 3 | **Storage Cost per TB** | $C_{storage} = \frac{AnnualCost}{TotalTBStored}$ | ₹/TB-month for cloud | | 4 | **Veracity Index** | $V_{index} = \frac{AccurateRecords}{TotalRecords}$ | Sensor noise: $V_{index} \approx 0.85$ | | 5 | **Sampling Rate (Nyquist)** | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Healthcare ECG: $f_s \geq 250$ Hz | | 6 | **Total Storage over Time** | $S(t) = S_0 + \int_{0}^{t} R_{gen}(t)\, dt$ | Cumulative petabyte growth | | 7 | **5 V Composite Score** | $V_{composite} = w_1V + w_2V + w_3V + w_4V + w_5V$ | Weighted domain relevance | | 8 | **Velocity in TPS** | $V_{tps} = \frac{N_{events}}{T_{seconds}}$ | NYSE peak: $V_{tps} \approx 10^6$ | | 9 | **Data Deduplication Ratio** | $DDR = \frac{\vert D_{unique} \vert}{\vert D_{total} \vert}$ | IoT deduplication: $DDR \approx 0.30$ | | 10 | **Information Entropy (Shannon)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Bits/symbol; measures data variety | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations are used by data architects to **size Hadoop clusters, estimate cloud bills, and justify infrastructure investments** for domain-specific big data platforms. KTU examiners frequently ask students to **compute storage growth** or **estimate TPS** from a given scenario. ## 2.3 The 5 V's — A More Granular Domain Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > **Each domain has a "dominant V" that drives its engineering decisions:** - **Scientific** → dominated by **Volume** (petabyte archival) - **Finance** → dominated by **Velocity** (millisecond latency) - **Healthcare** → dominated by **Veracity** (life-critical accuracy) - **IoT** → dominated by **Variety** (heterogeneous protocols) - **Smart Manufacturing** → balanced across all 5 V's # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Module Focus:** Since this topic is **conceptual-analytical** rather than purely numerical, the "derivations" below demonstrate **quantitative estimation techniques** that KTU examiners love to test in Part B questions. ## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Total Data Generated by a Domain ### Scenario Statement A hospital network has **500 ICU beds**, each equipped with a multi-parameter monitor generating **1,000 data points/second** (heart rate, SpO₂, BP, ECG waveform, respiration). The hospital wants to estimate the **daily data volume** to size its Hadoop cluster. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the per-second data rate per bed.** Each bed produces $1{,}000$ data points/second. Assuming each data point is a 64-bit floating point number: $$\text{Size per point} = 64 \text{ bits} = 8 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 2: Compute the per-second data rate per bed.** $$R_{bed} = 1{,}000 \text{ points/s} \times 8 \text{ bytes/point}$$ $$R_{bed} = 8{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} = 8 \text{ KB/s per bed}$$ **Step 3: Compute the aggregate rate for 500 beds.** $$R_{total} = 500 \times 8 \text{ KB/s}$$ $$R_{total} = 4{,}000 \text{ KB/s} = 4 \text{ MB/s}$$ **Step 4: Convert to daily volume.** There are $86{,}400$ seconds in a day. So: $$D_{daily} = 4 \text{ MB/s} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s/day}$$ $$D_{daily} = 345{,}600 \text{ MB/day}$$ **Step 5: Express in standard units.** $$D_{daily} = \frac{345{,}600}{1{,}024} \text{ GB/day} \approx 337.5 \text{ GB/day}$$ $$D_{daily} \approx 0.3375 \text{ TB/day}$$ $$D_{yearly} \approx 0.3375 \times 365 \approx 123.2 \text{ TB/year}$$ **Step 6: Apply 3x replication factor for Hadoop HDFS.** $$D_{HDFS} = 3 \times 123.2 \text{ TB} \approx 369.6 \text{ TB/year}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conversion Logic Recap:** We converted **points/s → bytes/s → MB/s → GB/day → TB/year**, applying Hadoop's default **3x replication** to get the actual disk requirement. This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Rate for a Medical ECG ### Scenario Statement An ECG signal's highest meaningful frequency is $f_{max} = 125$ Hz. Determine the minimum sampling rate to reconstruct the signal faithfully. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: State Nyquist Theorem.** $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ **Step 2: Substitute the value.** $$f_s \geq 2 \times 125 \text{ Hz}$$ $$f_s \geq 250 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 3: Interpret the result.** The ECG monitor **must sample at least 250 times per second** to avoid aliasing. Standard clinical ECGs sample at **250–500 Hz**, which matches our derived bound. **Step 4: Compute data volume for 24-hour Holter monitoring at 500 Hz with 12-bit resolution.** $$\text{Size per sample} = 12 \text{ bits} = 1.5 \text{ bytes}$$ $$R = 500 \text{ samples/s} \times 1.5 \text{ bytes/sample} = 750 \text{ bytes/s}$$ $$D_{24h} = 750 \times 86{,}400 \approx 64.8 \text{ MB per patient/day}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why It Matters:** KTU examiners often test whether students can connect a **signal processing theorem** with a **big data storage implication**. Showing this linkage earns full marks. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Dominant Big Data Characteristic per Domain > [!NOTE] > **This matrix is a favorite KTU "compare-and-contrast" question template.** | Domain | Primary Data Type | Dominant V | Example Volume | Typical Storage | |--------|-------------------|------------|----------------|-----------------| | **Particle Physics (LHC)** | Sensor/event streams | Volume | 1 PB/s raw | HDFS + Cold Tape | | **Genomics** | Sequences (FASTQ) | Volume + Veracity | 200 GB/genome | Object stores (S3) | | **Stock Trading** | Trade ticks | Velocity | 10⁶ events/s | In-memory (Redis, kdb+) | | **EHR Records** | Patient records | Veracity + Variety | 80 MB/patient/year | RDBMS + Data Lake | | **Smart Grid** | Meter readings | Velocity + Volume | 250 GB/day/million users | Time-series DB (InfluxDB) | | **Autonomous Vehicles** | LiDAR + Camera | Variety + Volume | 4 TB/day/vehicle | Edge + Cloud hybrid | | **Social Media** (for contrast) | Posts, images | All 5 V's | 500M tweets/day | HDFS + NoSQL | | **Agricultural IoT (Kerala)** | Soil, climate sensors | Velocity + Variety | 50 MB/day/farm | MQTT + InfluxDB | --- ## 3.4 Derivation: Shannon Information Entropy for Sensor Data Variety ### Scenario Statement A weather station reports 4 conditions: **Sunny (0.5), Rainy (0.25), Cloudy (0.15), Stormy (0.10)**. Compute the entropy in bits/symbol. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Write Shannon's entropy formula.** $$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$ **Step 2: Substitute each probability.** $$H(X) = -[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.10 \log_2 0.10]$$ **Step 3: Compute each term.** - $0.5 \log_2 0.5 = 0.5 \times (-1) = -0.5$ - $0.25 \log_2 0.25 = 0.25 \times (-2) = -0.5$ - $0.15 \log_2 0.15 \approx 0.15 \times (-2.737) \approx -0.411$ - $0.10 \log_2 0.10 = 0.10 \times (-3.322) \approx -0.332$ **Step 4: Sum and negate.** $$H(X) = -[(-0.5) + (-0.5) + (-0.411) + (-0.332)]$$ $$H(X) = -[-1.743] = 1.743 \text{ bits/symbol}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The weather station carries **1.743 bits of information per symbol** — a measure of its **variety** dimension in the 5 V's framework. Lower entropy means more predictability (less data value per observation). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Type:** Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow mapping the **multi-domain data generation ecosystem** into a unified big data pipeline. Designed for KTU 2024 scheme module-level comprehension. ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Multi-Domain Data Generation to Big Data Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Scientific Research
LHC Telescopes Genome Sequencers] -->|Petabyte Streams| P1[Ingestion Layer
Apache Kafka Flume Sqoop] A2[Healthcare Sector
EHR DICOM Wearables Genomics] -->|Patient Data Streams| P1 A3[Financial Markets
Stock Tick Data ATM Transactions] -->|Microsecond Events| P1 A4[IoT Sensor Networks
Smart Meters GPS RFID Tags] -->|Telemetry MQTT| P1 A5[Industrial 4.0
SCADA CNC Robotics MES] -->|Operational Logs| P1 A6[Energy and Utilities
Smart Grid Wind Solar] -->|Time Series| P1 A7[Transportation
Fleet Tracking LiDAR Autonomous| -->|GPS and Video| P1 A8[Government Public
Census Aadhaar Satellite Imagery] -->|Records Imagery| P1 P1 -->|Raw Storage| S1[Data Lake
HDFS Object Store] P1 -->|Stream Processing| S2[Real Time Engine
Spark Streaming Flink] S1 --> B1[Batch Analytics
MapReduce Hive Pig] S2 --> B2[Real Time Analytics
Spark ML Streaming Dashboards] B1 --> C1[Data Warehouse
HBase Cassandra Snowflake] B2 --> C1 C1 --> D1[Visualization and Decision
Tableau PowerBI Grafana ML Models] style A1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style A2 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff style A3 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style A4 fill:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff style A5 fill:#831843,color:#ffffff style A6 fill:#854d0e,color:#ffffff style A7 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style A8 fill:#374151,color:#ffffff style P1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff style S1 fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff style S2 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff style B1 fill:#16a34a,color:#ffffff style B2 fill:#65a30d,color:#ffffff style C1 fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff style D1 fill:#9333ea,color:#ffffff ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to Read This Diagram:** > - **Left column (A1–A8):** The **eight major domains** of data generation covered in this module. > - **Center (P1):** The **unified ingestion layer** — a key KTU concept showing that regardless of source, all big data must pass through a common gateway. > - **Right path (S1 → B1 → C1):** The **batch processing pipeline** (latency-tolerant, high-volume). > - **Upper-right path (S2 → B2 → C1):** The **stream processing pipeline** (low-latency, real-time). > - **Far right (D1):** The **decision layer** where insights are consumed. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: 5 V's Domain Mapping (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR V1[Volume] -->|Dominant in| D1[Scientific Research] V2[Velocity] -->|Dominant in| D2[Financial Trading] V3[Variety] -->|Dominant in| D3[IoT Networks] V4[Veracity] -->|Dominant in| D4[Healthcare] V5[Value] -->|Extracted in| D5[All Domains via Analytics] D1 --> ENG1[Archival HDFS Cold Storage] D2 --> ENG2[In Memory kdb Redis Spark] D3 --> ENG3[NoSQL and Data Lake] D4 --> ENG4[Validated RDBMS and Data Lake] D5 --> ENG5[BI and Machine Learning] style V1 fill:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff style V2 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff style V3 fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff style V4 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff style V5 fill:#a16207,color:#ffffff style D1 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style D2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style D3 fill:#4c1d95,color:#ffffff style D4 fill:#064e3b,color:#ffffff style D5 fill:#78350f,color:#ffffff style ENG1 fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff style ENG2 fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff style ENG3 fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff style ENG4 fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff style ENG5 fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Note:** This diagram visually reinforces that **each "V" maps to a specific engineering solution**. KTU examiners often ask: *"Which V is most prominent in healthcare?"* — and this diagram gives the answer: **Veracity**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assessment Structure (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** > - **Part A:** Short-answer questions, **3 marks** each. > - **Part B:** Long-answer questions with **internal choice**, **14 marks** each (typically split as **a) 7 marks + b) 7 marks**). > - All questions are mapped to **Course Outcomes (CO2)** and **Bloom's Levels (L2–L4)**. --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember (L1) | 3 Marks** > *"Define the term 'Data Generation from Other Fields' in the context of Big Data Analytics. Mention any two domains with one example of data source from each."* #### Model Answer (Key Points): 1. **Definition:** Data generation from other fields refers to the continuous and high-velocity production of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data from non-web domains such as scientific research, healthcare, finance, manufacturing, and IoT. 2. **Domain 1 — Healthcare:** Source — Electronic Health Records (EHR), generating patient vitals, prescriptions, and DICOM images. 3. **Domain 2 — Financial Markets:** Source — Stock exchange tick data, generating millions of trade events per second. 4. **Core idea:** Each domain contributes to the **5 V's** of big data with varying dominance. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand (L2) | 3 Marks** > *"List and briefly explain any three 'V's of Big Data. Give one real-world example of each from a non-web field."* #### Model Answer (Key Points): 1. **Volume** — Total size of data. *Example:* CERN's LHC generates **~1 petabyte/second** of collision data. 2. **Velocity** — Speed of data generation. *Example:* NYSE processes **~10⁶ trade events/second** during peak hours. 3. **Variety** — Different data formats. *Example:* Smart hospitals deal with **HL7 messages, DICOM images, and CSV vitals** simultaneously. 4. **Veracity** (optional 4th) — Data quality. *Example:* IoT soil sensors in Kerala's rubber plantations may have **15% noise** due to environmental interference. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **CO2 | Bloom Level: Apply + Analyze (L3-L4) | 14 Marks** > **a)** Explain in detail the **sources, types, and dominant characteristics** of data generation in the **healthcare domain**. Discuss at least **three sub-domains** (e.g., EHR, genomics, wearable devices) and identify the most prominent "V" of big data. **(7 marks)** > > **b)** A **smart grid deployment in Kerala** has **100,000 smart meters**, each sending one reading every **15 minutes**, with each reading being a **32-byte JSON payload**. Compute: > - (i) Daily data volume in MB and GB > - (ii) Monthly data volume in TB > - (iii) Identify the dominant V's and recommend a suitable storage strategy. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Introduction to Healthcare Data Generation (2 marks)** Healthcare is one of the **fastest-growing big data domains** globally. The Indian healthcare sector alone is expected to generate **~50 petabytes** of data by 2025. The data is generated from multiple touchpoints — clinical, administrative, genomic, and personal devices. **Step 2: Three Sub-Domains (3 marks)** 1. **Electronic Health Records (EHR):** Generated during patient consultations, hospitalizations, and lab tests. Each record contains demographics, diagnoses (ICD-10 codes), prescriptions, and progress notes — mostly **structured and semi-structured**. 2. **Medical Imaging (DICOM):** CT, MRI, X-ray, and ultrasound machines produce **DICOM-standard images**. A single CT scan may be **100–500 MB**; a 3D MRI can exceed **1 GB**. 3. **Wearables and Remote Monitoring:** Devices like Apple Watch, continuous glucose monitors (CGM), and ECG patches generate **continuous time-series data** — high velocity but smaller per-record size. **Step 3: Dominant "V" Identification (2 marks)** - The **dominant V in healthcare is Veracity** (data accuracy and trustworthiness are life-critical). - **Volume** and **Variety** are also significant. - Engineering solutions require **strong data validation, audit trails, and HIPAA/Discharge India compliance**. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Compute the per-meter daily data (2 marks)** - Readings per meter per day: $\frac{24 \times 60}{15} = 96$ readings - Size per reading: $32$ bytes - Daily per meter: $96 \times 32 = 3{,}072$ bytes $\approx 3 \text{ KB}$ **Step 2: Compute aggregate daily data (2 marks)** - Total daily volume: $100{,}000 \times 3{,}072 = 307{,}200{,}000$ bytes - Convert: $\frac{307{,}200{,}000}{10^6} = 307.2 \text{ MB/day}$ - Convert to GB: $\frac{307.2}{1{,}024} \approx 0.30 \text{ GB/day}$ **Step 3: Compute monthly volume (1.5 marks)** - Monthly: $0.30 \times 30 \approx 9 \text{ GB/month}$ - In TB: $\frac{9}{1{,}024} \approx 0.0088 \text{ TB/month}$ **Step 4: Dominant V's and Storage Strategy (1.5 marks)** - **Dominant V's:** **Velocity** (regular 15-min cycles) and **Volume** (scalable with deployment growth). - **Recommended Storage:** **Time-series database** (e.g., **InfluxDB, TimescaleDB**) for ingestion; **data lake (HDFS or S3)** for archival; **Grafana** dashboards for visualization. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (b):** Students frequently **forget to convert bytes → KB → MB → GB → TB** at the right step. Always show **each conversion explicitly**. Also, remember that $1 \text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes (binary), but in many KTU papers, decimal ($10^3$) is also accepted. **State your assumption clearly** to earn full marks. --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **CO2 | Bloom Level: Apply + Analyze (L3-L4) | 14 Marks** > **a)** Discuss in detail the **sources and characteristics of data generation in scientific research** (e.g., CERN's LHC, radio astronomy, genomic sequencing). Explain the concept of **Data Reduction Ratio (DRR)** with a worked example showing that LHC filters discard 99% of raw data. **(7 marks)** > > **b)** Compare and contrast the data generation patterns of the **financial domain** and the **IoT domain** along the **5 V's of Big Data**. Provide at least **two real-world examples** from Kerala's context (e.g., Kerala State Financial Enterprises, Kochi Metro smart cards). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Scientific Research Data Sources (2 marks)** - **CERN's Large Hadron Collider (LHC):** Produces ~1 PB/s of raw collision data from 4 detectors (ATLAS, CMS, ALICE, LHCb). - **Square Kilometre Array (SKA):** Radio telescope generating **~700 TB/s** of raw signal data. - **Genome Sequencers (Illumina, Nanopore):** Generate **FASTQ** files of **~200 GB per human genome**. **Step 2: Common Characteristics (2 marks)** - Data is **append-only, immutable, and time-stamped**. - Storage uses a **tiered architecture** (hot disk + cold tape). - Processing is **embarrassingly parallel** — ideal for **MapReduce and Spark**. **Step 3: Data Reduction Ratio (DRR) Worked Example (3 marks)** - LHC raw rate: $1 \text{ PB/s}$ - After hardware-level triggering: $\approx 1 \text{ GB/s}$ stored. - Using the formula: $$DRR = \frac{D_{raw} - D_{processed}}{D_{raw}} \times 100\%$$ $$DRR = \frac{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ GB/s} - 1 \text{ GB/s}}{1{,}000{,}000 \text{ GB/s}} \times 100\%$$ $$DRR = \frac{999{,}999}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 99.9999\%$$ - Practically, this is reported as **~99%** (the KTU board's expected answer). **Step 4: Real-World Implication (1 mark)** This filtering enables CERN to store **only the "interesting" collision events** on its 1+ exabyte grid, distributed across **170+ data centers worldwide** via the **Worldwide LHC Computing Grid (WLCG)**. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Financial Domain Data Generation (2 marks)** - **Sources:** NSE/BSE tick data, ATM transactions, UPI payments, KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) billing. - **Dominant V's:** **Velocity** (microsecond trades) and **Veracity** (regulatory compliance). - **Kerala Context:** **Kerala State Financial Enterprises (KSFE)** chit fund transactions generate millions of records monthly. **Step 2: IoT Domain Data Generation (2 marks)** - **Sources:** Smart meters, vehicle GPS, agricultural sensors, **Kochi Metro** smart card validators. - **Dominant V's:** **Variety** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP) and **Volume** (millions of devices). - **Kerala Context:** **Kochi Metro** smart card system generates **~50,000 tap events/day** — time-series data suitable for stream analytics. **Step 3: Side-by-Side Comparison Table (3 marks)** | Dimension | Financial Domain | IoT Domain | |-----------|------------------|-------------| | **Volume** | High (TB/day) | Massive (ZB/year globally) | | **Velocity** | Ultra-high (µs) | Moderate (seconds) | | **Variety** | Low (structured FIX) | High (heterogeneous) | | **Veracity** | Critical (regulatory) | Moderate (noisy sensors) | | **Value** | Trading signals, fraud | Smart city, automation | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B (a):** A common mistake is **confusing CERN's "1 PB/s" with the *stored* data rate**. The raw rate is 1 PB/s, but only ~1 GB/s is archived. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if students do not make this distinction explicit. Always state **"raw" vs. "filtered"** data. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before your KTU exam!)** ### ✅ Core Conceptual Takeaways - "Data Generation from Other Fields" covers **non-web big data sources** beyond social media. - The **5 V's — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value** — apply differently to each domain. - Each domain has a **dominant V** that drives its engineering and storage choices. ### ✅ Domain Quick-Reference - **Scientific** → Volume dominant → HDFS, WLCG, batch processing. - **Healthcare** → Veracity dominant → EHR systems, DICOM, data validation critical. - **Financial** → Velocity dominant → In-memory DBs (Redis, kdb+), Kafka streams. - **IoT** → Variety dominant → MQTT brokers, time-series DBs (InfluxDB). - **Industrial 4.0** → Balanced → Kafka + Spark Streaming + digital twins. - **Energy/Utilities** → Velocity + Volume → Smart grid analytics, load forecasting. - **Transportation** → Volume + Variety → GPS, LiDAR, autonomous vehicle data. - **Government** → Volume + Veracity → Census, Aadhaar, satellite imagery. ### ✅ Must-Know Formulas - $R_{gen} = \frac{D_{total}}{T}$ — Generation rate. - $DRR = \frac{D_{raw} - D_{processed}}{D_{raw}} \times 100\%$ — Reduction ratio. - $V_{index} = \frac{AccurateRecords}{TotalRecords}$ — Veracity index. - $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ — Nyquist sampling theorem (medical signals). - $H(X) = -\sum p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ — Shannon entropy (data variety). ### ✅ Common KTU Exam Traps 1. Confusing **raw vs. processed** data rates (CERN, LHC). 2. Forgetting the **3x HDFS replication factor** when computing storage. 3. Mixing **binary (1024)** and **decimal (1000)** conversion factors. 4. Treating "Big Data" as **only** social media — KTU penalizes narrow definitions. 5. Confusing **DICOM** (medical imaging) with **DHCP** (networking) or **DDoS** (security). ### ✅ Kerala-Context Bonus Points (Impress the Examiner!) - Reference **Kochi Metro** smart cards, **KSFE** chit data, **KSEB** smart meters, **Spice Board** agricultural sensors — these earn **bonus cognitive marks** for contextual awareness. - Mention **Kerala State IT Mission** projects for smart governance. ### ✅ Key Acronyms to Memorize - **EHR** — Electronic Health Record - **DICOM** — Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine - **LHC** — Large Hadron Collider - **SCADA** — Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition - **MQTT** — Message Queuing Telemetry Transport - **WLCG** — Worldwide LHC Computing Grid - **FIX** — Financial Information eXchange protocol - **PLC** — Programmable Logic Controller

topics.title: Big Data Acquisition - Data Collection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Big Data Acquisition - Data Collection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Acquisition** refers to the systematic process of identifying, gathering, filtering, cleaning, and transporting raw data from heterogeneous sources into a storage environment (such as HDFS, NoSQL databases, or data lakes) where it can be persistently stored, indexed, and subsequently processed for analytical workloads. It is the **first and most critical phase** of the Big Data analytics lifecycle, often termed the *Ingestion* stage.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, data acquisition encompasses three sub-processes:

1. **Data Collection (Identification & Gathering)** — discovering and pulling raw signals from sources.
2. **Data Ingestion** — transporting the collected data into a Big Data storage system.
3. **Data Pre-processing** — cleaning, transforming, and validating prior to analytics.

For **Module 2**, our exclusive focus is on **Data Collection**, i.e., the mechanisms and architectures used to physically capture raw data from diverse origins.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Sorting" Analogy

Imagine a massive national library (your Big Data Lake) that receives **10 million books every day** from thousands of different cities. The librarians cannot simply wait for trucks to dump books randomly in the parking lot. Instead, they require:

- **Conveyor belts** (ingestion pipelines like Kafka) that move books in real time.
- **Scheduled cargo trucks** (batch ingestion like Sqoop) that bring bulk deliveries nightly.
- **Quality inspectors** (Flume interceptors, schema validators) who reject damaged books at the gate.
- **Sorting hubs** (Kafka topics, Flume channels) that route books to the correct floor.

**Data Collection** is analogous to the **gates, scanning machines, and intake clerks** of the library — it determines *what* enters, *how much*, *when*, and *in what format*.

### 1.3 Classification of Big Data Sources

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks students to classify sources. Memorize the three-tier taxonomy below.

| **Source Type** | **Examples** | **Format** | **Velocity** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structured | RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL), OLTP systems, ERP, CRM, financial ledgers | Tables, rows, columns, fixed schema | Batch / periodic |
| Semi-Structured | XML, JSON, CSV, logs, sensor streams, emails, NoSQL exports (MongoDB collections) | Tagged, hierarchical, partially schema-ed | Stream / batch |
| Unstructured | Images, videos, audio, social media posts, PDFs, web pages, IoT telemetry, surveillance feeds | Binary, free-text, raw bytes | High-velocity continuous |

### 1.4 The "3 V's + 1" Framework Applied to Acquisition

The well-known Big Data characteristics directly govern acquisition strategy:

- **Volume** → dictates whether batch (high) or stream (continuous) is required.
- **Velocity** → dictates latency tolerance (real-time vs. near-real-time vs. offline).
- **Variety** → dictates the need for schema-on-read vs. schema-on-write.
- **Veracity** → dictates the depth of validation at the collection gate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** When asked to *justify* the choice of a collection method, always reference at least one of the 4 V's. Examiners award marks for explicit linkage to data characteristics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Canonical Data Collection Paradigms

#### 2.1.1 Batch Data Collection
