- Data is accumulated over a fixed time window and ingested in **bulk** at scheduled intervals.
- Ideal when **latency tolerance is high** (hours to days).
- Examples: nightly ETL from operational databases to data warehouse; monthly log aggregation.
- **KTU-relevant tools:** Apache Sqoop (RDBMS → HDFS), Oozie-coordinated jobs, traditional ETL platforms (Informatica, Talend).

#### 2.1.2 Real-Time (Stream) Data Collection

- Data is ingested **continuously, record-by-record** as it is generated.
- Latency tolerance is **milliseconds to seconds**.
- Examples: stock-tick streams, clickstream analysis, fraud detection, IoT sensor telemetry.
- **KTU-relevant tools:** Apache Kafka, Apache Flume, Amazon Kinesis, Google Pub/Sub.

#### 2.1.3 Mini-Batch / Micro-Batch Collection

- Compromise between batch and stream: small batches ingested at very short intervals (seconds).
- Example: Spark Streaming's micro-batch engine.
- Latency: **seconds to a few minutes**.

### 2.2 Architecture of a Data Collection System

A production-grade Big Data collection system comprises the following logical components:

> [!NOTE]
> **Six-Stage Data Collection Pipeline (KTU High-Yield):**
>
> 1. **Data Source** → origin of raw data (sensor, DB, log file, API endpoint).
> 2. **Data Generator/Producer** → the application/device that creates the data.
> 3. **Message Broker / Collector** → Kafka broker, Flume agent, or message queue.
> 4. **Channel / Buffer** → in-memory or disk-backed queue that decouples producers and consumers.
> 5. **Sink / Consumer** → the destination (HDFS, HBase, S3, Elasticsearch, Spark).
> 6. **Schema Registry & Validation Layer** → ensures data conforms to expected contracts.

### 2.3 Core Data Collection Methods — Detailed Breakdown

#### 2.3.1 Apache Flume

- **Purpose:** Distributed, reliable service for efficiently collecting, aggregating, and moving **large amounts of log data** from many different sources to a centralized data store.
- **Architecture:** Multi-tier **Agent-based** model.
- **Key components of a Flume Agent:**

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| Source | Receives events from the data generator (e.g., `netcat`, `exec`, `spooldir`, `kafka`, `http`, `avro`) |
| Channel | Passive store that buffers events between Source and Sink (e.g., `memory`, `file`, `JDBC`, `Kafka`) |
| Sink | Removes events from channel and stores them (e.g., `hdfs`, `hive`, `hbase`, `kafka`, `logger`, `avro`) |
| Interceptor | Performs lightweight in-flight transformations (regex filtering, masking, timestamping) |

- **Event anatomy in Flume:**
  $$\text{Event} = \{\text{Header} \cup \text{Body}\}$$
  where **Header** is a key-value metadata set and **Body** is the opaque byte payload.

#### 2.3.2 Apache Kafka

- **Purpose:** Distributed **publish-subscribe** messaging system designed for **high-throughput, fault-tolerant, real-time** data streams.
- **Core abstractions:**

| **Term** | **Definition** |
|---|---|
| Topic | A named stream of records (logical channel) |
| Partition | Ordered, append-only sequence within a topic; unit of parallelism |
| Offset | 64-bit integer identifying each record within a partition |
| Producer | Client that publishes records to a topic |
| Consumer | Client that subscribes to and processes records |
| Broker | Kafka server hosting partitions |
| Zookeeper / KRaft | Coordination service for cluster metadata |

- **Key formula — Throughput estimate:**
  $$\text{Throughput}_{\text{topic}} = \sum_{i=1}^{P} \text{Throughput}_{\text{partition}_i}$$

#### 2.3.3 Apache Sqoop

- **Purpose:** Bulk transfer tool for moving data between **RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL)** and **Hadoop (HDFS, Hive, HBase)**.
- Operates on **MapReduce** under the hood.
- Two primary operations:
  - **Import** → RDBMS → HDFS (parallel mappers extract chunks via SQL `WHERE` predicates).
  - **Export** → HDFS → RDBMS.
- **Connector architecture:** Optimized connectors for major databases; generic JDBC connector as fallback.

#### 2.3.4 Other Collection Mechanisms

| **Mechanism** | **Best Suited For** |
|---|---|
| REST API polling (e.g., Twitter Streaming API) | Social media, public web data |
| Web crawlers / Scrapers (Scrapy, Nutch) | Web-scale unstructured data |
| MQTT / CoAP brokers | IoT / sensor data with constrained devices |
| Log shippers (Logstash, Filebeat) | Server logs, container logs |
| Change Data Capture (CDC) | Real-time capture of DB mutations (Debezium) |

### 2.4 Data Formats Used During Collection

| **Format** | **Type** | **Strengths** |
|---|---|---|
| CSV / TSV | Text, row-based | Human-readable, universal |
| JSON | Semi-structured, hierarchical | Native web, self-describing |
| XML | Semi-structured, hierarchical | Strong schema (XSD), legacy enterprise |
| Avro | Binary, schema-based | Compact, schema evolution, Hadoop-native |
| Parquet | Binary, columnar | Compression, predicate pushdown, analytics |
| ORC | Binary, columnar | Optimized for Hive, ACID support |
| Protobuf | Binary, schema-based | High performance, gRPC integration |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative concept required for Part A/B answers in this module.

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Variables / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Flume event | $\text{Event} = \text{Header} \cup \text{Body}$ | Header: KV pairs, Body: bytes |
| Kafka throughput | $\text{TPS}_{\text{topic}} = \sum_{i=1}^{P} \text{TPS}_{\text{partition}_i}$ | $P$ = partitions, TPS = records/s |
| Parallelism limit | $\text{Consumer}_{\text{max}} = \sum_{i=1}^{T} P_i$ | $T$ = topics, $P_i$ = partitions of topic $i$ |
| Storage size estimate (Parquet) | $S \approx \frac{R \times C}{c}$ | $R$ = rows, $C$ = avg bytes/row uncompressed, $c$ = compression ratio (typ. 0.2 – 0.3) |
| Network bandwidth | $BW = \frac{\text{Data Size (bits)}}{\text{Time window (s)}}$ | bits per second |
| Sqoop parallelism | $N_{\text{mappers}} \le N_{\text{partitions}}_{\text{DB}}$ | DB-side physical partition count |
| End-to-end latency | $L_{e2e} = L_{\text{ingest}} + L_{\text{buffer}} + L_{\text{sink}}$ | Sum of stage-wise latencies (ms) |
| Backpressure (rate) | $R_{\text{consumer}} \ge R_{\text{producer}}$ | Sustained processing ≥ ingestion rate |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency of a Kafka-Based Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU question** because it integrates multiple components.

**Problem statement:** A Kafka-based ingestion pipeline contains 4 producers generating records at rates $r_1, r_2, r_3, r_4$ (records/sec). Each record is of size $s$ bytes. The cluster has $B$ brokers, and the topic has $P$ partitions. The consumer group has $C$ consumers each writing to HDFS at sustained throughput $w$ (bytes/sec). Derive an expression for the steady-state system latency.

#### Step 1 — Aggregate Arrival Rate

The total rate at which records enter the topic is the sum of producer rates:

$$
R_{\text{in}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} r_i
$$

#### Step 2 — Per-Partition Load (Ideal Distribution)

Assuming a uniform hash-based distribution, the per-partition rate is:

$$
R_{\text{partition}} = \frac{R_{\text{in}}}{P}
$$

#### Step 3 — Bandwidth Demand

Total bandwidth required at the broker ingress:

$$
\text{BW}_{\text{broker}} = \frac{R_{\text{in}} \times s}{B}
$$

provided that $\text{BW}_{\text{broker}} \le \text{BW}_{\text{link}}$ (link capacity). If exceeded, network becomes the bottleneck.

#### Step 4 — Consumer-Side Drain Condition

To prevent unbounded queue growth (no backpressure loss), the aggregate consumer throughput must satisfy:

$$
C \times w \;\ge\; R_{\text{in}} \times s
$$

Rearranging:

$$
C \;\ge\; \frac{R_{\text{in}} \times s}{w}
$$

#### Step 5 — End-to-End Latency Composition

The end-to-end latency decomposes into four additive terms:

$$
L_{e2e} = L_{\text{prod}} + L_{\text{broker}} + L_{\text{buffer}} + L_{\text{consume}}
$$

where:

- $L_{\text{prod}} = t_{\text{batch}} / 2$ (half the producer batching window, on average).
- $L_{\text{broker}} = d_{\text{replication}} \times t_{\text{RTT}}$ (depends on replication factor $d$).
- $L_{\text{buffer}} = \frac{R_{\text{in}} \times s}{C \times w - R_{\text{in}} \times s} \times t_{\text{unit}}$ (M/M/1-style queue approximation).
- $L_{\text{consume}} = t_{\text{HDFS\_write}}$.

#### Step 6 — Stability Condition

For the buffer not to grow unboundedly, the system must satisfy:

$$
C \times w > R_{\text{in}} \times s
$$

If equality holds, the buffer is marginal; if violated, the system is unstable.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Collection Tool

| **Requirement** | **Recommended Tool** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|
| RDBMS → HDFS nightly | Sqoop | Parallel JDBC + MR bulk transfer |
| Continuous log streams | Flume | Tuned for log sources, agent-based |
| High-throughput pub-sub for multiple consumers | Kafka | Decoupled, durable, replayable log |
| Social media APIs (Twitter, etc.) | Custom producer + Kafka | REST polling then publish to topic |
| IoT sensor fleet (constrained devices) | MQTT broker → Kafka bridge | Lightweight protocol, low bandwidth |
| Server log centralization | Filebeat → Logstash → Kafka | Beats are resource-light shippers |
| Web scale (billions of pages) | Apache Nutch / Scrapy → HDFS | Crawl + dump to distributed FS |

### 3.3 Worked Example — Sqoop Parallelism Selection

**Question:** A MySQL table `orders` contains **80 million rows** and is partitioned by range into **16 physical partitions**. We want to import to HDFS in minimum time using Sqoop with 4 mapper slots per node, and we have 5 worker nodes. Determine the recommended `--num-mappers` value.

**Solution:**

- **Step 1:** Identify the limiting factor. Database partitioning defines the maximum safe parallel readers = 16.
- **Step 2:** Compute cluster capacity = 5 nodes × 4 mappers = 20.
- **Step 3:** Apply the rule:
  $$
  N_{\text{mappers}} = \min(N_{\text{DB-partitions}},\; N_{\text{cluster-slots}}) = \min(16, 20) = 16
  $$
- **Step 4:** Each mapper reads one DB partition in parallel. Total wall time is dominated by the slowest partition, not the average.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often choose `num-mappers = 20` (cluster max), but this causes contention on DB partitions and may violate foreign-key constraints. The correct upper bound is the number of DB partitions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Notice

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs in this section are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `src1`, `kafka1`). Labels are plain uppercase text, free of markdown emphasis or HTML tags, to guarantee successful rendering.

### 4.2 High-Level Data Collection Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SOURCE 1 RDBMS] --> P1[PRODUCER 1]
    B[SOURCE 2 WEB API] --> P2[PRODUCER 2]
    C[SOURCE 3 IOT SENSORS] --> P3[PRODUCER 3]
    D[SOURCE 4 APPLICATION LOGS] --> P4[FLUME AGENT]
    P1 --> K1[KAFKA BROKER CLUSTER]
    P2 --> K1
    P3 --> K1
    P4 --> K1
    K1 --> T1[TOPIC A]
    K1 --> T2[TOPIC B]
    T1 --> C1[CONSUMER 1 HDFS WRITER]
    T1 --> C2[CONSUMER 2 SPARK STREAMING]
    T2 --> C3[CONSUMER 3 HBASE WRITER]
    T2 --> C4[CONSUMER 4 ELASTICSEARCH INDEXER]
    C1 --> ST1[HDFS DATA LAKE]
    C2 --> ST2[SPARK CLUSTER]
    C3 --> ST3[HBASE NOSQL STORE]
    C4 --> ST4[ELASTICSEARCH CLUSTER]
```

### 4.2.1 Functional Architecture Flow Explanation

- **SOURCES (1–4)** are heterogeneous — RDBMS, web API, IoT, and logs. They illustrate the **Variety** V.
- **PRODUCERS / FLUME AGENT** normalise ingestion: Flume for log-centric flows, Kafka producers for API and DB streams.
- **KAFKA BROKER CLUSTER** acts as the **durable, replayable message backbone** — the central decoupling layer.
- **TOPICS A and B** represent logical channels separating, for example, raw telemetry from enriched events.
- **CONSUMERS (1–4)** represent **independent downstream pipelines**, each with its own sink. This is the **publish-subscribe** power of Kafka.
- **STORAGE LAYERS (HDFS, Spark, HBase, Elasticsearch)** represent the **variety of analytical workloads** that can be served by the same collected data.

### 4.3 Flume Agent Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC1[EXTERNAL SOURCE] --> EV1[EVENT 1]
    SRC2[NETCAT SOURCE] --> EV2[EVENT 2]
    SRC3[SPOOLDIR SOURCE] --> EV3[EVENT 3]
    EV1 --> IN1[INTERCEPTOR 1 TIMESTAMP]
    EV2 --> IN2[INTERCEPTOR 2 REGEX FILTER]
    EV3 --> IN3[INTERCEPTOR 3 MASKING]
    IN1 --> CH1[CHANNEL MEMORY]
    IN2 --> CH1
    IN3 --> CH1
    CH1 --> SK1[SINK HDFS]
    CH1 --> SK2[SINK KAFKA]
    CH1 --> SK3[SINK HBASE]
    SK1 --> FD1[HDFS]
    SK2 --> FD2[KAFKA TOPIC]
    SK3 --> FD3[HBASE]
```

### 4.3.1 Flume Agent Explanation

- **External sources** push events into Flume **Sources** (three types shown).
- **Interceptors** run synchronously on each event to enrich (timestamp), filter (regex), or sanitize (PII masking) — KTU frequently tests interceptor use cases.
- **Channel** is the **transactional buffer**; it guarantees at-least-once delivery between source and sink.
- **Sinks** (one or more) drain the channel and persist to the destination. A single channel can have multiple sinks via **replicating or multiplexing channel selectors**.

### 4.4 Kafka Topic-Partition Internals

```mermaid
graph TD
    TP[TOPIC ORDERS] --> PA[PARTITION 0 LEADER]
    TP --> PB[PARTITION 1 LEADER]
    TP --> PC[PARTITION 2 LEADER]
    PA --> PA1[OFFSET 0]
    PA --> PA2[OFFSET 1]
    PA --> PA3[OFFSET 2]
    PA --> PA4[OFFSET 3 NEW]
    PB --> PB1[OFFSET 0]
    PB --> PB2[OFFSET 1]
    PC --> PC1[OFFSET 0]
    PC --> PC2[OFFSET 1]
    PC --> PC3[OFFSET 2]
    PA -.REPLICA.-> PR1[FOLLOWER BROKER 1]
    PB -.REPLICA.-> PR2[FOLLOWER BROKER 2]
    PC -.REPLICA.-> PR3[FOLLOWER BROKER 3]
```

### 4.4.1 Topic-Partition Explanation

- A **Topic** is split into an ordered set of **Partitions**.
- Each partition maintains an **immutable, monotonically increasing offset** sequence.
- **Leader/Follower** replication ensures fault tolerance (replication factor typically 3).
- **Ordering guarantee:** records within a single partition are strictly ordered; across partitions, ordering is **not** guaranteed — a critical point for KTU exam answers.

### 4.5 Batch vs. Stream — Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{WHAT IS THE LATENCY REQUIREMENT} -->|HOURS TO DAYS| B[BATCH COLLECTION SQOOP OOZIE]
    Q1 -->|SECONDS| Q2{IS THE SOURCE CONTINUOUS OR BURSTY}
    Q1 -->|MILLISECONDS| S[STREAM COLLECTION KAFKA FLUME KINESIS]
    Q2 -->|CONTINUOUS| S
    Q2 -->|BURSTY| M[MICRO BATCH SPARK STRUCTURED STREAMING]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q: Define Big Data Acquisition. List any four sources of Big Data.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Big Data Acquisition is the process of identifying, gathering, and transporting raw data from heterogeneous sources into a Big Data storage system for subsequent processing and analysis. It is the first stage of the Big Data analytics lifecycle. (1.5 marks)
> Four sources of Big Data: (i) Social media platforms (Twitter, Facebook), (ii) IoT sensors and devices, (iii) Enterprise databases (RDBMS), (iv) Web server logs. (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Differentiate between structured and unstructured data with two examples each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Structured data conforms to a predefined schema, resides in fixed fields, and is easily searchable using SQL. Examples: bank transaction records in MySQL; ERP inventory tables in Oracle. (1.5 marks)
> Unstructured data lacks a predefined schema and is typically stored in native format. Examples: surveillance video footage; customer email bodies; image files from CCTV. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Data Collection Answers:**
>
> 1. **Tool–Workload Mismatch:** Students often describe Kafka as a storage system or HDFS as a real-time tool. Examiners deduct 2 marks for these misrepresentations.
> 2. **Omitting At-Least-Once Semantics:** When describing Flume/Kafka delivery guarantees, always state explicitly whether the system is at-most-once, at-least-once, or exactly-once.
> 3. **Skipping Justification:** Listing a tool without justifying *why* it suits the scenario costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing Latency Analysis:** Real-time questions without any mention of latency bounds, batching, or backpressure are marked incomplete.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the three data collection paradigms (batch, real-time, micro-batch) with suitable examples and a comparative table. (7 marks)
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of Apache Kafka. List its key components and explain at-least-once and exactly-once delivery semantics. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

The three paradigms are:

**1. Batch Data Collection (2 marks)**
- Data accumulated over a window and processed in bulk.
- Latency: hours to days.
- Examples: nightly ETL from an OLTP MySQL database to a Hadoop data lake using **Apache Sqoop**; monthly aggregation of call detail records.

**2. Real-Time / Stream Data Collection (2 marks)**
- Records ingested one-by-one as they are produced.
- Latency: milliseconds to seconds.
- Examples: stock-tick ingestion via **Kafka**; clickstream analysis via **Flink**; sensor telemetry via **AWS Kinesis**.

**3. Micro-Batch Data Collection (1 mark)**
- Compromise: small batches processed at very short intervals.
- Example: Spark Structured Streaming's micro-batch engine with 2-second trigger intervals.

**Comparative Table (2 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Batch** | **Micro-Batch** | **Stream** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | Hours – Days | Seconds – Minutes | Milliseconds – Seconds |
| Throughput | Very High | High | Moderate – High |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Typical Tool | Sqoop | Spark Streaming | Kafka, Flink |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Kafka Block Diagram (3 marks)** — see Section 4.4 of these notes for the topic-partition diagram. Key components:

- **Producer:** publishes records to topics.
- **Broker:** Kafka server; stores partitions.
- **Topic & Partition:** logical stream divided into ordered sub-streams.
- **Consumer / Consumer Group:** subscribes and processes records.
- **Zookeeper / KRaft:** cluster coordination (controller election, metadata).

**Delivery Semantics (4 marks):**

- **At-most-once:** Producer does not retry on failure. Risk: data loss. Achieved by setting `acks=0` and not retrying.
- **At-least-once (default):** Producer retries on failure. Risk: duplicates. Achieved with `acks=all` and idempotent producers (`enable.idempotence=true`).
- **Exactly-once:** Combines idempotent producers + transactional APIs + read_committed consumers. Guarantees no loss and no duplicates across producer-to-topic and topic-to-sink flows.

**Equation — Idempotent Producer Constraint:**

$$
\text{Producer} \rightarrow \text{Producer ID} + \text{Sequence Number} \rightarrow \text{Broker deduplication}
$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q, Module 2 Alt]**
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of Apache Flume in detail. Discuss the roles of Source, Channel, Sink, and Interceptor with examples. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Compare and contrast Apache Sqoop and Apache Flume. When would you choose one over the other? (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Flume Architecture (3 marks):** A Flume **Agent** is a JVM process that hosts three primitives — Source, Channel, Sink. See Section 4.3 of these notes for the internal diagram.

- **Source (1 mark):** Receives events from external sources. Examples: `exec` (tail a file), `netcat` (TCP), `spooldir` (directory of new files), `http`, `kafka`.
- **Channel (1 mark):** Passive store between Source and Sink. Two common types:
  - `memory-channel` — fast, volatile (loses data on crash).
  - `file-channel` — durable, persistent.
- **Sink (1 mark):** Removes events from channel and writes them externally. Examples: `hdfs`, `hive`, `hbase`, `kafka`, `logger`.
- **Interceptor (1 mark):** Performs synchronous, in-flight transformation. Examples:
  - `timestamp` — adds event timestamp header.
  - `regex_extractor` — extracts regex groups into headers.
  - `regex_filter` — drops events that do not match.

**Delivery guarantee:** Flume channels use **transactions** — a source puts an event into a channel only after a successful `txn.begin/commit`; sinks remove it via the same transactional mechanism. This yields **at-least-once** delivery by default.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **Apache Sqoop** | **Apache Flume** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | RDBMS ↔ Hadoop bulk transfer | Streaming log/event collection |
| Data Type | Structured, tabular | Semi-structured, log-like |
| Mode | Batch (MapReduce) | Stream / continuous |
| Direction | Bidirectional (Import/Export) | Unidirectional (Source → Sink) |
| Architecture | Driver + Mappers | Agent (Source, Channel, Sink) |
| Schema | Required (column mapping) | Optional / opaque |
| Example | MySQL → HDFS import | Web server logs → HDFS |

**When to Choose (3 marks):**

- **Choose Sqoop** when the source is an **RDBMS**, the data has a **clear schema**, and ingestion is **periodic batch** (e.g., nightly dimension loads, periodic CRM extracts).
- **Choose Flume** when the source is **streaming logs or events**, the data is **semi/unstructured**, and **low-latency continuous** collection is needed (e.g., clickstream, app logs, sensor streams).
- **Hybrid scenario:** Use Sqoop for nightly reference data and Flume for continuous operational data, both landing in the same HDFS directory.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 / Data Collection**

- **Definition:** Data Collection is the *gathering* phase; Ingestion is the *transport* phase; both are part of Acquisition.
- **Three paradigms:** Batch (Sqoop), Stream (Kafka/Flume), Micro-Batch (Spark Streaming).
- **Flume primitives:** Source, Channel, Sink, Interceptor. Delivery is **at-least-once**.
- **Kafka primitives:** Producer, Broker, Topic, Partition, Offset, Consumer Group. Default delivery is **at-least-once**; use transactions for **exactly-once**.
- **Sqoop limitation:** parallel mappers ≤ DB physical partitions. Do not over-parallelise.
- **Formats:** Prefer **Avro** (schema evolution) or **Parquet** (columnar analytics) over CSV for Big Data.
- **Latency equation:** $L_{e2e} = L_{\text{prod}} + L_{\text{broker}} + L_{\text{buffer}} + L_{\text{consume}}$.
- **Stability condition:** $C \times w > R_{\text{in}} \times s$ to avoid unbounded buffer growth.
- **Ordering rule:** Per-partition ordered; across-partition unordered.
- **Justify every tool choice** by referencing the 3V/4V characteristics of the data.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Confusing HDFS (storage) with Kafka (transport); describing Kafka as a database; omitting delivery semantics in stream answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transportation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Data Transportation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Data Transportation** in Big Data Analytics refers to the systematic and controlled movement of structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data from heterogeneous source systems (databases, log servers, IoT sensors, social media, cloud storages) to destination systems (Data Lakes, Data Warehouses, HDFS, analytical engines) using specialized ingestion frameworks, messaging queues, serialization formats, and network protocols that guarantee fault-tolerance, scalability, and throughput. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, *Data Transportation* is positioned as the **second critical phase** of the Big Data pipeline (preceded by **Data Generation/Aggregation** and followed by **Data Storage**). It abstracts the mechanics of how bytes physically and logically move across the cluster fabric, dealing with: - **Ingestion Topologies** (Batch, Micro-batch, Real-time/Streaming) - **Transfer Protocols** (TCP/IP, HTTP/REST, FTP/SFTP, JDBC/ODBC, AMQP, MQTT) - **Serialization Standards** (Avro, Parquet, ORC, JSON, Protobuf, SequenceFile) - **Ingestion Engines** (Apache Sqoop, Apache Flume, Apache Kafka, Apache NiFi, Logstash, Filebeat) ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Postal System of Big Data > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – Think of Data Transportation as a National Postal Service** > > Imagine your data sources are **factories** in different cities producing goods (data). The **Data Transportation layer** is the entire postal system: the trucks, planes, sorting centers, packaging standards, and tracking barcodes. > > - The **box** in which goods are packed → **Serialization Format (Avro/Parquet)** > - The **truck/plane route** → **Transport Protocol (TCP/IP, HTTP)** > - The **dispatcher tracking every parcel** → **Messaging Broker (Apache Kafka)** > - The **specialized vehicle for liquid chemicals** → **Sqoop** (specialized for RDBMS extraction) > - The **specialized vehicle for flowing tap water** → **Flume** (specialized for log streams) > - The **post office that never sleeps** → **Real-time streaming pipeline** > - The **post office that delivers once a day** → **Batch pipeline** ## 1.3 Core Components Taxonomy The transportation layer is decomposed into **four orthogonal concerns** that KTU examiners frequently test: | Concern | Function | KTU Standard Tools | |:---|:---|:---| | **Source Connector** | Pulls data from origin system | Sqoop, JDBC, Debezium | | **Transport Channel** | Carries data in-motion | Kafka, Flume, NiFi | | **Serialization Schema** | Packs data into wire bytes | Avro, Parquet, Protobuf | | **Sink Destination** | Hands off data to storage | HDFS, HBase, S3, Hive | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize (Bold-Critical):** > - **Throughput**: Measured in **MB/s** or **messages/sec** > - **Latency**: Measured in **milliseconds (ms)** > - **At-least-once / At-most-once / Exactly-once**: Delivery guarantees > - **Batching Window**: Time-bound group size, e.g., **100 MB** or **60 seconds** > - **Backpressure**: Mechanism to slow down producers when consumers lag > - **Idempotency**: Property allowing safe re-processing of duplicate messages --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Data Transportation ### Pillar 1 — Ingestion Paradigm (When to Move Data?) Big Data transportation is broadly classified into three temporal models: **(a) Batch Ingestion** - Data is collected over a time window and moved as a single unit. - Latency is high (**minutes to hours**). - Used for: daily ETL, monthly reporting, historical analytics. - Example tools: **Apache Sqoop, Hadoop DistCp, Oozie-scheduled jobs.** **(b) Micro-batch Ingestion** - Data is moved in small, frequent chunks (typically every **2–10 seconds**). - Latency is moderate (**seconds**). - Example tool: **Apache Spark Streaming** (uses micro-batches internally). **(c) Real-time / Streaming Ingestion** - Data is moved record-by-record as soon as it is produced. - Latency is ultra-low (**sub-second, often < 100 ms**). - Example tools: **Apache Kafka, Apache Flume, Apache Pulsar, AWS Kinesis.** ### Pillar 2 — Transport Channel (How Data Moves) The transport channel determines reliability, ordering, and throughput: - **Message Brokers (Publish-Subscribe)**: Decouple producer and consumer via topics/queues. *Kafka* uses partitioned commit logs. - **Log Aggregators**: Specifically harvest logs from machine files. *Flume* uses the **Source → Channel → Sink** topology. - **Direct Connectors**: Single-hop, point-to-point. *Sqoop* uses parallel **MapReduce** jobs to dump RDBMS tables into HDFS. - **Flow-Based Processors**: GUI-driven, record- routing. *NiFi* provides visual flow definitions. ### Pillar 3 — Serialization (In What Shape Data Moves) > [!NOTE] > **Serialization** is the process of converting in-memory data structures into a byte stream suitable for storage or transmission. **Deserialization** is the reverse. The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes four serialization standards: 1. **Apache Avro** – Row-based, schema-in-data, compact binary, supports schema evolution. Ideal for **Kafka payloads**. 2. **Apache Parquet** – Columnar, highly compressed, predicate pushdown friendly. Ideal for **analytical queries on HDFS**. 3. **Apache ORC** – Optimized Row Columnar, similar to Parquet but with built-in indexes (min/max, bloom filters). Native to **Hive**. 4. **Protocol Buffers (Protobuf)** – Google's binary format, schema-first, very fast. Used in **gRPC microservices**. ## 2.2 Architectural Details of Key KTU Tools ### Apache Sqoop (SQL-to-Hadoop) - **Function**: Bidirectional transfer between **RDBMS** and **HDFS/Hive/HBase**. - **Mechanism**: Generates **MapReduce** jobs. The mappers extract rows in parallel by splitting the table on a **boundary column** (e.g., `--split-by id`). - **Critical Parameters**: `--connect`, `--username`, `--table`, `--target-dir`, `--as-avrodatafile`, `--incremental`, `--m (mappers)`. - **Modes**: Full load vs. Incremental load (`append` / `lastmodified`). ### Apache Flume (Log Streaming) - **Function**: Continuously streams log data from many sources into HDFS/HBase. - **Topology**: - **Source** (e.g., `exec`, `tail`, `spooldir`, `http`, `kafka`) - **Channel** (memory, file, JDBC, Kafka channel) - **Sink** (HDFS, HBase, Kafka, Logger) - **Reliability Mechanism**: Uses **transactions** between Source↔Channel and Channel↔Sink, achieving **end-to-end at-least-once** delivery. ### Apache Kafka (Distributed Messaging) - **Function**: High-throughput, distributed, fault-tolerant **publish-subscribe** messaging. - **Core Abstractions**: - **Topic** – a named stream of records. - **Partition** – a topic is split into ordered, append-only logs. - **Offset** – a monotonically increasing 64-bit integer identifying a record in a partition. - **Broker** – a Kafka server hosting partitions. - **Producer / Consumer** – clients writing/reading. - **Consumer Group** – a set of consumers that share the work of reading a topic. - **ZooKeeper / KRaft** – cluster coordination controller. - **Guarantees**: - Within a partition → **strict ordering**. - Across partitions → **no global order**. - Delivery → configurable: **at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once** (via transactions in Kafka ≥ 0.11). ### Apache NiFi (Flow-Based Programming) - **Function**: Visual dataflow with built-in provenance, backpressure, and prioritization. - **Concepts**: **FlowFile** (data + attributes), **Processor** (unit of work), **Connection** (queue between processors), **Process Group**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metrics Table > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations and constants appear regularly in KTU University Exam Part-B numerical/descriptive questions. **Memorize these.** | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Typical Value | |:---|:---|:---| | **Data Transfer Time (T)** | $$T = \frac{D}{B}$$ | D = Data size (bits), B = Bandwidth (bps) | | **Effective Throughput** | $$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{elapsed}}}$$ | Bytes/sec | | **Parallel Speedup** | $$S = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}}$$ | Dimensionless; max = number of mappers | | **Amdahl's Law for I/O** | $$S_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{N}}$$ | f = parallelizable fraction, N = mappers | | **Compression Ratio** | $$C_r = \frac{S_{\text{original}}}{S_{\text{compressed}}}$$ | Parquet/ORC typically 5×–10× | | **Latency Bound (Streaming)** | $$L = t_{\text{ingest}} + t_{\text{process}} + t_{\text{egress}}$$ | Sub-second target | | **Kafka Partition Capacity** | $$\text{Throughput} \approx N_{\text{partitions}} \times R_{\text{broker}}$$ | ~10–50 MB/s per partition | | **Bounded Queue Size** | $$Q = \lambda \times W$$ | λ = arrival rate, W = wait time (Little's Law) | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking**: Real-time fraud detection requires Kafka → Flink → Dashboard with < **500 ms** latency. - **E-Commerce**: Clickstream logs from web servers are streamed via **Flume** to **HDFS** and then processed by Spark. - **Telecom**: CDR (Call Detail Record) ingestion from 100M+ subscribers uses **Kafka** partitioned by user-ID to parallelize consumption. - **IoT**: Sensor data from millions of devices uses **MQTT** (lightweight) → **Kafka** → Time-series DB. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Worked Examples & Mathematical Depth ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Data Transfer Time for a Parallelized Sqoop Import **Problem Statement:** A relational table of size **D = 500 GB** must be imported from MySQL into HDFS using Apache Sqoop with **N = 8 parallel mappers**. The network bandwidth per mapper is **B = 80 Mbps** (megabits per second). Compute the **theoretical total transfer time**, assuming zero overhead and that data is split evenly. **Step 1 — Unit Standardization** Convert the data size from Gigabytes to Megabits, because bandwidth is in **Mbps**. $$ D_{\text{mbits}} = D_{\text{GB}} \times 8 \times 1024 $$ Substitute D = 500 GB: $$ D_{\text{mbits}} = 500 \times 8 \times 1024 = 4{,}096{,}000 \text{ Mbits} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Per-Mapper Data Share** With 8 mappers sharing the load uniformly: $$ D_{\text{per\_mapper}} = \frac{D_{\text{mbits}}}{N} $$ $$ D_{\text{per\_mapper}} = \frac{4{,}096{,}000}{8} = 512{,}000 \text{ Mbits} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Time per Mapper Using T = D / B** $$ T_{\text{mapper}} = \frac{D_{\text{per\_mapper}}}{B} $$ $$ T_{\text{mapper}} = \frac{512{,}000}{80} = 6{,}400 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to Hours** $$ T_{\text{hours}} = \frac{6{,}400}{3{,}600} \approx 1.78 \text{ hours} $$ **Final Answer:** The import theoretically completes in **≈ 1.78 hours** with 8 parallel mappers, versus **≈ 14.22 hours** sequentially. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kafka Throughput Estimation via Little's Law **Problem Statement:** A Kafka topic receives records at an arrival rate **λ = 20,000 messages/sec**. The average time a message spends inside the consumer pipeline (fetch + process + commit) is **W = 50 ms = 0.05 s**. Estimate the **in-flight message count** Q (the minimum partition buffer occupancy required for stability). **Step 1 — State Little's Law** Little's Law (from queueing theory) states: $$ Q = \lambda \times W $$ This holds for any system in **steady state** regardless of distribution. **Step 2 — Substitute Values** $$ Q = 20{,}000 \times 0.05 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate** $$ Q = 1{,}000 \text{ messages} $$ **Interpretation:** The system must hold at least **1,000 messages in-flight** at any moment. This justifies setting Kafka's `max.poll.records` and the consumer's internal queue depth to ≥ 1,000 to prevent backpressure and consumer lag accumulation. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Storage Savings from Parquet Columnar Compression **Problem Statement:** A CSV file of customer transactions occupies **S_csv = 1,200 MB** on HDFS. When converted to **Parquet** with Snappy compression, the size becomes **S_parquet = 180 MB**. Compute the **compression ratio C_r**, the **percentage saving**, and the **estimated I/O time saved** if the disk read bandwidth is **R = 200 MB/s**. **Step 1 — Compression Ratio** $$ C_r = \frac{S_{\text{csv}}}{S_{\text{parquet}}} $$ $$ C_r = \frac{1{,}200}{180} = 6.67 $$ **Step 2 — Percentage Saving** $$ \text{Saving}(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{S_{\text{parquet}}}{S_{\text{csv}}}\right) \times 100 $$ $$ \text{Saving}(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{180}{1{,}200}\right) \times 100 = 85\% $$ **Step 3 — Read Time for CSV** $$ T_{\text{csv}} = \frac{S_{\text{csv}}}{R} = \frac{1{,}200}{200} = 6 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 4 — Read Time for Parquet** $$ T_{\text{parquet}} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.9 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 5 — Time Saved** $$ \Delta T = 6 - 0.9 = 5.1 \text{ seconds} $$ **Engineering Insight:** This **5.1 s saved per scan** compounds across thousands of Spark/Hive queries per day — justifying the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasis on columnar formats for **analytical workloads**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Amdahl's Law Applied to Streaming Parallelism **Problem Statement:** A Flink job processes streamed events. The **serial fraction** (non-parallelizable coordination overhead) is **f_s = 0.05 (5%)** and the **parallel fraction** is **f_p = 0.95 (95%)**. If we scale from **N = 1** to **N = 32** parallel task slots, compute the maximum theoretical speedup. **Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law** $$ S_{\text{max}}(N) = \frac{1}{f_s + \frac{f_p}{N}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute N = 32** $$ S_{\text{max}}(32) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \frac{0.95}{32}} $$ $$ S_{\text{max}}(32) = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0297} = \frac{1}{0.0797} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate** $$ S_{\text{max}}(32) \approx 12.55 $$ **Interpretation:** Despite 32× hardware, we cap at **12.55× speedup** because of the irreducible 5% serial portion. This is the *theoretical ceiling* — KTU examiners love this insight to test if students understand scalability limits in data pipelines. --- ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Tools vs. Properties) | Property | Apache Sqoop | Apache Flume | Apache Kafka | Apache NiFi | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Primary Data Source** | RDBMS | Logs / Files | Any (via connectors) | Any (via processors) | | **Ingestion Mode** | Batch | Streaming | Streaming | Streaming + Batch | | **Transport Topology** | Pull (MR-based) | Push (agent-based) | Pub-Sub (broker) | Flow-based (visual) | | **Delivery Guarantee** | At-least-once | At-least-once | Exactly-once (with transactions) | At-least-once / Exactly-once | | **Typical Latency** | Minutes | Seconds | Sub-second (10–100 ms) | Configurable | | **Schema Awareness** | Yes (JDBC) | No (raw bytes) | Yes (with Schema Registry) | Yes (with Record readers) | | **Fault Tolerance** | Job retry | Channel transactions | ISR replication | Write-Ahead Log + flow repo | | **Best Use Case** | DB → HDFS bulk | Log aggregation | Event bus backbone | Complex routing pipelines | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Big Data Transportation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph srcA["SOURCE SYSTEMS"] srcA1["RDBMS MySQL"] srcA2["Log Files"] srcA3["IoT Sensors"] srcA4["REST APIs"] end subgraph ing["INGESTION LAYER"] ing1["Sqoop"] ing2["Flume"] ing3["Kafka Connect"] ing4["NiFi"] end subgraph trn["TRANSPORT BUS"] bus1["Apache Kafka Topic"] end subgraph fmt["SERIALIZATION"] fmt1["Avro Encoder"] fmt2["Parquet Writer"] end subgraph dst["DESTINATION STORAGE"] dst1["HDFS"] dst2["HBase"] dst3["S3 Data Lake"] dst4["Hive Warehouse"] end srcA1 --> ing1 srcA2 --> ing2 srcA3 --> ing3 srcA4 --> ing4 ing1 --> bus1 ing2 --> bus1 ing3 --> bus1 ing4 --> bus1 bus1 --> fmt1 fmt1 --> fmt2 fmt2 --> dst1 fmt2 --> dst2 fmt2 --> dst3 fmt2 --> dst4 ``` ## 4.2 Apache Flume Internal Architecture (Source → Channel → Sink) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Web Server Log File"] --> B["Flume Source exec/tail"] B --> C{"Channel Type"} C -->|Memory| D["Memory Channel"] C -->|File| E["File Channel"] C -->|Kafka| F["Kafka Channel"] D --> G["HDFS Sink"] E --> G F --> G G --> H["HDFS DataNode"] H --> I["Hive External Table"] style A fill:#f9e79f style H fill:#aed6f1 style I fill:#a9dfbf ``` ## 4.3 Apache Kafka Cluster Topology with Partitioned Topics ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph prod["PRODUCERS"] p1["Web App Producer"] p2["Mobile App Producer"] p3["IoT Producer"] end subgraph brokers["KAFKA BROKER CLUSTER"] b1["Broker 1"] b2["Broker 2"] b3["Broker 3"] end subgraph topicA["Topic: click_events 3 Partitions"] tA0["P0 Leader Broker1"] tA1["P1 Leader Broker2"] tA2["P2 Leader Broker3"] end subgraph cons["CONSUMER GROUPS"] cg1["Analytics Service"] cg2["Real-time Dashboard"] cg3["Data Lake Loader"] end p1 --> tA0 p2 --> tA1 p3 --> tA2 tA0 --> b1 tA1 --> b2 tA2 --> b3 tA0 --> cg1 tA1 --> cg2 tA2 --> cg3 style tA0 fill:#fadbd8 style tA1 fill:#fadbd8 style tA2 fill:#fadbd8 style b1 fill:#d5dbdb style b2 fill:#d5dbdb style b3 fill:#d5dbdb ``` ## 4.4 Sqoop Parallel Import — MapReduce Job Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR rdb["MySQL Table Orders
rows split on id"] --> spl{"Split-by column id"} spl --> m1["Mapper 1
rows 0 to 31250"] spl --> m2["Mapper 2
rows 31251 to 62500"] spl --> m3["Mapper 3
rows 62501 to 93750"] spl --> m4["Mapper 4
rows 93751 to 125000"] m1 --> h1["HDFS Block 1"] m2 --> h2["HDFS Block 2"] m3 --> h3["HDFS Block 3"] m4 --> h4["HDFS Block 4"] h1 --> hive["Hive Table orders_staging"] h2 --> hive h3 --> hive h4 --> hive style spl fill:#f5b7b1 style hive fill:#abebc6 ``` ## 4.5 Serialization Format Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Choose Serialization Format"] --> q1{"Is the workload
analytical OLAP?"} q1 -->|Yes| q2{"Need schema evolution
+ Kafka payload?"} q1 -->|No| q3{"Streaming event bus
+ schema registry?"} q2 -->|Yes| parq["Parquet on HDFS
+ Avro on Kafka"] q2 -->|No| orc["ORC on Hive"] q3 -->|Yes| avro["Avro with Schema Registry"] q3 -->|No| proto["Protobuf for gRPC"] style parq fill:#82e0aa style orc fill:#f9e79f style avro fill:#aed6f1 style proto fill:#f5b7b1 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. Differentiate between batch and real-time data ingestion with one suitable tool for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Batch Ingestion | Real-time Streaming Ingestion | |:---|:---|:---| | **Latency** | Minutes to hours | Sub-second (< 1 s) | | **Data Volume per Trigger** | Large (GB–TB) | Small (KB–MB) | | **Trigger** | Scheduled (cron, Oozie) | Event-driven (continuous) | | **Examples** | Apache Sqoop, DistCp, Hive LOAD | Apache Kafka, Flume, Flink | | **Use Case** | Daily ETL, monthly reports | Fraud detection, live dashboards | > ✅ **Award 1 mark per correct row**; 0.5 mark for partial correctness. **Examiner Tip:** Always mention the *latency* difference — it's a KTU favorite. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. What is the role of the Schema Registry in a Kafka-based data transportation pipeline?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Confluent Schema Registry** is a centralized service that stores and manages **Avro / Protobuf / JSON-Schema** definitions used by Kafka producers and consumers. Its roles are: 1. **Schema Versioning (1 mark):** Producers register a schema; consumers fetch the latest compatible version — this enables **schema evolution** (backward/forward compatibility). 2. **Payload Size Reduction (1 mark):** Records are written with a 4-byte magic byte + schema ID instead of embedding the full schema, saving bandwidth. 3. **Data Contract Enforcement (1 mark):** Rejects malformed records at the producer side, ensuring **end-to-end data quality** before data ever leaves the source. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the **Apache Kafka** distributed messaging system. Discuss the roles of **Producer, Broker, Topic, Partition, Offset, Consumer Group**, and **ZooKeeper/KRaft controller**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Apache Flume** architecture. Explain its **Source, Channel, Sink** components and how it achieves **end-to-end reliability** for log data transportation into HDFS. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition (1 mark):** Apache Kafka is a distributed, partitioned, replicated commit-log service that functions as a high-throughput pub-sub messaging system for real-time data transportation. **Core Components (4 marks):** - **Producer**: Application that publishes (writes) records to a Kafka topic. Producers choose which partition to write to (round-robin, key-hash, or custom). - **Broker**: A Kafka server that hosts topic partitions. Multiple brokers form a Kafka cluster. - **Topic**: A named, logical category/stream of records. Topics are split into **partitions** for parallelism. - **Partition**: An ordered, immutable, append-only log file on disk. Each partition has a **leader** (handles reads/writes) and **followers** (replicas providing fault tolerance via **ISR** — In-Sync Replicas). - **Offset**: A 64-bit monotonically increasing integer assigned to each record within a partition, used by consumers to track read position. - **Consumer Group**: A set of consumers that cooperatively consume a topic, with each partition assigned to exactly one consumer in the group, enabling **horizontal scaling**. - **ZooKeeper / KRaft Controller**: Manages cluster metadata, leader election, and membership. Modern Kafka (≥ 2.8) supports **KRaft mode** (self-managed, no ZooKeeper dependency). **Workflow (1 mark):** Producer → Topic Partition (Leader) → Replication to Followers → Consumer pulls records by offset. **Key Properties (1 mark):** Ordering within partition, durability via replication, **at-least-once / exactly-once** delivery semantics (via idempotent producer + transactional API). **Reference Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes — the Kafka Cluster Topology diagram shows all the above components.) --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Definition (1 mark):** Apache Flume is a distributed, reliable service for efficiently **collecting, aggregating, and transporting** large volumes of log data from many sources to a centralized data store such as HDFS or HBase. **Architecture (3 marks):** A Flume **Agent** is a JVM process consisting of three components: - **Source**: The ingress point that receives events from external sources. Examples: `exec` (runs a Unix command like `tail -F`), `spooldir` (reads new files from a directory), `http`, `kafka source`, `netcat`. - **Channel**: A passive store that buffers events between Source and Sink. Types: **Memory Channel** (fast, volatile), **File Channel** (durable on disk, recommended for production), **Kafka Channel** (uses Kafka topic as buffer). - **Sink**: The egress point that writes events to the destination. Examples: `HDFS sink`, `HBase sink`, `Kafka sink`, `Logger sink`. **Reliability Mechanism (2 marks):** Flume uses a **two-stage transactional** model: - **Source → Channel transaction**: Source puts events into the channel and commits; only then does it acknowledge upstream. - **Channel → Sink transaction**: Sink reads events, writes to HDFS, and only then commits the offset. - This guarantees **end-to-end at-least-once** delivery: a failure mid-transaction rolls back, and the event is replayed. **Topology Use Case (1 mark):** Multiple agents can be chained — Agent 1 on each web server → Agent 2 aggregator → HDFS — providing **horizontal scalability**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A* > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **Apache Sqoop** architecture for transferring data between **RDBMS and HDFS**. Discuss the difference between **full load and incremental load** with command-line examples. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the **serialization formats — Avro, Parquet, ORC, and Protobuf** — used during data transportation. Recommend the most suitable format for (i) Kafka event payloads, (ii) analytical queries on HDFS, (iii) Hive warehouse tables, (iv) gRPC microservices. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition (1 mark):** Apache Sqoop is a tool designed for **bulk transfer of data between Hadoop and structured datastores** (RDBMS, data warehouses, NoSQL). **Architecture (2 marks):** Sqoop generates and submits **MapReduce** jobs: - The **Sqoop client** connects to the RDBMS via JDBC, retrieves **table metadata**, and computes **min/max bounds** of the split-by column. - It launches **N mappers** (controlled by `-m` flag), each querying the RDBMS with a `WHERE id >= lower AND id < upper` predicate (or using `ROWID` range queries for Oracle). - Each mapper writes its slice directly into HDFS as **delimited text, Avro, SequenceFile, ORC, or Parquet**. **Full Load vs Incremental Load (3 marks):** | Mode | Behavior | Command | |:---|:---|:---| | **Full Load** | Imports the entire table every run. | `sqoop import --connect jdbc:mysql://host/db --table employees --target-dir /data/emp --m 4` | | **Incremental – append** | Imports only rows with id > last-value. | `sqoop import ... --incremental append --check-column id --last-value 1000` | | **Incremental – lastmodified** | Imports rows modified after a timestamp. | `sqoop import ... --incremental lastmodified --check-column updated_at --last-value "2024-01-01"` | **Use Case (1 mark):** Full load for initial bootstrap; incremental for daily CDC (Change Data Capture) updates. --- **Model Solution for (b):** | Format | Type | Schema | Compression | Best Suited For | |:---|:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Avro** | Row-based, binary | Schema in header (JSON) | Snappy, Deflate | **(i) Kafka event payloads** — schema evolution support is critical for evolving event streams. | | **Parquet** | Columnar, binary | Schema in footer | Snappy, GZIP, ZSTD | **(ii) Analytical queries on HDFS** — predicate pushdown and column pruning drastically reduce I/O. | | **ORC** | Columnar, binary | Schema in file footer | ZLIB, Snappy | **(iii) Hive warehouse tables** — built-in indexes (min/max, bloom filters) and ACID support are Hive-native. | | **Protobuf** | Row-based, binary | `.proto` schema | None by default | **(iv) gRPC microservices** — strongly typed, language-agnostic, generated stubs for Java/Python/Go. | **Award marks:** 1 mark for correctly identifying the property of each format, 1 mark per correct mapping (4 mappings = 4 marks), 2 marks for justification in the comparison. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data Transportation Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Kafka's "topic" with a "queue".** A Kafka topic is a **log**, not a queue — messages are not deleted on consumption. (-1 mark) > 2. **Stating Sqoop is "real-time".** Sqoop is **batch-only**; writing Sqoop is for real-time will be marked wrong. (-2 marks) > 3. **Forgetting replication factor in Kafka.** A production Kafka topic should have `replication.factor ≥ 3`. Not mentioning it loses application-level marks. > 4. **Mixing up Sqoop export and import.** Import = RDBMS → HDFS; Export = HDFS → RDBMS. Reversing these in a question loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Ignoring idempotency in Flume/Kafka.** KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **exactly-once** semantics — failing to mention transactions, idempotent producers, or write-ahead-logs loses marks. > 6. **Forgetting units in numericals.** Mbps vs MB/s confusion is the #1 source of calculation errors. Always state units. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorize Bullet Points (for last-night revision):** > > - **Data Transportation** = movement of data from sources → storage using ingestion tools, protocols, and serialization formats. > - **Three ingestion paradigms**: Batch (Sqoop), Micro-batch (Spark Streaming), Streaming (Kafka, Flume, NiFi). > - **Sqoop**: RDBMS ↔ HDFS via MapReduce; supports full + incremental load. > - **Flume**: Source → Channel → Sink; **two-stage transactions** = at-least-once. > - **Kafka**: Partitioned commit log; **ordering per partition**; topics are split into partitions replicated across brokers. > - **Kafka delivery**: at-most-once, at-least-once, **exactly-once** (idempotent producer + transactions). > - **Avro** = row-based + schema evolution → best for Kafka. > - **Parquet** = columnar + compression → best for analytical HDFS reads. > - **ORC** = Hive-native columnar with indexes. > - **Protobuf** = best for gRPC microservices. > - **Schema Registry** = central schema versioning for Kafka. > - **Key metrics**: Throughput (MB/s), Latency (ms), Compression ratio, Speedup (Amdahl's Law). > - **Standard formula**: $T = D / B$ for transfer time; $S = 1 / (f_s + f_p/N)$ for parallel speedup. > - **Real-time latency target** is typically **< 100 ms**; batch latency is **minutes–hours**. > - **Flume channel types**: Memory (fast/risky), File (durable/slower), Kafka (scalable). > - **Kafka cluster controller**: ZooKeeper (legacy) or **KRaft** (modern, no ZK). ---

topics.title: Data Pre-processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Pre-processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition
> **Data Pre-processing** is the foundational phase of the Knowledge Discovery in Databases (KDD) and Big Data Analytics pipeline that systematically transforms *raw, heterogeneous, incomplete, noisy, and inconsistent* big data into a *clean, integrated, normalized, and analytically-ready* format. It is formally defined as the set of techniques, algorithms, and procedures applied to data **before** it is fed into a machine learning model, statistical engine, or analytical algorithm to enhance data quality, reduce computational cost, and improve the accuracy of downstream analytical tasks.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, data pre-processing is positioned as a **mandatory precursor** to descriptive and predictive analytics because the **4 V's of Big Data** (Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity) inherently introduce data quality issues at unprecedented scale.

### 1.2 The Major Tasks of Data Pre-processing (Taxonomy)

The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes data pre-processing into **five canonical tasks**, each addressing a specific dimension of data quality:

| Task | Core Objective | Big Data Relevance |
|------|---------------|-------------------|
| Data Cleaning | Handle missing, noisy, inconsistent values | Critical due to sensor failures, network drops |
| Data Integration | Merge heterogeneous sources | Mandatory across HDFS, NoSQL, RDBMS |
| Data Transformation | Normalize, scale, aggregate | Required for ML convergence |
| Data Reduction | Reduce volume/dimensionality | Essential for storage & compute cost |
| Data Discretization | Convert continuous to categorical | Useful for association rule mining |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] Real-World Analogy — The Kitchen Prep Station
> Imagine you are a professional chef preparing a massive banquet for 5,000 guests (this is your **Big Data analytics task**). You cannot simply toss unwashed, unpeeled, moldy, and mixed vegetables directly into the pot.
>
> - **Data Cleaning** is like *washing vegetables, removing rotten parts, and discarding stones mixed in with the rice*.
> - **Data Integration** is like *combining ingredients from the cold storage, the local market, and the spice garden into one unified prep table*.
> - **Data Transformation** is like *chopping, dicing, and measuring ingredients into standardized portions (cups, grams)*.
> - **Data Reduction** is like *removing the parts you don't need — peels, stems, seeds — to make the cooking manageable*.
> - **Data Discretization** is like *sorting ingredients into labeled bins: "Vegetables", "Spices", "Dairy"*.
>
> Without this prep work, your dish (model) will be inedible (inaccurate), or you will exhaust your time and fuel (compute resources) before it's ready.

### 1.4 Why Data Pre-processing is Non-Negotiable in Big Data

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU High-Yield Fact
> Industry studies (and a recurring theme in KTU 2024 ESE questions) consistently show that data scientists spend **60–80%** of their project time on data pre-processing. Furthermore, the standard KTU textbook reference states: *"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO) — the quality of output is bounded by the quality of input."*

The **Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO)** principle dictates that even the most sophisticated Hadoop/Spark cluster or neural network cannot recover meaningful insights from fundamentally flawed data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Task 1 — Data Cleaning (Noise, Missing Values, Inconsistency)

**2.1.1 Missing Values**
Missing data occurs when no data value is stored for an attribute. In big data streams (Kafka, IoT), this arises from sensor failure, network latency, or deliberate omission.

**Strategies to handle missing values:**
- **Ignore the tuple**: Viable when class label is missing and dataset is massive.
- **Manual imputation**: Time-consuming, infeasible at petabyte scale.
- **Constant/global imputation**: Replace with a global constant (e.g., "Unknown").
- **Mean/Median/Mode imputation**: Replace with the attribute's central tendency.
  - *Mean is sensitive to outliers; Median is the KTU-preferred robust choice for skewed big data.*
- **Most probable value (KNN, Regression-based imputation)**: Predictive but computationally expensive in distributed environments.

**2.1.2 Noisy Data**
Noise is a random error or variance in a measured variable. Detection techniques include:
- **Binning**: Smoothing ordered values using bin means, medians, or boundaries.
- **Regression**: Fitting data to a function (linear/polynomial) to smooth noise.
- **Clustering**: Outliers detected as points lying outside detected clusters.
- **Combined computer + human inspection**: Manual outlier review.

**2.1.3 Inconsistent Data**
Caused by data integration errors, legacy code (e.g., "F" vs "Female" vs "f"), and human entry errors. Resolved via domain-specific knowledge and reference datasets (e.g., postal codes).

### 2.2 Task 2 — Data Integration

Combines data from multiple sources (relational DBs, flat files, HDFS, APIs). The chief problems are:
- **Schema Integration**: Unified metadata across heterogeneous sources.
- **Entity Identification Problem**: Detecting when `cust_id` in DB1 and `customer_number` in DB2 refer to the same entity.
- **Tuple Duplication**: Redundant rows increase bias.
- **Data Value Conflict**: Same attribute represented differently (e.g., currency: USD vs INR vs EUR).

### 2.3 Task 3 — Data Transformation

Data is consolidated into forms appropriate for mining. Key sub-techniques:

**2.3.1 Normalization** (also called *Min-Max scaling* or *feature scaling*): scales data to a small, specified range — typically $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, 1]$. Required for distance-based algorithms (K-Means, KNN) and gradient-descent-based learners (Neural Networks, Logistic Regression).

**2.3.2 Aggregation**: Combines two or more attributes (or objects) into a single one (e.g., daily sales aggregated to monthly).

**2.3.3 Attribute Construction (Feature Engineering)**: New attributes are constructed and added from the given set of attributes to help the mining process.

### 2.4 Task 4 — Data Reduction

Obtains a reduced representation of the dataset that is much smaller in volume but produces the same (or nearly the same) analytical results. Three main sub-tasks:

- **Dimensionality Reduction (Wavelet Transforms, PCA, Attribute Subset Selection)**
- **Numerosity Reduction (Regression & Log-Linear Models, Histograms, Clustering, Sampling)**
- **Data Compression**

### 2.5 Task 5 — Data Discretization

Particularly useful for **classification** and **association rule mining** (e.g., Apriori algorithm requires categorical data). Discretization techniques include:
- Binning (top-down)
- Histogram Analysis (top-down, unsupervised)
- Cluster Analysis (top-down, unsupervised)
- Decision Tree Analysis (top-down, supervised)
- Correlation Analysis (top-down, unsupervised)

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU ESE cycle.

| \# | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Use Case |
|---|--------------|------------------------|----------|
| 1 | Min-Max Normalization | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min$ | Scale to $[0,1]$ |
| 2 | Z-Score Normalization (Standardization) | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ | When $\mu,\sigma$ are known or data has outliers |
| 3 | Decimal Scaling Normalization | $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$ where $j$ is smallest integer s.t. $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$ | Preserve digit semantics |
| 4 | Mean Absolute Deviation | $s_M = \dfrac{1}{n}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert x_i - \mu \vert \right)$ | Robust z-score variant |
| 5 | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2$ | Noise smoothing error metric |
| 6 | Information Gain (Entropy-based) | $Info(D) = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} p_i \log_2(p_i)$ | Discretization via decision tree |
| 7 | PCA Variance Ratio | $\dfrac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \lambda_j}$ | Dimensionality reduction screening |
| 8 | Sampling Rate (Simple Random) | $n_{sample} = n \cdot r$ where $r$ is sampling fraction | Numerosity reduction |

> [!NOTE] Boundary Conditions
> - Min-Max requires $\max(A) \neq \min(A)$ to avoid division by zero.
> - Z-Score assumes data is approximately Gaussian; for heavy-tailed big data, prefer **Robust Scaler** (uses median and IQR).
> - Decimal scaling is sensitive to the choice of $j$.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Finance**: Z-score normalization is mandatory for fraud-detection models trained on transactions spanning multiple currencies.
- **Healthcare (IoT)**: Mean imputation is applied to missing heart-rate sensor streams before feeding into LSTM models.
- **Recommendation Engines (Netflix, Amazon)**: Data reduction via PCA reduces 10,000+ feature vectors into 50 principal components for collaborative filtering.
- **Smart Cities**: Histogram-based discretization converts continuous traffic-flow values into categorical "Low/Medium/High" bins for rule-based traffic optimization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Min-Max Normalization — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE] KTU 2024 Favourite — Practice this derivation until it is muscle memory.

**Given Data (Income attribute $A$ in '000 INR):**  
Raw vector $A = [12, 18, 25, 35, 50, 80, 100]$ (in thousands).  
**Target Range:** $[0, 1]$.

**Step 1 — Identify min and max of $A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min(A) &= 12 \\
\max(A) &= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Min-Max formula** $v' = \dfrac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min$:

Since $new\_max = 1$ and $new\_min = 0$, the formula simplifies to:

$$
v' = \frac{v - 12}{100 - 12} = \frac{v - 12}{88}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each transformed value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_1 &= \frac{12 - 12}{88} = 0.0000 \\
v'_2 &= \frac{18 - 12}{88} = 0.0682 \\
v'_3 &= \frac{25 - 12}{88} = 0.1477 \\
v'_4 &= \frac{35 - 12}{88} = 0.2614 \\
v'_5 &= \frac{50 - 12}{88} = 0.4318 \\
v'_6 &= \frac{80 - 12}{88} = 0.7727 \\
v'_7 &= \frac{100 - 12}{88} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification**:
- The new minimum $v'_{min} = 0$ ✓
- The new maximum $v'_{max} = 1$ ✓
- Relative spacing is preserved (proportional transformation).

> [!IMPORTANT] Examiner Tip: Always state the formula before substitution, and verify boundary conditions. Partial credit is awarded for correct formula statement even if arithmetic slips.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-Score Normalization — Worked Numerical Example

**Given the same vector** $A = [12, 18, 25, 35, 50, 80, 100]$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean $\mu$:**

$$
\mu = \frac{12 + 18 + 25 + 35 + 50 + 80 + 100}{7} = \frac{320}{7} \approx 45.714
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Standard Deviation $\sigma$:**  
First compute the squared deviations $\sum (x_i - \mu)^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(12 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 1137.55 \\
(18 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 768.22 \\
(25 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 429.32 \\
(35 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 114.65 \\
(50 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 18.35 \\
(80 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 1174.69 \\
(100 - 45.714)^2 &\approx 2942.41
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum $\approx 6585.19$  
Variance $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{6585.19}{7} \approx 940.74$  
Standard Deviation $\sigma = \sqrt{940.74} \approx 30.67$

**Step 3 — Apply Z-Score formula** $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_1 &= \frac{12 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx -1.10 \\
v'_2 &= \frac{18 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx -0.90 \\
v'_3 &= \frac{25 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx -0.68 \\
v'_4 &= \frac{35 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx -0.35 \\
v'_5 &= \frac{50 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx 0.14 \\
v'_6 &= \frac{80 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx 1.12 \\
v'_7 &= \frac{100 - 45.714}{30.67} \approx 1.77
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification**: Mean of new vector $\approx 0$, Standard Deviation $\approx 1$. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Decimal Scaling Normalization

**Given:** $A = [-78, 12, 354, -1024, 5678]$.

**Step 1 — Find $j$**, the smallest integer such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$:

Since the largest absolute value is $\vert -1024 \vert = 1024$, we need $10^j > 1024 \Rightarrow j = 4$.

**Step 2 — Apply** $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^{j}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_1 &= \frac{-78}{10000} = -0.0078 \\
v'_2 &= \frac{12}{10000} = 0.0012 \\
v'_3 &= \frac{354}{10000} = 0.0354 \\
v'_4 &= \frac{-1024}{10000} = -0.1024 \\
v'_5 &= \frac{5678}{10000} = 0.5678
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Normalization to Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification |
|----------|--------------------|---------------|
| Known bounded range (image pixels [0,255]) | Min-Max | Preserves exact range semantics |
| Unknown range, Gaussian distribution | Z-Score | Centers data with $\mu=0$, $\sigma=1$ |
| Outliers present in big data | Z-Score or Robust Scaler | Mean/Std are less sensitive than Min/Max |
| Sparse high-dimensional data (text TF-IDF) | L2 Normalization | Preserves sparsity, scales row vectors |
| Need to preserve decimal structure | Decimal Scaling | Digit-level interpretability |
| Neural network input layer | Min-Max to $[0,1]$ | Avoids activation saturation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — The Complete Data Pre-processing Pipeline (KDD Phase)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Big Data Sources] --> B[Data Cleaning]
    B --> C[Data Integration]
    C --> D[Data Transformation]
    D --> E[Data Reduction]
    E --> F[Data Discretization]
    F --> G[Clean Analytically-Ready Dataset]
    G --> H[Analytics and ML Models]

    subgraph CleanStage["Step 1 - Data Cleaning"]
        B1[Handle Missing Values] --> B2[Remove Noise]
        B2 --> B3[Resolve Inconsistencies]
    end

    subgraph IntegStage["Step 2 - Data Integration"]
        C1[Schema Matching] --> C2[Entity Resolution]
        C2 --> C3[Conflict Resolution]
    end

    subgraph TransStage["Step 3 - Data Transformation"]
        D1[Normalization] --> D2[Aggregation]
        D2 --> D3[Attribute Construction]
    end

    subgraph RedStage["Step 4 - Data Reduction"]
        E1[Dimensionality Reduction] --> E2[Numerosity Reduction]
        E2 --> E3[Data Compression]
    end

    subgraph DiscStage["Step 5 - Data Discretization"]
        F1[Binning] --> F2[Histogram Analysis]
        F2 --> F3[Clustering based Binning]
        F3 --> F4[Decision Tree Binning]
    end
```

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Data Cleaning Sub-Tasks (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Ingested Data] --> detect{Missing Data?}
    detect -- Yes --> imp[Imputation Engine]
    imp --> mean[Mean Imputation]
    imp --> median[Median Imputation]
    imp --> knn[KNN Imputation]
    detect -- No --> noise{Noise Present?}
    noise -- Yes --> smooth[Smoothing Filters]
    smooth --> bin[Binning]
    smooth --> reg[Regression]
    smooth --> clust[Clustering]
    noise -- No --> cons{Inconsistencies?}
    cons -- Yes --> resolve[Domain Rule Resolver]
    cons -- No --> out[Clean Data Output]
    resolve --> out
    bin --> out
    reg --> out
    clust --> out
    mean --> out
    median --> out
    knn --> out
```

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Big Data Pre-processing Tool Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sources[Data Sources - IoT, Logs, Social, DB] --> ingest[Ingestion Layer]
    ingest --> sqoop[Apache Sqoop]
    ingest --> flume[Apache Flume]
    ingest --> kafka[Apache Kafka]
    sqoop --> hdfs[HDFS Storage]
    flume --> hdfs
    kafka --> hdfs
    hdfs --> prep[Pre-processing Engine]
    prep --> pig[Apache Pig Latin]
    prep --> hive[Apache HiveQL]
    prep --> spark[Apache Spark]
    pig --> cleaned[Cleaned Data on HDFS]
    hive --> cleaned
    spark --> cleaned
    cleaned --> ml[MLlib and Analytics]
    cleaned --> viz[Visualization Tools]
```

> [!NOTE] Diagram Interpretation
> This block diagram is the **canonical exam figure** for Module 2. KTU 2024 ESE questions frequently ask students to "Draw and explain the Big Data pre-processing pipeline" — drawing the three layers (Ingestion → Storage → Pre-processing) with **at least two named tools per layer** typically secures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
*What is data pre-processing? List any four major tasks involved in data pre-processing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Data pre-processing is the phase in the KDD process that prepares raw data for analysis by cleaning, integrating, transforming, reducing, and discretizing it. The four major tasks are:

1. **Data Cleaning** — handling missing values, noise, and inconsistencies.
2. **Data Integration** — combining data from multiple heterogeneous sources.
3. **Data Transformation** — normalizing and scaling data into suitable formats.
4. **Data Reduction** — reducing volume through dimensionality or numerosity reduction.

*(2 marks for definition + 1 mark for listing four tasks. Award full marks for any valid four of the five tasks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Distinguish between Min-Max Normalization and Z-Score Normalization with formulas.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Normalization |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Formula | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min$ | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ |
| Output Range | Typically $[0,1]$ or $[-1,1]$ | Unbounded, centered at 0 |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | High | Moderate |
| Best for | Bounded data (images) | Gaussian-distributed data |

*(1 mark each: Min-Max formula + property, Z-Score formula + property, comparison line.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the major tasks in data pre-processing with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The major tasks in data pre-processing are as follows:

1. **Data Cleaning (2 marks):** Real-world data is incomplete (missing sensor readings), noisy (erroneous temperature values), and inconsistent (e.g., "M" vs "Male"). Cleaning fills missing values via mean/median imputation, smooths noise via binning or regression, and resolves inconsistencies using domain rules.

2. **Data Integration (1.5 marks):** Combines data from multiple sources — e.g., merging a customer's transaction history from a MySQL DB with their social media activity from a MongoDB collection. Requires schema matching, entity resolution, and conflict handling.

3. **Data Transformation (1.5 marks):** Normalizes data so that features with different units become comparable. *Example:* Customer income (in thousands) and age (in years) must be scaled to $[0,1]$ before feeding into a K-Means clustering algorithm, otherwise the income feature will dominate the distance metric.

4. **Data Reduction (1 mark):** Reduces dataset size while preserving analytical integrity. *Example:* Applying Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce 10,000 gene-expression features to 50 principal components in bioinformatics.

5. **Data Discretization (1 mark):** Converts continuous attributes into categorical ones. *Example:* Age attribute transformed into bins ["0-18", "19-35", "36-60", "60+"] for association rule mining in marketing analytics.

---

**(b)** Suppose the following values for the attribute *Age* are given: **13, 15, 16, 16, 19, 20, 20, 21, 22, 22, 25, 25, 25, 25, 30, 33, 33, 35, 35, 35, 35, 36, 40, 45, 46, 52, 70**.  
Apply the following techniques and show the result:  
(i) Min-Max normalization by mapping the data to the range $[0, 1]$. **(3.5 marks)**  
(ii) Z-score normalization (assume $\mu = 29.81$, $\sigma = 12.94$). **(3.5 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Min-Max Normalization (3.5 marks):**  
$\min(A) = 13$, $\max(A) = 70$, range = 57.

Apply: $v' = \dfrac{v - 13}{70 - 13} = \dfrac{v - 13}{57}$

| $v$ | $v'$ |
|-----|------|
| 13 | 0.0000 |
| 15 | 0.0351 |
| 16 | 0.0526 |
| 19 | 0.1053 |
| 20 | 0.1228 |
| 21 | 0.1404 |
| 22 | 0.1579 |
| 25 | 0.2105 |
| 30 | 0.2982 |
| 33 | 0.3509 |
| 35 | 0.3860 |
| 36 | 0.4035 |
| 40 | 0.4737 |
| 45 | 0.5614 |
| 46 | 0.5789 |
| 52 | 0.6842 |
| 70 | 1.0000 |

*(1 mark formula + 1.5 marks for 5–6 sample calculations + 1 mark for verification that min=0, max=1.)*

**(ii) Z-Score Normalization (3.5 marks):**  
Apply: $v' = \dfrac{v - 29.81}{12.94}$

| $v$ | $v'$ |
|-----|------|
| 13 | -1.30 |
| 20 | -0.76 |
| 25 | -0.37 |
| 30 | 0.01 |
| 35 | 0.40 |
| 40 | 0.79 |
| 46 | 1.25 |
| 70 | 3.10 |

*(1 mark formula + 1.5 marks for 5–6 sample calculations + 1 mark for noting that the outlier 70 yields the highest z-score 3.10, demonstrating outlier detection capability.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** What is data cleaning? Explain the different methods for handling missing values in a dataset. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Data Cleaning (1 mark):** Data cleaning is the process of detecting, correcting, or removing corrupt, inaccurate, or irrelevant records from a dataset. It addresses three main issues: missing values, noisy data, and inconsistencies.

**Methods to Handle Missing Values (6 marks):**

1. **Ignore the Tuple (1 mark):** Simply discard records with missing values. Suitable when class label is missing and dataset is large. Inefficient when multiple attributes have missing values per tuple.

2. **Manual Imputation (1 mark):** A domain expert manually fills missing values. Highly accurate but infeasible for big data (petabyte scale).

3. **Global/Constant Imputation (1 mark):** Replace missing values with a constant like "Unknown" or -1. Simple but may introduce bias.

4. **Attribute Central Tendency Imputation (1.5 marks):**
   - *Mean imputation* (for symmetric data).
   - *Median imputation* (preferred for skewed data, robust to outliers).
   - *Mode imputation* (for categorical attributes).

5. **Predictive Imputation (1.5 marks):**
   - **Regression imputation** — Predict missing value using a regression model built on other attributes.
   - **KNN imputation** — Find $k$ most similar records and use their weighted average.
   - These are accurate but computationally expensive in distributed environments.

---

**(b)** Explain the binning technique for data smoothing. Use the sorted data **4, 8, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 28, 34** to illustrate **binning by mean** and **binning by boundaries**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Binning Technique (2 marks):** Binning is a local smoothing technique that sorts data into bins and replaces each bin's values with a representative value (mean, median, or boundary). It is a discretization technique that also reduces noise.

**Binning by Mean (2.5 marks):** Partition into 3 equal-frequency bins (3 values each), then replace with bin mean.

- *Bin 1:* $\{4, 8, 15\}$ → mean $= 9$ → $\{9, 9, 9\}$
- *Bin 2:* $\{21, 21, 24\}$ → mean $= 22$ → $\{22, 22, 22\}$
- *Bin 3:* $\{25, 28, 34\}$ → mean $= 29$ → $\{29, 29, 29\}$

**Binning by Boundaries (2.5 marks):** Replace each value with the nearest bin boundary (min or max of that bin).

- *Bin 1:* $\{4, 8, 15\}$ → boundaries are 4 and 15. Result: $\{4, 4, 15\}$
- *Bin 2:* $\{21, 21, 24\}$ → boundaries are 21 and 24. Result: $\{21, 21, 24\}$
- *Bin 3:* $\{25, 28, 34\}$ → boundaries are 25 and 34. Result: $\{25, 25, 34\}$

*(Examiner note: Acceptable variations like equal-width binning should receive partial credit if logic is sound.)*

---

> [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout
> **Common mistakes students make — and where marks are lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify boundary conditions** in Min-Max (must check that $\min \neq \max$). *Loses 1 mark.*
> 2. **Mixing up formula symbols** — writing $v' = (v - \sigma)/\mu$ instead of $(v - \mu)/\sigma$. *Loses 1.5 marks and reflects conceptual confusion.*
> 3. **Confusing data cleaning with data integration** — many students merge the two tasks in long-answer questions. *Examiner deducts 1–2 marks for ambiguous definition.*
> 4. **Skipping the "why" in long answers** — Module 2 questions are descriptive, so writing *only* formulas without explaining *when* and *why* they are used loses 50% of the marks allocated to the sub-question.
> 5. **Binning errors** — students often use equal-width binning when the question states equal-frequency. Always re-read the question carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist — Re-read this section 24 hours before the exam.

- **Data Pre-processing Definition** → Transformation of raw data into clean, integrated, normalized form for analytics.
- **GIGO Principle** → Garbage In, Garbage Out; model accuracy is bounded by data quality.
- **Five Major Tasks** → Cleaning, Integration, Transformation, Reduction, Discretization.
- **Missing Value Strategies** → Ignore, Constant, Mean, Median, Mode, KNN, Regression.
- **Noise Detection** → Binning, Regression, Clustering, Combined inspection.
- **Inconsistencies** → Caused by integration errors; resolved by domain knowledge.
- **Min-Max Normalization** → $v' = (v - \min)/(\max - \min)$ — sensitive to outliers.
- **Z-Score Normalization** → $v' = (v - \mu)/\sigma$ — robust to outliers; assumes Gaussian.
- **Decimal Scaling** → $v' = v / 10^j$ — preserves digit semantics.
- **Data Reduction Types** → Dimensionality (PCA, Wavelet), Numerosity (Sampling, Histograms, Clustering), Compression.
- **Discretization Techniques** → Binning, Histogram, Cluster, Decision Tree, Correlation analysis.
- **Big Data Tools** → Sqoop, Flume, Kafka (ingestion); Pig, Hive, Spark (pre-processing).
- **Exam Tip 1** → Always state the formula **before** substitution.
- **Exam Tip 2** → Verify $\min=0, \max=1$ at the end of Min-Max problems.
- **Exam Tip 3** → For Z-Score, mention that the result is unitless.
- **Exam Tip 4** → For Binning, clarify whether the question asks for equal-width or equal-frequency.
- **Exam Tip 5** → For Pipeline diagrams, label the five stages and mention at least two real-world scenarios per stage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage System for Massive Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage System for Massive Data
