<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Storage System for Massive Data — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A *Storage System for Massive Data* refers to a horizontally scalable, fault-tolerant, distributed persistence layer engineered to store, retrieve, and manage petabyte-to-exabyte scale datasets that exceed the capacity, throughput, and processing limits of traditional single-node relational database management systems (RDBMS).

In the KTU 2024 Big Data Analytics framework, **Storage Systems for Massive Data** form the foundational layer of the **Big Data Stack**, sitting beneath processing engines (MapReduce, Spark) and analytics frameworks. The defining characteristics mandated by the syllabus are:

- **Horizontal Scalability** — capacity grows by adding commodity nodes (scale-out), not by upgrading a single server (scale-up).
- **Data Locality** — computation is pushed toward where data resides, minimizing network I/O.
- **Fault Tolerance** — replication and erasure coding ensure no single point of failure (SPOF).
- **Schema Flexibility** — semi-structured and unstructured data are first-class citizens.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Schema Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly categorizes massive data storage into three families:
> 1. **Distributed File Systems** (e.g., HDFS, GFS)
> 2. **NoSQL Columnar/Wide-Column Stores** (e.g., HBase, Cassandra)
> 3. **NoSQL Document/Key-Value Stores** (e.g., MongoDB, DynamoDB)

## 1.2 The Core Problem — Why Traditional Storage Fails

Conventional RDBMS (Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL) adhere to **ACID** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) and rely on **vertical scaling**. When data crosses the **tera-to-peta boundary**, the following bottlenecks emerge:

| Bottleneck | Traditional RDBMS | Massive Data Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Storage ceiling | ~ 100 TB per node | Petabytes (linear growth) |
| Schema rigidity | Fixed schema | Schema-on-read |
| Data types | Structured only | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
| Cost | Proprietary hardware | Commodity x86 servers |
| Fault tolerance | RAID + Backup | Built-in replication |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Warehouse vs. The Shipping Container Yard

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: Storing a Million Books**
>
> Imagine you own a single, beautiful mahogany bookcase (the traditional RDBMS). It can hold maybe 500 books. When you acquire 1,000,000 books, you cannot make the bookcase taller indefinitely — it collapses.
>
> A **Massive Data Storage System** is like a vast shipping-container yard. You don't build one giant container; you acquire thousands of standard 20-foot containers, stack them in rows, label each one, and keep multiple copies of the most important containers in different yards (replication). Any worker (compute node) can walk to any container, open it, and read exactly the book (data block) they need. If one yard burns down, you still have the book — copied elsewhere.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of Massive Data Storage (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Partitioning (Sharding)** — dataset is split across nodes using hash or range partitioning.
2. **Replication** — each partition is copied $N$ times (typical $N = 3$) for fault tolerance.
3. **Consistency Protocol** — governs how replicas converge (e.g., Paxos, Raft, eventual consistency).

$$ N_{total\_nodes} = P \times R $$

where $P$ = number of partitions, $R$ = replication factor. For HDFS, default values are $P = 128\text{ MB blocks}$ and $R = 3$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distributed File Systems (DFS) — Operational Architecture

The canonical example is the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)**. HDFS follows the **master–worker (NameNode–DataNode)** pattern.

### 2.1.1 HDFS Write Path — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Client Request** — A client (e.g., a MapReduce job) requests to write a file of size $S$ bytes.
2. **Block Splitting** — The client contacts the **NameNode** to obtain a list of DataNode hosts. The file is logically split into blocks of fixed size $B = 128\text{ MB}$ (default in Hadoop 2.x+).
3. **Pipeline Replication** — The client writes the first block to DataNode 1, which forwards to DataNode 2, which forwards to DataNode 3, forming a **replication pipeline**.
4. **Acknowledgment** — Pipeline ack flows back to client; next block is processed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight (Why 128 MB?):** Large block size minimizes **NameNode metadata overhead** (each block ≈ 150 bytes of metadata) and maximizes **sequential I/O throughput** by reducing the seek-to-transfer ratio on spinning disks.

### 2.1.2 HDFS Read Path — Logic Steps

1. Client queries the **NameNode** for file metadata.
2. NameNode returns the ordered list of block locations (DataNode IPs).
3. Client reads blocks from the *nearest* DataNode (rack-aware selection).
4. If a block is corrupted (checksum mismatch), the client requests a replica from another DataNode and reports the failure to the NameNode.

### 2.1.3 Rack Awareness

$$ D_{optimal} = \min_{d \in D} \left( \text{hops}(client, d) + \alpha \cdot \text{rackPenalty}(d) \right) $$

where $\alpha$ is a weight factor; default HDFS replica policy: **1 replica on local rack, 1 on same rack, 1 on different rack**.

## 2.2 NoSQL Storage Models

### 2.2.1 The Four NoSQL Families

| Family | Data Model | Example | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key-Value** | Hash map | Redis, DynamoDB | Caching, session store |
| **Document** | JSON/BSON | MongoDB, CouchDB | Content management, catalogs |
| **Column-Family (Wide-Column)** | Sparse multi-dimensional map | HBase, Cassandra | Time-series, write-heavy OLTP |
| **Graph** | Nodes & Edges | Neo4j | Social networks, recommendations |

### 2.2.2 CAP Theorem — The Iron Triangle

> [!WARNING]
> **CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000):** In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **only two** of the following three properties:
> - **C** — Consistency (every read sees the latest write)
> - **A** — Availability (every request receives a non-error response)
> **P** — Partition tolerance (system operates despite network partitions)

The theorem is formally stated as:

$$ \forall \text{ distributed store } D, \quad \lvert \{ C, A, P \} \cap \text{guaranteed}(D) \rvert \leq 2 $$

| System | Choice | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| HDFS / HBase | **CP** (Consistency + Partition tolerance) | Reduced availability during NameNode failure |
| Cassandra / DynamoDB | **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) | Eventual consistency window |
| Traditional RDBMS (single-node) | **CA** | Not partition-tolerant (not truly distributed) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for Storage System Calculations**

| Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of blocks per file | $N_{blocks} = \lceil S / B \rceil$ | $S$ = file size (MB), $B$ = block size (MB) |
| Total storage consumed | $T_{storage} = S \times R$ | $R$ = replication factor |
| NameNode memory footprint | $M_{NN} \approx N_{blocks} \times 150\text{ bytes}$ | Critical for capacity planning |
| Disk I/O throughput | $T_{IO} = \frac{S}{t_{seek} + S / t_{transfer}}$ | Seek-dominated for small blocks |
| HDFS write throughput | $W = \frac{B}{t_{replica} \cdot R}$ | Pipeline replication penalty |
| Consistency level (Cassandra) | $W + R > N$ | $W$=write quorum, $R$=read quorum, $N$=replicas |
| Erasure coding overhead | $O_{EC} = k + m$ where $m$ parity shards | HDFS-EC reduces storage from 3x to ~1.4x |
| Shard distribution (consistent hashing) | $P_i = \text{hash}(key) \bmod P$ | $P$ = number of partitions |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are deployed by data engineers for **Sizing Hadoop Clusters**, estimating **cloud storage bills** (AWS S3, Azure Data Lake), and configuring **Cassandra consistency levels** for SLA-bound applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Block Size for HDFS

We derive the block size $B$ that minimizes the **NameNode metadata overhead** while maximizing **throughput**.

### Step 1 — Define the Constraints

Let:
- $S$ = total dataset size (MB)
- $B$ = block size (MB), with $B > 0$
- $M_{block}$ = metadata per block = **150 bytes** (HDFS specification)
- $M_{RAM}$ = NameNode RAM budget (bytes)
- $t_{seek}$ = average disk seek time (ms)
- $t_{transfer}$ = disk transfer rate (MB/s)

### Step 2 — Number of Blocks Constraint

The total number of blocks is:

$$ N_{blocks} = \frac{S}{B} $$

### Step 3 — NameNode Memory Constraint

For the NameNode to fit all metadata in RAM:

$$ N_{blocks} \times M_{block} \leq M_{RAM} $$

Substituting Step 2:

$$ \frac{S}{B} \times 150 \leq M_{RAM} $$

Rearranging for minimum block size:

$$ B \geq \frac{150 \cdot S}{M_{RAM}} $$

### Step 4 — Throughput Constraint

Sequential read time for one block:

$$ t_{read} = t_{seek} + \frac{B}{t_{transfer}} $$

Throughput per block:

$$ T_{block} = \frac{B}{t_{seek} + B / t_{transfer}} $$

### Step 5 — Take the Limit

As $B \to \infty$, the seek overhead $t_{seek}$ becomes negligible:

$$ \lim_{B \to \infty} T_{block} = \lim_{B \to \infty} \frac{B}{t_{seek} + B / t_{transfer}} = t_{transfer} $$

This shows that **larger blocks approach the disk's peak transfer rate**.

### Step 6 — Combined Optimization

Setting the two constraints together, with typical values $S = 1\text{ PB} = 10^6\text{ MB}$, $M_{RAM} = 128\text{ GB} = 1.28 \times 10^{11}\text{ bytes}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &\geq \frac{150 \times 10^6}{1.28 \times 10^{11}} \\[6pt]
B &\geq 1.17 \times 10^{-3}\text{ MB} \\[6pt]
B &\geq 1.17\text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a *lower bound*. HDFS uses **128 MB** in practice — a value chosen empirically to satisfy **throughput dominance** while keeping $N_{blocks}$ manageable.

> **Conclusion:** $B = 128\text{ MB}$ is a deliberate trade-off favoring **I/O efficiency** over memory conservation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cassandra Quorum Convergence

### Step 1 — Define Parameters

- $N$ = total replicas of a key
- $W$ = write quorum (nodes that must acknowledge a write)
- $R$ = read quorum (nodes contacted during a read)
- $t_{w}$ = write latency (ms)
- $t_{r}$ = read latency (ms)

### Step 2 — Consistency Condition

To guarantee that a read returns the latest write, the read and write quorums must **overlap**:

$$ W + R > N $$

### Step 3 — Worked Example (Common KTU Question Type)

Given $N = 3$, choose $W = 2$, $R = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W + R &= 2 + 2 = 4 \\[4pt]
N &= 3 \\[4pt]
\therefore W + R &= 4 > 3 = N \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Quorum** consistency level — the strongest available in Cassandra's tunable consistency.

### Step 4 — Trade-off with Latency

Total write time = $W \times t_{w}$ (parallel). Total read time = $R \times t_{r}$. For a fixed $N$:

$$ W + R = N + 1 \implies \text{strong consistency} $$

$$ W + R < N + 1 \implies \text{eventual consistency window} $$

> **Engineering Implication:** Increasing $W$ reduces write throughput; increasing $R$ reduces read throughput. **Tunable consistency** allows per-query trade-offs.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — System Selection Guide

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Use this matrix to justify your storage system choice in design questions.

| Workload | Recommended System | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Batch analytics on logs | **HDFS + MapReduce** | High throughput, write-once-read-many |
| Real-time random reads/writes | **HBase** | In-memory MemStore + LSM-tree on HDFS |
| Global time-series IoT | **Cassandra** | Masterless, AP, tunable consistency |
| Document catalog with flexible schema | **MongoDB** | Native JSON, secondary indexes |
| Web session caching | **Redis** | In-memory key-value, sub-ms latency |
| Recommendation engine | **Neo4j** | Native graph traversal, relationship queries |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Master–Worker Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Client Application] -->|1. Request Metadata| nn1[NameNode Master]
    nn1 -->|2. Return Block Locations| client1
    client1 -->|3. Write Block 1 Pipeline| dn1a[DataNode 1 Rack A]
    dn1a -->|4. Forward| dn1b[DataNode 2 Rack A]
    dn1b -->|5. Forward| dn1c[DataNode 3 Rack B]
    dn1c -->|6. Ack Pipeline| client1
    nn1 -.->|Heartbeat and Block Report| dn1a
    nn1 -.->|Heartbeat and Block Report| dn1b
    nn1 -.->|Heartbeat and Block Report| dn1c
    snn1[Secondary NameNode] -.->|Checkpointing| nn1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read carefully:** The NameNode is the *metadata brain*; DataNodes are the *storage muscle*. Failure of the NameNode halts the cluster unless a **Standby NameNode** (HA configuration) takes over via ZooKeeper.

## 4.2 Wide-Column Store Logical Model (HBase / Cassandra)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    table1[Table: UserActivity] --> row1[Row Key user001]
    table1 --> row2[Row Key user002]
    table1 --> row3[Row Key user003]
    row1 --> cf1a[Column Family profile]
    row1 --> cf1b[Column Family metrics]
    row2 --> cf2a[Column Family profile]
    row2 --> cf2b[Column Family metrics]
    row3 --> cf3a[Column Family profile]
    row3 --> cf3b[Column Family metrics]
    cf1a --> col1a1[Column name age]
    cf1a --> col1a2[Column name country]
    cf1b --> col1b1[Column clicks 1280]
    cf1b --> col1b2[Column logins 47]
    cf2a --> col2a1[Column name age]
    cf2a --> col2a2[Column name country]
    cf2b --> col2b1[Column clicks 902]
    cf2b --> col2b2[Column logins 12]
    cf3a --> col3a1[Column name age]
    cf3a --> col3a2[Column name country]
    cf3b --> col3b1[Column clicks 3400]
    cf3b --> col3b2[Column logins 88]
```

> **Insight:** Rows are physically ordered by **Row Key** (lexicographic). Column Families are stored in separate files, enabling **fast scans** of related columns without touching the whole row.

## 4.3 CAP Theorem Decision Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start1[Distributed Data Store] --> q1{Network Partition Possible?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Optimize for Consistency?}
    q2 -->|Yes| cp1[CP System e.g. HBase, HDFS]
    q2 -->|No| ap1[AP System e.g. Cassandra, DynamoDB]
    q1 -->|No| ca1[CA System Traditional RDBMS]
```

## 4.4 Storage Tier Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    data1[Massive Data Ingestion] --> t1{Schema Known?}
    t1 -->|Yes, Fixed| t1a[RDBMS or HDFS with Avro Parquet]
    t1 -->|No, Flexible| t1b[NoSQL Document Store]
    t1b --> q1{Read Heavy or Write Heavy?}
    q1 -->|Read Heavy| q1a[MongoDB CouchDB]
    q1 -->|Write Heavy| q1b[Cassandra HBase]
    t1a --> q2{Access Pattern?}
    q2 -->|Batch Analytics| q2a[HDFS plus MapReduce Spark]
    q2 -->|Random Real Time| q2b[HBase on HDFS]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** Differentiate between **HDFS** and **HBase**. Mention the type of data access each is optimized for. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | HDFS | HBase |
> |---|---|---|
> | Access pattern | Write-once, read-many (batch) | Random read/write (real-time) |
> | Data model | Flat files split into blocks | Wide-column sparse tables |
> | Latency | High (seconds–minutes) | Low (milliseconds) |
> | Underlying layer | OS file system | Runs *on top* of HDFS |
>
> HDFS is best for **batch analytics**; HBase is best for **online transactional** access to large tables. *(3 marks — 1 for each differentiating axis + 1 for use case)*

### Question 2
**Q:** State the **CAP Theorem**. Give one example of a system that prioritizes **AP** over **CP**. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]`

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed data store can guarantee **only two** of the three properties: **Consistency**, **Availability**, and **Partition tolerance**.
>
> Example: **Apache Cassandra** prioritizes **Availability + Partition tolerance (AP)**, providing *eventual consistency* even when network partitions occur. This makes it suitable for always-on applications like social media feeds.
> *(1 mark — statement, 1 mark — explanation, 1 mark — example with justification)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has two sub-parts, typically `(a)` for 7 marks and `(b)` for 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B within the choice set.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — HDFS Deep Dive**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Describe the role of **NameNode**, **DataNode**, and **Secondary NameNode**. `(7 marks)`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **HDFS follows a master–worker architecture:**
>
> 1. **NameNode (Master):** Stores metadata — filename, block locations, replication factor, permissions. Maintains the entire filesystem namespace in RAM. Acts as the *routing brain* of HDFS.
> 2. **DataNode (Worker):** Stores the actual data blocks. Sends periodic **heartbeat** signals (default every 3 seconds) and **block reports** (default every 6 hours) to the NameNode.
> 3. **Secondary NameNode:** Despite its name, it is *not* a failover NameNode. It periodically merges the **fsimage** (filesystem snapshot) with the **edit log** to prevent the edit log from growing unboundedly. In **HDFS HA** setups, it is replaced by a **Standby NameNode** coordinated via **ZooKeeper**.
>
> **Write flow:** Client → NameNode (metadata) → Pipeline write to $R$ DataNodes.
> **Read flow:** Client → NameNode (block locations) → Read from nearest DataNode.
>
> *(Block diagram from Section 4.1 referenced — 3 marks; NameNode/DataNode roles — 2 marks; Secondary NameNode clarification — 2 marks)*

**(b)** A company stores **2 TB** of log files in HDFS with a **block size of 256 MB** and a **replication factor of 3**. Calculate:
1. The number of blocks per file (for a single 2 TB log).
2. The total physical storage consumed.
3. The NameNode metadata memory footprint (assume 150 bytes/block). `(7 marks)`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:** $S = 2\text{ TB} = 2 \times 1024 \times 1024\text{ MB} = 2{,}097{,}152\text{ MB}$, $B = 256\text{ MB}$, $R = 3$, $M_{block} = 150\text{ bytes}$.
>
> **Step 1 — Number of blocks:**
>
> $$ N_{blocks} = \frac{S}{B} = \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{256} = 8{,}192\text{ blocks} $$
>
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 2 — Total physical storage:**
>
> $$ T_{storage} = S \times R = 2{,}097{,}152 \times 3 = 6{,}291{,}456\text{ MB} = 6\text{ TB} $$
>
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **Step 3 — NameNode memory:**
>
> $$ M_{NN} = 8{,}192 \times 150 = 1{,}228{,}800\text{ bytes} \approx 1.17\text{ MB} $$
>
> *(2 marks)*
>
> **Conclusion:** 2 TB logical data expands to **6 TB** physical due to 3x replication, and the NameNode only needs ~1.2 MB of RAM for this file's metadata. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — NoSQL & CAP**

`[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **four types of NoSQL databases** with suitable use-case examples. `(7 marks)`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> | Type | Data Model | Strengths | Example | Use Case |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **Key-Value** | Hash map $(K \rightarrow V)$ | Ultra-fast, simple | Redis, DynamoDB | Session caching, leaderboards |
> | **Document** | JSON/BSON documents | Schema flexibility, queryable | MongoDB, CouchDB | Catalogs, CMS, IoT metadata |
> | **Wide-Column** | Sparse column families | Massive scale, write-heavy | Cassandra, HBase | Time-series, write-heavy OLTP |
> | **Graph** | Nodes + Edges | Relationship traversal | Neo4j, JanusGraph | Social networks, fraud detection |
>
> Key-Value sacrifices query flexibility for speed; Document stores add secondary indexes; Wide-Column stores scale to petabytes; Graph stores optimize relationship traversals. *(7 marks — 1.5 per row + 1 for synthesis)*

**(b)** Explain the **CAP Theorem** with a Venn-diagram-style description. For each of **HBase** and **Cassandra**, justify which two properties they prioritize. `(7 marks)`

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000):** In any distributed data store experiencing a network partition, the designer must choose between **Consistency (C)** and **Availability (A)**. Partition tolerance (P) is *non-negotiable* in real distributed systems.
>
> **HBase — CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance):**
> - HBase is built on HDFS and uses a strongly consistent model.
> - All reads return the latest committed write.
> - **Trade-off:** During a NameNode or RegionServer outage, some requests may fail (reduced availability).
>
> **Cassandra — AP (Availability + Partition tolerance):**
> - Masterless ring architecture with tunable consistency.
> - Every node can serve reads and writes even during partitions.
> - **Trade-off:** Reads may return *stale* data; consistency is *eventual* (typically < 1 second convergence).
>
> **Validation via quorum equation:**
> - HBase: enforces $W + R > N$ strictly (strong consistency).
> - Cassandra: allows operators to set $W + R \leq N$ for higher availability.
>
> *(2 marks CAP statement, 2 marks HBase, 2 marks Cassandra, 1 mark for comparison)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HDFS with HBase** — HDFS is a *file system*; HBase is a *database* running **on** HDFS. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this error.
> 2. **Calling Secondary NameNode a "backup"** — It is a *checkpoint helper*, not a hot failover. In HA setups, a **Standby NameNode** is the failover. Misnaming costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **CAP mis-statement** — Many students write "you can choose any two," but in practice, **P is mandatory** in distributed systems. The real choice is **C vs A** under partitions. Phrase it correctly.
> 4. **Forgetting replication factor in storage calculations** — Total storage = Logical size $\times R$. Forgetting $R = 3$ in Part-B numericals will cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. **NoSQL comparison without use cases** — A bare table of features without real-world examples loses at least 1 mark per family.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage System for Massive Data — Key Takeaways**

- **HDFS** = distributed, fault-tolerant, write-once-read-many file system. Default **block size = 128 MB**, **replication factor = 3**.
- **NameNode** stores **metadata in RAM**; **DataNodes** store **actual blocks** on local disks.
- **Secondary NameNode** performs **checkpointing**, not failover (use **Standby NameNode + ZooKeeper** for HA).
- **HBase** = wide-column NoSQL store running on HDFS, providing **random real-time read/write** access.
- **Cassandra** = masterless AP system with **tunable consistency** governed by $W + R > N$.
- **CAP Theorem** = choose 2 of $\{C, A, P\}$, but **P is non-negotiable** in distributed systems.
- **Storage formula:** Total physical storage = Logical size $\times$ Replication factor.
- **Block count:** $N_{blocks} = \lceil S / B \rceil$.
- **NoSQL families:** Key-Value, Document, Wide-Column, Graph — each with distinct use cases.
- **Rack awareness** in HDFS places replicas across **at least 2 racks** to survive rack-level failures.
- **Erasure coding** (HDFS-EC) reduces storage overhead from 3x to ~1.4x by replacing full replication with $k+m$ parity shards.
- **Master–worker** pattern is the dominant architecture; **masterless** rings (Cassandra) eliminate the SPOF but complicate consistency.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Storage System for Massive Data Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 / PECPT755 Big Data Analytics*

topics.title: Distributed Storage System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distributed Storage System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Distributed Storage System (DSS)** is a coordinated collection of autonomous computing nodes interconnected via a high-speed network that collectively function as a single, logical storage repository. In the context of Big Data Analytics (KTU 2024 Scheme, PECPT755), a DSS is formally defined as a horizontally scalable, fault-tolerant data substrate engineered to persistently store, retrieve, and manage petabyte-to-exabyte scale datasets across commodity hardware clusters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):** A Distributed Storage System decouples data placement from physical hardware by distributing chunks/blocks of files across multiple machines, ensuring data availability through replication, partitioning, and consensus protocols, while exposing a unified namespace to client applications.

The three canonical pillars of a DSS are:
1. **Partitioning** — splitting large datasets into manageable chunks.
2. **Replication** — maintaining multiple copies across nodes for fault tolerance.
3. **Access Protocol** — a unified client-facing interface (e.g., HDFS API, POSIX-like GFS interface).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "National Library Chain" Intuition

Imagine a single library is overwhelmed by 10 million books. So, the government opens **1,000 branch libraries** across the country. Instead of one huge building, every book is **cut into 64-page chapters**, and each chapter is photocopied **3 times** and placed in 3 different branch libraries (so if one burns down, the chapter is still safe). A central **catalogue** (NameNode in HDFS) tells you which branch holds which chapter. When you request a book, the librarian fetches the chapters from the nearest branch that holds a copy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping the Analogy to Engineering Terminology:**
> - **Branch Libraries** → DataNodes / Chunkservers
> - **Chapters (64 pages)** → Data Blocks (default 64 MB in HDFS, 64 MB chunks in GFS)
> - **3 photocopies** → Replication Factor (RF = 3)
> - **Central Catalogue** → Master/NameNode (Metadata Server)
> - **You requesting the book** → Client Read Operation
> - **Librarian fetching chapters** → Block-level Data Transfer Pipeline

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Design Constants

The following bold-faced constants appear frequently in KTU valuation keys and must be memorized:

- **Default Block Size in HDFS = 64 MB** (configurable up to 128 MB / 256 MB in modern Hadoop 3.x).
- **Default Replication Factor = 3** (one copy on local rack, one on different rack, one on second different rack).
- **Default Chunk Size in GFS = 64 MB**.
- **Heartbeat Interval = 3 seconds** (DataNode → NameNode liveness signal).
- **Block Report Interval = 6 hours** (DataNode → NameNode full block inventory).
- **Safe Mode Duration** = NameNode startup window where no replication occurs.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' Favourite:** KTU papers frequently test the default value of replication factor and block size. A 1-mark slip on these defaults can cascade into 3-mark losses in descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) — Architectural Pillars

HDFS is the canonical KTU-prescribed DSS. It follows a **Master-Slave (Leader-Follower)** architecture with strict separation of metadata and data planes.

### 2.1.1 NameNode (The Master / Metadata Server)
- Stores **all filesystem metadata**: filenames, directory tree, block-to-DataNode mapping, replication counts, permissions, modification timestamps.
- Maintains two critical in-memory data structures:
  - **FsImage** — snapshot of the filesystem metadata.
  - **EditLog** — journal of every metadata mutation since the last FsImage.
- Operates as a **single point of failure** in Hadoop 1.x; resolved via **NameNode HA (High Availability)** using Zookeeper-based leader election in Hadoop 2.x+.

### 2.1.2 DataNode (The Slave / Storage Worker)
- Stores the **actual data blocks** on local disk.
- Sends a **heartbeat** every **3 seconds** to the NameNode.
- Sends a **Block Report** every **6 hours** listing all blocks it hosts.
- On heartbeat failure (default timeout = 10 minutes × 30 = 300 s), the NameNode marks the DataNode as **dead** and triggers re-replication.

### 2.1.3 Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)
- **Misnomer alert:** It is **not a hot standby** NameNode.
- Periodically merges FsImage + EditLog → new FsImage to prevent EditLog from growing unbounded.
- In Hadoop 2.x+, replaced by **CheckpointNode** or **BackupNode**.

## 2.2 GFS (Google File System) — The Reference Architecture

GFS is historically the **blueprint** for modern DSS designs (HDFS, HBase HDFS backend, Cosmos, etc.). It is **NOT** taught for code-level mastery in KTU but is mandatory for conceptual/descriptive questions.

| GFS Component | Function | HDFS Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Master** | Metadata + chunk location + lease management | NameNode |
| **Chunkserver** | Stores 64 MB chunks on local disk | DataNode |
| **Client** | Issues control + data requests | HDFS Client |
| **Chunk Handle** | 64-bit unique block ID | Block ID |
| **Operation Log** | Persistent metadata journal | EditLog + FsImage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** In GFS, the **client** interacts directly with the chunkserver (data plane), not through the Master. The Master is contacted only for metadata. This minimizes the Master's data throughput burden — a key insight in KTU 14-mark questions on "Compare GFS and HDFS".

## 2.3 The Read/Write Protocol — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.3.1 HDFS File Read (Why 3 Network Hops?)
1. **Client → NameNode:** RPC call `getBlockLocations(filename)`.
2. **NameNode → Client:** Returns ordered list of DataNodes hosting each block (sorted by network proximity).
3. **Client → nearest DataNode:** Establishes TCP socket, streams block bytes.
4. **Client-side checksum verification** confirms data integrity. If mismatch → reads from next replica.

### 2.3.2 HDFS File Write (The Pipeline Replication Trick)
1. **Client → NameNode:** RPC `create(filename)` → NameNode allocates block ID.
2. **Client** splits file into packet-sized chunks (typically 64 KB).
3. **Client → first DataNode in pipeline** (typically local rack).
4. **Pipeline propagation:** D1 → D2 → D3 (each DataNode forwards + stores + acks).
5. **Ack chain** returns D3 → D2 → D1 → Client.
6. Block marked "committed" only after **all replicas** ack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Rack Awareness" Rule (Frequently Asked):** The first replica is placed on the **same node as the client** (or a co-located node). The second replica is placed on a **different rack**. The third replica is placed on **a second different rack** (i.e., the same remote rack as replica 2). This guarantees that loss of an entire rack still preserves data availability — a cornerstone of HDFS fault tolerance.

## 2.4 Fault Tolerance & Replication Mathematics

### 2.4.1 Replication Factor vs. Storage Overhead
If raw data size is $D$ bytes and replication factor is $R$, the **physical storage consumed** is:

$$S_{\text{physical}} = D \times R$$

The **storage overhead ratio** is therefore $R \div 1$. For the KTU-default $R = 3$, you consume **3× the logical data size**.

### 2.4.2 Mean Time to Data Loss (MTTDL)
For a system with $R$ independent replicas, where each node has an individual failure rate of $\lambda$ (failures/hour), the system-level data loss rate is approximately:

$$\lambda_{\text{system}} \approx \binom{N}{N - R + 1} \cdot \lambda^{(R)} \quad \text{(very low order)}$$

The MTTDL is its reciprocal:

$$\text{MTTDL} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{system}}}$$

A simplified practical KTU-formula for **R = 3** across $N$ independent nodes (assuming identical $\lambda$):

$$\text{MTTDL}_{\text{HDFS}} \approx \frac{1}{N^2 \cdot \lambda^3}$$

This **cubic inverse relationship** explains why raising $R$ from 2 to 3 yields dramatic reliability gains.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Value / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Logical Data Size | $D$ | Bytes | Input dataset volume |
| 2 | Physical Storage Consumed | $S = D \cdot R$ | Bytes | Cluster capacity planning |
| 3 | Replication Factor (HDFS default) | $R$ | **3** | Fault tolerance parameter |
| 4 | Block Size (HDFS default) | $B$ | **64 MB** | Determines metadata size |
| 5 | Number of Blocks | $N_b = \lceil D / B \rceil$ | Integer | NameNode memory budget |
| 6 | NameNode Memory (rule of thumb) | $M \approx 150 \cdot N_b$ | Bytes | Hardware sizing |
| 7 | Write Throughput | $T_w = (B \cdot R) / t_{\text{ack}}$ | MB/s | Pipeline write performance |
| 8 | MTTDL (R=3) | $\approx 1 / (N^2 \lambda^3)$ | Hours | Reliability benchmarking |
| 9 | Heartbeat Interval | $h$ | **3 s** | Failure detection latency |
| 10 | Block Report Interval | $b_r$ | **6 h** | Metadata refresh cycle |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility — Capacity Planning:** For a 1 PB logical dataset with $R=3$ and $B=64$ MB, KTU examiners often ask: "How many DataNodes are required if each has 1 TB usable disk space?" The answer lies in cascading the formulas — try it in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Total Cluster Storage Requirement

**Problem (KTU 2019-style):** A retail analytics firm stores **2 PB** of raw sales logs in HDFS with a replication factor of **3**. The block size is configured at **128 MB**. Calculate:
(a) The number of blocks.
(b) The total physical storage consumed.
(c) The approximate NameNode memory footprint (use 150 bytes/block rule).

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**(a) Number of Blocks:**

$$N_b = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil$$

First, convert units to a common base (Megabytes):

$$D = 2 \text{ PB} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ GB} = 2 \times 10^{9} \text{ MB}$$

Now substitute:

$$N_b = \left\lceil \frac{2 \times 10^{9} \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB/block}} \right\rceil$$

$$N_b = \lceil 15{,}625{,}000 \text{ blocks} \rceil = 15{,}625{,}000 \text{ blocks}$$

**(b) Total Physical Storage Consumed:**

$$S_{\text{physical}} = D \times R$$

$$S_{\text{physical}} = 2 \text{ PB} \times 3 = 6 \text{ PB}$$

**(c) NameNode Memory Footprint:**

$$M \approx 150 \text{ bytes/block} \times N_b$$

$$M \approx 150 \times 15{,}625{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

$$M \approx 2.34375 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes} \approx 2.34 \text{ GB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the cluster holds **6 PB of physical data**, the NameNode only needs roughly **2.34 GB of RAM** to manage it. This is why HDFS can scale to billions of blocks while keeping the metadata server compact — a KTU 14-mark favourite.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: MTTDL Calculation for a 100-Node Cluster

**Problem:** A 100-node HDFS cluster has each DataNode failing independently at $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour. Calculate the Mean Time to Data Loss (MTTDL) for $R = 3$.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

Using the simplified KTU MTTDL formula:

$$\text{MTTDL} = \frac{1}{N^2 \cdot \lambda^3}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\text{MTTDL} = \frac{1}{(100)^2 \cdot (0.001)^3}$$

Compute numerator/denominator step by step:

$$N^2 = 100^2 = 10{,}000$$

$$\lambda^3 = (10^{-3})^3 = 10^{-9}$$

$$N^2 \cdot \lambda^3 = 10{,}000 \cdot 10^{-9} = 10^{-5}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{MTTDL} = \frac{1}{10^{-5}} = 10^{5} \text{ hours}$$

Converting to days:

$$\text{MTTDL} = \frac{10^{5}}{24} \approx 4{,}166.67 \text{ days} \approx 11.4 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Increasing $R$ from 3 to 4 would push MTTDL from ~11 years to **> 10,000 years** for the same cluster — a near-astronomical improvement. This justifies the KTU-default of $R=3$ as an engineering trade-off between cost and reliability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of HDFS vs GFS vs Modern DSS

| Dimension | HDFS | GFS | Modern DSS (HBase, Cassandra) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | Batch analytics (Hadoop) | Google's internal workloads | Low-latency random access |
| Consistency Model | Write-once, read-many (WORM) | Relaxed consistency | Tunable (strong / eventual) |
| Block/Chunk Size | 64 MB (default) | 64 MB | Variable (KB to MB) |
| Metadata Architecture | Centralized NameNode | Centralized Master | Distributed (consistent hashing) |
| Failure Recovery | Re-replication | Re-replication | Hinted handoff, anti-entropy |
| Append Optimization | Yes (since Hadoop 0.21+) | Native strength | Native |
| CAP Trade-off | AP-leaning (in HA) | AP | Configurable |
| Hardware Assumption | Commodity | Commodity | Commodity / Cloud |

## 3.4 Worked Derivation #3: Block Report Size & Network Bandwidth

**Problem:** A DataNode hosts 50,000 blocks. The Block Report is sent every 6 hours. If each block ID consumes 8 bytes and each block checksum 4 bytes, calculate the daily bandwidth consumed by Block Reports.

### 3.4.1 Solution

Per-block metadata size:

$$m = 8 + 4 = 12 \text{ bytes/block}$$

Per-DataNode report size:

$$R_{\text{size}} = 12 \text{ bytes} \times 50{,}000 = 600{,}000 \text{ bytes} = 600 \text{ KB}$$

Reports per day:

$$N_{\text{day}} = \frac{24 \text{ h}}{6 \text{ h/report}} = 4 \text{ reports/day}$$

Daily bandwidth per DataNode:

$$B_{\text{day}} = 600 \text{ KB} \times 4 = 2{,}400 \text{ KB} \approx 2.34 \text{ MB/day}$$

For a 1000-DataNode cluster:

$$B_{\text{cluster}} = 2.34 \text{ MB} \times 1000 = 2.34 \text{ GB/day}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Takeaway:** Block Report traffic is **negligible** compared to data plane traffic — proving the architectural elegance of separating control plane (NameNode) from data plane (DataNodes).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ClientA[Client Application] -->|RPC Metadata Request| NN[NameNode]
    ClientB[Hadoop CLI / MapReduce Job] -->|RPC Metadata Request| NN
    NN -->|Block Locations| ClientA
    NN -->|Block Locations| ClientB
    ClientA -->|Direct Data Stream| DN1[DataNode 1 - Rack 1]
    ClientA -->|Direct Data Stream| DN2[DataNode 2 - Rack 2]
    ClientB -->|Direct Data Stream| DN3[DataNode 3 - Rack 2]
    ClientB -->|Direct Data Stream| DN4[DataNode 4 - Rack 3]
    DN1 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block Report 6h| NN
    DN2 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block Report 6h| NN
    DN3 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block Report 6h| NN
    DN4 -->|Heartbeat 3s + Block Report 6h| NN
    SNN[Secondary NameNode Checkpoint] -->|Merges FsImage + EditLog| NN
    ZK[Zookeeper Ensemble] -->|Leader Election HA| NN
    style NN fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style SNN fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ZK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ClientA fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ClientB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DN1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DN2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DN3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style DN4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 HDFS Write Pipeline — Three Replica Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Client Splits File into Packets] -->|Packet 1 64KB| D1A[DataNode 1 Same Rack]
    D1A -->|Forward and Store| D2A[DataNode 2 Rack 2]
    D2A -->|Forward and Store| D3A[DataNode 3 Rack 2]
    D3A -->|Ack 1| D2A
    D2A -->|Ack 1| D1A
    D1A -->|Ack 1| C
    C -->|Packet 2 64KB| D1A
    D1A -->|Forward| D2A
    D2A -->|Forward| D3A
    D3A -->|Ack 2| D2A
    D2A -->|Ack 2| D1A
    D1A -->|Ack 2| C
    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D1A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D2A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D3A fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Failure Detection & Re-Replication Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NameNode Starts Heartbeat Timer] --> B{Heartbeat Received within 10 min x 30}
    B -->|Yes| C[DataNode Marked Alive]
    B -->|No| D[DataNode Marked Dead]
    D --> E[NameNode Scans Block Reports for Missing Replicas]
    E --> F{Replication Count equals R}
    F -->|No Under Replicated| G[NameNode Selects New Target DataNode]
    G --> H[Initiates Re Replication via Data Plane]
    H --> I[Block Replica Count Restored to R]
    F -->|Yes Properly Replicated| J[No Action Required]
    I --> K[End Recovery]
    J --> K
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 CAP Theorem Mapping for HDFS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAP[CAP Theorem Trade off] --> C[Consistency]
    CAP --> A[Availability]
    CAP --> P[Partition Tolerance]
    C --> CS[Strong Consistency in NameNode Metadata]
    A --> AS[DataNode Failures Tolerated via Replication]
    P --> PS[Network Partitions Handled via Rack Awareness]
    CS --> HDFS[HDFS Final Choice AP Leaning]
    AS --> HDFS
    PS --> HDFS
    style CAP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style HDFS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Distributed Storage System. List any FOUR desirable properties. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A Distributed Storage System (DSS) is a networked collection of independent storage nodes that present a unified, logical storage interface to client applications, distributing data across machines for scalability and reliability.

**Four desirable properties:**
1. **Scalability** — horizontal addition of nodes increases capacity linearly.
2. **Fault Tolerance** — survives individual node failures without data loss.
3. **Transparency** — clients see a single namespace; physical location is hidden.
4. **Consistency / Availability trade-off** — guarantees per the CAP theorem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "speed" instead of "scalability" or "reliability" instead of "fault tolerance". Use the **exact KTU terminology** — examiners are bound by the scheme's keyword list.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the NameNode in HDFS? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**
The **NameNode** is the master server in HDFS architecture. It manages the filesystem namespace and stores all metadata, including:
- The directory tree of files and directories.
- Mapping of each file to its constituent data blocks.
- Mapping of each block to the DataNodes hosting its replicas.
- File permissions, replication factor, and access timestamps.

The NameNode does **not** store actual file data; that responsibility belongs to the DataNodes. It monitors DataNode liveness through heartbeats and triggers re-replication when a DataNode fails.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A: (a) Explain the HDFS architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode. (7 marks)
       (b) With an example, describe the HDFS file write operation. Explain the concept of replica placement policy. (7 marks)

### Question B: (a) Compare HDFS and GFS architectures. Highlight at least FIVE points of difference. (7 marks)
       (b) A 500 TB dataset is stored in HDFS with a default block size of 64 MB and a replication factor of 3. Calculate: (i) Number of blocks, (ii) Total physical storage, (iii) Approximate NameNode memory using the 150 bytes/block rule. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A(a): HDFS Architecture

HDFS follows a **Master-Slave architecture** with the NameNode acting as the master and a set of DataNodes as slaves. The client interacts with the NameNode for metadata operations and directly with the DataNodes for data transfer.

**1. NameNode (Master):**
- Maintains the filesystem tree and metadata for all files and directories.
- Stores two persistent files: **FsImage** (snapshot) and **EditLog** (transaction log).
- Receives heartbeats from every DataNode every 3 seconds.
- Decides replica placement based on rack awareness.
- **Single point of failure** in Hadoop 1.x — resolved by HA in 2.x+.

**2. DataNode (Slave):**
- Stores actual data blocks on local disk.
- Sends **heartbeat** (3 s) and **block report** (6 h) to NameNode.
- Serves read/write requests directly to/from clients.
- On receiving a write, propagates data to the next DataNode in the pipeline.

**3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):**
- Periodically downloads FsImage + EditLog from NameNode.
- Merges them to produce a new FsImage.
- Uploads the new FsImage back to NameNode.
- Prevents EditLog from growing unbounded.
- **It is NOT a standby NameNode** — a very common misconception.

*(Diagram: see Section 4.1 above.)*

---

### Solution to Question A(b): HDFS File Write & Replica Placement

**HDFS File Write Process (Step-by-Step):**
1. The client invokes `create()` on the NameNode via RPC.
2. NameNode performs a **namespace check** (file does not exist, permissions OK) and returns a list of DataNodes for the first block, optimized by rack awareness.
3. The client splits the file into **64 KB packets** (default packet size).
4. The client pushes the first packet to the **first DataNode** in the pipeline.
5. The first DataNode stores the packet locally and forwards it to the **second DataNode**.
6. The second DataNode stores and forwards to the **third DataNode**.
7. An **acknowledgment chain** returns from D3 → D2 → D1 → Client.
8. Only when all replicas acknowledge is the block marked "committed".

**Replica Placement Policy (Rack Awareness):**
- **Replica 1:** Placed on the **same node as the client** (or a node on the same rack if the client is outside the cluster).
- **Replica 2:** Placed on a **different rack** (cross-rack).
- **Replica 3:** Placed on **the same remote rack as Replica 2** (but a different DataNode).

**Rationale:** This policy balances **reliability** (one full rack failure does not lose data) with **write performance** (two of three writes are intra-rack, saving cross-switch bandwidth).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often claim Replica 3 goes on a "third different rack" — **this is wrong**. The KTU/Hadoop default keeps Replica 2 and Replica 3 on the same remote rack to minimize cross-rack traffic. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this error.

---

### Solution to Question B(a): HDFS vs GFS Comparison

| # | Feature | HDFS | GFS |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open Source | Yes (Apache) | No (Google proprietary) |
| 2 | Metadata Architecture | Single NameNode | Single Master (with shadow masters) |
| 3 | Block/Chunk Size | 64 MB default | 64 MB fixed |
| 4 | Data Plane | Client ↔ DataNode direct | Client ↔ Chunkserver direct |
| 5 | Consistency | Write-once, read-many (WORM) | Relaxed consistency, record-level appends |
| 6 | Snapshot/Checkpoint | Secondary NameNode | Shadow Masters |
| 7 | Failure Recovery | Re-replication | Re-replication |
| 8 | Primary Use Case | Hadoop batch analytics | Google's web index, Bigtable backend |
| 9 | Heartbeat Interval | 3 seconds | Periodic lease renewal |
| 10 | API Access | Hadoop FS shell, Java API | GFS client library |

**Key Architectural Similarity:** Both decouple metadata from data; both use a centralized metadata server; both use chunk/block-based distribution with replication factor ≥ 3.

---

### Solution to Question B(b): Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $D = 500$ TB
- $B = 64$ MB
- $R = 3$

**(i) Number of Blocks:**

$$N_b = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{500 \text{ TB}}{64 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$$

Convert to common units (MB):

$$500 \text{ TB} = 500 \times 10^{6} \text{ MB} = 5 \times 10^{8} \text{ MB}$$

$$N_b = \left\lceil \frac{5 \times 10^{8}}{64} \right\rceil = \lceil 7{,}812{,}500 \rceil = 7{,}812{,}500 \text{ blocks}$$

**(ii) Total Physical Storage:**

$$S_{\text{physical}} = D \times R = 500 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 1500 \text{ TB} = 1.5 \text{ PB}$$

**(iii) NameNode Memory:**

$$M \approx 150 \text{ bytes/block} \times 7{,}812{,}500$$

$$M = 1{,}171{,}875{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1.17 \text{ GB}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Distributed Storage System Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Secondary NameNode with Standby NameNode** — they are architecturally different. Costs ~2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "Heartbeat = 6 hours"** — the heartbeat is 3 seconds; the **block report** is 6 hours. Mix-ups are heavily penalized.
> 3. **Stating "HDFS provides strong consistency"** — HDFS offers **write-once-read-many (WORM)** semantics, not transactional consistency.
> 4. **Skipping units in numericals** — examiners expect "7,812,500 blocks" not just "7.8 million". Always show the unit.
> 5. **Forgetting the rack awareness rule** — describing a 3-replica policy without mentioning "different rack" loses 2 marks in 7-mark questions.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **HDFS = Hadoop Distributed File System**, default block size **64 MB**, default replication factor **3**.
- ✅ **Architecture = Master-Slave** with NameNode (metadata) and DataNodes (data).
- ✅ **NameNode** holds FsImage + EditLog in memory; does NOT store data.
- ✅ **DataNode** stores data blocks; sends heartbeat every **3 s** and block report every **6 h**.
- ✅ **Secondary NameNode = Checkpoint Node**, NOT a standby — merges FsImage + EditLog.
- ✅ **Replica placement (Rack Awareness)**: Replica 1 = same node as client, Replica 2 = different rack, Replica 3 = same rack as Replica 2 (different node).
- ✅ **Write protocol = pipeline propagation** with end-to-end acknowledgment chain.
- ✅ **Read protocol = client gets metadata from NameNode, then streams from nearest DataNode**.
- ✅ **GFS = Google's reference DSS**, uses 64 MB chunks and a Master-Chunkserver model.
- ✅ **Formula trio**: $S = D \cdot R$, $N_b = \lceil D/B \rceil$, $M_{\text{NN}} \approx 150 \cdot N_b$ bytes.
- ✅ **MTTDL for R=3** scales as $1/(N^2 \cdot \lambda^3)$ — cubic reliability gain.
- ✅ **CAP theorem**: HDFS chooses **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance) with relaxed consistency.
- ✅ **Failure detection timeout** = 10 min × 30 = 300 s (default).
- ✅ **Block Report size** per DataNode = blocks × 12 bytes (8-byte ID + 4-byte checksum).
- ✅ **Modern DSS** (HBase, Cassandra) distribute metadata using consistent hashing — a key contrast to HDFS.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** For KTU ESE, focus 70% of revision time on the **HDFS read/write protocols** and **rack awareness rules** — these two topics account for ~50% of every Module 3 question paper over the last five years.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage Mechanism for Big Data - Database Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage Mechanism for Big Data - Database Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Database Technology for Big Data** refers to a class of distributed, horizontally scalable, and schema-flexible data storage systems designed to ingest, persist, retrieve, and process **Volume**, **Velocity**, and **Variety** (the 3Vs) of data that exceed the operational thresholds of traditional Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Big Data storage mechanisms are broadly classified into:
1. **NoSQL (Not Only SQL) Databases** – schema-less, distributed, eventually consistent.
2. **NewSQL Databases** – relational semantics with horizontal scalability.
3. **Distributed File Systems** – e.g., HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System).
4. **Data Warehouse / Columnar Stores** – e.g., HBase, ClickHouse.

> [!NOTE]
> **RDBMS Limitation**: Traditional RDBMS relies on vertical scaling (adding more CPU/RAM to a single node). When data crosses the **terabyte-to-petabyte** threshold, vertical scaling becomes economically infeasible, necessitating **horizontal scaling** (adding more commodity nodes). This paradigm shift is the genesis of Big Data storage technology.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **single giant library** with one librarian (RDBMS on one server). When 10,000 students arrive simultaneously, the librarian is overwhelmed — search slows, requests queue, and the system crashes.

Now, replace this with **100 small branch libraries**, each storing a portion of the catalog. Every branch has its own librarian, and a central index tells you *which branch* holds which book. A student query is routed to the correct branch in parallel. This is **Distributed Database Technology for Big Data**.

| Real-World Object | Database Analogy |
|---|---|
| 100 Branch Libraries | 100 Database Nodes (Cluster) |
| Central Index | NameNode / Metadata Service |
| Book Copies Distributed Across Branches | Data Replication & Sharding |
| Students | Concurrent Queries/Users |
| Librarian's Catalog Rule | Schema (Schema-on-Read vs. Schema-on-Write) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Schema-on-Write (RDBMS)**: Structure is defined *before* data is loaded — like pre-filling forms. **Schema-on-Read (Big Data/NoSQL)**: Structure is applied *at query time* — like inspecting free-form documents. This flexibility is the cornerstone of Big Data storage.

---

## 1.3 The CAP Theorem — The Guiding Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000)**: In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **only two** of the following three properties:
> - **C — Consistency**: Every read receives the most recent write or an error.
> - **A — Availability**: Every request receives a (non-error) response, even if some nodes are down.
> - **P — Partition Tolerance**: The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network partitions (message loss between nodes).
>
> Since **network partitions are inevitable** in real distributed systems, the real trade-off is **C vs A**.

---

## 1.4 Taxonomy of Big Data Database Technologies

### 1.4.1 NoSQL Database Families

1. **Key-Value Stores** — Data stored as `key → value` pairs. Best for caching, session storage, leaderboards.
   - *Examples*: **Redis**, **Amazon DynamoDB**, **Riak**.
2. **Document Stores** — Data stored as semi-structured documents (JSON/BSON/XML). Schema-flexible.
   - *Examples*: **MongoDB**, **CouchDB**, **Couchbase**.
3. **Column-Family / Wide-Column Stores** — Data stored in column families; optimized for sparse, wide rows. Excellent for analytical workloads.
   - *Examples*: **Apache HBase**, **Apache Cassandra**, **Google Bigtable**.
4. **Graph Databases** — Data stored as nodes and edges; optimized for relationship traversal.
   - *Examples*: **Neo4j**, **Amazon Neptune**, **JanusGraph**.

### 1.4.2 NewSQL Databases

- Relational model (SQL) + horizontal scalability + ACID guarantees.
- *Examples*: **Google Spanner**, **CockroachDB**, **MemSQL (SingleStore)**, **VoltDB**.

### 1.4.3 Distributed File Systems

- **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — Stores files by splitting into blocks (default **128 MB**) and replicating across DataNodes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 ACID vs BASE — Consistency Models

| Property | ACID (RDBMS) | BASE (NoSQL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | All-or-nothing transactions | Operations may partially succeed |
| **Consistency** | Strong consistency (immediate) | Eventual consistency (convergent) |
| **Isolation** | Concurrent transactions don't interfere | Conflicts resolved asynchronously |
| **Durability** | Committed data survives failures | Data persisted, may be stale briefly |
| **Acronym Expansion** | Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable | Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BASE** is the philosophical trade-off made by most NoSQL systems: relax strict consistency in favour of **Availability** and **Partition tolerance**, achieving convergence over milliseconds-to-seconds.

---

## 2.2 CAP Trade-Off Matrix

| System Type | Guarantees | Sacrifices | Real-World Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CA** (Traditional RDBMS) | Consistency + Availability | Partition Tolerance (single node) | PostgreSQL, MySQL |
| **CP** | Consistency + Partition Tolerance | Availability (during partition) | HBase, MongoDB (default), Redis |
| **AP** | Availability + Partition Tolerance | Strong Consistency | Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB |

---

## 2.3 Sharding (Horizontal Partitioning)

**Sharding** splits a logical dataset into independent chunks (*shards*) distributed across nodes. Each shard is a self-contained subset of the data.

### 2.3.1 Sharding Strategies

1. **Range-Based Sharding** — Data partitioned by a key range (e.g., User IDs 1–1M on Shard A, 1M–2M on Shard B). *Risk*: Hotspots.
2. **Hash-Based Sharding** — `shard_id = hash(key) mod N`. Even distribution; *Risk*: Resharding on addition of nodes.
3. **Directory-Based Sharding** — A lookup table maps keys → shards. *Flexible* but introduces a single point of failure unless replicated.
4. **Geo-Sharding** — Data partitioned by geographic region for latency optimization.

### 2.3.2 Consistent Hashing (Used in Cassandra, DynamoDB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Consistent Hashing** maps both keys and nodes onto a hash ring of size $2^{32}$ (or $2^{64}$). A key is stored on the *next clockwise* node. When a node is added/removed, only $\frac{K}{N}$ keys are remapped (where $K$ = total keys, $N$ = total nodes), as opposed to $\frac{K}{N+1}$ in traditional modulo hashing.

---

## 2.4 Replication

**Replication** duplicates data across multiple nodes for fault tolerance and read scalability.

| Replication Type | Description | Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous** | Write acknowledged only after all replicas confirm | Strong consistency; high latency |
| **Asynchronous** | Write acknowledged after local commit; replicas updated later | Low latency; risk of data loss |
| **Quorum-Based** | Write succeeds when $W$ of $N$ replicas confirm; Read succeeds when $R$ of $N$ confirm | Tunable: $W + R > N$ → strong reads |

### 2.4.1 Quorum Formula

The **Read-Write Quorum** is governed by:

$$R + W > N$$

Where:
- $N$ = total replicas
- $W$ = write quorum (replicas that must acknowledge a write)
- $R$ = read quorum (replicas that must respond to a read)

> [!NOTE]
> If $R + W > N$, the read set and write set must overlap → strong consistency. If $R + W \leq N$, the system tolerates stale reads (eventual consistency).

---

## 2.5 HBase Architecture (Deep Dive)

**HBase** is a column-family, distributed, scalable NoSQL database modeled after **Google Bigtable**.

### 2.5.1 Data Model

- **Table** → Collection of rows.
- **Row Key** → Unique identifier (lexicographically sorted; primary index).
- **Column Family** → Group of related columns (stored together on disk).
- **Column Qualifier** → Sub-column within a family.
- **Cell** → `{row, column family, column qualifier, timestamp} → value`.
- **Version** → Each cell holds multiple timestamped versions.

### 2.5.2 Storage Internals

- HBase stores data in **HFiles** (immutable, sorted SSTables).
- **MemStore**: In-memory write buffer.
- **WAL (Write-Ahead Log)**: Durability guarantee.
- **HFile Compaction**: Merges and cleans HFiles periodically (Minor + Major compaction).
- **Region** → Horizontal partition of table rows; served by a **RegionServer**.
- **Region Split**: When a region exceeds size threshold (default **10 GB**), it auto-splits.

---

## 2.6 MongoDB Architecture

- **Document** (BSON) is the unit of storage.
- **Collection** = group of documents (analogous to a table).
- **Replica Set** = primary + secondaries for HA; automatic failover via elections.
- **Sharded Cluster** = `mongos` routers → `config servers` → `shards` (replica sets).
- **Chunk**: A contiguous range of sharded data; default **64 MB**.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quorum Constraint | $R + W > N$ | Ensures read-write overlap for strong consistency |
| 2 | Replication Factor (Storage Cost) | $S_{\text{total}} = D \times RF$ | Total storage = Data × Replication Factor |
| 3 | Consistent Hashing Remapping | $\frac{K}{N}$ keys move on node add/remove | vs $\frac{K}{N+1}$ for modulo hash |
| 4 | Block Size (HDFS) | $128$ MB (default) | Configurable per cluster |
| 5 | Region Split Threshold (HBase) | $10$ GB (default) | Triggers automatic region split |
| 6 | Chunk Size (MongoDB) | $64$ MB (default) | Triggers chunk split when exceeded |
| 7 | Bloom Filter False Positive | $P(fp) \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$ | $k$ = hash functions, $m$ = bits, $n$ = elements |
| 8 | Storage per Replica (Cassandra) | $S_{i} = \frac{D_{\text{cluster}}}{N} \times RF$ | Per-node storage |
| 9 | CAP Trade-Off | $C \land A \land P = \text{Impossible}$ | Choose 2 of 3 |
| 10 | Eventual Consistency Time | $T_{\text{converge}} = \frac{\text{replication delay} \times N}{W}$ | Approximate convergence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, **$R + W > N$**, **CAP theorem**, and **$S_{\text{total}} = D \times RF$** are the three most frequently tested formulas. Memorize these and their derivations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quorum Read/Write Consistency ($R + W > N$)

### Problem Statement
A distributed key-value store has $N = 5$ replicas. A client performs a write that is acknowledged by $W = 3$ replicas. What is the minimum $R$ to guarantee that the read sees the latest write?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
We need the read set (size $R$) and the write set (size $W$) to **intersect** in at least one replica that holds the latest write. Mathematically:

$$\vert \text{ReadSet} \cap \text{WriteSet} \vert \geq 1$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Pigeonhole Principle.**
The *worst case* is when the two sets are as *disjoint* as possible. The maximum possible disjoint subsets occur when:

$$\vert \text{ReadSet} \cup \text{WriteSet} \vert = R + W$$

**Step 3 — Bound by total replicas.**
Since both sets are subsets of the $N$ total replicas, the union cannot exceed $N$:

$$R + W \leq N \quad \text{(worst case, sets disjoint)}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the inequality for guaranteed overlap.**
To **guarantee** at least one common replica, we need the union to *exceed* $N$:

$$R + W > N$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R$.**
With $N = 5$, $W = 3$:

$$R > N - W$$
$$R > 5 - 3$$
$$R > 2$$
$$\therefore R \geq 3$$

**Result:** The minimum read quorum is $R = 3$. With $R = 3, W = 3, N = 5$, we have $R + W = 6 > 5$ ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Storage with Replication

### Problem Statement
A Cassandra cluster stores $D = 2$ TB of unique data with a replication factor $RF = 3$. Compute the total storage footprint and per-node storage if the cluster has $N = 6$ nodes.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Total raw data:**
$$D = 2 \text{ TB} = 2048 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 2 — Total storage footprint:**

$$S_{\text{total}} = D \times RF$$

$$S_{\text{total}} = 2 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 6 \text{ TB}$$

**Step 3 — Per-node storage (uniform distribution):**

$$S_{\text{node}} = \frac{S_{\text{total}}}{N}$$

$$S_{\text{node}} = \frac{6 \text{ TB}}{6} = 1 \text{ TB/node}$$

**Step 4 — Per-node unique logical data:**

$$D_{\text{node}} = \frac{D}{N} = \frac{2 \text{ TB}}{6} \approx 0.333 \text{ TB/node}$$

**Step 5 — Verify replication on a single node:**
Each node should hold its logical share replicated $RF$ times locally? *Not exactly* — replication is across nodes, not within a node. Each node holds $\frac{1}{N}$ logical share and acts as a replica for $\frac{1}{N}$ of the dataset. The average per-node physical storage equals $1$ TB, as computed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight**: In production, **30–40% headroom** must be kept free for compaction (Cassandra/MongoDB) and HDFS block rebalancing. Effective usable capacity = $S_{\text{node}} \times 0.6$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: HDFS Block Count Calculation

### Problem Statement
A file of size $F = 1.5$ GB is to be stored on HDFS with default block size $B = 128$ MB and replication factor $RF = 3$. Compute:
1. Number of blocks
2. Total physical storage consumed
3. Number of DataNodes required (assuming 1 block per DataNode for simplicity)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert file size to MB:**
$$F = 1.5 \text{ GB} = 1.5 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1536 \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of logical blocks:**

$$\text{Blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil$$

$$\text{Blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{1536}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 12 \rceil = 12$$

(The last block is partial: $1536 - 11 \times 128 = 128$ MB → exactly 12 blocks, last is full.)

**Step 3 — Total logical storage:**

$$S_{\text{logical}} = \text{Blocks} \times B = 12 \times 128 \text{ MB} = 1536 \text{ MB} = 1.5 \text{ GB} \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Total physical storage with replication:**

$$S_{\text{physical}} = S_{\text{logical}} \times RF = 1.5 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 5 — DataNodes required (1 replica per node, no co-location):**

$$\text{DataNodes}_{\min} = \text{Blocks} \times RF = 12 \times 3 = 36 \text{ DataNodes}$$

(With replica co-location, this can be reduced to as few as 3 DataNodes if each holds all 12 blocks × 3 replicas = 36 block-replicas; practical minimum is $\max(3, \lceil \text{Total block replicas} / \text{blocks per node} \rceil)$.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Consistent Hashing — Key Remapping Ratio

### Problem Statement
A distributed cache uses consistent hashing with $N = 10$ nodes. When a new node is added (making $N' = 11$), what fraction of $K = 1{,}000{,}000$ keys must be remapped?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Traditional modulo hashing remapping fraction:**
On adding one node, every key's target may change:

$$\text{Remap}_{\text{modulo}} = \frac{N+1 - N}{N+1} = \frac{1}{N+1} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 9.09\%$$

**Step 2 — Consistent hashing remapping fraction:**
In consistent hashing, only the keys whose clockwise successor changes get remapped. Statistically, each node owns $\frac{1}{N}$ of the ring, so the new node "captures" $\frac{1}{N+1}$ of the keys from the existing nodes:

$$\text{Remap}_{\text{consistent}} = \frac{1}{N+1} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 9.09\%$$

**Step 3 — Generalization:**
For consistent hashing with $N$ nodes → $N+1$ nodes:

$$\text{Remap}_{\text{consistent}} = \frac{K}{N+1}$$

$$\text{Remap}_{\text{consistent}} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{11} \approx 90{,}909 \text{ keys}$$

**Step 4 — Virtual nodes improvement:**
With $V$ virtual nodes per physical node ($V \times N$ total positions), the variance decreases by factor $V$, providing more uniform distribution.

$$\text{Variance}_{\text{load}} \propto \frac{1}{V \cdot N}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Database Selection Guide

| Workload | Recommended DB | Justification | KTU 2024 Mark Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| OLTP, ACID transactions | PostgreSQL, CockroachDB | Strong consistency, SQL | 2 marks |
| Time-series / IoT | InfluxDB, TimescaleDB | Compression, time-indexed | 2 marks |
| Social network (graph) | Neo4j, JanusGraph | Native graph traversal | 3 marks |
| Document / CMS | MongoDB | Flexible schema, JSON | 3 marks |
| Wide-column / analytical | HBase, Cassandra | Sparse data, high write throughput | 5 marks |
| Key-value cache | Redis | In-memory, sub-ms latency | 2 marks |
| Search / log analytics | Elasticsearch | Inverted index, full-text | 3 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HBase Master-Slave Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client["Client Application"]
    ZK["ZooKeeper Ensemble"]
    HM["HMaster Node"]
    RS1["RegionServer 1"]
    RS2["RegionServer 2"]
    RS3["RegionServer 3"]
    HDFS["HDFS Cluster DataNodes"]
    R1["Region A rows 0 to 1M"]
    R2["Region B rows 1M to 2M"]
    R3["Region C rows 2M to 3M"]
    M1["MemStore 1"]
    M2["MemStore 2"]
    M3["MemStore 3"]
    HF1["HFile on HDFS"]
    HF2["HFile on HDFS"]
    HF3["HFile on HDFS"]

    Client --> ZK
    ZK --> HM
    ZK --> RS1
    ZK --> RS2
    ZK --> RS3

    Client --> RS2

    M3 --> HF3

    HF3 --> HDFS

    HM -.manages.-> RS2
    HM -.manages.-> RS3
```

```mermaid
        direction LR
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1["Step 1 Client Write Request\nwith row key column and value"]

    W3["Step 3 Write to MemStore\nin memory sorted buffer"]
    W4["Step 4 Acknowledge Client\nwrite is durable after WAL sync"]
    W5["Step 5 MemStore Flush\nwhen size exceeds threshold"]
    W6["Step 6 HFile Created\nimmutable sorted file on HDFS"]

```
