<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the CAP theorem. Why is Partition Tolerance non-negotiable in modern distributed systems?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **CAP Theorem** (proposed by **Eric Brewer, 2000**) states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most **two of the three** guarantees: **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)**.
>
> - **Consistency**: Every read returns the most recent write.
> - **Availability**: Every request receives a non-error response.
> - **Partition Tolerance**: The system continues to function despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes.
>
> **Why P is non-negotiable (1 mark):** In real-world distributed systems spanning multiple data centres, racks, or cloud availability zones, **network partitions are inevitable** due to hardware failures, switch reboots, congestion, or undersea cable cuts. Since we cannot *prevent* partitions, we must *tolerate* them. Therefore, the practical design choice reduces to **C vs A**.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Row-Oriented and Column-Oriented storage. Give one example database for each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Row-Oriented | Column-Oriented |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Storage Unit** | Entire row stored contiguously | Entire column stored contiguously |
> | **Best For** | OLTP (many columns per row read) | OLAP (few columns, many rows aggregated) |
> | **Compression** | Low (mixed data types in row) | High (same data type per column) |
> | **Example** | MySQL, PostgreSQL, HBase (rows) | HBase (qualifiers), Cassandra, ClickHouse |
> | **I/O Pattern** | Reads full rows | Reads only required columns |
>
> **Example (1 mark):** Row-oriented → **PostgreSQL**; Column-oriented → **Apache Cassandra** (or **ClickHouse**).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **HBase data model** in detail with a suitable example. Differentiate between **Row-Oriented** and **Column-Oriented** storage with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **HBase architecture** with a neat diagram. Explain the role of **HMaster**, **RegionServer**, **ZooKeeper**, **MemStore**, **HFile**, and **WAL** in the read/write path. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**1. HBase Data Model (4 marks):**
HBase is a sparse, distributed, persistent, multi-dimensional sorted map. The model has the following components:

- **Table**: A collection of rows.
- **Row Key**: A unique byte-array identifier; rows are stored in **lexicographic order** of row keys. This is the *only* indexed attribute.
- **Column Family**: A grouping of related columns. Defined at table creation; columns within a family share a storage region.
- **Column Qualifier**: A sub-column name within a family. Not predefined — added dynamically.
- **Cell**: The intersection of `{row, column family, column qualifier, timestamp}` storing a value.
- **Timestamp/Version**: Each cell stores multiple versions, identified by a 64-bit integer timestamp.

**Example (3 marks):**

```
Table: WebLogs
Row Key:     "user_42"                              "user_99"
Columns:     info:    clicks:    pageviews:         info:    clicks:
             name,    url,       session_id,        name,    url,
             email,   timestamp  referrer           email    timestamp
```

Each row may have a *different* set of columns (sparsity).

**Row vs Column-Oriented Storage (Bonus, 0 marks, contextual):**

- **Row-Oriented**: `R1[all cols] R2[all cols] R3[all cols]` — efficient for transactional row reads.
- **Column-Oriented**: `C1[all rows] C2[all rows] C3[all rows]` — efficient for analytical aggregations on few columns.

HBase is technically a **key-value store** that groups values by column families, behaving like a column-family store.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**HBase Architecture (Diagram + Explanation, 7 marks):**

The HBase cluster has three major component types:

1. **ZooKeeper Ensemble (1 mark)**:
   - Maintains cluster coordination, leader election for HMaster, and region assignment metadata.
   - Stores the "meta" table location and cluster state.
   - Provides **highly available coordination service**.

2. **HMaster (1 mark)**:
   - The master node that manages **region assignment** to RegionServers.
   - Handles **DDL operations** (table creation, schema changes).
   - Performs **load balancing** and **failover** of RegionServers.
   - *Note*: HMaster is **not** in the data path — it is a control plane node.

3. **RegionServer (2 marks)**:
   - Serves reads/writes for one or more **Regions** (horizontal partitions of table rows).
   - Each Region contains an **MemStore** (in-memory sorted buffer for writes) and **HFiles** (immutable, sorted on-disk files in HDFS).
   - When a MemStore fills up, it is flushed to a new HFile.
   - When a Region exceeds size threshold (**10 GB** default), it **auto-splits** into two regions.

4. **Write-Ahead Log (WAL) (1 mark)**:
   - Every write is first appended to the WAL (stored in HDFS) for **durability**.
   - On RegionServer failure, the WAL is replayed to recover the in-memory state.

5. **Read Path (1 mark)**:
   - Client consults ZooKeeper → finds meta region → locates RegionServer.
   - RegionServer checks **MemStore** → then **BlockCache** (read cache) → then **HFiles** (with **Bloom filters** to skip non-matching HFiles).
   - Merged result returned.

6. **Write Path (1 mark)**:
   - Append to **WAL** → write to **MemStore** → acknowledge client.
   - Asynchronous **MemStore → HFile flush** when threshold is reached.
   - Periodic **compaction** merges small HFiles into larger ones.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Warning:**
> - Students often confuse **HMaster** with the data-serving node. HMaster is a **control plane** only; RegionServers serve the data.
> - Don't forget to mention **Bloom filters** in HFiles — this is a frequent 1-mark question.
> - Mentioning **time-to-live (TTL)** and **cell versions** demonstrates depth and earns bonus marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **CAP theorem** with a neat diagram. Discuss how **Cassandra** and **HBase** are positioned on the CAP triangle, justifying your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain **sharding strategies** in distributed databases. Compare **range-based** and **hash-based** sharding. Describe **consistent hashing** and its advantage over modulo-based hashing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**CAP Theorem (3 marks):**
The **CAP Theorem**, formulated by **Eric Brewer** (2000) and proved by **Gilbert & Lynch** (2002), states that any distributed data store can provide at most **two of the three** properties simultaneously:

- **Consistency (C)**: All nodes see the same data at the same time (linearizability).
- **Availability (A)**: Every request receives a non-error response (no timeout).
- **Partition Tolerance (P)**: The system continues operating despite network partitions.

**Diagram (1 mark):**
A triangle with vertices C, A, P and three system families CA, CP, AP.

**Cassandra → AP (1.5 marks):**
Cassandra is designed as an **AP system**. It prioritizes **Availability** and **Partition Tolerance** by:
- Using **leaderless replication** (no single master).
- Supporting **tunable consistency** (configurable $W$ and $R$).
- Allowing reads/writes even when some replicas are down.
- Trades off strong consistency for **eventual consistency** (BASE).

**HBase → CP (1.5 marks):**
HBase is positioned as a **CP system**. It prioritizes **Consistency** and **Partition Tolerance**:
- Has a **single HMaster** (though with backup) and strong leader-based coordination.
- Uses **ZooKeeper** for consistent metadata.
- During a partition, HBase may **refuse writes** to maintain consistency (sacrificing availability).
- Relies on **WAL** for strict durability.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Sharding (2 marks):**
Sharding is the process of splitting a large dataset into smaller, independent chunks (*shards*) distributed across multiple nodes. Each shard is a self-contained database; together they form a logical whole.

**Range-Based Sharding (1.5 marks):**
- Data is partitioned by a contiguous key range.
- **Example**: `users` table sharded by `user_id`:
  - Shard A: `user_id` 1 to 1,000,000
  - Shard B: `user_id` 1,000,001 to 2,000,000
  - Shard C: `user_id` 2,000,001 to 3,000,000
- **Advantage**: Range queries are efficient (one shard serves the query).
- **Disadvantage**: **Hotspots** if recent IDs are accessed more (e.g., new users).

**Hash-Based Sharding (1.5 marks):**
- `shard_id = hash(key) mod N`
- **Example**: `hash("user_42") mod 3 = 1` → Shard 1.
- **Advantage**: Even distribution; no hotspots.
- **Disadvantage**: Range queries hit all shards; **resharding** required on node add/remove.

**Consistent Hashing (2 marks):**
Consistent hashing maps both **keys** and **nodes** onto a circular hash space of size $2^{32}$.

- Each key is assigned to the **next clockwise node** on the ring.
- When a node is added/removed, only $\frac{K}{N+1}$ keys are remapped (vs nearly all keys in modulo hashing).
- **Virtual nodes (vnodes)**: Each physical node is assigned multiple positions on the ring to balance load.
- **Used in**: **Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra, Riak**.

**Advantage over Modulo Hashing (Bonus for completeness):**
Adding a node in modulo hashing remaps nearly $\frac{K}{N+1} \cdot N$ keys; consistent hashing remaps only $\frac{K}{N+1}$ keys — **minimal disruption**, smoother scaling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Warning:**
> - **Don't write "CAP = 3"** — it's a *trade-off*, not a multiplier. State clearly: *only two* can be guaranteed.
> - Students often forget that **P is mandatory** in real systems. Saying "we choose P" alone does not answer the question — explain **why**.
> - For sharding, don't just list types — give a **numeric example** (e.g., `hash("user_42") mod 4 = 2`).
> - For consistent hashing, a **diagram of the ring** is worth 2 marks easily. Draw it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Storage Mechanism for Big Data**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **CAP Theorem**: Choose **2 of 3** (C, A, P); **P is non-negotiable**, so real choice is C vs A.
- ☐ **ACID vs BASE**: ACID = strict, RDBMS; BASE = eventual, NoSQL.
- ☐ **Sharding** = horizontal partitioning; **Replication** = copying data.
- ☐ **Consistent Hashing**: Maps keys/nodes on a $2^{32}$ ring; minimal remapping on scaling.

### Database Types
- ☐ **Key-Value**: Redis, DynamoDB → caching, sessions.
- ☐ **Document**: MongoDB, CouchDB → CMS, catalogs.
- ☐ **Column-Family**: HBase, Cassandra → time-series, IoT, analytics.
- ☐ **Graph**: Neo4j, Neptune → social networks, recommendations.
- ☐ **NewSQL**: Spanner, CockroachDB → global ACID + scale.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ☐ **Quorum**: $R + W > N$ → strong consistency.
- ☐ **Total storage**: $S_{\text{total}} = D \times RF$.
- ☐ **Per-node storage**: $S_{\text{node}} = \frac{D \times RF}{N}$.
- ☐ **HDFS block count**: $\lceil F / B \rceil$, $B = 128$ MB.
- ☐ **Consistent hashing remap**: $\frac{K}{N+1}$ keys on node add.

### HBase Specifics
- ☐ Components: **HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper, WAL, MemStore, HFile, BlockCache, Bloom Filter**.
- ☐ Default **region split threshold**: **10 GB**.
- ☐ Default **HDFS block size**: **128 MB**.
- ☐ Cell versioning: multiple timestamped values per cell.

### CAP Positioning (Favourite Exam Question)
- ☐ **CA**: PostgreSQL, MySQL (single-node RDBMS).
- ☐ **CP**: HBase, MongoDB, Redis.
- ☐ **AP**: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.

### Replication Settings (Favourite 3-Mark Question)
- ☐ $N = 3, W = 2, R = 2$ → $R + W = 4 > 3$ ✓ (strong).
- ☐ $N = 3, W = 1, R = 1$ → $R + W = 2 \leq 3$ (eventual).
- ☐ Tunable consistency is a hallmark of **Cassandra**.

### Frequently Asked Trivia
- ☐ **HBase is modeled on Google Bigtable** (2006 paper).
- ☐ **Cassandra was inspired by Amazon Dynamo and Google Bigtable**.
- ☐ **MongoDB stores BSON (Binary JSON)**, max document size **16 MB**.
- ☐ **CAP was proved by Gilbert & Lynch in 2002** (after Brewer's 2000 conjecture).
- ☐ **Sharding in MongoDB** uses a **config server** with `mongos` routers.

### Bloom Filter
- ☐ Used in HBase HFiles to **skip non-matching files** during reads.
- ☐ Probabilistic; may yield **false positives** but **never false negatives**.
- ☐ False positive rate: $P(fp) \approx \left(1 - e^{-kn/m}\right)^{k}$.

### Last-Minute Diagram Checklist
- ☐ HBase architecture (HMaster, RegionServer, ZooKeeper, HDFS).
- ☐ CAP triangle with examples.
- ☐ MongoDB sharded cluster (mongos, config servers, shards).
- ☐ Consistent hashing ring.
- ☐ LSM-tree write path (WAL → MemStore → HFile → Compaction).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Big Data Analytics, **Design Factors** refer to the critical architectural, infrastructural, and computational parameters that must be carefully evaluated and tuned when designing a distributed big data storage and processing system. According to the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus (Module 3), these factors govern *how* a distributed file system (primarily HDFS), a NoSQL datastore, or a MapReduce-style processing framework is configured to handle the **5V characteristics** — Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value — of massive datasets.

Mathematically, the overall system design efficiency $E$ can be expressed as a function of multiple design parameters:

$$E = f(V, V_e, V_a, V_r, V_l, S, F, C, P, L)$$

where:
- $V$ = **Volume** of data
- $V_e$ = **Velocity** (rate of data ingestion)
- $V_a$ = **Variety** (structural heterogeneity)
- $V_r$ = **Veracity** (data quality / noise)
- $V_l$ = **Value** (insight density)
- $S$ = **Scalability** factor
- $F$ = **Fault tolerance** threshold
- $C$ = **Cost** constraint
- $P$ = **Performance** / throughput requirement
- $L$ = **Latency** tolerance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 3, *Design Factors* is treated as a foundational precursor to HDFS architecture discussions. The examiner frequently tests whether the student can **map** the 5V characteristics to concrete HDFS configuration parameters such as block size, replication factor, and Namenode memory sizing.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are an **architect designing a massive public library** that must store **10 million books** of all shapes, sizes, and languages, with **new books arriving every second** from around the world, and where **no single librarian can ever leave their post**, and yet **no book can ever be lost**.

Before you draw a single blueprint, you must decide:

| Design Question | Library Analogy | Big Data Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| *How big should each shelf compartment be?* | Size of a bookshelf slot | **HDFS Block Size** (default 128 MB) |
| *How many duplicate copies of every book?* | Photocopies in different wings | **Replication Factor** (default 3) |
| *Where do we keep the master catalog?* | Central index room | **Namenode** (metadata server) |
| *How do we organize buildings across a city?* | Geographic distribution | **Rack Awareness** |
| *What if a building burns down?* | Fire-safety planning | **Fault Tolerance** |
| *How fast can someone find a book?* | Librarian response time | **Latency & Throughput** |

This library planning exercise is **exactly** what "Design Factors" in Big Data means — answering these decisions *before* deploying a single server.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Every design factor in HDFS is a **trade-off**. Increasing block size speeds up sequential reads but reduces parallelism. Increasing replication factor improves fault tolerance but consumes 3× the storage. KTU questions almost always test these trade-offs.

---

## 1.3 The 5V Characteristics as Foundational Design Drivers

The 5V model of Big Data provides the **upstream constraints** that every downstream design factor must satisfy.

> [!NOTE]
> **5V Model Definitions (KTU Standard)**
> - **Volume** — Terabytes to Zettabytes of data. Drives decisions on storage capacity, compression, partitioning.
> - **Velocity** — Speed of data generation and movement. Drives decisions on ingestion rate, streaming vs. batch.
> - **Variety** — Structured, semi-structured, unstructured. Drives decisions on schema-on-read vs. schema-on-write.
> - **Veracity** — Trustworthiness and noise in data. Drives decisions on validation, cleansing layers.
> - **Value** — Business insight extracted. Drives decisions on analytics workload mix (batch vs. interactive vs. ML).

These five characteristics act as **boundary conditions** for the design equation $E = f(\dots)$ introduced above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Design Factors

Design factors in a Big Data system can be classified into **three operational layers**:

### Layer 1 — Data Characteristics Layer (5V)
- **Volume** $\rightarrow$ Block size selection, Namenode memory allocation, cluster size.
- **Velocity** $\rightarrow$ Streaming engine choice (Spark Streaming, Flink), batch window size.
- **Variety** $\rightarrow$ File format choice (Parquet, ORC, Avro, JSON, SequenceFile).
- **Veracity** $\rightarrow$ Validation framework, schema registry.
- **Value** $\rightarrow$ Query engine choice (Hive, Impala, Presto), indexing strategy.

### Layer 2 — System Architecture Layer
- **Scalability** — Ability to add nodes without redesign. Measured by horizontal scaling coefficient.
- **Fault Tolerance** — System continues despite node failures. Achieved via replication and re-execution.
- **Data Locality** — Computation is moved to data, not vice-versa. Reduces network congestion.
- **Rack Awareness** — Replicas placed on different racks to survive rack-level failures.

### Layer 3 — Operational Quality Layer
- **Performance / Throughput** — Jobs completed per unit time.
- **Latency** — Time to first result.
- **Cost** — Capital + operational expenditure.
- **Security** — Authentication (Kerberos), authorization (ACLs), encryption (at-rest, in-transit).

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Factor

### 2.2.1 Block Size ($B$)
- **Why it matters:** A file of size $F$ MB is split into $\lceil F / B \rceil$ blocks. A larger $B$ reduces Namenode metadata load but reduces parallelism for MapReduce.
- **How to decide:** Default in Apache Hadoop 3.x is **128 MB**; CDH/HDP used 128 MB; legacy was 64 MB. For very large files (e.g., genomic data), increase to 256 MB or 512 MB.

### 2.2.2 Replication Factor ($R$)
- **Why it matters:** Each block is stored on $R$ different DataNodes. The HDFS Namenode enforces that at all times.
- **How to decide:** Default is **3**. Higher $R$ = more fault tolerance, more storage cost. Mathematically:

$$S_{total} = F \times R$$

where $S_{total}$ is total cluster storage consumed for a dataset of raw size $F$.

### 2.2.3 Namenode Memory ($M_{NN}$)
- **Why it matters:** Namenode stores all file → block → DataNode mappings in RAM. Each block entry consumes roughly **150–200 bytes** of heap.
- **How to size it:** For $N_{blocks}$ blocks in the cluster:

$$M_{NN} \approx N_{blocks} \times 200 \text{ bytes}$$

### 2.2.4 Data Locality
- **Why it matters:** Network bandwidth is the bottleneck in distributed systems. Moving computation to data is **10–100× faster** than moving data to computation.
- **How to achieve:** HDFS exposes block locations to MapReduce schedulers (YARN), which try to place mappers on nodes holding the input block.

### 2.2.5 Rack Awareness
- **Why it matters:** Replicas placed on a single rack are vulnerable to rack switch failure. HDFS replica placement policy:
  - 1st replica on local node (or random node if writer is outside cluster).
  - 2nd replica on a different rack.
  - 3rd replica on the same rack as the 2nd but different node.
- **Engineering value:** With $R=3$, this guarantees survival of **at least one rack failure and one node failure** simultaneously.

### 2.2.6 Fault Tolerance
- **Why it matters:** In commodity clusters, mean time between failures (MTBF) is short. Hadoop assumes failure is normal.
- **How to achieve:**
  - Data-level: Replication ($R \geq 3$).
  - Job-level: Task re-execution on YARN.
  - Namenode-level: Checkpointing + Standby Namenode (HA).

### 2.2.7 Scalability
- **Why it matters:** Big Data systems must scale from 10 nodes to 10,000 nodes.
- **How to achieve:** HDFS Namenode horizontal scalability is the **classic bottleneck** — addressed by Hadoop 3.x Federation feature.

### 2.2.8 Latency vs. Throughput Trade-off
- **Latency-sensitive** workloads (real-time fraud detection) require Storm/Flink.
- **Throughput-sensitive** workloads (log aggregation, ETL) use batch MapReduce/Spark.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S_{total} = F \times R$ | Total storage consumed given raw file size $F$ and replication $R$ | MB, GB, TB, PB |
| 2 | $N_{blocks} = \lceil F / B \rceil$ | Number of blocks for a file of size $F$ with block size $B$ | dimensionless integer |
| 3 | $M_{NN} \approx N_{blocks} \times 200$ | Namenode heap memory requirement | bytes (≈ 1 GB per 5 million blocks) |
| 4 | $T_{replication} = T_{write} \times R$ | Effective write latency (pipeline replication) | milliseconds |
| 5 | $\text{Storage Efficiency } \eta = 1 / R$ | Useful storage per unit raw storage | $0 < \eta \leq 1$ (33% for $R=3$) |
| 6 | $\text{Availability } A = 1 - (1-p)^R$ | Cluster availability with node failure prob $p$ and replication $R$ | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $\text{Heartbeat interval} = 3$ s | DataNode → Namenode heartbeat | seconds |
| 8 | $\text{Block re-replication threshold} = 1$ | Minimum replicas before re-replication triggers | integer |
| 9 | $\text{Replication factor default} = 3$ | HDFS default | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Block size default} = 128$ MB | Hadoop 3.x default | MB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize formulas (1), (2), (3), and (6). These appear in **at least one calculation question** in every end-semester exam under Module 3.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Design Factors |
|---|---|
| **Banking / FinTech** | Real-time fraud detection → high $V_e$ (Velocity) demands low-latency stream processing + Kerberos-based security. |
| **Healthcare Genomics** | Petabyte-scale DNA sequences → high $V$ (Volume) demands large $B$ (block size 256–512 MB), $R=2$ (since data is reproducible). |
| **Social Media (Twitter / Meta)** | Mixed batch + stream workloads → variety of file formats (Parquet for analytics, Avro for logs). |
| **IoT / Smart Cities** | Billions of sensor events/sec → high $V$ + $V_e$ demands edge computing + write-optimized storage. |
| **E-commerce Recommendation** | ML training on user clickstreams → high $V_l$ (Value) demands columnar formats (Parquet/ORC) for fast column scans. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Cluster Storage with Replication

**Problem Setup:** A banking client stores **1.2 TB** of daily transaction logs in HDFS. The HDFS cluster is configured with a block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Compute:
(a) The total number of blocks.
(b) The total storage consumed in the cluster.
(c) The Namenode memory required (using the 200-bytes-per-block rule).

---

### Step 1 — Convert File Size to Common Unit

We work in **MB** for precision.

$$F = 1.2 \text{ TB} = 1.2 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB}$$

$$
F = 1.2 \times 1{,}048{,}576 = 1{,}258{,}291.2 \text{ MB}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Compute Number of Blocks

Apply formula $N_{blocks} = \lceil F / B \rceil$ with $B = 128$ MB.

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}258{,}291.2}{128} \right\rceil
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{1{,}258{,}291.2}{128} = 9{,}830.4
$$

Applying the ceiling function:

$$
N_{blocks} = \lceil 9{,}830.4 \rceil = 9{,}831 \text{ blocks}
$$

> **Logic:** The ceiling is used because HDFS cannot store a partial block — any leftover data (here, 0.4 of a block ≈ 51.2 MB) is padded into a fresh block.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Total Cluster Storage

Apply formula $S_{total} = F \times R$ with $R = 3$.

$$
S_{total} = 1.2 \text{ TB} \times 3 = 3.6 \text{ TB}
$$

> **Logic:** Every byte written by the client is replicated 3 times across distinct DataNodes. The cluster must reserve 3.6 TB of raw disk capacity for 1.2 TB of logical data.

---

### Step 4 — Compute Namenode Memory

Apply formula $M_{NN} \approx N_{blocks} \times 200$ bytes.

$$
M_{NN} = 9{,}831 \times 200 = 1{,}966{,}200 \text{ bytes}
$$

Convert to MB:

$$
M_{NN} = \frac{1{,}966{,}200}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 1.875 \text{ MB}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the **heap memory** occupied in the Namenode's RAM for metadata. While 1.875 MB seems small, remember this is for **one file**. A real cluster with 100 million blocks needs ≈ 20 GB of Namenode heap.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Efficiency vs. Availability Trade-off

**Problem Setup:** A single DataNode has a per-month failure probability of $p = 0.01$. Compare two configurations:
- **Config A:** $R = 3$, no rack awareness.
- **Config B:** $R = 2$, with rack awareness.

Compute the **availability** $A$ of a block in each configuration using:

$$A = 1 - (1-p)^R$$

---

### Step 1 — Config A (R = 3)

$$
A_A = 1 - (1 - 0.01)^3
$$

$$
= 1 - (0.99)^3
$$

$$
= 1 - 0.970299
$$

$$
A_A = 0.029701 \approx 2.97\%
$$

> **Wait — this is wrong!** If the formula is $1 - (1-p)^R$ and $p$ is failure probability, then $(1-p)^R$ is the probability that **all R replicas** simultaneously fail. That is a *risk*, not availability. Let me correct the convention.

**Corrected Convention:** $p$ = probability that a single node is **up** (availability), so failure prob = $1-p$. Probability all $R$ replicas fail simultaneously:

$$
P_{loss} = (1-p)^R
$$

Cluster **block availability**:

$$
A = 1 - (1-p)^R
$$

With $p = 0.99$ (node up-probability), $1-p = 0.01$:

**Config A: R = 3**

$$
P_{loss} = (0.01)^3 = 10^{-6} = 0.0001\%
$$

$$
A_A = 1 - 10^{-6} \approx 0.999999 = 99.9999\%
$$

**Config B: R = 2**

$$
P_{loss} = (0.01)^2 = 10^{-4} = 0.01\%
$$

$$
A_B = 1 - 10^{-4} = 0.9999 = 99.99\%
$$

---

### Step 2 — Storage Efficiency Comparison

Storage efficiency $\eta = 1 / R$.

- Config A: $\eta_A = 1/3 = 33.33\%$
- Config B: $\eta_B = 1/2 = 50.00\%$

---

### Step 3 — Trade-off Matrix

| Metric | Config A ($R=3$) | Config B ($R=2$) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability | 99.9999% | 99.99% | Config A |
| Storage Efficiency | 33.33% | 50.00% | Config B |
| Cost (1 TB logical) | 3 TB | 2 TB | Config B |
| Risk of 1 node failure | Survives | Survives (1 copy left) | Tie |
| Risk of 2 simultaneous node failures | Survives | **Data loss** | Config A |

> **Engineering Conclusion:** Config A costs 1.5× more storage but provides a **"four nines" vs. "five nines" guarantee**. Banks and healthcare systems prefer Config A. Log/clickstream data often uses Config B.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Namenode Heap Sizing for Production Cluster

**Problem Setup:** A production cluster stores **500 TB** of data with **128 MB** blocks and **R = 3**. Calculate the Namenode heap memory.

---

### Step 1 — Total Logical Data (without replication)

$$
F = 500 \text{ TB} = 500 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 524{,}288{,}000 \text{ MB}
$$

---

### Step 2 — Number of Blocks

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{524{,}288{,}000}{128} \right\rceil = 4{,}096{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Namenode Heap

$$
M_{NN} = 4{,}096{,}000 \times 200 = 819{,}200{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
M_{NN} = \frac{819{,}200{,}000}{1024^3} \approx 0.763 \text{ GB}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Adding Replication Overhead in Metadata

Each block has $R$ replicas; Namenode tracks every replica. So actual metadata is:

$$
M_{NN,actual} = 4{,}096{,}000 \times 3 \times 200 = 2{,}457{,}600{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.29 \text{ GB}
$$

> **KTU Reality Check:** Production clusters provision **64–128 GB** Namenode heaps to handle namespace objects (files + directories + blocks + replicas + edit logs) with a safety margin of 5–10×.

---

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Design Factor | Configuration Variable | Increase Impact | Decrease Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Size $B$ | `dfs.blocksize` | ↑ Sequential read speed, ↓ Namenode load | ↑ Parallelism, ↑ Metadata |
| Replication $R$ | `dfs.replication` | ↑ Fault tolerance, ↑ Cost | ↓ Cost, ↑ Risk |
| Namenode Heap $M_{NN}$ | `HADOOP_NAMENODE_OPTS` | ↑ Namespace size support | ↓ Cost |
| Heartbeat Interval | `dfs.heartbeat.interval` | ↓ Namenode load | ↑ Failure detection time |
| Packet Size | `dfs.write.packet.size` | ↑ Network throughput | ↑ Retry overhead |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 5V Design Factor Decision Tree

The following Mermaid flowchart maps each 5V characteristic to its corresponding HDFS design decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Big Data Workload Arrives] --> B{Identify Dominant V}
    B -->|Volume| C[Set Block Size B]
    B -->|Velocity| D[Select Batch vs Stream Engine]
    B -->|Variety| E[Choose File Format]
    B -->|Veracity| F[Add Validation Layer]
    B -->|Value| G[Choose Query Engine]

    C --> C1[Small files → 64 MB B]
    C --> C2[Large files → 256 MB B]
    C --> C3[Huge files → 512 MB B]

    D --> D1[Batch: Hadoop MapReduce]
    D --> D2[Stream: Spark / Flink]

    E --> E1[Columnar: Parquet ORC]
    E --> E2[Row: Avro JSON]
    E --> E3[Binary: SequenceFile]

    F --> F1[Schema Registry]
    F --> F2[Cleansing UDFs]

    G --> G1[Interactive: Impala Presto]
    G --> G2[Batch SQL: Hive]

    C1 --> H[Final HDFS Configuration]
    C2 --> H
    C3 --> H
    D1 --> H
    D2 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
    E3 --> H
    F1 --> H
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
```

---

## 4.2 HDFS Replica Placement (Rack Awareness) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client Writer] -->|Block Write Request| NN[Namenode Metadata Server]

    subgraph ClusterRack1[Rack 1]
        DN1A[DataNode A1]
        DN1B[DataNode A2]
        DN1C[DataNode A3]
    end

    subgraph ClusterRack2[Rack 2]
        DN2A[DataNode B1]
        DN2B[DataNode B2]
        DN2C[DataNode B3]
    end

    subgraph ClusterRack3[Rack 3]
        DN3A[DataNode C1]
        DN3B[DataNode C2]
        DN3C[DataNode C3]
    end

    NN -->|Replica 1 Local| DN1A
    NN -->|Replica 2 Different Rack| DN2A
    NN -->|Replica 3 Same Rack as R2| DN2B
    NN -->|Additional Replica Random| DN3A
    NN -->|Additional Replica Random| DN1B
```

---

## 4.3 Design Factor Trade-off Spider Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Center[HDFS Configuration Space]
        CORE[Design Decisions]
    end

    subgraph TF1[Performance Factors]
        F1[Block Size]
        F2[Packet Size]
        F3[Heartbeat Interval]
    end

    subgraph TF2[Reliability Factors]
        F4[Replication Factor]
        F5[Rack Awareness]
        F6[Heartbeat Timeout]
    end

    subgraph TF3[Cost Factors]
        F7[Raw Storage TB]
        F8[Network Bandwidth]
        F9[Namenode RAM GB]
    end

    F1 --> CORE
    F2 --> CORE
    F3 --> CORE
    F4 --> CORE
    F5 --> CORE
    F6 --> CORE
    F7 --> CORE
    F8 --> CORE
    F9 --> CORE

    CORE --> OUT1[Configuration File hdfs-site.xml]
    CORE --> OUT2[Configuration File core-site.xml]
    CORE --> OUT3[Configuration File yarn-site.xml]
```

---

## 4.4 Namenode Memory Sizing Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Total Raw Data F TB] --> CalcBlock[Compute N blocks = F / B]
    CalcBlock --> CalcReplica[Multiply by R for replicas]
    CalcReplica --> CalcBytes[N blocks x R x 200 bytes]
    CalcBytes --> CalcHeap[Total Namenode Heap GB]
    CalcHeap --> Decision{Heap exceeds 64 GB?}
    Decision -->|Yes| Fed[Enable HDFS Federation]
    Decision -->|No| Single[Single Namenode OK]
    Fed --> Fed1[Mount multiple Namespaces]
    Fed --> Fed2[Scale to billions of blocks]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the HDFS architecture" or "explain rack-aware replica placement," examiners award full marks **only if** the diagram shows (a) the Namenode as a central metadata authority, (b) distinct racks, and (c) at least 2 different racks containing replicas. The Mermaid diagram in **Section 4.2** above covers all three requirements.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the 5V characteristics of Big Data. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The 5V characteristics of Big Data are:

1. **Volume** — The massive size of data, ranging from terabytes to zettabytes, generated by systems, sensors, and users.
2. **Velocity** — The speed at which data is generated, captured, and processed, often in real-time.
3. **Variety** — The different forms of data: structured (databases), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (images, video, logs).
4. **Veracity** — The trustworthiness, quality, and noise level of data, including inconsistencies and ambiguities.
5. **Value** — The business insight and useful information that can be extracted from the data through analytics.

These five characteristics collectively drive the **design factors** of a Big Data system, such as storage capacity, processing engine choice, and file format selection. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: What is meant by "Data Locality" in HDFS? Why is it a critical design factor? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

**Data Locality** in HDFS refers to the principle of **moving computation to the location of data** rather than moving data to the location of computation. When a MapReduce or YARN job is scheduled, the framework attempts to place the processing task on a DataNode that already holds the required input block.

It is a critical design factor because:

1. **Network bandwidth is the bottleneck** in distributed systems. Moving petabytes of data across the network is impractical.
2. **Reduces network congestion** and improves cluster throughput.
3. **Improves job completion time** by 10× to 100× compared to moving data.
4. **Reduces infrastructure cost** since less network hardware is needed.

HDFS exposes block locations via the Namenode, enabling YARN's ApplicationMaster to make locality-aware scheduling decisions. *(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *HDFS Storage Sizing Problem* [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

A healthcare analytics company uses HDFS to store patient genomic data.

**Given Data:**
- Total file size: $F = 2.5$ TB
- Block size: $B = 256$ MB
- Replication factor: $R = 3$

**Solve the following:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate:
1. The number of HDFS blocks.
2. The total cluster storage consumed.
3. The Namenode memory required (using 200 bytes per block including replicas).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the design factors considered when choosing the block size of 256 MB for genomic data. Justify why this might be different from the Hadoop default of 128 MB, and explain the trade-off with parallelism.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Convert file size to MB**

$$
F = 2.5 \text{ TB} = 2.5 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 2{,}621{,}440 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2: Number of blocks**

$$
N_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}621{,}440}{256} \right\rceil
$$

$$
= \left\lceil 10{,}240 \right\rceil = 10{,}240 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3: Total cluster storage**

$$
S_{total} = F \times R = 2.5 \times 3 = 7.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4: Namenode memory (with replicas)**

$$
M_{NN} = 10{,}240 \times 3 \times 200 = 6{,}144{,}000 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
M_{NN} = \frac{6{,}144{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 5.86 \text{ MB}
$$

**Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Number of blocks | 10,240 |
| Total cluster storage | 7.5 TB |
| Namenode memory | ≈ 5.86 MB |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Design Factor Justification for B = 256 MB:**

1. **Volume Characteristic:** Genomic data files are typically large (often 100 GB+ per patient's whole-genome sequence). A larger block size of 256 MB **reduces the total number of blocks**, which in turn:
   - Reduces Namenode memory pressure.
   - Reduces Namenode RPC overhead during block listing.
   - Improves Namenode startup time (FSD image loading).

2. **Sequential Access Pattern:** Bioinformatics workloads (BLAST, GATK, BWA) perform **highly sequential reads** over the entire file. A 256 MB block enables longer sequential streams, improving disk I/O throughput.

3. **Trade-off with Parallelism:** With 256 MB blocks, a 100 GB file produces only ≈ 400 blocks. A MapReduce job can launch only 400 parallel map tasks maximum (one per block). This is **acceptable** for genomic workloads because the processing is per-sample, not per-block.

4. **Comparison with 128 MB default:** For 100 GB file:
   - At 128 MB: 800 blocks → 800 mappers, but Namenode holds 2× more metadata.
   - At 256 MB: 400 blocks → 400 mappers, Namenode holds half the metadata.

**Conclusion:** For genomic data where Volume is dominant and access is sequential, a larger block size (256 MB or even 512 MB) is a justified design choice. For mixed workloads with many small files (web logs, IoT events), the default 128 MB or even 64 MB is preferable to maintain parallelism.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Fault Tolerance and Availability Trade-off* [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A cluster has individual DataNode availability $p = 0.99$ (i.e., 1% of nodes are down at any moment). Compare two replica configurations ($R=2$ and $R=3$) by calculating:
1. Probability of complete data loss for each configuration.
2. Effective cluster availability.
3. Storage efficiency in each case.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how **Rack Awareness** in HDFS complements the replication factor to provide fault tolerance. Draw a block diagram showing rack-aware placement of 3 replicas across 2 racks.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Probability of Data Loss (All R Replicas Fail)**

Failure probability of a single node = $1 - p = 0.01$

**Configuration R = 2:**

$$
P_{loss}(R=2) = (0.01)^2 = 0.0001 = 0.01\%
$$

**Configuration R = 3:**

$$
P_{loss}(R=3) = (0.01)^3 = 0.000001 = 0.0001\%
$$

**Step 2: Cluster Block Availability**

**Configuration R = 2:**

$$
A(R=2) = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999 = 99.99\%
$$

**Configuration R = 3:**

$$
A(R=3) = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999 = 99.9999\%
$$

**Step 3: Storage Efficiency $\eta = 1/R$**

| Configuration | $P_{loss}$ | Availability | $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R = 2$ | 0.01% | 99.99% | 50% |
| $R = 3$ | 0.0001% | 99.9999% | 33.33% |

**Conclusion:** $R=3$ offers **100× better data-loss protection** and **one extra nine of availability**, but consumes **50% more storage** than $R=2$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Rack Awareness Explanation:**

Rack Awareness is a HDFS feature where the Namenode uses a script (typically `topology.script.file.name`) to determine the **rack ID** of each DataNode. With this knowledge, the Namenode implements a **smart replica placement policy**:

**Default Replica Placement Policy (R = 3):**

1. **Replica 1** → Placed on the **local node** of the writer (or a random node if the writer is outside the cluster).
2. **Replica 2** → Placed on a **different rack** from Replica 1.
3. **Replica 3** → Placed on the **same rack as Replica 2** but on a **different node**.

**Why This Matters:**

- If the **local rack switch fails**, Replica 1 may become inaccessible, but Replicas 2 and 3 (on a different rack) remain available.
- If a **single DataNode fails**, at least 2 of the 3 replicas are still readable.
- **Network traffic** is minimized: writes use local rack for 2 replicas, only 1 replica traverses inter-rack link.

**Block Diagram of Rack-Aware Placement:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Client[Client Writes Block] --> N1[Replica 1 on Node A1 in Rack 1]
    N1 --> N2[Replica 2 on Node B1 in Rack 2]
    N2 --> N3[Replica 3 on Node B2 in Rack 2]

    subgraph R1[Rack 1]
        A1[DataNode A1]
        A2[DataNode A2]
    end

    subgraph R2[Rack 2]
        B1[DataNode B1]
        B2[DataNode B2]
        B3[DataNode B3]
    end

    Client -.->|Local Write| A1
    N1 -.->|Inter-Rack Copy| B1
    N2 -.->|Intra-Rack Copy| B2
```

**Engineering Outcome:** HDFS with $R=3$ and Rack Awareness can tolerate:
- Any **1 rack switch failure** (replicas exist in 2 racks).
- Any **1 individual DataNode failure** (2 replicas remain).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in $N_{blocks} = \lceil F/B \rceil$ — HDFS never stores partial blocks. If you write $F/B$ directly, you will lose **1 mark** in part (a) of every sizing question.
>
> 2. **Using 200 bytes per block but forgetting to multiply by replication factor** — The Namenode stores **per-replica** metadata. Examiners specifically check whether you multiplied by $R$ in memory calculations.
>
> 3. **Writing replication factor as 2 by default** — Hadoop's default is **3**, not 2. Writing 2 will lose **0.5 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing availability formula direction** — If $p$ is the *uptime probability*, then $1-p$ is the failure probability. Many students invert this and get availability < 0%, which is mathematically impossible.
>
> 5. **Drawing HDFS diagrams without distinguishing racks** — A flat list of DataNodes is worth **0 marks** for the rack-awareness question. You **must** show at least 2 racks as separate containers.
>
> 6. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — Always write MB, GB, TB explicitly. Unit-less numbers lose **0.5 mark** in KTU valuation.
>
> 7. **Not justifying the choice of design parameters** — When asked "why 256 MB block size?", simply stating "because files are large" is incomplete. You must mention the **trade-off** between Namenode load and parallelism.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist: Design Factors (Module 3)**

- ⭐ **5V Model:** Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value — these are the **upstream drivers** of all design decisions.
- ⭐ **Block Size Default:** **128 MB** in Hadoop 3.x. Increase for large sequential files; decrease for many small files to improve parallelism.
- ⭐ **Replication Factor Default:** **3**. HDFS guarantees this is maintained at all times; under-replicated blocks trigger re-replication.
- ⭐ **Total Storage Formula:** $S_{total} = F \times R$. For 1 TB logical with $R=3$, cluster reserves **3 TB**.
- ⭐ **Namenode Memory Formula:** $M_{NN} = N_{blocks} \times R \times 200$ bytes. Always multiply by replication for accurate sizing.
- ⭐ **Rack Awareness Policy (R=3):** 1st replica local, 2nd replica different rack, 3rd replica same rack as 2nd but different node.
- ⭐ **Heartbeat Interval:** 3 seconds (DataNode → Namenode). Timeout: 10 minutes by default.
- ⭐ **Data Locality Principle:** Move **computation to data**, not data to computation. Saves 10–100× network bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Availability Formula:** $A = 1 - (1-p)^R$ where $p$ is node uptime probability. $R=3$ gives "five nines" (99.999%).
- ⭐ **Storage Efficiency:** $\eta = 1/R$. For $R=3$, only **33%** of cluster capacity is user-addressable; 66% is replication overhead.
- ⭐ **CAP Theorem (Big Data context):** Distributed systems trade between Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance. HDFS chooses **Availability + Partition tolerance**, with eventual consistency.
- ⭐ **Cost vs. Reliability Trade-off:** Higher $R$ = better fault tolerance but more storage cost. Banks choose $R=3$; temporary log data may use $R=2$.
- ⭐ **Scalability Strategy:** When Namenode heap exceeds **64 GB**, enable **HDFS Federation** to mount multiple namespaces.
- ⭐ **File Format Choice:** Use **Parquet/ORC** for analytical column-scan workloads; use **Avro** for row-based write-heavy streaming; use **SequenceFile** for MapReduce intermediate output.
- ⭐ **MTBF Reality:** Commodity hardware has MTBF of ~3 years for individual disks, but cluster-level MTBF (for *any* node failure) is days. Hence replication is mandatory.
- ⭐ **KTU Module 3 Mapping:** Design Factors $\rightarrow$ HDFS Architecture $\rightarrow$ Data Read/Write flows $\rightarrow$ Namenode/Datanode internals.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Database Programming Model: Hadoop and MapReduce

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hadoop** is an open-source, distributed, fault-tolerant software framework written in **Java** that enables the storage (via **HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System**) and the batch processing (via the **MapReduce** programming model) of extremely large datasets (petabytes/exabytes) across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming paradigms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MapReduce** is a functional, divide-and-conquer programming model proposed by Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004) that abstracts parallel distributed computation over massive datasets into two primary user-defined functions: a **Mapper** function (which processes input key-value pairs to emit intermediate key-value pairs) and a **Reducer** function (which merges/aggregates all intermediate values associated with the same intermediate key).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian asked to count the number of times every distinct word appears across **10,000 boxes of books** delivered to a warehouse:

- **HDFS** is the warehouse shelving system: instead of placing all 10,000 boxes in one giant room (single machine), you scatter them across many smaller rooms (DataNodes) and write a **manifest** (NameNode) that says *"Box #537 is in Room 12, Shelf B"*.
- **MapReduce** is the workforce strategy: you hire 100 librarians (**Mappers**), each given one pile of pages. Every librarian reads their pages and writes a quick note like *("Hadoop", 1), ("Data", 1)*. Then another set of librarians (**Reducers**) gathers all notes about the same word from across the warehouse and totals them up: *("Hadoop", 4,562), ("Data", 8,901)*.

If one librarian calls in sick, you simply re-assign their pile — this is **fault tolerance**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Default Value (KTU-referenced) |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Size (HDFS)** | Smallest unit of read/write | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) |
| **Replication Factor** | Number of copies maintained | **3** |
| **NameNode** | Master metadata server | 1 per cluster (active) |
| **DataNode** | Slave storage node | Many (commodity) |
| **Mapper** | User function emitting K/V pairs | One per split |
| **Reducer** | User function aggregating values | Configurable (default 1) |
| **JobTracker** | Master for MapReduce jobs | Hadoop 1.x only |
| **TaskTracker** | Slave running map/reduce tasks | Hadoop 1.x only |
| **YARN** | Yet Another Resource Negotiator | Hadoop 2.x+ replacement |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HDFS Architecture — The Storage Layer

HDFS follows a **master/slave** topology:

- **NameNode (Master)**: Maintains the **file system namespace** (directory tree, file-to-block mapping, block locations) in RAM and persists it to disk via **FsImage** and the **EditLog**. The NameNode is the single point of metadata truth.
- **DataNodes (Slaves)**: Store the actual **data blocks**. Periodically send a **heartbeat** (every 3 seconds) and a **BlockReport** (every 6 hours by default) to the NameNode. There are typically hundreds of DataNodes per cluster.
- **Secondary NameNode**: Despite its name, it does **NOT** act as a hot standby. It periodically merges the EditLog with FsImage to prevent the EditLog from growing unbounded (a process called **checkpointing**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication & Rack Awareness**: Hadoop's default **replication factor is 3**. The **Rack-Aware Placement Policy** places:
> - **1 replica** on a node in the **local rack** (different node)
> - **1 replica** on a **different node** in the **same rack**
> - **1 replica** on a node in a **different rack**
>
> This guarantees **at least 2 racks** are involved, providing both fault tolerance and cross-rack bandwidth optimization.

## 2.2 MapReduce Programming Model — The Analysis Layer

A MapReduce job executes the following **sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: InputSplit & RecordReader
- The input file is logically divided into **InputSplits** (default 1 per HDFS block).
- A **RecordReader** converts raw bytes into logical `<key, value>` records.
- For text files: `key = byte offset (LongWritable)`, `value = line of text (Text)`.

### Phase 2: Map Phase
- The user-defined `map(k1, v1) → List<k2, v2>` is invoked once per record.
- Outputs are buffered in a **circular memory buffer** (default **100 MB**).

### Phase 3: Spill (Combiner + Partition)
- When the buffer reaches a threshold (default **80%**), background threads **spill** to disk.
- Before spilling: data is **partitioned** (default: `hash(key) mod R`, where R = number of reducers) and **sorted**.
- A **Combiner** (an optional mini-reducer) may run on each partition to reduce data shipped across the network.
- Spill files are **merged** into a single sorted, partitioned file.

### Phase 4: Shuffle & Sort
- Each Reducer **fetches** its partition from every Mapper's output via **HTTP**.
- All fetched segments are **merge-sorted** so that all values for the same key arrive contiguously at the Reducer.

### Phase 5: Reduce Phase
- The user-defined `reduce(k2, List<v2>) → List<v3>` is called once per distinct `k2`.
- Output is written directly to **HDFS** as the final result (no intermediate Reducer-to-Reducer communication).

### Phase 6: OutputFormat & RecordWriter
- Writes `<k3, v3>` pairs back to HDFS in the desired format (e.g., `TextOutputFormat`).

## 2.3 Fault Tolerance Mechanisms

| Failure Type | Hadoop Response |
|---|---|
| **DataNode failure** | NameNode marks node *dead*; re-replicates blocks from other replicas to maintain factor of 3 |
| **NameNode failure** | Critical — recovered from **FsImage + EditLog** checkpoint via Secondary NameNode |
| **Mapper task failure** | JobTracker/YARN AppMaster re-schedules the task on another node |
| **Reducer task failure** | Same — task is re-executed; completed map outputs on the failed node are recomputed |
| **Slow task (straggler)** | **Speculative execution** launches a duplicate copy of the slow task; whichever finishes first wins |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready** and frequently appear in KTU numericals.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HDFS Total Raw Storage | $S_{raw} = N \times B \times R$ | Bytes | N = DataNodes, B = Block size, R = Replication factor |
| 2 | Usable Logical Storage | $S_{logical} = N \times B$ | Bytes | Dedup of replication |
| 3 | Number of Mappers | $M = \left\lceil \dfrac{D_{input}}{S_{split}} \right\rceil$ | Integer | $D_{input}$ = input size; $S_{split}$ = split size |
| 4 | Data Locality Fraction | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{local}}{M}$ | Ratio 0..1 | Ideal $\eta = 1$ |
| 5 | Network Traffic (Shuffle) | $T_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} O_i$ | Bytes | O_i = output of mapper i after combiner |
| 6 | MapReduce Job Wall Time | $T_{job} \approx T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}$ | Seconds | Overlap possible with YARN |
| 7 | Combiner Reduction Ratio | $\rho_c = 1 - \dfrac{O_{post}}{O_{pre}}$ | Ratio 0..1 | Higher = better data reduction |
| 8 | Speculative Task SLA | $T_{spec} = \alpha \cdot T_{median}$ | Seconds | $\alpha$ typically 1.0–1.5 |

> **Engineering Utility**: This storage/processing model powers **enterprise data lakes** at companies like Yahoo, Facebook, and Netflix for log analytics, recommendation engines, and ETL pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — HDFS Storage Consumption with Replication

**Problem Setup:** A Hadoop cluster has $N = 50$ DataNodes. Each DataNode hosts $d = 8$ disks of capacity $C = 4 \text{ TB}$ each. The HDFS block size is $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ and the replication factor is $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute total raw physical storage in the cluster.**

$$
S_{physical} = N \times d \times C
$$

$$
S_{physical} = 50 \times 8 \times 4 \text{ TB} = 1600 \text{ TB} = 1.6 \text{ PB}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of distinct HDFS blocks in 1 PB of user data.**

$$
D_{input} = 1 \text{ PB} = 1 \times 1024 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
n_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{D_{input}}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1{,}048{,}576}{128} \right\rceil = 8{,}192 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the on-disk footprint after replication.**

$$
S_{raw} = n_{blocks} \times B \times R
$$

$$
S_{raw} = 8{,}192 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 3{,}145{,}728 \text{ MB} \approx 3 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify replication does not exceed cluster capacity.**

Since the cluster holds $1.6 \text{ PB} = 1600 \text{ TB}$ of raw capacity, the 1 PB dataset consumes only:

$$
\text{Capacity Utilization} = \frac{3 \text{ TB}}{1600 \text{ TB}} \times 100\% = 0.1875\%
$$

**Conclusion:** With $R = 3$, every 1 PB of logical user data consumes 3 PB of physical storage, but the cluster is dimensioned to absorb this.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Number of Mappers from Input Split

**Problem Setup:** A log file of size $D = 2.5 \text{ TB}$ is loaded into HDFS with block size $B = 128 \text{ MB}$. By default, each HDFS block becomes **one InputSplit**, so each block spawns **one Mapper task**.

**Step 1 — Convert input size to MB.**

$$
D_{MB} = 2.5 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 2{,}621{,}440 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the mapper-count formula.**

$$
M = \left\lceil \frac{D_{MB}}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}621{,}440}{128} \right\rceil
$$

$$
M = \left\lceil 20{,}480 \right\rceil = 20{,}480 \text{ Mappers}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify via raw byte math.**

$$
\text{MBytes per block} = 128 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 134{,}217{,}728 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{Input bytes} = 2.5 \times 1024^4 = 2{,}748{,}779{,}069{,}440 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
M = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}748{,}779{,}069{,}440}{134{,}217{,}728} \right\rceil = 20{,}480 \text{ Mappers} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The job will launch **20,480 parallel Mapper tasks** across the cluster (subject to cluster slot capacity).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Network Bandwidth Saved by a Combiner

**Problem Setup:** A WordCount job produces 10 TB of raw Mapper output. A Combiner (local mini-reducer) is applied and reduces the data by a factor of $\rho_c = 0.75$ (i.e., 75% reduction). The cluster has $R = 200$ Reducers and the network bandwidth is $1 \text{ Gbps}$ per node.

**Step 1 — Output volume before combiner.**

$$
O_{pre} = 10 \text{ TB} = 10 \times 8 \times 10^{12} \text{ bits} = 8 \times 10^{13} \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Output volume after combiner.**

$$
O_{post} = O_{pre} \times (1 - \rho_c) = 10 \text{ TB} \times 0.25 = 2.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 3 — Network traffic per Reducer (assuming uniform distribution).**

$$
T_{perReducer} = \frac{O_{post}}{R} = \frac{2.5 \text{ TB}}{200} = 12.5 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to transfer per Reducer over 1 Gbps link.**

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{12.5 \text{ GB} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{10^9 \text{ bits/s}} = 100 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to the no-combiner scenario.**

$$
T_{noCombiner} = \frac{50 \text{ GB} \times 8}{10^9} = 400 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute bandwidth saved.**

$$
\Delta T = 400 - 100 = 300 \text{ seconds} \quad \text{(75% reduction in shuffle time)}
$$

**Conclusion:** A well-designed Combiner can reduce shuffle traffic by **up to an order of magnitude**, often the single most impactful MapReduce optimization.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use / Not Use MapReduce

| Criterion | Use MapReduce | Avoid MapReduce |
|---|---|---|
| Data volume | Terabytes to petabytes | Megabytes to a few GB |
| Processing model | Batch, embarrassingly parallel | Real-time, low-latency |
| Iterations | Few passes (1–2 ideal) | Many iterative passes (use Spark) |
| Schema | Schema-on-read friendly | Rigid SQL transactions needed |
| Example use cases | Log aggregation, indexing, ETL, batch ML training | Online transaction processing (OLTP) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Master-Slave Architecture (Rack-Aware)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Client[Client Application] --> NN[NameNode Master]
    NN --> SNN[Secondary NameNode]
    NN --> FSImage[FsImage + EditLog]
    NN --> DN1R1[DataNode Rack1 Node1]
    NN --> DN2R1[DataNode Rack1 Node2]
    NN --> DN3R1[DataNode Rack1 Node3]
    NN --> DN1R2[DataNode Rack2 Node1]
    NN --> DN2R2[DataNode Rack2 Node2]
    NN --> DN3R2[DataNode Rack2 Node3]
    DN1R1 -. Block Replica 1 .-> DN1R1
    DN2R1 -. Block Replica 2 same rack .-> DN2R1
    DN3R2 -. Block Replica 3 different rack .-> DN3R2
```

**Reading the diagram:** A file with 3 replicas has one copy on a different node in the local rack, one on another node in the same rack, and one on a node in a separate rack.

## 4.2 MapReduce Complete Data Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input HDFS File] --> B[InputSplit 128MB]
    B --> C[RecordReader]
    C --> D[Mapper Function]
    D --> E[In Memory Buffer 100MB]
    E --> F{80 percent threshold}
    F -->|Yes| G[Spill to Disk]
    G --> H[Partition Sort]
    H --> I[Combiner Optional]
    I --> J[Merged Spill Files]
    J --> K[Shuffle HTTP Fetch]
    K --> L[Merge Sort]
    L --> M[Reducer Function]
    M --> N[Output HDFS Files]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Data flows left-to-right from HDFS input through the Map, Shuffle, and Reduce phases, finally writing structured output back to HDFS.

## 4.3 Hadoop 1.x JobTracker / TaskTracker Master-Slave

```mermaid
graph TD
    ClientA[Client] --> JT[JobTracker Master]
    JT --> TT1[TaskTracker Slave 1]
    JT --> TT2[TaskTracker Slave 2]
    JT --> TT3[TaskTracker Slave 3]
    TT1 --> MapTask1[Map Task Slot]
    TT1 --> ReduceTask1[Reduce Task Slot]
    TT2 --> MapTask2[Map Task Slot]
    TT2 --> ReduceTask2[Reduce Task Slot]
    TT3 --> MapTask3[Map Task Slot]
    TT3 --> ReduceTask3[Reduce Task Slot]
    TT1 -. Heartbeat every 3 sec .-> JT
    TT2 -. Heartbeat every 3 sec .-> JT
    TT3 -. Heartbeat every 3 sec .-> JT
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **Hadoop 2.x+**, the JobTracker/TaskTracker pair is replaced by the **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** architecture: a global **ResourceManager** + per-application **ApplicationMaster** + per-node **NodeManager** triad, decoupling resource management from job scheduling.

## 4.4 MapReduce WordCount Logical Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input1[Hello World] --> M1
    Input2[Hello Hadoop] --> M2
    Input3[Goodbye World] --> M3
    M1[Mapper 1] --> A1[Hadoop 1]
    M1 --> B1[World 1]
    M2 --> A2[Hadoop 1]
    M2 --> C1[Hello 1]
    M3 --> B2[World 1]
    M3 --> D1[Goodbye 1]
    A1 --> R1[Reducer Hadoop]
    A2 --> R1
    B1 --> R2[Reducer World]
    B2 --> R2
    C1 --> R3[Reducer Hello]
    D1 --> R4[Reducer Goodbye]
    R1 --> Out1[Hadoop 2]
    R2 --> Out2[World 2]
    R3 --> Out3[Hello 1]
    R4 --> Out4[Goodbye 1]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each line of input is mapped to `(word, 1)` pairs. The framework groups identical keys, and Reducers sum the counts.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q1.** Differentiate between **HDFS** and **MapReduce** in Hadoop. Why are they considered complementary components?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** is the **storage layer** that splits large files into blocks (default **128 MB**) and stores them redundantly (default replication factor **3**) across commodity DataNodes, managed by a central NameNode. *(1 mark)*
- **MapReduce** is the **processing layer** — a programming model with two functions (`map()` and `reduce()`) that performs parallel, distributed computation on data resident in HDFS. *(1 mark)*
- They are **complementary**: HDFS provides fault-tolerant, scalable storage; MapReduce leverages data locality (running computation on the node where data resides) to process that storage efficiently. Together they form a complete **storage + analysis** platform. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** What is a **Combiner** in MapReduce? When is it safe to use?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Combiner** is an **optional, user-defined function** that runs on the output of a Mapper **on the same node**, performing a local aggregation (mini-reduce) to shrink the volume of data shuffled to Reducers. *(1.5 marks)*
- It is **safe to use** when the reducer function is **commutative and associative** (e.g., `SUM`, `MAX`, `MIN`). It is **not safe** for operations like `MEAN` or `MEDIAN` where intermediate aggregation alters the result. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B pattern: any **one** of two alternatives per slot, each 14 marks with two 7-mark sub-parts.

### ⭐ OPTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Slot Q11a]

> **Q.A (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS architecture**. Discuss the roles of NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Architecture Overview** *(2 marks)* — HDFS follows a **master/slave** model. The **NameNode** is the master; **DataNodes** (typically hundreds) are slaves. A **Secondary NameNode** runs alongside for checkpointing.
2. **NameNode** *(2 marks)* — Stores **metadata** in RAM: the filesystem tree, the mapping of files → blocks, and the locations of block replicas. Persists via **FsImage** (snapshot) and **EditLog** (transaction log).
3. **DataNode** *(1.5 marks)* — Stores the actual **data blocks**; sends a **heartbeat** every 3 seconds and a **BlockReport** every 6 hours to the NameNode. Performs block read/write on client request.
4. **Secondary NameNode** *(1.5 marks)* — Periodically downloads FsImage + EditLog from the NameNode, merges them into a new FsImage, and uploads it back. **Does NOT** act as a hot standby.

(Diagram: Master-Slave architecture with NameNode at top, DataNodes in racks, and Secondary NameNode as checkpointing assistant — refer to Section 4.1.)

---

> **Q.A (b)** Explain the complete **MapReduce execution flow** with a WordCount example. Identify each phase and the data structures involved. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Job Submission & Initialization** *(1 mark)* — `JobClient` submits the job to `JobTracker` which copies resources (JAR, config) to HDFS and schedules tasks.
2. **InputSplit & RecordReader** *(1 mark)* — Input file is split (default = HDFS block size = **128 MB**). `RecordReader` yields `<LongWritable, Text>` pairs.
3. **Map Phase** *(1.5 marks)* — `map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)` emits intermediate pairs, e.g., `("Hadoop", 1)`. For WordCount: split line on whitespace and emit `(word, 1)`.
4. **Shuffle & Sort** *(1.5 marks)* — Mappers spill to disk when buffer is 80% full; data is partitioned by `hash(key) % R` and merge-sorted. Reducers fetch their partition over HTTP.
5. **Reduce Phase** *(1 mark)* — `reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)` sums all values for each key, e.g., `("Hadoop", 42)`.
6. **Output Writing** *(1 mark)* — `RecordWriter` writes final `<key, value>` pairs to HDFS using the configured `OutputFormat` (e.g., `TextOutputFormat`).

(Logical flow diagram: see Section 4.4.)

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Slot Q11b]

> **Q.B (a)** Discuss the **fault tolerance mechanisms** in Hadoop. How does HDFS handle DataNode failure, and how does MapReduce handle task failure? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **HDFS DataNode Failure** *(2 marks)* — When the NameNode stops receiving heartbeats from a DataNode for **>10 minutes** (timeout), it marks the node *dead*. It then consults its block-replica map and instructs other DataNodes to **re-replicate** the under-replicated blocks to maintain the factor of 3.
2. **NameNode Failure Recovery** *(1.5 marks)* — The NameNode persists FsImage + EditLog. On failure, the cluster is restarted using the **last checkpoint** (merged by the Secondary NameNode) plus the remaining EditLog tail.
3. **MapReduce Task Failure** *(1.5 marks)* — `JobTracker` (or YARN `ApplicationMaster`) detects failure via missed heartbeats. The failed task is **rescheduled** on a different node. Completed map outputs on the failed node are *discarded* (lost) and must be re-executed.
4. **Speculative Execution** *(1 mark)* — If a task runs significantly slower than the median (`T > α · T_median`), Hadoop launches a duplicate "speculative" copy. The first to finish wins; the other is killed. This mitigates the **straggler problem**.
5. **Rack Awareness** *(1 mark)* — Ensures replicas span ≥ 2 racks, so a single rack switch failure cannot lose all copies of a block.

---

> **Q.B (b)** A Hadoop cluster has **40 DataNodes**, each with **6 TB** of usable disk. The HDFS block size is **128 MB** and the replication factor is **3**. A user uploads a **500 GB** log file. Calculate:
> - (i) The number of HDFS blocks generated.
> - (ii) The total physical storage consumed by this file.
> - (iii) The cluster-wide capacity utilization percentage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of HDFS blocks** *(2 marks)*

$$
D_{MB} = 500 \times 1024 = 512{,}000 \text{ MB}
$$

$$
n_{blocks} = \left\lceil \frac{512{,}000}{128} \right\rceil = 4{,}000 \text{ blocks}
$$

**(ii) Total physical storage consumed** *(2.5 marks)*

$$
S_{raw} = n_{blocks} \times B \times R = 4{,}000 \times 128 \text{ MB} \times 3
$$

$$
S_{raw} = 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ MB} \approx 1.5 \text{ TB}
$$

**(iii) Cluster-wide capacity utilization** *(2.5 marks)*

$$
S_{cluster} = 40 \times 6 \text{ TB} = 240 \text{ TB}
$$

$$
\text{Utilization} = \frac{1.5 \text{ TB}}{240 \text{ TB}} \times 100\% = 0.625\%
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) 4,000 blocks, (ii) 1.5 TB, (iii) 0.625 %.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss traps in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing NameNode with Secondary NameNode** — Many students wrongly state the Secondary NameNode is a "backup NameNode that takes over on failure." It is **NOT** — it only performs **checkpointing** (merging FsImage + EditLog). The hot-standby concept is provided by **HA (High Availability) NameNodes** in Hadoop 2.x+.
> 2. **Wrong Combiner classification** — A Combiner is **NOT** a separate phase; it runs *during* the Mapper's spill stage. Do not list it as a sixth phase of MapReduce.
> 3. **Skipping the shuffle/sort cost in numericals** — When asked for job wall time, students often sum only map+reduce and **omit shuffle time** — typically worth 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Rack-aware replication default** — State all **three replica locations** explicitly in HDFS questions; merely saying "3 copies" loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mapper count formula** — Use **ceiling** (`⌈ ⌉`), not floor; an extra partial block still spawns a mapper.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Hadoop = HDFS (storage) + MapReduce (processing) + YARN (resource management, 2.x+).**
- ✅ **HDFS block size = 128 MB** (default since Hadoop 2.x); **replication factor = 3** (default).
- ✅ **Rack-Aware Policy**: 1 local-rack replica (different node), 1 same-rack replica, 1 different-rack replica.
- ✅ **NameNode** stores **metadata only**; **DataNodes** store **actual data blocks**.
- ✅ **Secondary NameNode ≠ Backup NameNode** — it is a **checkpointing assistant**.
- ✅ **MapReduce phases**: InputSplit → RecordReader → **Map** → Spill (Partition+Sort+Combiner) → **Shuffle & Sort** → **Reduce** → OutputFormat.
- ✅ **Mapper count** $M = \lceil D_{input} / S_{split} \rceil$.
- ✅ **Combiner** is optional, runs on Mapper output, requires **commutative + associative** reducer logic.
- ✅ **Fault tolerance**: re-replication (HDFS), task re-execution & speculative execution (MapReduce).
- ✅ **Hadoop 1.x**: JobTracker/TaskTracker. **Hadoop 2.x+**: ResourceManager/NodeManager/ApplicationMaster (YARN).
- ✅ **Heartbeat interval ≈ 3 s**; **BlockReport interval ≈ 6 h**; **DataNode timeout ≈ 10 min**.
- ✅ **Formulas to memorize**:
  - $S_{raw} = n_{blocks} \times B \times R$
  - $M = \lceil D_{input} / S_{split} \rceil$
  - $T_{job} \approx T_{map} + T_{shuffle} + T_{reduce}$
  - $\rho_c = 1 - O_{post} / O_{pre}$
- ✅ **When NOT to use MapReduce**: low-latency real-time, iterative ML, small datasets (use Spark / RDBMS instead).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison with Other Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison with Other Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Big Data Analytics**, *Comparison with Other Systems* refers to the systematic, criterion-based analytical evaluation of **distributed, scalable data management and processing frameworks** (such as **Hadoop HDFS, HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, and Apache Spark**) against one another and against **legacy centralized systems** (such as **Relational Database Management Systems — RDBMS**). This evaluation is performed across the dimensions defined by **Brewer's CAP Theorem**, the **ACID vs BASE consistency paradigms**, the underlying **data models** (key-value, columnar, document, graph), the **horizontal scalability** profile, the **fault-tolerance strategy**, and the **latency-throughput** characteristics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme High-Yield Definition:**
> A *comparative analysis* of Big Data systems is a structured benchmarking exercise that quantifies trade-offs in **Consistency (C)**, **Availability (A)**, and **Partition Tolerance (P)** for distributed data stores, alongside the qualitative differences in **schema flexibility, query model, and consistency model** relative to traditional RDBMS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library System"

Imagine a small town library that fits inside a single building:

- A librarian knows exactly where every book is → **Traditional RDBMS**.
- Every book has a strict catalog card (schema), the data is highly consistent, and you can find any book in seconds — but the building can only hold **10,000 books** and one entrance door (single point of failure).

Now imagine a **nationwide library chain** that must serve **100 million books** and survive an earthquake in one city:

- Each city branch holds a *subset* of the catalog (sharding) → **NoSQL/Distributed Systems**.
- Some branches may temporarily disagree on whether a book is available (eventual consistency) → **BASE properties**.
- The system is designed to keep serving readers even if some branches go offline → **Partition Tolerance (P)**.
- Different branches are organized differently: some by author (columnar), some by genre tags (document), some by borrower ID (key-value).

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off is **inevitable and mathematically proven by the CAP Theorem**: in a distributed system facing a network partition, the architect must choose between **strict consistency** and **continuous availability** — you cannot maximize all three simultaneously.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Comparison (KTU-Standard)

The following quantitative and qualitative metrics form the **canonical comparison axes** in KTU's Big Data Analytics examination:

- **CAP Properties:** Consistency (C), Availability (A), Partition Tolerance (P)
- **Consistency Model:** Strong / Eventual / Causal / Read-your-writes
- **Data Model:** Relational, Key-Value, Column-Family, Document, Graph
- **Query Language:** SQL / CQL / MapReduce / Spark SQL
- **Latency Class:** OLTP-grade (milliseconds, **<10 ms**) vs OLAP-grade (seconds to minutes, **10²–10⁴ ms**)
- **Throughput Class:** Low (hundreds of ops/sec) vs High (millions of ops/sec)
- **Failure Recovery:** Single-master failover vs masterless replication
- **Scaling Pattern:** Vertical (scale-up) vs Horizontal (scale-out)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Trade-off Theorem (Brewer, 2000):**
> In any asynchronous distributed system subject to network partition, it is impossible to simultaneously guarantee all three of {**C, A, P**}. At most **two** can be satisfied. This is the **mathematical foundation** of every "Comparison with Other Systems" table.
