# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CAP Theorem — The Heart of Every Comparison

The CAP Theorem, proposed by **Eric Brewer in 2000** and formally proven by **Gilbert and Lynch in 2002**, states that a distributed data store can simultaneously provide **at most two** of the following three guarantees:

| Property | Symbol | Formal Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Consistency** | C | Every read receives the most recent write *or* an error |
| **Availability** | A | Every request receives a non-error response (no timeout) |
| **Partition Tolerance** | P | The system continues operating despite arbitrary network message loss between nodes |

### 2.1.1 The Three Strategic Archetypes

1. **CA Systems** (Consistency + Availability): Achievable *only* when there is *no* network partition — practically limited to single-node or tightly-clustered systems. **Example: Traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL, Oracle) running in a non-distributed configuration.**
2. **CP Systems** (Consistency + Partition Tolerance): Sacrifice availability during partitions to preserve strong consistency. **Examples: HBase, MongoDB (with majority write concern), Redis (cluster mode).**
3. **AP Systems** (Availability + Partition Tolerance): Sacrifice strict consistency for continuous uptime; conflicts are resolved after the partition heals. **Examples: Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB.**

## 2.2 ACID vs BASE — The Consistency Paradigms

### 2.2.1 ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)

The four guarantees provided by classical RDBMS, ensuring **strict transactional integrity**:

- **Atomicity:** A transaction either completes entirely or has no effect at all.
  $$ \text{Transaction } T: \text{commit}(T) \iff \forall \, \text{operations } o_i \in T, \text{ all } o_i \text{ applied} $$
- **Consistency:** Every committed transaction transitions the database from one *valid state* to another, preserving all declared invariants.
- **Isolation:** Concurrent transactions execute as if they were serial — their intermediate states are invisible to one another.
  $$ \text{Serializable Schedule} \iff \text{equivalent to some serial order of } T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n $$
- **Durability:** Once a transaction commits, its effects survive any subsequent failure (power loss, crash, etc.).

### 2.2.2 BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent)

The relaxed guarantees of modern distributed NoSQL systems, optimized for **horizontal scalability and partition tolerance**:

- **Basically Available:** The system guarantees availability — it *responds* to every request, even if the response may be stale.
- **Soft State:** The state of the system may change over time *even without input*, due to eventual-consistency reconciliation processes running in the background.
- **Eventual Consistency:** In the absence of new writes and after a bounded time interval, all replicas converge to the same value.
  $$ \lim_{t \to \infty} P(\text{replica}_i(t) = \text{replica}_j(t)) = 1 \quad \forall \, i, j \in \text{cluster} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Insight:** ACID is the *default* in financial systems (banking ledgers, payment gateways) because monetary values must never be lost or double-counted. BASE is the *default* in social media feeds, IoT telemetry, and recommendation engines where the cost of temporary inconsistency is far lower than the cost of system unavailability.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Comparison Metrics

| # | Metric / Formula | Symbol | Typical Unit / Value | System Where Prominent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CAP Combination** | $\text{CAP} \in \{CA, CP, AP\}$ | 2-of-3 trade-off | All distributed stores |
| 2 | **Eventual Consistency Convergence** | $\lim_{t \to T_c} \Delta(t) = 0$ | $T_c$ = convergence time | Cassandra, DynamoDB |
| 3 | **Replication Factor** | $RF = \frac{\text{copies stored}}{\text{logical record}}$ | Integer, $RF \geq 1$ | HDFS, HBase, Cassandra |
| 4 | **Horizontal Scalability Index** | $S_N = \frac{T_1}{T_N}$ | $S_N \leq N$ (ideal) | Hadoop, Cassandra |
| 5 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup Limit)** | $S(p) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | $f$ = parallel fraction, $p$ = processors | All MapReduce-class systems |
| 6 | **Strong Consistency Latency** | $L_{SC} = 2 \cdot RTT_{quorum}$ | milliseconds | CP systems (HBase) |
| 7 | **Eventual Consistency Latency** | $L_{EC} \approx 1 \cdot RTT_{local}$ | sub-millisecond | AP systems (Cassandra) |
| 8 | **Data Volume Ceiling** | $V_{max}$ | Petabytes ($\geq 10^{15}$ bytes) | HDFS, Cassandra |
| 9 | **Consistency Level (Cassandra)** | $CL \in \{\text{ONE, QUORUM, ALL}\}$ | quorum factor $Q = \lfloor RF/2 \rfloor + 1$ | Cassandra |
| 10 | **Schema Flexibility** | Binary: strict vs dynamic | enum | RDBMS (strict) vs MongoDB (dynamic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** In Mermaid diagrams below, **never use the pipe symbol `\|` inside table cells**; use `\vert` instead. The vertical pipe is a markdown table delimiter and breaks rendering. Similarly, in mathematical expressions use `\mid` for the *divides* relation and `\vert` for the *such that* / absolute value vertical bar.

## 2.4 Why This Comparison Matters in Real Engineering

In industrial Big Data deployments (e.g., Netflix, Uber, Facebook), the choice of storage system directly determines:

- **Cost of ownership** (commodity hardware vs high-end servers)
- **Time-to-insight** (latency for end-user-facing applications)
- **Operational risk** (downtime cost, data-loss exposure)
- **Team skill requirement** (SQL fluency vs Java/Python DSL fluency)

A wrong choice (e.g., using a CA RDBMS for a globally distributed write-heavy workload) leads to **catastrophic vertical-scaling cost** and **unrecoverable single points of failure**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a CA System Cannot Survive a Partition

**Step 1 — Formal Setup.**
A distributed system consists of $N$ nodes $\{n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_N\}$ that communicate by sending messages over a network. A *partition* is a network event that causes a subset of nodes to become mutually unreachable.

**Step 2 — Assume the System is CA.**
By definition, a CA system guarantees *both* Consistency (C) and Availability (A) under all conditions. This means:

- (A) Every request to any node returns a non-error response within bounded time.
- (C) Every read returns the result of the most recent committed write.

**Step 3 — Introduce a Partition.**
A network partition splits the cluster into two disjoint subsets $G_1$ and $G_2$, with $G_1 \cup G_2 = \{n_1, \ldots, n_N\}$ and $G_1 \cap G_2 = \emptyset$. A write request $w$ is dispatched to $G_1$; a read request $r$ is dispatched to $G_2$.

**Step 4 — Identify the Contradiction.**
To satisfy (A), $G_2$ must respond to $r$. But $G_2$ cannot receive the update from $w$ (partition blocks the message). Therefore, the read in $G_2$ must return a *stale* value. This violates (C).

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

$$ \text{CA} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{requires } \neg P $$

A CA system can exist *only* in a non-distributed, single-node, partition-free configuration. This is exactly why **traditional RDBMS** are deployed as **single-node or primary-replica clusters** with synchronous replication — they cannot tolerate a network split.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Hadoop vs Spark

**Step 1 — Amdahl's Law Statement.**

$$ S(p) = \frac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} $$

where $f$ is the fraction of the workload that is *parallelizable* and $p$ is the number of parallel processors (nodes).

**Step 2 — Hadoop MapReduce Profile.**
MapReduce is *disk-bound*: between Map and Reduce stages, data is **materialized to HDFS**. This means a substantial serial fraction $f_s$ arises from disk I/O and shuffle overhead.

$$ f_{\text{Hadoop, parallel}} \approx 0.65 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{\text{Hadoop, serial}} = 1 - 0.65 = 0.35 $$

**Step 3 — Compute Theoretical Speedup at $p = 100$ nodes.**

$$ S_{\text{Hadoop}}(100) = \frac{1}{0.35 + \dfrac{0.65}{100}} = \frac{1}{0.35 + 0.0065} = \frac{1}{0.3565} \approx 2.81 $$

**Step 4 — Apache Spark Profile.**
Spark keeps intermediate data **in-memory (RDD lineage)**, drastically reducing the serial I/O fraction.

$$ f_{\text{Spark, parallel}} \approx 0.95 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{\text{Spark, serial}} = 0.05 $$

**Step 5 — Compute Theoretical Speedup at $p = 100$ nodes.**

$$ S_{\text{Spark}}(100) = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{100}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0095} = \frac{1}{0.0595} \approx 16.81 $$

**Step 6 — Comparative Ratio.**

$$ \frac{S_{\text{Spark}}(100)}{S_{\text{Hadoop}}(100)} = \frac{16.81}{2.81} \approx 5.98 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Spark is theoretically **up to ~6× faster** than Hadoop MapReduce for the same workload at 100 nodes, *purely* due to the reduction of the serial fraction from disk I/O. This is the *quantitative* basis for Spark's dominance in iterative machine learning workloads (K-Means, PageRank, Logistic Regression), where the same dataset is reprocessed tens to hundreds of times.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Master Table (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

| **Comparison Axis** | **Traditional RDBMS (Oracle/PostgreSQL/MySQL)** | **HBase** | **Cassandra** | **MongoDB** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Model** | Relational (tables, rows, columns) | Wide-column (sparse, sorted) | Wide-column (BigTable-inspired) | Document (BSON/JSON) |
| **CAP Classification** | CA (single-node) / CP (sync replica) | **CP** | **AP** | **CP** (default) |
| **Consistency Model** | Strong (ACID) | Strong (per-row) | Eventual / Tunable (per-query) | Strong (with majority write concern) |
| **Query Language** | SQL (declarative, ANSI) | Java API / Phoenix SQL | CQL (SQL-like) | MQL (JSON-like) / Aggregation Pipeline |
| **Scaling Strategy** | **Vertical** (scale-up, bigger box) | **Horizontal** (RegionServers + HDFS) | **Horizontal** (masterless ring) | **Horizontal** (sharded replica sets) |
| **Master Node** | Single primary (S.P.O.F. in vanilla) | HMaster (with backup) | **None (masterless)** | mongod primary per shard |
| **Failure Recovery** | Manual failover / RAC | Automatic Zookeeper-coordinated | Gossip-based, no single point | Replica set election (10-30 sec) |
| **Schema** | **Rigid, predefined** | Schema-less within column families | Schema-less within tables | **Dynamic, schemaless** |
| **Best-Use Case** | OLTP banking, ERP, reporting | Random read/write on huge tables, time-series | IoT, write-heavy, multi-DC | Content management, catalogs, mobile apps |
| **Typical Latency** | 1–10 ms (local) | 5–50 ms | 1–10 ms (with CL=ONE) | 5–20 ms |
| **Data Volume Ceiling** | Terabytes ($10^{12}$ bytes) | Petabytes ($10^{15}$ bytes) | Petabytes+ | Petabytes |
| **Joins / Transactions** | **Full multi-table ACID joins** | Single-row only (no joins) | No joins (denormalize at write) | Limited multi-doc transactions (v4.0+) |
| **Indexing** | B-tree, hash, bitmap, full-text | Row-key only (primary index) | Primary key + secondary indexes | B-tree, geospatial, text |

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation: HBase vs Cassandra Decision

**Step 1 — Identify the Workload Character.**
A read-mostly analytical workload on time-series sensor data (10 billion readings/day) requires:
- Fast range scans over sorted row-keys (HBase strength).
- Strong consistency for "what was the temperature at time $t$?".

**Step 2 — Map to CAP Preferences.**
- A read-mostly workload that needs correctness prefers **CP** → HBase is the natural fit.
- A write-mostly workload from millions of devices with tolerance for stale reads prefers **AP** → Cassandra is the natural fit.

**Step 3 — Quantify the Trade-off.**

$$ \text{Cassandra write throughput at CL=ONE} \approx 10 \times \text{HBase write throughput} $$

$$ \text{HBase read-after-write latency at strong consistency} \approx 50\% \text{ lower than Cassandra at CL=QUORUM} $$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule.**

$$ \text{Choose HBase} \iff \frac{\text{Read Correctness Importance}}{\text{Write Throughput Requirement}} > 1 $$

$$ \text{Choose Cassandra} \iff \frac{\text{Write Throughput Requirement}}{\text{Read Correctness Importance}} > 1 $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** Uber's historical trip-log storage uses **Cassandra** (write-heavy, AP-acceptable) for ingest; the analytical dashboard layer reads from **HBase** (read-strong, CP-required) populated by a downstream Spark streaming job. This *polyglot persistence* pattern is the canonical production answer to "which is better — HBase or Cassandra?" The correct engineering response is: **both, for different layers**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — 3 Marks
**"Differentiate between ACID and BASE properties. State one real-world use case where each is preferred."**

**Model Answer (Model — 3 mark scheme):**

| ACID | BASE |
|---|---|
| **Atomic, Consistent, Isolated, Durable** transactions | **Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent** |
| Strong, synchronous, blocking | Weak, asynchronous, non-blocking |
| RDBMS — Oracle, PostgreSQL, MySQL | NoSQL — Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB |
| Use case: **Banking ledger** (a debit/credit must be atomic; partial application is unacceptable) | Use case: **Social media feed** (stale likes are acceptable; downtime is catastrophic) |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 3 Marks
**"With a neat sketch, explain the CAP theorem and classify HBase, Cassandra, and MongoDB."**

**Model Answer (Model — 3 mark sketch + classification):**

The CAP theorem is a triangle with vertices **C (Consistency)**, **A (Availability)**, and **P (Partition Tolerance)**. In any asynchronous distributed system facing a network partition, at most two of the three can be guaranteed simultaneously.

- **HBase → CP** (strong per-row consistency; sacrifices availability during a partition).
- **Cassandra → AP** (always available for writes; eventual consistency).
- **MongoDB → CP** (with default majority write concern; strong consistency within replica set).

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Compare the **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** with the **Google File System (GFS)** in terms of architecture, fault tolerance, data access pattern, and consistency model. Discuss the key design influences and engineering divergences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Apache HBase** with **Apache Cassandra** across the dimensions of CAP classification, data model, consistency model, master/slave topology, query language, and write throughput. Provide a justified recommendation for two contrasting real-world scenarios. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Architecture (2 marks):**
HDFS is a Java re-implementation of the GFS paper (Ghemawat et al., 2003). Both follow a **single-master, multi-chunk-server** topology. HDFS uses a **Namenode** (master) storing the filesystem metadata in RAM and **Datanodes** (chunkservers) holding 128 MB blocks. GFS uses a **Master** node and **Chunkservers** holding 64 MB chunks. The Namenode is the **single point of failure** in vanilla HDFS; this is mitigated in production by **Namenode HA** (Hot Standby) using Zookeeper-based leader election.

**Fault Tolerance (2 marks):**
Both systems rely on **replication** (default $RF = 3$). A block is considered "safely stored" only when the Namenode receives acknowledgment from at least $RF$ distinct Datanodes on distinct racks. The *re-replication* daemon continuously scans for under-replicated blocks and triggers background copy operations.

**Data Access Pattern (2 marks):**
Both are optimized for **sequential, append-only, write-once-read-many (WORM)** workloads. Random writes are not supported; mutations are implemented as full-block rewrite. The systems explicitly trade off POSIX semantics for **throughput at petabyte scale**.

**Consistency Model (1 mark):**
Both provide a *relaxed consistency* model: after a write, readers may see a *stale* value until replication completes. GFS uses a *lease* + mutation-order model; HDFS uses a similar *pipeline* of Datanodes for write propagation.

**Conclusion:** HDFS is essentially an open-source, Java-based *derivative* of GFS; the lineage is unmistakable and the comparison is *not* "which is better" but "which is the production deployment for a given cluster".

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

| Dimension | **HBase** | **Cassandra** |
|---|---|---|
| CAP | **CP** | **AP** |
| Data Model | BigTable wide-column, sorted by row-key | BigTable wide-column, partitioned by hash |
| Master | HMaster (with backup) | **None — fully masterless ring topology** |
| Consistency | Strong per-row (linearizable) | **Tunable per query** (ONE, QUORUM, ALL) |
| Query Language | Java API, Phoenix SQL | **CQL** (SQL-like) |
| Write Path | Write-Ahead Log + MemStore + HFile | Commit Log + Memtable + SSTable (LSM-tree) |
| Read Path | BlockCache + Bloom filters + HFile scan | Bloom filter + partition key index + SSTable merge |
| Write Throughput | ~10K–50K writes/sec/node | **~100K+ writes/sec/node** |
| Failure Domain | RegionServer crash → ~30s recovery | Gossip-based, no recovery delay |
| Single Point of Failure | HMaster (mitigated by HA) | **None** |

**Scenario 1 (HBase) — Banking transaction history lookup (7 marks distributed):** A bank needs to retrieve every transaction for a specific account ID with **strict consistency** ("no missing entries, no double-counts"). Account-ID is a natural row-key enabling fast range scan in time order. **HBase is the right choice.**

**Scenario 2 (Cassandra) — Global IoT sensor ingestion (7 marks distributed):** A fleet of 10 million sensors streams temperature readings every second across three continents. Occasional loss of a reading is tolerable; downtime of any regional cluster is not. **Cassandra's masterless multi-DC replication is the right choice.**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **CAP theorem** in detail. With a labelled diagram, classify the following systems: **HDFS, HBase, Cassandra, MongoDB, PostgreSQL (single-node), and DynamoDB**. Justify each classification with one architectural reason. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **MapReduce (Hadoop)** with **Apache Spark** in terms of execution model, intermediate data handling, fault tolerance, iterative workload performance, and latency class. State with justification which framework is preferred for (i) batch ETL on 500 TB of logs and (ii) iterative K-Means clustering on the same dataset. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks

**CAP Theorem Definition (2 marks):**
The CAP theorem (Brewer, 2000; formally proven by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002) states that a distributed data store can provide *at most two* of three guarantees: **Consistency**, **Availability**, **Partition Tolerance**.

**Labelled Diagram (1 mark):** A triangle with vertices C, A, P and the systems placed on the appropriate edges.

**Classifications (4 marks):**

- **HDFS → CP:** The Namenode coordinates all metadata; during a partition, a client cannot complete a write that requires Namenode acknowledgment. Strong consistency of file namespace is preserved.
- **HBase → CP:** RegionServers require Zookeeper coordination; strong per-row consistency; an unreachable Region during a partition causes read/write failure for that row-key range.
- **Cassandra → AP:** Masterless design; any node accepts writes; uses hinted handoff and read-repair to converge after a partition.
- **MongoDB → CP (default):** Primary node in a replica set must acknowledge writes by majority; a network split causes the minority side to become read-only.
- **PostgreSQL (single-node) → CA:** No network partition is possible (single node); provides full ACID.
- **DynamoDB → AP:** AWS-managed, always writable, uses vector clocks (or LWW) for convergence.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks

| Dimension | **Hadoop MapReduce** | **Apache Spark** |
|---|---|---|
| Execution Model | Two-stage: Map → Shuffle → Reduce | DAG of operators, lazy evaluation |
| Intermediate Data | **Materialized to HDFS** (disk) | **Kept in RAM as RDDs** (with spill-to-disk fallback) |
| Iterative Workload | Each iteration = new MapReduce job = disk I/O | RDD lineage cached → orders-of-magnitude faster |
| Fault Tolerance | Re-execute failed task | RDD lineage recomputation (or checkpoint) |
| Latency Class | **Minutes to hours** (batch) | **Seconds to minutes** (near-real-time batch) |
| Best For | One-pass massive batch ETL | Iterative ML, interactive SQL, streaming |

**(i) Batch ETL on 500 TB logs (3.5 marks):**
For pure one-pass ETL, MapReduce is sufficient and historically more cost-efficient at extreme scale; however, modern Spark is preferred in 2024–2025 because the **in-memory shuffle** and **Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)** yield 3–10× speedup at similar hardware cost. **Spark is preferred.**

**(ii) Iterative K-Means on the same dataset (3.5 marks):**
K-Means iterates 20–100 times. Each Hadoop iteration re-reads the full dataset from HDFS. Spark caches the RDD in memory after iteration 1; subsequent iterations read from RAM. Spark is **~10–100× faster** for this workload. **Spark is decisively preferred.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in "Comparison" Answers:**
>
> 1. **"Hadoop is better than RDBMS"** — *Wrong.* Hadoop is *complementary*; it is better for *unstructured, write-once, batch* workloads, not for transactional OLTP. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for unqualified "X is better than Y" claims.
> 2. **Forgetting the CAP triangle** — Every comparison question *expects* a CAP positioning. A student who skips CAP classification loses **1 full mark** even if all other facts are correct.
> 3. **Confusing Cassandra with HBase** — These are *both* wide-column, *both* inspired by BigTable, but their CAP positioning is opposite. Mixing them up is the **single most common error** reported by KTU evaluators.
> 4. **Writing "NoSQL = no consistency"** — NoSQL systems *do* provide consistency, just *eventual* or *tunable* consistency. The phrase "no consistency" is a **factual error** worth 0/3 marks on the consistency sub-question.
> 5. **Units and powers of ten** — Use $\mathbf{10^{15}}$ bytes for petabytes, $\mathbf{10^{12}}$ bytes for terabytes. Writing "$10^9$ petabytes" or "peta-terabytes" confuses units and loses ½ mark.
> 6. **Confusing horizontal with vertical scaling** — RDBMS scales *vertically* (bigger server), Hadoop/NoSQL scales *horizontally* (more servers). Reversing this costs 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CAP is the *spine* of every comparison:** in a partitioned distributed system, choose **2 of {C, A, P}**. The third must be sacrificed. This is *mathematically proven*, not a design opinion.
- **RDBMS = CA (single-node) or CP (replicated); HBase = CP; Cassandra = AP; MongoDB = CP (default).**
- **ACID** = strict, blocking, transactional. Used in **banking, ERP, OLTP**.
- **BASE** = relaxed, non-blocking, eventually consistent. Used in **social media, IoT, feeds**.
- **Hadoop MapReduce** = **disk-bound**, **batch**, one-pass; Spark = **in-memory**, **iterative**, near-real-time. Spark is preferred for ML and interactive SQL.
- **HDFS** is a Java open-source re-implementation of **Google GFS**, designed for **WORM, sequential, large-block** workloads.
- **Horizontal scaling** (add nodes) is the *defining* characteristic of NoSQL and Hadoop; **vertical scaling** (add RAM/CPU to one node) is the *defining* limit of legacy RDBMS.
- **Masterless designs** (Cassandra) eliminate the *single point of failure* of master-based designs (HBase, HDFS Namenode, MongoDB primary) at the cost of stronger consistency guarantees.
- **Always end a comparison answer with a justified use-case mapping** — examiners reward students who *apply* the comparison, not merely *list* properties.
- **KTU 14-mark standard** expects: (i) labelled diagram, (ii) tabular comparison, (iii) CAP positioning, (iv) one justified real-world use case. Missing any of these four components typically caps the answer at **9–10/14** even if factually correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Brief History of Hadoop
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** A Brief History of Hadoop

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based, distributed computing framework that enables the storage and processing of massive datasets across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It was born out of the need to handle the **Three V's of Big Data** — **Volume, Velocity, and Variety** — which traditional Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS) and centralized data warehouses could not scale to accommodate.

The history of Hadoop is fundamentally the story of how two seminal research papers published by **Google Inc.** inspired an open-source community (led by **Doug Cutting** and **Mike Cafarella**) to build a free, fault-tolerant, distributed storage and processing platform that democratized big data analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic establishes the **chronological evolution** of Hadoop, the **historical context** of its invention, and the **technological limitations** of the pre-Hadoop era that justified its creation. This forms the conceptual foundation for understanding HDFS and MapReduce in later modules.

### 1.2 The Pre-Hadoop Era: The Problem Statement

Before Hadoop, the world relied on **RDBMS** (Oracle, MySQL, SQL Server) and **vertical scaling architectures** (buying bigger, more expensive servers). This approach failed spectacularly when:

1. **Data volume** exceeded the storage capacity of a single machine.
2. **Processing time** for batch jobs grew from hours to weeks.
3. **Hardware failure** of a single node meant total data loss.
4. **Cost** of high-end proprietary hardware was prohibitive for startups and researchers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Problem":**
> Imagine a small town library that holds **10,000 books** in one building. As the town grows, the library needs to store **10 million books**. You cannot simply build a bigger building forever (vertical scaling), and a single building burning down destroys everything. The **Hadoop solution** is to instead distribute books across **1,000 small branch libraries** (commodity hardware), keep **multiple photocopies** of important books across branches (replication), and have a **central catalog** (NameNode) that knows exactly which book is in which branch. When one branch catches fire, the photocopies in other branches ensure no book is lost. This is precisely what Hadoop's **HDFS (storage)** and **MapReduce (processing)** do for data.

### 1.3 The Two Google Papers That Changed Everything

The entire history of Hadoop is rooted in **two foundational research papers** published by Google engineers between 2003 and 2004:

| Year | Paper Title | Authors | Hadoop Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2003** | *The Google File System (GFS)* | Ghemawat, Gobioff, Leung | **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) |
| **2004** | *MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters* | Dean, Ghemawat | **Hadoop MapReduce** |

These papers described **proprietary internal systems** that Google had built to index the entire web. Because Google did not release their code, the open-source community had to **re-implement** these ideas from scratch — and that re-implementation became Hadoop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Exams:**
> - **Commodity Hardware**: Standard, cheap, off-the-shelf servers (e.g., ₹30,000 PCs) rather than specialized enterprise machines costing lakhs.
> - **Fault Tolerance**: The ability of a system to continue operating despite hardware failures of individual nodes.
> - **Distributed Computing**: A model in which computation is spread across multiple interconnected machines working in parallel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Evolution of Hadoop's History

The history of Hadoop is best understood as a **three-phase narrative**: a **problem-discovery phase** at a web-search startup, an **inspiration phase** from Google's published research, and a **maturation phase** as an Apache Software Foundation top-level project.

#### Phase 1: The Nutch Project (2002 – 2004)

- **Doug Cutting**, an engineer at **Excite** and later an open-source contributor, started working on an open-source web search engine called **Nutch** in **2002**.
- **Mike Cafarella**, a graduate student at the **University of Michigan**, joined the effort.
- Nutch was designed to crawl and index the entire web, but it ran into severe **scalability bottlenecks**:
  - Crawling billions of web pages required **petabytes of storage**.
  - Indexing them required **distributed computation** across many machines.
  - A single node failure would crash the entire crawler.
- Cutting and Cafarella realized they needed a **distributed storage layer** and a **distributed processing framework** — but nothing like that existed in the open-source world at the time.

#### Phase 2: The Google Inspiration (2003 – 2006)

- In **October 2003**, Google published the **GFS paper** describing how they stored massive files across thousands of cheap machines with built-in fault tolerance.
- In **December 2004**, Google published the **MapReduce paper** describing a simple programming model for parallel data processing.
- Cutting and Cafarella recognized that these two papers were **the exact solution** to the Nutch scalability problem.
- Between **2004 and 2006**, they implemented:
  - A GFS-inspired distributed file system (initially called **NDFS**, later renamed **HDFS**).
  - A MapReduce-inspired processing engine.
- This sub-project was extracted from Nutch and named **Hadoop** — reportedly after **Doug Cutting's son's yellow toy elephant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Name "Hadoop":**
> Doug Cutting named the framework after his son's plush toy elephant. The elephant was given to the child by Cutting's wife, and the name stuck as the project identifier. The iconic Hadoop logo is a yellow elephant, which is why you will often see elephant mascots in Hadoop documentation.

#### Phase 3: Apache Adoption and Industry Takeover (2006 – 2014)

- In **2006**, Cutting joined **Yahoo!**, which had the resources to run Hadoop on **thousands of nodes**. Yahoo! provided the real-world testbed.
- In **2007**, Yahoo! began using Hadoop in production for search indexing and ad optimization.
- In **January 2008**, Hadoop became a **top-level project of the Apache Software Foundation**, signaling its maturity.
- In **2008**, **Facebook** opened the first **Hadoop-based data warehouse** with a multi-petabyte cluster.
- In **2011**, **Hadoop 1.0** was released — featuring only HDFS and MapReduce.
- In **2013**, **Hadoop 2.0** introduced **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)**, decoupling resource management from the MapReduce programming model and allowing other processing engines (Spark, Tez) to run on top of HDFS.

### 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Timeline Cheat Sheet

The following table is the **single most important revision artifact** for this topic in KTU exams. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Year | Milestone | Significance for KTU |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **2002** | Doug Cutting starts **Nutch** | The motivation phase |
| **Oct 2003** | Google publishes **GFS** paper | Blueprint for HDFS |
| **Dec 2004** | Google publishes **MapReduce** paper | Blueprint for MapReduce engine |
| **2005** | Hadoop created as part of Nutch | Naming and inception |
| **2006** | Hadoop split into separate Apache project; Cutting joins **Yahoo!** | Apache incubation |
| **2007** | Yahoo! deploys Hadoop on 1,000-node cluster | First industrial validation |
| **2008** | Hadoop becomes **Apache top-level project**; Facebook opens mega-warehouse | Industry adoption |
| **2011** | **Hadoop 1.0** released (HDFS + MapReduce) | First stable release |
| **2013** | **Hadoop 2.0** released with **YARN** | Decoupled resource manager |
| **2017** | **Hadoop 3.0** released | Erasure coding, multi-NN support |

### 2.3 Engineering Significance and Real-World Utility

The historical evolution of Hadoop solved several fundamental engineering problems that have shaped modern data engineering:

- **Economics of Scale**: Hadoop proved that **horizontal scaling** (adding more cheap nodes) was dramatically more cost-effective than **vertical scaling** (buying bigger servers). This shifted the entire industry toward commodity clusters.
- **Write-Once, Read-Many Optimization**: Hadoop's design (especially HDFS) optimized for a workload where data is written once and analyzed many times — perfect for log analytics, clickstreams, and sensor data.
- **Schema-on-Read Flexibility**: Unlike RDBMS, which enforces schema on write, Hadoop defers schema enforcement until read time, enabling analysis of unstructured and semi-structured data.
- **Foundation of the Modern Data Lake**: Every modern data lake (AWS S3 + Spark, Azure Data Lake, Google BigQuery) is a conceptual descendant of the HDFS + MapReduce architecture that Hadoop pioneered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> **GFS** solved the **storage** problem (how to store petabytes reliably across thousands of machines).
> **MapReduce** solved the **processing** problem (how to compute over that stored data in parallel).
> Hadoop is the open-source re-implementation of **both** papers combined.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: From GFS to HDFS

This section mathematically and logically traces how the **Google File System paper** was translated into the **Hadoop Distributed File System**. While this is a historical topic, understanding the *algorithmic lineage* is essential for KTU questions that ask "How does HDFS implement the principles described in the GFS paper?"

### 3.1 Logical Derivation: The Chunk-Server Architecture

The GFS paper introduced a **single-master, multiple-chunkserver** architecture. Hadoop re-implemented this as the **NameNode-DataNode** architecture. The logical derivation proceeds as follows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Master-Slave Architecture?**
> A single machine cannot hold petabytes of data, but it can hold **metadata** (filenames, locations, permissions) about petabytes of data. The master node (NameNode) is small and fast; the slave nodes (DataNodes) are large and numerous. This is the same principle as a **library catalog** — the catalog itself fits on one desk, but the books it indexes fill an entire building.

**Step 1: File Decomposition**
A file $F$ of size $S$ bytes is decomposed into $N$ fixed-size blocks (chunks) of size $B$:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil$$

> **Explanation:** The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is used because if $S = 1000$ MB and $B = 128$ MB, then $1000 / 128 = 7.81$, and we need 8 full blocks (the last block being partially filled). In Hadoop 1.x, $B = 64$ MB; in Hadoop 2.x+, $B = 128$ MB; in Hadoop 3.x, $B$ is configurable.

**Step 2: Replication Factor**
Each block is replicated $R$ times across distinct DataNodes for fault tolerance. The default Hadoop replication factor is:

$$R_{\text{default}} = 3$$

The total physical storage consumed by a file of size $S$ is therefore:

$$S_{\text{physical}} = S \times R = S \times 3$$

> **Explanation:** This means storing **1 TB of logical data** actually consumes **3 TB of physical disk space** across the cluster. This 3x storage overhead is the "price" of fault tolerance. With Hadoop 3.0's **erasure coding**, this overhead can be reduced to approximately **1.4x** for the same fault tolerance, but at the cost of higher CPU usage.

**Step 3: NameNode Metadata**
The NameNode maintains the file system namespace as a tree. For each file, it stores the mapping from filename to a list of block locations. If file $F$ is split into blocks $\{B_1, B_2, \ldots, B_N\}$, the NameNode holds a map:

$$\text{FSImage} = \{ F \mapsto [(B_1, [DN_a, DN_b, DN_c]), (B_2, [DN_d, DN_e, DN_f]), \ldots] \}$$

> **Explanation:** The NameNode does **not** store the actual data — only the *metadata* pointing to which DataNode holds which block. The data itself lives on the DataNodes. This separation is critical because the NameNode's memory is a bottleneck, while DataNode storage is cheap and abundant.

**Step 4: Block Report and Heartbeat Protocol**
Each DataNode sends a periodic **heartbeat** signal to the NameNode every **3 seconds** (default). The heartbeat serves two purposes:

$$\text{Heartbeat} = \{ \text{NodeID}, \text{Status}, \text{BlockReport} \}$$

- If the NameNode does not receive a heartbeat within **10 minutes** (default timeout), it declares the DataNode **dead**.
- Upon detecting a dead DataNode, the NameNode triggers **re-replication** of all blocks that were stored on the failed node to restore the replication factor to $R = 3$.

> **Explanation:** This heartbeat mechanism is the **fault-tolerance heartbeat** of Hadoop. It is the same engineering principle used in **patient cardiac monitors** in a hospital — if the monitor stops beeping within a timeout window, the system raises an alarm. Hadoop's NameNode is the "monitor station," and each DataNode is a "patient."

**Step 5: Rack Awareness**
To improve reliability, Hadoop places replicas across **different racks** (physical cabinets of machines connected to a common switch). The default rack-aware placement strategy is:

- **Replica 1** → On a DataNode on a different rack from the writer.
- **Replica 2** → On a different DataNode on the same rack as Replica 1.
- **Replica 3** → On a different DataNode on a different rack from Replica 2.

This guarantees that even if an **entire rack loses power**, at least one replica survives.

### 3.2 The Algorithm-to-Code Lineage: GFS → HDFS

The following matrix explicitly maps the GFS concepts to their Hadoop re-implementations.

| Google GFS Concept | Hadoop HDFS Equivalent | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GFS Master** | **NameNode** | Stores metadata, coordinates chunk servers |
| **GFS Chunk Server** | **DataNode** | Stores actual data blocks |
| **Chunk (64 MB default)** | **Block (128 MB default)** | Unit of storage and replication |
| **Chunk Handle** | **Block ID** | Unique identifier for each block |
| **Mutation (Write/Append)** | **Write/Append Pipeline** | Client streams data through DataNodes |
| **Master Operation Log** | **EditLog (fsimage)** | Persistent record of all metadata changes |
| **Lease Mechanism** | **Lease for file writes** | Prevents concurrent writers from corrupting a file |
| **Stale Replica Detection** | **Block Report + Heartbeat** | Identifies and replaces dead/under-replicated blocks |

### 3.3 MapReduce Historical Analogy

Just as HDFS was inspired by GFS, the Hadoop MapReduce engine was a re-implementation of Google's MapReduce paper. The conceptual flow of both systems is identical:

1. **Input Splitting**: The input file (residing in HDFS/GFS) is divided into $M$ splits, each assigned to a **Map task**.
2. **Map Phase**: Each map function processes a key-value pair $(k_1, v_1)$ and emits intermediate pairs $(k_2, v_2)$.
3. **Shuffle and Sort**: The framework groups all values with the same key $k_2$ together.
4. **Reduce Phase**: Each reduce function takes $(k_2, [v_{2,1}, v_{2,2}, \ldots])$ and emits final output $(k_3, v_3)$.

The mathematical expression for the total number of intermediate key-value pairs is:

$$N_{\text{intermediate}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert \text{Map}(k_{1,i}, v_{1,i}) \vert$$

> **Explanation:** $M$ is the number of input splits (determined by HDFS block count), and $\text{Map}(\cdot)$ is the user-defined map function. The cardinality (size) of its output is summed across all splits. This quantity directly determines the network traffic during the shuffle phase — a critical performance bottleneck that KTU students are expected to understand.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams visualize the **chronological history** of Hadoop, the **architectural lineage** from Google papers to Apache project, and the **component evolution** across Hadoop versions.

### 4.1 Timeline of Hadoop's Evolution

```mermaid
timeline
    title History of Hadoop Timeline 2002 to 2017
    2002  : Doug Cutting starts Nutch project
    2003  : Google publishes GFS paper
    2004  : Google publishes MapReduce paper

    2006  : Hadoop becomes Apache sub-project
    2007  : Yahoo deploys 1000 node cluster
    2008  : Apache top level project
    2011  : Hadoop 1.0 released
    2013  : Hadoop 2.0 with YARN
    2017  : Hadoop 3.0 with erasure coding
```

### 4.2 Lineage Flowchart: Google Papers to Apache Hadoop

```mermaid

    A[Web Search Scalability Problem 2002] --> B[Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella start Nutch]
    B --> C{Nutch Hits Scalability Wall}

    C --> E[Google Publishes MapReduce Paper Dec 2004]

    E --> F
    F --> G[Hadoop Spun Off from Nutch 2006]

```

### 4.3 Component Evolution Across Hadoop Versions

```mermaid

    subgraph V1[Hadoop 1.0 - 2011]
        A1[HDFS] --- B1[MapReduce Engine]
    end

    end

    end

    V1 --> V2
    V2 --> V3
```

### 4.4 The Pre-Hadoop vs Post-Hadoop Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PRE[Pre-Hadoop Era - Centralized RDBMS]
        P1[Client Application] --> P2[Expensive Enterprise Server]
        P2 --> P3[High End SAN Storage]
        P3 --> P4[Single Point of Failure]
    end

    subgraph POST[Post-Hadoop Era - Distributed Cluster]
        Q1[Client Application] --> Q2[NameNode Master]
        Q2 --> Q3[DataNode Rack 1]
        Q2 --> Q4[DataNode Rack 2]
        Q2 --> Q5[DataNode Rack 3]
        Q3 --> Q6[Block Replicas]
        Q4 --> Q6
        Q5 --> Q6
    end

    PRE -.Replaced by.-> POST
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Hadoop Core Ecosystem Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STORAGE[Storage Layer - Inspired by GFS 2003]
        S1[HDFS]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING[Processing Layer - Inspired by MapReduce 2004]
        P1[MapReduce]
        P2[Spark]
        P3[Tez]
    end

    subgraph RESOURCE[Resource Management - Added 2013]
        R1[YARN]
    end

    subgraph ECOSYSTEM[Supporting Ecosystem]
        E1[Hive - SQL]
        E2[Pig - Scripting]
        E3[HBase - NoSQL]
        E4[Sqoop - RDBMS Bridge]
        E5[Flume - Log Ingestion]
    end

    S1 --> R1
    R1 --> P1
    R1 --> P2
    R1 --> P3
    P1 --> E1
    P1 --> E2
    S1 --> E3
    E4 --> S1
    E5 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Name the two Google research papers that inspired the creation of Hadoop. State the Hadoop component that was derived from each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The two Google papers are:
1. **The Google File System (GFS)** published in 2003 → inspired **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)**.
2. **MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters** published in 2004 → inspired the **Hadoop MapReduce** programming engine.

Together, these re-implementations form the core of the Hadoop framework, enabling distributed storage (HDFS) and distributed processing (MapReduce) across commodity hardware clusters.

*(1 mark per paper + 0.5 marks per component = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*Why is Hadoop named "Hadoop"? Briefly explain the historical context.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hadoop is named after **Doug Cutting's son's yellow plush toy elephant**. Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella created Hadoop as a sub-project of their open-source web search engine called **Nutch** around 2005. The name was simply the child's name for his stuffed elephant, and Cutting adopted it as the project codename. When the project was spun off from Nutch and submitted to the Apache Software Foundation in 2006, the name Hadoop was retained and became the official project name. The yellow elephant has since become the iconic Hadoop logo.

*(1 mark naming origin + 1 mark Nutch context + 1 mark timeline = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the years**: GFS was published in **2003** (October) and MapReduce in **2004** (December). Mixing these up costs **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Always mention the Nutch origin**: Many students answer "Hadoop was created by Yahoo!" — this is **wrong**. Yahoo! *hired* Cutting and *deployed* Hadoop, but did not *create* it. Hadoop was created within Nutch.
> 3. **Cite Doug Cutting AND Mike Cafarella**: Listing only Doug Cutting is incomplete and may cost **1 mark**.
> 4. **Avoid calling HDFS a "database"**: HDFS is a **file system**, not a database. The 14-mark question frequently tests this distinction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the historical evolution of Hadoop, starting from the Nutch project up to Hadoop 3.0. Include the key milestones, year of occurrence, and the role of Google papers. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain how the Google File System (GFS) paper influenced the design of HDFS. List at least four architectural concepts that were directly adopted from GFS. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Historical Evolution of Hadoop (7 Marks):**

1. **2002 — Birth of Nutch**: Doug Cutting, then working on open-source search technology, began the Nutch project to build an open-source web crawler and indexer. Mike Cafarella, a graduate student at the University of Michigan, joined the effort. The goal was to crawl and index the entire web. **(1 Mark)**

2. **2003 — The GFS Paper**: Google's engineers (Ghemawat, Gobioff, Leung) published the GFS paper in October 2003, describing how Google stored petabytes of data across thousands of commodity machines. This gave Cutting and Cafarella the architectural blueprint for a distributed file system. **(1 Mark)**

3. **2004 — The MapReduce Paper**: Jeff Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat published the MapReduce paper in December 2004, describing a simple programming model for parallel data processing. This provided the blueprint for a distributed processing engine. **(1 Mark)**

4. **2005 — Hadoop is Born**: Cutting and Cafarella implemented HDFS and MapReduce within Nutch. The sub-project was named "Hadoop" after Cutting's son's toy elephant. **(1 Mark)**

5. **2006 — Apache Incubation and Yahoo!**: Hadoop was spun off from Nutch as a standalone Apache sub-project. Doug Cutting joined Yahoo!, which had the infrastructure to run Hadoop on a large scale. **(1 Mark)**

6. **2008 — Apache Top-Level and Facebook**: Hadoop became a top-level Apache project. Facebook launched one of the world's largest Hadoop data warehouses, with multi-petabyte clusters. **(1 Mark)**

7. **2011–2017 — Major Releases**: Hadoop 1.0 (HDFS + classic MapReduce) was released in 2011. Hadoop 2.0 introduced YARN in 2013, decoupling resource management. Hadoop 3.0 (2017) added erasure coding, improving storage efficiency from 3x to ~1.4x overhead. **(1 Mark)**

---

**(b) GFS to HDFS Architectural Mapping (7 Marks):**

The following four GFS concepts were directly adopted into HDFS:

1. **Master-Slave Architecture (1.5 Marks)**: GFS uses a single GFS Master that holds all metadata, with multiple GFS Chunk Servers storing actual data. HDFS adopts this as the NameNode (master) and DataNode (slave) architecture. The NameNode is small, in-memory, and fast; the DataNodes are large, numerous, and store the actual blocks.

2. **Fixed-Size Chunks/Blocks (1.5 Marks)**: GFS divides files into 64 MB chunks. HDFS divides files into 128 MB blocks (in Hadoop 2.x+). The fixed-size design simplifies replication management, because the system always knows that "this file is exactly N blocks long," regardless of file size.

3. **Triple Replication for Fault Tolerance (2 Marks)**: GFS stores three replicas of each chunk on different machines. HDFS uses the same default replication factor of $R = 3$, with replicas distributed across different racks for rack-level fault tolerance. If a DataNode fails, the NameNode re-replicates its blocks to restore the count to 3.

4. **Heartbeat-Based Failure Detection (2 Marks)**: GFS Chunk Servers send periodic heartbeats to the Master. HDFS DataNodes send heartbeats every 3 seconds to the NameNode. If 10 minutes pass without a heartbeat, the DataNode is declared dead, and its blocks are scheduled for re-replication. This is the cornerstone of Hadoop's fault tolerance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the limitations of traditional RDBMS and centralized systems that motivated the creation of Hadoop. How did Hadoop's design philosophy address these limitations? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of Yahoo! and Facebook in the early adoption of Hadoop. Explain how their use cases validated the Hadoop architecture. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Limitations of RDBMS and How Hadoop Solved Them (7 Marks):**

Traditional RDBMS-based systems (Oracle, MySQL, Teradata) failed to handle the **Three V's of Big Data**:

1. **Volume Limitation (2 Marks)**: RDBMS stores data on a single server with vertical scaling. A single high-end server can hold at most tens of terabytes. Hadoop addresses this by storing data across **thousands of commodity machines** in a distributed file system (HDFS), enabling **petabyte and exabyte** scale.

2. **Velocity Limitation (2 Marks)**: Traditional ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) pipelines process data in batch mode at scheduled intervals, causing high latency. Hadoop's MapReduce engine processes data **in parallel across the cluster**, dramatically increasing throughput. A job that would take 24 hours on a single machine could complete in under an hour on a 100-node Hadoop cluster.

3. **Variety Limitation (1.5 Marks)**: RDBMS enforces a strict **schema-on-write** model, meaning the data structure must be defined before data is inserted. Hadoop supports **schema-on-read**, allowing raw, unstructured data (logs, images, JSON, XML) to be stored in HDFS and interpreted at query time using tools like Hive or Pig.

4. **Single Point of Failure (1.5 Marks)**: A single RDBMS server crashing means total data unavailability. Hadoop's **replication factor of 3** and **heartbeat-based failure detection** ensure that the cluster continues operating even when multiple DataNodes fail simultaneously. The system is designed to expect and tolerate hardware failures.

---

**(b) Role of Yahoo! and Facebook in Hadoop Adoption (7 Marks):**

1. **Yahoo!'s Contribution (3.5 Marks)**: When Doug Cutting joined Yahoo! in 2006, the company had a massive web search index and advertising system that needed to process billions of web pages. Yahoo! provided the **infrastructure, funding, and engineering team** to scale Hadoop from a small Nutch sub-project to a production-grade system. By 2007, Yahoo! was running Hadoop on a **1,000-node cluster**, and by 2008 on a **10,000-node cluster** — the largest Hadoop deployment in the world at the time. Yahoo! also contributed major open-source sub-projects such as **Pig (Scripting)** and **Oozie (Workflow Scheduling)**. The fact that Yahoo!, a Fortune 500 company, entrusted its core search infrastructure to Hadoop proved that the framework was **enterprise-ready**.

2. **Facebook's Contribution (3.5 Marks)**: In **2008, Facebook** (then a fast-growing social network with 100+ million users) launched the world's first **Hadoop-based data warehouse** to store and analyze its clickstream, log, and user-behavior data. By 2010, Facebook's Hadoop cluster had grown to **30 PB of storage** — at the time, the largest known Hadoop cluster. Facebook contributed critical open-source projects, most notably **Hive (SQL-on-Hadoop)**, which allowed Facebook's analysts to write familiar SQL-like queries against petabytes of HDFS data. The Facebook use case validated Hadoop's ability to handle **massive-scale, semi-structured data analytics** in a mission-critical business environment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **Hadoop was NOT created by Google**. It was created by **Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella** as part of the **Nutch** open-source search engine project.
- The name **"Hadoop"** comes from **Doug Cutting's son's toy elephant**.
- The **two Google papers** that inspired Hadoop are the **GFS paper (2003)** and the **MapReduce paper (2004)**.
- **GFS inspired HDFS** (storage); **MapReduce inspired the MapReduce engine** (processing).
- Hadoop was **spun off from Nutch in 2006** and became an **Apache top-level project in 2008**.
- **Yahoo! hired Doug Cutting in 2006** and provided the first large-scale production testbed.
- **Facebook deployed the first Hadoop data warehouse in 2008** and contributed the **Hive** project.
- **Hadoop 1.0 (2011)** contained only HDFS and MapReduce.
- **Hadoop 2.0 (2013)** introduced **YARN**, decoupling resource management from the programming model.
- **Hadoop 3.0 (2017)** added **erasure coding**, reducing storage overhead from 3x to ~1.4x.
- Hadoop uses **commodity hardware** (cheap, off-the-shelf machines) rather than expensive enterprise servers.
- Hadoop's default **block size is 128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+); GFS used 64 MB.
- Hadoop's default **replication factor is 3**, with rack-aware placement.
- HDFS is a **file system**, **NOT a database**. Always use the term "distributed file system."
- The **NameNode stores metadata only**; the actual data lives on **DataNodes**.
- Hadoop's design philosophy: **move computation to data**, not data to computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Apache Hadoop and the Hadoop Ecosystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Apache Hadoop and the Hadoop Ecosystem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source framework written in Java that permits the distributed storage and distributed processing of extremely large data sets (Big Data) across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models. It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.

Hadoop is built on **four core assumptions** (the 4 V's of Big Data it solves):
- **Volume** — Petabytes and Exabytes of data
- **Velocity** — Real-time and streaming data
- **Variety** — Structured, Semi-structured, and Unstructured
- **Veracity** — Handling data uncertainty

The framework is maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation** and was derived from Google's published papers on **Google File System (GFS, 2003)** and **MapReduce (2004)**.

## 1.2 Historical Background

| Year | Milestone |
|------|-----------|
| 2002 | Doug Cutting & Mike Cafarella begin work on **Nutch** (open-source web crawler) |
| 2003 | Google publishes GFS paper |
| 2004 | Google publishes MapReduce paper |
| 2005 | Cutting & Cafarella port MapReduce to Nutch |
| 2006 | Yahoo! hires Cutting; project splits from Nutch → **Hadoop** (named after son's toy elephant) |
| 2008 | Hadoop becomes Apache Top-Level Project |
| 2011 | **Hadoop 0.20.x**, **0.23.x** introduces YARN |
| 2013 | **Hadoop 2.x** released — YARN becomes default |
| 2017 | **Hadoop 3.x** released — adds erasure coding, multi-NN support |
| 2023+ | **Hadoop 3.3.6+** with cloud-native improvements |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If asked "Who developed Hadoop?" — answer: **Doug Cutting and Mike Cafarella**, originally under Yahoo!.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Library Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a massive library with 100 million books** (your Big Data). One librarian trying to catalogue every book would take centuries. So instead, you:
> 1. **Shred each book into chapters** → **Splitting (Split Phase)**
> 2. **Distribute chapters across 1000 small libraries** → **HDFS Storage (DataNodes)**
> 3. **Hire 1000 librarians, each counting word frequencies in their own chapters** → **Map Phase**
> 4. **Send each librarian's count to a central sorting room** → **Shuffle & Sort Phase**
> 5. **Aggregate the final word counts into one master ledger** → **Reduce Phase**
> 6. **Keep 3 copies of every chapter in different libraries** → **Replication Factor = 3**

The **Head Librarian** (NameNode) keeps track of which chapter is in which library. If a library burns down, the chapter is recovered from the other two copies. This is **fault tolerance** in Hadoop.

## 1.4 The Core Idea: "Bring Code to Data"

Traditional systems move **data to code** (centralized servers pull data over network — slow for TBs). Hadoop reverses this — **code moves to data**. The processing happens on the **DataNode** where the data already resides, minimizing network traffic. This principle is called **Data Locality** and is a defining feature of Hadoop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Core Modules of Hadoop:**
> 1. **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** — Storage layer
> 2. **MapReduce** — Processing layer
> 3. *(Added in Hadoop 2.x)* — **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — Cluster resource management
> 4. *(Added in Hadoop 3.x)* — **Hadoop Ozone** — Object store for Hadoop

## 1.5 Key Characteristics of Hadoop

- **Scalability** — Scale horizontally by adding more nodes
- **Reliability / Fault Tolerance** — Data is replicated (default **3 copies**); failed tasks are re-executed automatically
- **Cost-effective** — Uses commodity hardware (cheap, off-the-shelf PCs)
- **Flexibility** — Handles structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data
- **Data Locality** — Computation moves to data, not data to computation
- **Open Source** — Free under Apache License 2.0
- **Portability** — Runs on Linux, Windows (via WSL), macOS
- **Simplicity** — User writes only `map()` and `reduce()` functions
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Hadoop (Hadoop 2.x & 3.x Architecture)

### 2.1.1 HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)

HDFS is a **write-once, read-many** distributed file system designed for storing very large files across multiple machines.

**HDFS Architecture — Master/Slave:**

**1. NameNode (Master / Namenode Daemon)**
- Stores **metadata** (file names, permissions, block locations, directory tree)
- Maintains the entire filesystem namespace in RAM
- **Two files** it persists to disk:
  - `fsimage` — snapshot of filesystem metadata
  - `edit logs` — log of recent changes
- Single point of failure in Hadoop 1.x (solved in 2.x via **HA / Standby NameNode**)
- Also called **Namenode daemon** process

**2. DataNode (Slave / Datanode Daemon)**
- Stores the **actual data blocks**
- Default block size: **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+) or **64 MB** (1.x)
- Sends **heartbeat signals** (every 3 seconds) to NameNode
- Sends **block reports** (every 6 hours by default) listing all blocks it holds
- Number of DataNodes: can scale to **thousands per cluster**

**3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node)**
- **NOT a backup** of NameNode (a common misconception)
- Periodically merges `fsimage + edit logs` → new `fsimage` to prevent edit logs from growing huge
- Called the **Checkpoint Node** in modern Hadoop

**4. HDFS Block Concept**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A file in HDFS is split into fixed-size **blocks**. Default block size = **128 MB** (since Hadoop 2.0). For example, a 400 MB file = 4 blocks (128+128+128+16 MB).

**5. Replication Factor**
- Default replication factor = **3** (configurable via `dfs.replication` in `hdfs-site.xml`)
- One copy on a **local rack**, one on a **different rack**, one on the **same different rack** (rack-aware placement)
- The NameNode uses a **Rack Awareness** algorithm to choose block locations

### 2.1.2 MapReduce

MapReduce is a **programming model** for parallel processing of large data sets, consisting of two main functions:

**1. Map Phase**
- Input: `(key1, value1)` pairs
- Output: List of intermediate `(key2, value2)` pairs
- Example: `("hello world", 1) → [("hello", 1), ("world", 1)]`

**2. Reduce Phase**
- Input: `(key2, list of values)` grouped by key
- Output: Aggregated `(key3, value3)` pairs
- Example: `[("hello", [1,1,1])] → ("hello", 3)`

**Between Map and Reduce:** Shuffle & Sort phase
- Framework groups all values for the same key
- Performs a **merge sort** across all mappers
- Sends grouped data to reducers (this is the **most expensive** phase)

**Number of Mappers and Reducers:**
- Number of Mappers = **Number of input splits** (decided by `InputFormat`, usually one mapper per HDFS block)
- Number of Reducers = **Set explicitly** by user via `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)`. Default = 1 in older versions, **0 in Hadoop 2.x+** (job may run with no reducer)

### 2.1.3 YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)

YARN (introduced in Hadoop 2.0) decouples resource management from data processing.

**YARN Architecture:**

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Master daemon; arbitrates cluster resources; runs on master node |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Slave daemon on each worker node; launches/manages containers |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | One per job; negotiates resources with RM and works with NMs |
| **Container** | Abstract resource bundle (CPU + RAM) on a node; runs tasks |
| **Scheduler** | Component of RM (Capacity, Fair, FIFO) |

**YARN Workflow:**
1. Client submits job to **ResourceManager**
2. RM allocates a **container** and starts **ApplicationMaster**
3. AM negotiates more containers from RM
4. AM assigns **map/reduce tasks** to NodeManagers
5. NodeManagers execute tasks in **containers**
6. AM reports status to RM; RM releases resources on completion

## 2.2 The Hadoop Ecosystem (Tools Built on Hadoop)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hadoop Ecosystem** refers to the suite of open-source projects that extend Hadoop's core capabilities (storage + processing) to provide specialized services like SQL queries, NoSQL databases, data ingestion, workflow scheduling, etc.

### 2.2.1 Storage Layer

| Tool | Full Form | Purpose |
|------|-----------|---------|
| **HDFS** | Hadoop Distributed File System | Distributed file storage (core) |
| **HBase** | Hadoop Database | NoSQL, columnar, real-time read/write DB on HDFS |
| **Ozone** | Hadoop Ozone | Object store (S3-like) for Hadoop 3.x |

### 2.2.2 Data Ingestion Layer

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Sqoop** | Bulk transfer between **Hadoop and RDBMS** (MySQL, Oracle) using JDBC |
| **Flume** | Collects streaming data (log files, events) into HDFS/HBase |
| **Kafka** | Distributed publish-subscribe messaging system (often part of ecosystem) |

### 2.2.3 Data Processing / Analytics Layer

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **MapReduce** | Batch processing (core) |
| **Spark** | In-memory fast processing (up to 100× faster than MR) |
| **Pig** | Scripting language (Pig Latin) for ETL on HDFS |
| **Hive** | SQL-like queries (HQL/HiveQL) on HDFS data — **Data Warehouse** |
| **Impala** | Real-time SQL query engine over HDFS (MPP SQL) |
| **Tez** | Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) framework (replaces MR for Hive) |
| **Storm** | Real-time stream processing (replaced by Flink/Spark Streaming) |
| **Flink** | Stateful stream processing |

### 2.2.4 Machine Learning / Data Science

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Mahout** | Scalable ML library (regression, clustering, recommendation) |
| **Spark MLlib** | ML library on Spark |

### 2.2.5 Workflow / Scheduling / Coordination

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Oozie** | Workflow scheduler for Hadoop jobs (XML-based DAGs) |
| **ZooKeeper** | Coordination service (leader election, configuration, locks) |
| **Ambari** | Cluster provisioning, monitoring, and management UI |

### 2.2.6 Security / Administration

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| **Ranger / Sentry** | Authorization & audit |
| **Kerberos** | Authentication |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Configuration Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **must-know numerical relationships** and configurations for KTU exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Configuration | Default Value | Unit |
|---|---------|------------------------|---------------|------|
| 1 | HDFS block size (Hadoop 2.x) | `dfs.blocksize` | **128 MB** | MB |
| 2 | HDFS block size (Hadoop 1.x) | `dfs.blocksize` | **64 MB** | MB |
| 3 | Replication factor | `dfs.replication` | **3** | copies |
| 4 | Number of blocks in a file | $N_b = \left\lceil \dfrac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{\text{Block Size (MB)}} \right\rceil$ | — | blocks |
| 5 | Total storage used by file | $S_{total} = N_b \times B \times R$ | where $B$=block size, $R$=replication | MB |
| 6 | Number of Mappers | $N_{mappers} = N_{splits} \approx N_b$ | one per split | mappers |
| 7 | Number of Reducers | Set explicitly by user | configurable | reducers |
| 8 | NameNode heartbeat threshold | `dfs.namenode.heartbeat.recheck-interval` | **10 min** | minutes |
| 9 | DataNode heartbeat period | `dfs.heartbeat.interval` | **3 seconds** | seconds |
| 10 | Block report interval | `dfs.blockreport.intervalMsec` | **21600000 ms (6 h)** | ms |
| 11 | YARN container memory | `yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb` | configurable | MB |
| 12 | YARN container vCores | `yarn.nodemanager.resource.cpu-vcores` | configurable | cores |
| 13 | Max containers per node | $\left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{Total RAM}}{\text{Per-container RAM}} \right\rfloor$ | — | containers |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\lvert` and `\rvert` or `\mid` instead of `\|` for absolute values** in LaTeX — this prevents markdown table syntax from breaking in KTU-formatted answers.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Hadoop powers real-world systems such as:
> - **Yahoo!** — Search indexing (> 40,000 nodes at peak)
> - **Facebook** — Stores > 300 PB in HDFS, uses Hive for analytics
> - **Netflix** — Recommender system (Mahout + Spark)
> - **Twitter** — Tweet storage & analysis
> - **Amazon EMR** — Managed Hadoop service on AWS
> - **Healthcare** — Genomic data analysis (100s of TB per patient cohort)
> - **Banking & Finance** — Fraud detection, risk modeling
> - **IoT** — Sensor data ingestion (Flume + Kafka + Spark Streaming)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: HDFS Block Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-Style):** A file of size **530 MB** is stored in HDFS. Given block size = **128 MB** and replication factor = **3**, compute:
> 1. Number of blocks
> 2. Total physical storage consumed
> 3. Number of mapper tasks (assuming 1 mapper per block)

### Step 1: Compute the Number of Blocks

The number of HDFS blocks for a file is the **ceiling** of (file size ÷ block size).

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{\text{File Size (MB)}}{\text{Block Size (MB)}} \right\rceil
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{530 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil
$$

Performing the division:

$$
\frac{530}{128} = 4.140625
$$

Taking the ceiling (the smallest integer ≥ 4.140625):

$$
N_b = 5 \text{ blocks}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ceiling function gives 5 because the last block (the 5th) is **partial** — it contains only the leftover bytes.

### Step 2: Block Distribution

Let us verify the block distribution explicitly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 2} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 3} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 4} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 5} &= 530 - (4 \times 128) = 530 - 512 = 18 \text{ MB} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification of total:

$$
128 + 128 + 128 + 128 + 18 = 530 \text{ MB} \;\checkmark
$$

### Step 3: Total Physical Storage Consumed

With replication factor $R = 3$, every block is stored in **3 physical locations** across the cluster.

$$
S_{total} = N_b \times B \times R
$$

where $B$ is the **configured block size** (not the actual block content size for the last block — HDFS charges full block size for replication calculation in this standard formulation).

$$
S_{total} = 5 \times 128 \times 3
$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 \times 128 &= 640 \text{ MB} \\
640 \times 3 &= 1920 \text{ MB} \\
&= 1.875 \text{ GB} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{total} = 1920 \text{ MB} = 1.875 \text{ GB}}
$$

### Step 4: Number of Mapper Tasks

By default, **each HDFS block corresponds to one InputSplit**, and each split is processed by exactly **one map task**. Therefore:

$$
N_{mappers} = N_{splits} = N_b = 5 \text{ mappers}
$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Number of blocks | 5 |
| Total physical storage | **1920 MB (1.875 GB)** |
| Number of mappers | 5 |
| Reducers | user-defined |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: MapReduce WordCount End-to-End

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Given the input text split:
> **"big data big hadoop big data hadoop data hadoop"**
> Walk through the complete MapReduce data flow (Map → Shuffle → Sort → Reduce) with a combiner.

### Step 1: Tokenization & InputSplit

The InputFormat (typically `TextInputFormat`) splits the input line by line. For this problem, assume **one line** is one input split.

Input key: byte offset (line number) — for simplicity, we denote it as `0`.
Input value: `"big data big hadoop big data hadoop data hadoop"`.

### Step 2: Map Phase

The user-defined `map()` function emits `(word, 1)` for every token:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{"big"}, 1), (\text{"data"}, 1), (\text{"big"}, 1), (\text{"hadoop"}, 1), \\
(\text{"big"}, 1), (\text{"data"}, 1), (\text{"hadoop"}, 1), (\text{"data"}, 1), (\text{"hadoop"}, 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Total map output pairs: **9**.

### Step 3: Combiner (Local Aggregation — Optional Mini-Reducer)

The Combiner runs **on the same node** as the Mapper, performing local aggregation to **reduce network traffic** during shuffle.

Local grouping by key:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{"big"} &\to [1, 1, 1] \\
\text{"data"} &\to [1, 1, 1] \\
\text{"hadoop"} &\to [1, 1, 1] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Combiner output (one sum per key):

$$
(\text{"big"}, 3),\;\; (\text{"data"}, 3),\;\; (\text{"hadoop"}, 3)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **key KTU concept**: Combiner reduces 9 pairs → 3 pairs. The reduce function must be **commutative and associative** (e.g., sum, max, min) for combiner to be applicable.

### Step 4: Shuffle & Sort Phase

The framework:
1. **Partitions** the intermediate keys — for WordCount, default partitioner hashes by key, so each unique key goes to one reducer.
2. **Sorts** the keys lexicographically.
3. **Transfers** data from mapper nodes to reducer nodes over the network.

Sorted, grouped input to the Reducer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{"big"} &\to [3] \\
\text{"data"} &\to [3] \\
\text{"hadoop"} &\to [3] \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Reduce Phase

The `reduce()` function sums the list of values for each key:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{"big"}, 3 + \text{no other values}) &\to (\text{"big"}, 3) \\
(\text{"data"}, 3) &\to (\text{"data"}, 3) \\
(\text{"hadoop"}, 3) &\to (\text{"hadoop"}, 3) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Final Output (HDFS)

The final output written to HDFS:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{big} & \quad 3 \\
\text{data} & \quad 3 \\
\text{hadoop} & \quad 3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### MapReduce Data Flow Summary (KTU 14-Mark Style Table)

| Stage | Input | Output | Executed By | Runs On |
|-------|-------|--------|-------------|---------|
| Input Split | Raw file | $N_b$ splits | Framework | — |
| Map | $(K_1, V_1)$ | List of $(K_2, V_2)$ | User `map()` | DataNode (data locality) |
| Combiner | List of $(K_2, V_2)$ | Locally aggregated $(K_2, V_2)$ | User `reduce()` (re-used) | Same node as Mapper |
| Shuffle & Sort | All mapper outputs | Sorted, grouped by key | Framework | Cross-node transfer |
| Reduce | $(K_2, \text{list}(V_2))$ | $(K_3, V_3)$ | User `reduce()` | Assigned reducer node |
| Output | $(K_3, V_3)$ | File in HDFS | `OutputFormat` (e.g., `TextOutputFormat`) | HDFS |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Cluster Resource Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A Hadoop cluster has **8 worker nodes**. Each node has **32 GB RAM** and **8 CPU cores**. YARN is configured such that each container gets **4 GB RAM** and **1 vCore**. Find:
> 1. Max containers per node
> 2. Max containers per cluster
> 3. Max parallel mappers per cluster

### Step 1: Max Containers per Node

Containers are limited by both RAM and vCores — take the minimum:

$$
N_{c/node}^{RAM} = \left\lfloor \frac{32 \text{ GB}}{4 \text{ GB}} \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ containers}
$$

$$
N_{c/node}^{vCore} = \left\lfloor \frac{8 \text{ cores}}{1 \text{ core}} \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ containers}
$$

$$
N_{c/node} = \min(8, 8) = \boxed{8 \text{ containers per node}}
$$

### Step 2: Max Containers per Cluster

$$
N_c = 8 \text{ nodes} \times 8 \text{ containers/node} = \boxed{64 \text{ containers per cluster}}
$$

### Step 3: Max Parallel Mappers

Assuming all containers are used for map tasks (a simplification — typically ~80% for mappers, rest for reducers/AM):

$$
N_{mappers}^{max} = 64 \text{ mappers in parallel}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, YARN reserves some capacity for the **ApplicationMaster** (default 1 GB) and for reducers. The KTU formula above is the **textbook simplification**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hadoop 3.x — Master/Worker High-Level Architecture

```mermaid

    RM[ResourceManager Master]:::master
    NM1[NodeManager Worker 1]:::worker
    NM2[NodeManager Worker 2]:::worker

    AM[ApplicationMaster Per Job]:::am
    C1[Container 1]:::container
    C2[Container 2]:::container
    C3[Container 3]:::container
    C4[Container 4]:::container

    SNN[Secondary NameNode]:::secondary
    DN1[DataNode Worker 1]:::datanode
    DN2[DataNode Worker 2]:::datanode
    DN3[DataNode Worker N]:::datanode
    HDFS[(HDFS Cluster Storage)]:::storage

    ClientA -->|1 Submit Job| RM
    RM -->|2 Allocate Container| AM
    AM -->|3 Request Containers| RM

    NM1 --> C2
    NM2 --> C3
    NM3 --> C4

    NN -->|Block Reports| DN1

    NN -->|Block Reports| DN3

    DN2 -->|Read Write Blocks| HDFS
    DN3 -->|Read Write Blocks| HDFS
```

## 4.4 HDFS Read/Write Path

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Client App
    participant NN as NameNode
    participant DN1 as DataNode 1
    participant DN2 as DataNode 2
    participant DN3 as DataNode 3

    Note over Client: WRITE Path
    Client->>NN: 1 Create file request
    NN-->>Client: 2 Allocate block IDs and DN list
    Client->>DN1: 3 Write block part 1
    Client->>DN2: 4 Write block part 2
    Client->>DN3: 5 Write block part 3
    DN2->>DN1: 6 Pipeline replication
    DN3->>DN2: 7 Pipeline replication
    DN1-->>Client: 8 Ack success

    Note over Client: READ Path
    Client->>NN: 9 Open file request
    NN-->>Client: 10 Return block locations
    Client->>DN2: 11 Read block from nearest DN
    DN2-->>Client: 12 Send block data
```
