# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Questions are tagged with KTU-style past-year simulation tags. Marks are distributed as per KTU 2024 Scheme (Part A = 3 marks × 2, Part B = 14 marks with internal choice).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Q: Define Apache Hadoop. List any four characteristics of Hadoop.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Apache Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based framework that enables the **distributed storage and distributed processing** of large data sets across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models.

**Four characteristics of Hadoop:**

1. **Scalability** — Hadoop can scale horizontally from a single node to thousands of nodes by simply adding more machines to the cluster.
2. **Fault Tolerance** — Data is automatically replicated (default 3 copies) across nodes; if a node fails, the data is recovered from another replica and the failed task is re-executed on a healthy node.
3. **Cost-Effectiveness** — Hadoop uses commodity hardware (low-cost, off-the-shelf machines) instead of expensive high-end servers, making Big Data processing affordable.
4. **Data Locality** — Hadoop moves the **computation to the data** rather than moving data to the computation, drastically reducing network traffic and improving performance.

*(Also acceptable: Flexibility, Open Source, Portability, Simplicity, Reliability.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Q: Differentiate between HDFS and MapReduce.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | HDFS (Storage) | MapReduce (Processing) |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Distributed **storage** of large files | Distributed **processing** of large data |
| **Type** | File system | Programming model + execution engine |
| **Master** | NameNode | JobTracker (1.x) / ApplicationMaster (2.x+) |
| **Slave** | DataNode | TaskTracker (1.x) / NodeManager (2.x+) |
| **Unit of work** | Block (128 MB) | Map task and Reduce task |
| **Data model** | Files split into blocks | Key-value pairs |
| **Function** | Read/Write files | `map()` and `reduce()` functions |
| **Position in Hadoop** | Bottom layer | Top of HDFS + YARN |
| **Failure handling** | Re-replication of blocks | Re-execution of failed tasks |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS architecture** in detail with a neat diagram. Describe the role of **NameNode, DataNode, and Secondary NameNode**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A file of size **800 MB** is stored in HDFS with block size = **128 MB** and replication factor = **3**. Compute the **number of blocks, total physical storage consumed, and number of map tasks**. Show all calculations step-by-step. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to (a) — HDFS Architecture

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** follows a **master/slave architecture**:

**1. NameNode (Master):**
- Runs on the master node and stores **metadata** of all files (file names, directory tree, block locations, permissions, replication factor).
- Loads metadata into **RAM** at startup for fast access.
- Persists state to two files on local disk: `fsimage` (snapshot) and `edit logs` (recent changes).
- Receives **heartbeats** (every 3 sec) and **block reports** (every 6 hours) from all DataNodes.
- Single point of failure in Hadoop 1.x (solved in 2.x via High Availability with Standby NameNode).

**2. DataNode (Slave):**
- Runs on every worker node and stores the **actual data blocks** of files.
- Default block size in HDFS is **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x+).
- Sends **heartbeats** to NameNode every **3 seconds** to indicate it is alive.
- Sends **block reports** listing all HDFS blocks it stores, every **6 hours**.
- Performs block creation, deletion, and replication as instructed by the NameNode.

**3. Secondary NameNode (Checkpoint Node):**
- **Misconception:** It is **NOT a backup** of the NameNode.
- It periodically downloads `fsimage` and `edit logs` from the NameNode, **merges** them into a new `fsimage`, and uploads it back. This prevents `edit logs` from growing too large.
- In Hadoop 2.x+, this role is often played by the **Standby NameNode** in an HA setup.

**HDFS Read/Write Path:**

- **Write:** Client requests NameNode → gets DataNode list → writes block in a **pipeline** (DN1 → DN2 → DN3).
- **Read:** Client requests metadata from NameNode → reads from **nearest** DataNode.

**Diagram (Already shown in Section 4.1 & 4.4 — students may redraw a simplified version).**

---

#### Solution to (b) — HDFS Block Calculations

**Given:**
- File size $F = 800$ MB
- Block size $B = 128$ MB
- Replication factor $R = 3$

**Step 1: Number of Blocks**

$$
N_b = \left\lceil \frac{F}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{800}{128} \right\rceil
$$

Computing the division:

$$
\frac{800}{128} = 6.25
$$

Taking the ceiling:

$$
N_b = \lceil 6.25 \rceil = 7 \text{ blocks}
$$

**Step 2: Block-wise distribution (verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block 1} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 2} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 3} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 4} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 5} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 6} &= 128 \text{ MB} \\
\text{Block 7} &= 800 - (6 \times 128) = 800 - 768 = 32 \text{ MB} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification:

$$
128 \times 6 + 32 = 800 \text{ MB} \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Total Physical Storage**

$$
S_{total} = N_b \times B \times R = 7 \times 128 \times 3
$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
7 \times 128 &= 896 \text{ MB} \\
896 \times 3 &= 2688 \text{ MB} \\
&= 2.625 \text{ GB} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{total} = 2688 \text{ MB} = 2.625 \text{ GB}}
$$

**Step 4: Number of Map Tasks**

By default, **one InputSplit per HDFS block**, and **one map task per InputSplit**:

$$
N_{mappers} = N_{splits} = N_b = \boxed{7 \text{ mappers}}
$$

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the role of **Mapper, Combiner, Partitioner, and Reducer**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Hadoop Ecosystem** with a neat diagram. Briefly describe the roles of **HBase, Hive, Pig, Sqoop, Flume, and Oozie**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution to (a) — MapReduce Programming Model

**MapReduce** is a parallel programming model proposed by Google (2004) for processing massive data sets. The user writes only two functions — `map()` and `reduce()` — and the framework handles distribution, fault tolerance, and parallel execution.

**1. Mapper Phase**
- Input: a list of `(key1, value1)` pairs from an InputSplit.
- The user-defined `map()` function emits a list of intermediate `(key2, value2)` pairs.
- Example: For WordCount, `map()` reads a line of text and emits `(word, 1)` for every word.

**2. Combiner (Optional)**
- A **mini-reducer** that runs **locally on the mapper node** after the map phase.
- Aggregates intermediate values for the same key on the same node, drastically **reducing network traffic** during shuffle.
- Applicable only when the reduce function is **commutative and associative** (e.g., sum, max).

**3. Partitioner**
- Decides **which reducer** should receive a given intermediate key.
- Default: `HashPartitioner(key) mod numReducers`.
- Crucial for **load balancing** across reducers.

**4. Shuffle & Sort Phase (Framework-controlled)**
- All intermediate `(K, V)` pairs are **grouped by key** and **sorted** before reaching the reducer.
- This is the **most network-intensive** phase of MapReduce.

**5. Reducer Phase**
- Input: `(key2, list of values)` — the grouped/sorted output of all mappers.
- The user-defined `reduce()` function processes the list and emits the final `(key3, value3)` pairs.
- Example: For WordCount, `reduce()` sums the list `[1, 1, 1]` to emit `(word, 3)`.

**6. Output**
- Final output is written to HDFS via an `OutputFormat` (e.g., `TextOutputFormat`).

**Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3.)*

**MapReduce Execution Example (WordCount):**

| Phase | Key | Value(s) | Output |
|-------|-----|----------|--------|
| Map (input) | 0 | "big data big" | — |
| Map (output) | big | 1; big → 1; data → 1 | 3 pairs |
| Combiner | big | [1,1] | (big, 2) |
| Combiner | data | [1] | (data, 1) |
| Shuffle & Sort | — | — | (big, [2]); (data, [1]) |
| Reduce | big | [2] | (big, 2) |
| Reduce | data | [1] | (data, 1) |

---

#### Solution to (b) — Hadoop Ecosystem

The **Hadoop Ecosystem** is a collection of open-source projects that work with Hadoop to provide specialized services — ingestion, storage, processing, analytics, ML, scheduling, and administration.

**Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid ecosystem diagram in Section 4.2.)*

**Roles of Key Tools:**

**1. HBase** — *Hadoop Database*
- A **NoSQL, columnar, distributed database** running on top of HDFS.
- Provides **real-time random read/write** access to Big Data (HDFS is batch-oriented).
- Inspired by Google's **Bigtable** paper.
- Suitable for sparse data sets and OLTP workloads on Hadoop.

**2. Hive** — *Data Warehouse Infrastructure*
- Provides **SQL-like query interface** (HiveQL / HQL) over HDFS data.
- Translates HQL queries into MapReduce / Tez / Spark jobs.
- Used for **ad-hoc querying, analytics, and ETL** on large structured data.

**3. Pig** — *Scripting Platform for ETL*
- Provides a scripting language called **Pig Latin** for data transformation.
- Ideal for **complex ETL pipelines** where SQL is insufficient.
- Internally compiles Pig Latin scripts into MapReduce / Tez jobs.

**4. Sqoop** — *SQL to Hadoop*
- Tool for **bulk transfer of data between Hadoop (HDFS/Hive/HBase) and RDBMS** (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL).
- Uses JDBC underneath; imports data in parallel using MapReduce.

**5. Flume** — *Log and Event Collection*
- A **distributed, reliable service** for collecting, aggregating, and moving large amounts of **streaming log data** into HDFS/HBase.
- Architecture: Source → Channel → Sink.
- Used for clickstream data, server logs, IoT sensor events.

**6. Oozie** — *Workflow Scheduler*
- A **server-based workflow scheduler** for Hadoop jobs (MapReduce, Pig, Hive, Sqoop).
- Workflows are defined as **XML-based DAGs** (hPDL).
- Supports time-based and data-availability triggers (e.g., cron-like scheduling).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Conflating Secondary NameNode with a backup** — it is NOT a backup; it is a Checkpoint Node.
> 2. **Writing default block size as 64 MB** — that was Hadoop 1.x; for Hadoop 2.x/3.x the default is **128 MB**.
> 3. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in block count — students write $800/128 = 6$ instead of $7$.
> 4. **Conflating MapReduce with Hadoop** — MapReduce is a **module** of Hadoop, not the framework itself.
> 5. **Confusing HBase with Hive** — HBase = NoSQL real-time; Hive = SQL batch warehouse.
> 6. **Writing 1 mapper per file** instead of **1 mapper per block/input split**.
> 7. **Saying "Combiner runs on reducer node"** — Combiner runs on the **same node as the mapper** (local aggregation).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision before KTU exam — read this section twice.**

- ☐ **Hadoop** = open-source Java framework for **distributed storage + processing** of Big Data.
- ☐ Created by **Doug Cutting & Mike Cafarella** (Yahoo!, 2006); inspired by Google GFS + MapReduce papers.
- ☐ **Core motto:** "Write once, read many" (HDFS) + "Move code to data" (data locality).
- ☐ **Three pillars (Hadoop 2.x/3.x):** HDFS (storage), MapReduce (processing), YARN (resource management).
- ☐ **HDFS default block size = 128 MB** (2.x/3.x); replication = **3**.
- ☐ **HDFS components:** NameNode (metadata master), DataNode (block slave), Secondary NameNode (checkpoint only).
- ☐ **NameNode files:** `fsimage` (snapshot) + `edit logs` (changes).
- ☐ **DataNode heartbeat = 3 s**; **block report = 6 h**.
- ☐ **MapReduce flow:** InputSplit → Map → Combiner (optional) → Partitioner → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce → Output.
- ☐ **Number of mappers ≈ number of HDFS blocks**; **reducers are user-set**.
- ☐ **YARN components:** ResourceManager (master) + NodeManager (slave) + ApplicationMaster (per job) + Container.
- ☐ **Ecosystem ingestion:** Sqoop (RDBMS↔HDFS), Flume (logs), Kafka (streaming).
- ☐ **Ecosystem processing:** Spark (in-memory), Pig (Pig Latin), Hive (HQL), Tez (DAG), Impala (real-time SQL).
- ☐ **Ecosystem storage:** HBase (NoSQL columnar), Ozone (object store).
- ☐ **Ecosystem ML:** Mahout, Spark MLlib.
- ☐ **Ecosystem management:** Oozie (workflow), ZooKeeper (coordination), Ambari (UI).
- ☐ **Combiner is optional** but **mandatory-conditions:** reduce function must be **commutative & associative**.
- ☐ **Most expensive phase of MapReduce:** **Shuffle & Sort** (cross-node network transfer).
- ☐ **Total storage formula:** $S_{total} = N_b \times B \times R$.
- ☐ **Block count formula:** $N_b = \lceil F / B \rceil$.
- ☐ Hadoop uses **commodity hardware** (cost-effective) and **Java** language.
- ☐ Default scheduler in YARN: **Capacity Scheduler** (production) or **Fair Scheduler** (shared clusters).
- ☐ Hadoop 3.x added: **Erasure Coding** (saves ~50% storage vs replication), **Multi-NameNode HA**, **GPU support**.
- ☐ Hadoop 3.x minimum supported Java: **Java 8** (Java 11 from 3.3.x+).
- ☐ **Rack Awareness algorithm** distributes replicas across 2 racks for fault tolerance.
- ☐ HDFS follows **WORM model** — Write Once, Read Many; modifications create new files.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** A Weather Dataset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: A Weather Dataset — Foundational Exploratory Data Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Weather Dataset (in Big Data Analytics context):** A *Weather Dataset* refers to a structured, time-series tabular collection of meteorological observations (temperature, humidity, pressure, wind speed, precipitation, etc.) used as a canonical pedagogical artifact for teaching **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**, **DataFrame manipulation**, and **data wrangling** operations using Python's **Pandas** library.

The dataset is typically stored in **CSV (Comma Separated Values)** format with rows representing temporal observations (e.g., daily or hourly readings) and columns representing measured meteorological features. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic falls under the broader umbrella of *Data Preprocessing and Feature Engineering*, which is a critical precursor to advanced analytics and machine learning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Weather Logbook":**
> Imagine a meteorologist maintaining a daily logbook where every row is one day and every column is a measurement (max temperature, min temperature, humidity, wind speed, etc.). The *Weather Dataset* is the digital version of this logbook. Before predicting tomorrow's weather (the *ML model*), the analyst must first **clean the logbook** (remove torn/missing pages), **understand its structure** (how many days, what kind of measurements), and **summarize the patterns** (average rainfall, hottest month). This entire preparatory ritual is what we call **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Weather Data

The following quantities are universally tracked in a weather dataset:

- **Mean Temperature ($\mu_T$):** Arithmetic average of temperature readings.
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma_T$):** Measure of variability/spread in temperature.
- **Humidity (%):** Relative moisture content of air (range: 0% to 100%).
- **Pressure (hPa):** Atmospheric pressure, typically near **1013.25 hPa** at sea level.
- **Wind Speed (km/h or m/s):** Rate of horizontal air movement.
- **Precipitation (mm):** Cumulative water equivalent of rainfall/snowfall.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students are expected to demonstrate the ability to load, inspect, clean, and transform a real-world dataset such as the weather dataset using **Pandas DataFrame operations** as a foundational skill before progressing to distributed analytics (Hadoop, Spark).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Weather Dataset

A canonical weather dataset is composed of the following structural layers:

| **Layer** | **Description** | **Pandas Object** |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Raw Data File** | `.csv` file containing comma-delimited rows | File on disk |
| **Loaded Object** | In-memory tabular representation | `DataFrame` |
| **Single Column** | A single meteorological variable | `Series` |
| **Cell** | A single observation value | Scalar (`int`, `float`, `str`) |
| **Index** | Row labels (default: 0, 1, 2, ... or `DatetimeIndex`) | `Index` object |

### 2.2 Core Pandas Operations on a Weather Dataset

The operational workflow is divided into **five logical stages**:

**Stage 1 — Data Ingestion**
- `pd.read_csv('weather.csv')` — Load the dataset into memory.
- `parse_dates=['Date']` — Parse date columns into `datetime64[ns]` objects.

**Stage 2 — Initial Inspection**
- `df.head(n)` — Display first *n* rows (default 5).
- `df.tail(n)` — Display last *n* rows.
- `df.shape` — Return tuple `(rows, columns)`.
- `df.columns` — List of column names.
- `df.dtypes` — Data type of each column.
- `df.info()` — Concise summary including non-null counts.
- `df.describe()` — Statistical summary of numerical columns.

**Stage 3 — Missing Data Diagnosis**
- `df.isnull()` — Boolean DataFrame marking NaN cells.
- `df.isnull().sum()` — Column-wise count of missing values.
- `df.dropna()` — Remove rows/columns with missing values.
- `df.fillna(value)` — Impute (replace) missing values.

**Stage 4 — Indexing & Selection**
- `df['Temperature']` — Select single column (returns Series).
- `df[['Temp', 'Humidity']]` — Select multiple columns (returns DataFrame).
- `df.loc[label]` — Label-based selection.
- `df.iloc[index]` — Integer-position-based selection.
- `df[df['Temp'] > 30]` — Boolean filtering (Boolean Masking).

**Stage 5 — Statistical Aggregation**
- `df.mean()`, `df.median()`, `df.std()` — Central tendency.
- `df.groupby('Month').mean()` — Group-wise aggregation.
- `df.corr()` — Pairwise correlation matrix.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Table — Statistical Formulas Applied to Weather Data**

| **Formula** | **LaTeX Form** | **Engineering Meaning** |
|-------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Arithmetic Mean | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Average temperature, humidity, etc. |
| Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$ | Spread of weather observations |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Typical deviation from mean |
| Median | $\text{Med} = \begin{cases} x_{(n+1)/2} & n \text{ odd} \\ \frac{x_{(n/2)} + x_{(n/2+1)}}{2} & n \text{ even} \end{cases}$ | Robust central value (immune to outliers) |
| Pearson Correlation | $r = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$ | Linear dependence between two variables |
| Missing Data Imputation (Mean) | $\hat{x}_i = \mu_{\text{column}}$ | Replace NaN with column mean |
| Forward Fill (Time Series) | $\hat{x}_t = x_{t-1}$ | Replace NaN with previous valid reading |
| Z-Score Normalization | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardize for ML pipelines |
| Heat Index (Apparent Temp) | $HI = -42.379 + 2.049 T + 10.143 R - 0.225 TR - 0.007 T^2 - 0.000182 T^2 R$ | "Feels like" temperature |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Markdown Safety:** All absolute values and conditional expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

Weather datasets serve as the **backbone** of:
1. **Climate Change Modeling** — Identifying long-term trends via statistical regression.
2. **Agricultural Planning** — Crop selection based on rainfall/temperature patterns.
3. **Disaster Prediction** — Cyclone, flood, and drought early warning systems.
4. **Energy Demand Forecasting** — Predicting HVAC load based on temperature.
5. **Aviation Safety** — Wind shear and turbulence prediction.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Mean Temperature of a Weather Dataset

Consider a weather dataset with the following 7 daily maximum temperature readings (in °C):

$$T = \{32.5,\ 31.0,\ 33.2,\ 34.1,\ 30.8,\ 29.5,\ 35.0\}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Arithmetic Mean $\mu_T$:**

$$\mu_T = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\mu_T = \frac{1}{7}(32.5 + 31.0 + 33.2 + 34.1 + 30.8 + 29.5 + 35.0)$$

**Step 3 — Sum the observations:**

$$\sum T_i = 32.5 + 31.0 + 33.2 + 34.1 + 30.8 + 29.5 + 35.0 = 226.1$$

**Step 4 — Divide by n = 7:**

$$\mu_T = \frac{226.1}{7} = 32.3 \text{ °C}$$

**Result:** $\mu_T = 32.3\ °C$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Standard Deviation of Humidity

Let the humidity values (%) be: $H = \{65, 70, 68, 72, 60, 75, 80\}$, and $\mu_H = 70$.

**Step 1 — Compute squared deviations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(65 - 70)^2 &= (-5)^2 = 25 \\
(70 - 70)^2 &= (0)^2 = 0 \\
(68 - 70)^2 &= (-2)^2 = 4 \\
(72 - 70)^2 &= (2)^2 = 4 \\
(60 - 70)^2 &= (-10)^2 = 100 \\
(75 - 70)^2 &= (5)^2 = 25 \\
(80 - 70)^2 &= (10)^2 = 100
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the squared deviations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{7}(H_i - \mu_H)^2 = 25 + 0 + 4 + 4 + 100 + 25 + 100 = 258$$

**Step 3 — Compute variance:**

$$\sigma_H^2 = \frac{258}{7} = 36.857 \ \%^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Take square root:**

$$\sigma_H = \sqrt{36.857} \approx 6.071 \ \%$$

**Result:** $\sigma_H \approx 6.07\%$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Pearson Correlation Between Temperature and Humidity

Suppose the paired data is:

| Day | Temp ($T$) | Humidity ($H$) |
|-----|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | 30 | 70 |
| 2 | 32 | 65 |
| 3 | 34 | 60 |
| 4 | 36 | 55 |
| 5 | 38 | 50 |

**Step 1 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{T} = \frac{30+32+34+36+38}{5} = \frac{170}{5} = 34$$

$$\bar{H} = \frac{70+65+60+55+50}{5} = \frac{300}{5} = 60$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator $\sum(T_i - \bar{T})(H_i - \bar{H})$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(30-34)(70-60) &= (-4)(10) = -40 \\
(32-34)(65-60) &= (-2)(5) = -10 \\
(34-34)(60-60) &= (0)(0) = 0 \\
(36-34)(55-60) &= (2)(-5) = -10 \\
(38-34)(50-60) &= (4)(-10) = -40 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = -40 - 10 + 0 - 10 - 40 = -100$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator $\sqrt{\sum(T_i - \bar{T})^2 \cdot \sum(H_i - \bar{H})^2}$:**

$$\sum(T_i - \bar{T})^2 = 16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16 = 40$$

$$\sum(H_i - \bar{H})^2 = 100 + 25 + 0 + 25 + 100 = 250$$

$$\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{40 \times 250} = \sqrt{10000} = 100$$

**Step 4 — Compute correlation coefficient:**

$$r = \frac{-100}{100} = -1.0$$

**Interpretation:** A perfect **negative linear correlation** — as temperature increases, humidity decreases perfectly linearly. This is consistent with real-world physics (hotter air holds moisture as relative humidity drops).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Missing Value Imputation (Mean Strategy)

Suppose a weather dataset has 5 days, and the *Pressure* column contains one missing value (NaN):

$$P = \{1013.2,\ 1012.5,\ \text{NaN},\ 1014.1,\ 1013.8\}$$

**Step 1 — Compute mean of non-missing values:**

$$\mu_P = \frac{1013.2 + 1012.5 + 1014.1 + 1013.8}{4} = \frac{4053.6}{4} = 1013.4 \text{ hPa}$$

**Step 2 — Replace NaN with $\mu_P$:**

$$P_{\text{imputed}} = \{1013.2,\ 1012.5,\ \mathbf{1013.4},\ 1014.1,\ 1013.8\}$$

**Verification:** No information loss in terms of central tendency; alternative strategies include forward-fill ($x_t = x_{t-1}$) or interpolation.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Weather Dataset EDA Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw CSV File] --> B[Load with pd.read_csv]
    B --> C[Initial Inspection]
    C --> C1[df.head and df.tail]
    C --> C2[df.shape and df.columns]
    C --> C3[df.dtypes and df.info]
    C --> C4[df.describe for Stats]
    C1 --> D{Data Quality Check}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D -->|Missing Values Found| E[isnull and sum]
    E --> F{Imputation Strategy}
    F -->|Mean or Median| G[fillna with Mean]
    F -->|Forward Fill| H[fillna method ffill]
    F -->|Drop| I[dropna]
    G --> J[Clean DataFrame]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    D -->|No Missing Values| J
    J --> K[Indexing and Selection]
    K --> K1[Column Selection df col]
    K --> K2[Boolean Masking]
    K --> K3[loc and iloc]
    K1 --> L[Statistical Aggregation]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    L --> L1[GroupBy Month or Year]
    L --> L2[Correlation Matrix]
    L1 --> M[Visualization and Insights]
    L2 --> M
    M --> N[End: EDA Report]
```

### 4.2 DataFrame Memory Layout (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ROW_INDEX["Row Index (DatetimeIndex)"]
        I0[Day 1]
        I1[Day 2]
        I2[Day 3]
        I3[Day 4]
    end
    subgraph COL_T["Column: Temperature"]
        T0[32.5]
        T1[31.0]
        T2[33.2]
        T3[34.1]
    end
    subgraph COL_H["Column: Humidity"]
        H0[65]
        H1[70]
        H2[68]
        H3[72]
    end
    subgraph COL_P["Column: Pressure"]
        P0[1013.2]
        P1[1012.5]
        P2[NaN]
        P3[1014.1]
    end
    I0 --- T0
    I0 --- H0
    I0 --- P0
    I1 --- T1
    I1 --- H1
    I1 --- P1
    I2 --- T2
    I2 --- H2
    I2 --- P2
    I3 --- T3
    I3 --- H3
    I3 --- P3
```

### 4.3 Missing Value Treatment Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Detect Missing Values via isnull] --> B{Percentage Missing}
    B -->|Less than 5 percent| C{Missing Pattern}
    B -->|5 to 40 percent| D[Consider Advanced Imputation]
    B -->|Greater than 40 percent| E[Drop the Column]
    C -->|Random| F[Mean or Median Imputation]
    C -->|Time Series| G[Forward Fill or Interpolation]
    D --> D1[KNN Imputer]
    D --> D2[Regression Imputation]
    D --> D3[Multiple Imputation]
    F --> H[Validate via df.info]
    G --> H
    D1 --> H
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    E --> H
    H --> I[Cleaned DataFrame Ready]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Explain the difference between `df.head()`, `df.tail()`, and `df.info()` methods in Pandas with respect to a weather dataset.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Method** | **Purpose** | **Output Type** | **Use Case** |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|--------------|
| `df.head(n)` | Displays the **first n rows** (default 5) | DataFrame subset | Quick sanity check of loaded data |
| `df.tail(n)` | Displays the **last n rows** (default 5) | DataFrame subset | Verify end of dataset, check for truncation |
| `df.info()` | Prints **concise summary** including dtypes, non-null counts, memory usage | Console text output | Detect missing values and column types |

In a weather dataset, `df.head()` lets a student verify that the date column parsed correctly, while `df.info()` reveals which columns have NaN values (e.g., a missing pressure reading).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any three strategies for handling missing values in a weather dataset. When is each strategy most appropriate?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Drop rows with NaN (`df.dropna()`):** Appropriate when missing values are **<5%** of total rows and **Missing Completely At Random (MCAR)**. Risk: data loss.
2. **Mean/Median Imputation (`df.fillna(df.mean())`):** Appropriate for **numerical, normally-distributed** features like temperature. Use median for skewed data (e.g., precipitation).
3. **Forward Fill (`df.fillna(method='ffill')`):** Appropriate for **time-series weather data** where the current value is likely similar to the previous one (e.g., temperature at consecutive hours).

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

## ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned with Dec 2023 pattern]**

**(a)** Consider a weather dataset containing daily maximum temperatures (°C) for 10 consecutive days: $\{33, 35, 32, 36, 34, 31, 37, 33, 35, 34\}$.
- (i) Compute the **mean** and **median** temperature. (3 Marks)
- (ii) Compute the **variance** and **standard deviation**. (4 Marks)

**(b)** Explain with a suitable Python Pandas code snippet how you would:
- (i) Load a CSV file `weather.csv` containing columns `['Date', 'Temp', 'Humidity', 'Pressure']` into a DataFrame and parse the `Date` column as datetime. (3 Marks)
- (ii) Identify and count the missing values in each column, and impute the missing `Pressure` values with the column mean. (4 Marks)

### Model Solution:

**(a)(i) Mean and Median Computation:**

**Step 1 — Sort the data:**

$$\text{Sorted } T = \{31, 32, 33, 33, 34, 34, 35, 35, 36, 37\}$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean:**

$$\mu_T = \frac{1}{10}(33+35+32+36+34+31+37+33+35+34)$$

$$\mu_T = \frac{1}{10}(340) = 34.0 \ °C$$

**Step 3 — Compute median (n = 10, even):**

$$\text{Median} = \frac{T_{(5)} + T_{(6)}}{2} = \frac{34 + 34}{2} = 34.0 \ °C$$

**Result:** $\mu_T = 34.0\ °C$, $\text{Median} = 34.0\ °C$.

---

**(a)(ii) Variance and Standard Deviation:**

**Step 1 — Compute squared deviations from mean (34):**

$$\begin{aligned}
(33-34)^2 &= 1 \\
(35-34)^2 &= 1 \\
(32-34)^2 &= 4 \\
(36-34)^2 &= 4 \\
(34-34)^2 &= 0 \\
(31-34)^2 &= 9 \\
(37-34)^2 &= 9 \\
(33-34)^2 &= 1 \\
(35-34)^2 &= 1 \\
(34-34)^2 &= 0 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{10}(T_i - \mu_T)^2 = 1+1+4+4+0+9+9+1+1+0 = 30$$

**Step 3 — Variance:**

$$\sigma_T^2 = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0 \ °C^2$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_T = \sqrt{3.0} \approx 1.732 \ °C$$

**Result:** $\sigma_T^2 = 3.0\ °C^2$, $\sigma_T \approx 1.732\ °C$.

---

**(b)(i) Loading the CSV with Datetime Parsing:**

```python
import pandas as pd

# Load the dataset and parse Date as datetime
df = pd.read_csv('weather.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])

# Verify
print(df.head())
print(df.dtypes)
```

**Explanation:** The `parse_dates=['Date']` argument instructs Pandas to interpret the `Date` column as `datetime64[ns]` rather than `object` (string), enabling time-series operations.

---

**(b)(ii) Missing Value Detection and Imputation:**

```python
# Step 1: Identify and count missing values per column
print("Missing values per column:")
print(df.isnull().sum())

# Step 2: Impute missing Pressure values with column mean
df['Pressure'] = df['Pressure'].fillna(df['Pressure'].mean())

# Step 3: Verify no missing values remain
print(df.isnull().sum())
```

**Explanation:**
- `df.isnull()` returns a boolean DataFrame; chaining `.sum()` aggregates NaN counts per column.
- `fillna(df['Pressure'].mean())` replaces every NaN in the `Pressure` column with the arithmetic mean of the non-missing pressure values.
- Verification step confirms the imputation succeeded.

---

## ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Question aligned with May 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) workflow** for a weather dataset. List the key Pandas functions used at each stage. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Given a weather dataset's `Humidity` column with values: $\{60, 65, \text{NaN}, 70, 68, \text{NaN}, 72, 75\}$:
- (i) Apply **mean imputation** and report the resulting series. (3 Marks)
- (ii) Apply **forward fill** and report the resulting series. (2 Marks)
- (iii) Which strategy is more appropriate for time-series weather data? Justify. (2 Marks)

### Model Solution:

**(a) EDA Workflow Diagram and Pandas Functions:**

The EDA workflow for a weather dataset consists of **five sequential stages**:

| **Stage** | **Operations** | **Key Pandas Functions** |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| **1. Data Ingestion** | Load CSV, parse dates | `pd.read_csv()`, `parse_dates` |
| **2. Inspection** | View structure, types, statistics | `df.head()`, `df.tail()`, `df.shape`, `df.info()`, `df.describe()` |
| **3. Cleaning** | Handle missing values, duplicates | `df.isnull()`, `df.dropna()`, `df.fillna()`, `df.drop_duplicates()` |
| **4. Transformation** | Filter, sort, derive new columns | `df.loc[]`, `df.iloc[]`, boolean masking, `df.apply()` |
| **5. Aggregation & Visualization** | Group, correlate, plot | `df.groupby()`, `df.corr()`, `df.plot()`, `df.hist()` |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ingestion] --> B[Inspection]
    B --> C[Cleaning]
    C --> D[Transformation]
    D --> E[Aggregation and Visualization]
    E --> F[Insights]
```

**Engineering Justification:** This staged workflow ensures **reproducibility**, **auditability**, and **defensibility** of any subsequent machine learning model. Skipping the cleaning stage (e.g., failing to handle NaN) will cause most scikit-learn estimators to throw `ValueError`.

---

**(b)(i) Mean Imputation:**

**Step 1 — Identify non-missing values:** $\{60, 65, 70, 68, 72, 75\}$

**Step 2 — Compute mean:**

$$\mu_H = \frac{60+65+70+68+72+75}{6} = \frac{410}{6} \approx 68.333$$

**Step 3 — Replace NaN with $\mu_H$:**

$$H_{\text{imputed}} = \{60,\ 65,\ \mathbf{68.33},\ 70,\ 68,\ \mathbf{68.33},\ 72,\ 75\}$$

---

**(b)(ii) Forward Fill:**

Forward fill propagates the **last valid observation forward**:

$$H_{\text{ffill}} = \{60,\ 65,\ \mathbf{65},\ 70,\ 68,\ \mathbf{68},\ 72,\ 75\}$$

Each NaN is replaced by the immediately preceding non-NaN value.

---

**(b)(iii) Justification:**

> **Forward fill is more appropriate for time-series weather data** because meteorological variables exhibit **temporal autocorrelation** — today's humidity is strongly correlated with yesterday's humidity. Mean imputation, by contrast, injects an artificial "average" value that breaks the temporal continuity and reduces variance artificially. For weather data, methods like **forward fill, backward fill, or linear interpolation** preserve the underlying dynamics and are therefore preferred by meteorologists and climate scientists.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse `df.shape` with `df.describe()`.** `shape` is an *attribute* (no parentheses); `describe()` is a *method* (requires parentheses).
> 2. **Always parse datetime columns explicitly** using `parse_dates=`; otherwise, time-series filtering will fail.
> 3. **Avoid using mean for skewed distributions** (e.g., precipitation) — use median instead.
> 4. **Dropping rows vs. columns:** `df.dropna(axis=0)` drops rows; `df.dropna(axis=1)` drops columns. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 5. **Standard deviation divisor:** Python's `df.std()` uses **n-1 (sample std)** by default (`ddof=1`), not population std. This is a common 1-mark trap.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — A Weather Dataset**

- ✅ A **weather dataset** is a structured, time-series tabular dataset used for teaching EDA in Big Data Analytics.
- ✅ Core Pandas loading function: `pd.read_csv('file.csv', parse_dates=['Date'])`.
- ✅ **Inspection toolkit:** `head()`, `tail()`, `shape`, `columns`, `dtypes`, `info()`, `describe()`.
- ✅ **Missing value detection:** `df.isnull().sum()` is the gold-standard diagnostic.
- ✅ **Three imputation strategies:** (i) Drop (`dropna`), (ii) Mean/Median (`fillna(df.mean())`), (iii) Forward Fill (`fillna(method='ffill')`).
- ✅ **Indexing:** `loc` is **label-based**; `iloc` is **integer-position-based** — never confuse the two.
- ✅ **Boolean masking** is the primary filtering mechanism: `df[df['Temp'] > 30]`.
- ✅ **Mean formula:** $\mu = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum x_i$. **Variance:** $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum(x_i - \mu)^2$. **Std Dev:** $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$.
- ✅ **Pearson Correlation $r$** ranges from $-1$ to $+1$; $r = 0$ implies no linear correlation.
- ✅ **Forward fill** is preferred over mean imputation for **time-series weather data** due to temporal autocorrelation.
- ✅ **GroupBy** is the canonical aggregation pattern: `df.groupby('Month').mean()`.
- ✅ **`df.std()`** uses **sample standard deviation (n-1)** by default — a frequent KTU trap.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** climate modeling, agriculture, disaster prediction, energy forecasting, aviation.
- ✅ Always verify with `df.info()` after cleaning to confirm no NaN remains.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analyzing the Data with Unix Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Analyzing the Data with Unix Tools # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Unix Tools** refer to a rich, pre-installed collection of small, single-purpose **command-line utilities** in Unix/Linux operating systems that operate on **text streams** using a philosophy known as the **"Unix Philosophy"**: *Write programs that do one thing well, work together, and handle text streams as the universal interface.* In the context of **Big Data Analytics (KTU PECPT755)**, Unix tools form the **first layer of data exploration and pre-processing** that an analyst uses *before* invoking heavy distributed frameworks such as **Hadoop, Spark, or Hive**. They excel at **fast, streaming, line-oriented analysis** of semi-structured data (logs, CSV, TSV, JSONL, plain text) with near-zero setup cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** > *Analyzing the Data with Unix Tools — leveraging command-line utilities such as `head`, `tail`, `wc`, `sort`, `uniq`, `cut`, `paste`, `join`, `grep`, `sed`, `awk`, `tr`, and `xargs` to perform preliminary data inspection, summarization, filtering, and transformation on raw datasets.* ## 1.2 The Three Standard Streams Every Unix process is born with three communication channels: | Stream | File Descriptor | Default Destination | Purpose | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **stdin** | **0** | Keyboard | Standard **input** to the program | | **stdout** | **1** | Terminal | Normal **output** of the program | | **stderr** | **2** | Terminal | **Error / diagnostic** messages | The redirection operators are the "control valves" of these streams: $$ \texttt{cmd} \;>\; \texttt{file} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{redirect stdout to file (overwrite)} $$ $$ \texttt{cmd} \;>>\; \texttt{file} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{redirect stdout to file (append)} $$ $$ \texttt{cmd} \;<\; \texttt{file} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{take stdin from file} $$ $$ \texttt{cmd}_1 \;\vert\; \texttt{cmd}_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{pipe: stdout of cmd}_1 \text{ becomes stdin of cmd}_2 $$ ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Modular Kitchen" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition for a First-Time Learner** > Imagine a **professional modular kitchen**: > - Each **knife, peeler, slicer, and blender** does exactly **one** job (e.g., the *slicer* only slices). > - You don't need a giant all-in-one machine; you **connect** these small tools via a **conveyor belt** (the *pipe* $\vert$). > - Raw vegetables go in one end, and a finished salad comes out the other. > - You can **swap** the slicer for a grater at any time without redesigning the kitchen. > - If a vegetable falls off (an *error*), it falls into a separate dustbin (**stderr**), keeping the main conveyor (**stdout**) clean. > > **Unix tools work the same way:** each command is a small specialist, pipes are the conveyor belts, and stdin/stdout/stderr are the input, output, and dustbin respectively. ## 1.4 Why Unix Tools Matter in Big Data > [!TIP] > **Engineering Justification (High Yield for KTU Viva)** > 1. **Zero install footprint** — already present in every Hadoop/Spark edge node. > 2. **Streaming behavior** — tools like `grep`, `awk`, `sed` operate on **one line at a time**, so memory usage is $O(1)$ with respect to file size. > 3. **Composability** — `O(n)` small tools compose into a powerful ETL pipeline. > 4. **Speed** — implemented in C, often **10x–100x faster** than equivalent Python/R scripts for text I/O. > 5. **Foundation for Hadoop Streaming** — the Hadoop `Streaming` API literally accepts Unix commands as mappers/reducers. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Unix Filter Pattern (Core Architecture) A **filter** is any command that **reads stdin, transforms it, and writes the result to stdout**. The generic pipeline is: $$ \text{Producer} \;\longrightarrow\; \big[\, F_1 \,\big] \;\longrightarrow\; \big[\, F_2 \,\big] \;\longrightarrow\; \cdots \;\longrightarrow\; \big[\, F_n \,\big] \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Result} $$ where each $F_i$ is a **filter**. The pipeline executes in parallel where the OS allows: $F_2$ can begin processing lines as soon as $F_1$ produces them, achieving **streaming concurrency** without threads. ## 2.2 Detailed Tool Taxonomy ### (A) Inspection & Viewing Tools | Command | Purpose | Typical Usage | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `cat` | Dump entire file to stdout | `cat data.txt` | | `less` / `more` | Paged viewer (read-only) | `less huge.log` | | `head` | First **N** lines | `head -n 10 data.csv` | | `tail` | Last **N** lines; `-f` follows live | `tail -f server.log` | ### (B) Measurement Tools | Command | Purpose | Output Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `wc -l` | Count **lines** | Number of records | | `wc -w` | Count **words** | Token frequency basis | | `wc -c` | Count **bytes** | Storage size | | `wc -m` | Count **characters** | Multilingual text | ### (C) Sorting & Counting | Command | Purpose | Key Flags | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `sort` | Sort lines lexicographically or numerically | `-n` numeric, `-r` reverse, `-k` key, `-t` delimiter | | `uniq` | Collapse **adjacent** duplicates | `-c` count, `-d` duplicates only, `-u` unique only | > [!WARNING] > `uniq` only merges **adjacent** identical lines. You **must** `sort` first if duplicates are scattered. ### (D) Column & Field Manipulation | Command | Purpose | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `cut` | Extract character ranges or fields | `cut -d',' -f1,3 data.csv` | | `paste` | Merge files **side-by-side** (horizontal join) | `paste a.txt b.txt` | | `join` | Relational-style **join on a key field** | `join -t',' -1 1 -2 1 a.csv b.csv` | | `tr` | **Translate / delete / squeeze** characters | `tr 'a-z' 'A-Z'` (case fold) | ### (E) Pattern Matching & Transformation | Command | Purpose | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `grep` | Filter lines matching a **regex** | `grep -E "ERROR" log` | | `sed` | **Stream editor** — substitution, deletion, insertion | `sed 's/foo/bar/g' file` | | `awk` | Full **pattern–action** language; column-aware | `awk -F',' '$3 > 100' data.csv` | ### (F) Workflow & Orchestration | Command | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | | `xargs` | Convert **stdin into command arguments** for tools that don't accept piped streams | | `find` | Recursively locate files by name, type, size, mtime | | `tee` | **T-junction**: send stdin to both a file *and* stdout | ## 2.3 The "KTU High-Yield" Operator Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the table below — these appear in **at least one Part-A question every semester**. | Operator | Syntax | Semantics | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Pipe | `a` | `b` | stdout of $a$ → stdin of $b$ | `cat f` | `grep x` | | Output Redirect | `a > f` | stdout of $a$ → file $f$ (overwrite) | `ls > list.txt` | | Append Redirect | `a >> f` | stdout of $a$ → file $f$ (append) | `echo x >> log` | | Input Redirect | `a < f` | file $f$ → stdin of $a$ | `sort < names.txt` | | Error Redirect | `a 2> f` | stderr of $a$ → file $f$ | `cmd 2> err.log` | | Combined | `a > out 2> err` | Both streams split into files | — | | Tee | `a | tee f | b` | Branch stream to file and pipe | `cat f | tee copy.log | grep x` | | Process Subst. | `a <(cmd)` | Treat output of `cmd` as a file | `diff <(sort a) <(sort b)` | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Data Engineering**: `awk`, `sed`, `grep` perform **schema-on-read cleanup** before HDFS ingestion. - **SRE / DevOps**: `tail -f` + `grep` is the de-facto **log-tailing** stack. - **Hadoop Streaming**: Custom MapReduce jobs can be written in any language that reads stdin and writes stdout — *exactly* the Unix filter contract. - **Bioinformatics**: `awk`, `grep` operate on FASTA/FASTQ files (sometimes **terabytes** of genomic data) at memory-limited speed. - **Cybersecurity**: `grep -E` with PCRE is the backbone of **incident triage** on packet dumps. ## 2.5 Limitations (Honest Engineering Trade-offs) > [!WARNING] > **Boundary Conditions / When Unix Tools FAIL** > 1. **Single-machine throughput** — bounded by RAM and disk I/O. Datasets larger than ~**100 GB** on a single node become slow. > 2. **No parallel processing** within a tool (must use `xargs -P` or `GNU parallel` to fan-out). > 3. **No distributed coordination** — no built-in concept of nodes, replication, or fault tolerance. > 4. **Weak type system** — everything is text; numeric aggregation requires `awk -v` tricks. > 5. **CSV fragility** — embedded commas/newlines in fields break naïve `cut`/`awk` parsing. These limitations are precisely *why* we transition to **Hadoop, Spark, and Hive** in later modules. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Worked Examples > [!NOTE] > All examples below operate on a representative **web-server access log** (one of the most common Big-Data analytical inputs). The file `access.log` is assumed to be in **Common Log Format (CLF)**. ``` 127.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 10.0.0.5 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:37] "GET /login HTTP/1.1" 200 512 10.0.0.5 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:38] "POST /api/data HTTP/1.1" 500 128 192.168.1.20 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:39] "GET /home HTTP/1.1" 404 0 127.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:40] "GET /static/a.js HTTP/1.1" 200 1024 10.0.0.5 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:41] "GET /favicon.ico HTTP/1.1" 200 318 192.168.1.20 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:42] "POST /api/data HTTP/1.1" 500 128 10.0.0.5 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:43] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 ``` --- ## 3.1 Example 1 — Record Count & Field Inspection **Task:** Count the number of requests and print the **first 3** and **last 2** lines. **Step 1 — Total line count:** $$ \texttt{wc -l access.log} $$ This counts the newline characters. The result prints three numbers (lines, words, bytes), e.g.: ``` 8 96 768 access.log ``` **Step 2 — First 3 records:** $$ \texttt{head -n 3 access.log} $$ **Step 3 — Last 2 records (proves stream-style operation):** $$ \texttt{tail -n 2 access.log} $$ > [!TIP] > `tail -f access.log` (follow mode) keeps the pipe **open** and prints new lines as they are appended by the web server — a classic real-time monitoring trick. --- ## 3.2 Example 2 — Top-5 Client IPs by Request Volume **Task:** Identify the **5 client IPs that issued the most requests**. The IP address is the **first whitespace-delimited field**, so we use `awk` with the default delimiter (whitespace collapses runs). **Step-by-step pipeline construction:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$1\}' access.log} $$ > *Logic:* For every line, print the first field ($\$1$), which is the client IP. **Step 2 — Sort the IPs so duplicates become adjacent:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$1\}' access.log \; \vert \; \texttt{sort} $$ **Step 3 — Collapse duplicates and count occurrences:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$1\}' access.log \; \vert \; \texttt{sort} \; \vert \; \texttt{uniq -c} $$ **Step 4 — Sort by count in descending numeric order:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$1\}' access.log \; \vert \; \texttt{sort} \; \vert \; \texttt{uniq -c} \; \vert \; \texttt{sort -rn} $$ > *Logic:* `-r` reverses, `-n` forces **numeric** (not lexicographic) comparison. Without `-n`, "100" would sort before "9". **Step 5 — Keep the top 5:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$1\}' access.log \; \vert \; \texttt{sort} \; \vert \; \texttt{uniq -c} \; \vert \; \texttt{sort -rn} \; \vert \; \texttt{head -n 5} $$ **Expected output:** ``` 3 10.0.0.5 2 127.0.0.1 2 192.168.1.20 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The General Pattern — remember this 5-stage template for ANY "Top-K" problem:** > $$\text{extract} \;\rightarrow\; \text{sort} \;\rightarrow\; \text{uniq -c} \;\rightarrow\; \text{sort -rn} \;\rightarrow\; \text{head -n K}$$ --- ## 3.3 Example 3 — Error-Rate Analysis (HTTP 4xx/5xx) **Task:** Count the number of **HTTP 5xx (server error)** responses. The status code is the **9th whitespace-delimited field** in CLF. **Step 1 — Extract the status code column:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$9\}' access.log} $$ **Step 2 — Keep only lines starting with "5":** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$9\}' access.log} \; \vert \; \texttt{grep '\^{}5'} $$ > *Logic:* `'\^{}5'` is an **Extended Regex** anchor matching any string *starting* with `5`. **Step 3 — Count them:** $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$9\}' access.log} \; \vert \; \texttt{grep '\^{}5'} \; \vert \; \texttt{wc -l} $$ **Result for our sample:** `2` > [!TIP] > **Engineering nuance:** The `\^{}` anchor matters! Without it, `grep 5` would also match the byte `5` *anywhere* in the line (e.g., a URL containing "5"). The `^` anchors to line start. --- ## 3.4 Example 4 — URL Extraction & Frequency **Task:** Find the **3 most-requested URLs**. The URL lives inside double quotes and is field **\$7** in CLF. $$ \texttt{awk '\{print \$7\}' access.log} \; \vert \; \texttt{sort} \; \vert \; \texttt{uniq -c} \; \vert \; \texttt{sort -rn} \; \vert \; \texttt{head -n 3} $$ --- ## 3.5 Example 5 — Per-Hour Bandwidth Aggregation with `awk` **Task:** Compute the **total bytes served per hour** from the log. The hour is inside square brackets, e.g., `[10/Oct/2024:13:55:36]`. We use `awk`'s `split()` and an **associative array** (its built-in hash map). **Pipeline:** ```bash awk '{ # Field 4 looks like: [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36] split($4, a, /[:\/[]/); # a[2]=day, a[3]=mon, a[4]=year, a[5]=hour hour = a[5]; bytes[hour] += $10; # $10 is the response size } END { for (h in bytes) print h, bytes[h]; }' access.log ``` **Execution trace (line-by-line):** For the first record `127.0.0.1 - - [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326`: - `split($4, a, /[:\/[]/)` tokenizes `[10/Oct/2024:13:55:36]` on `:` `/` or `[` → `a = { "", "10", "Oct", "2024", "13", "55", "36" }`. - `hour = a[5]` = `"13"`. - `bytes["13"] += 2326` → `bytes["13"] = 2326`. After all 8 lines are processed, the `END` block prints: ``` 13 6844 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **complete map-only aggregation in a single awk program** — no pipes required! This is exactly the pattern reused in **Hadoop Streaming** and **Spark `mapPartitions`**. --- ## 3.6 Example 6 — Substituting & Cleaning with `sed` **Task:** Convert all dates from `10/Oct/2024` format to ISO `2024-10-10` format. $$ \texttt{sed -E 's!([0-9]+)/([A-Za-z]+)/([0-9]+)!\3-\2-\1!g' access.log} $$ > *Logic:* `sed -E` enables Extended Regex. The `!` delimiter avoids escaping `/`. Captures day/month/year, then rear-ranges them. This is non-destructive by default. To overwrite the file **in place**, add `-i`: $$ \texttt{sed -i -E 's!([0-9]+)/([A-Za-z]+)/([0-9]+)!\3-\2-\1!g' access.log} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Unix Pipeline as a Data-Flow Architecture The diagram below illustrates how a typical **"Top-K IP" analytics pipeline** is wired together. Each box is an independent filter; arrows represent the **pipe** ($\vert$) operator and stream direction. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef sourceNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1 classDef filterNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#bf360c classDef sinkNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1b5e20 classDef countNode fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px,color:#4a148c fileA["access.log
RAW FILE
8 lines, 768 bytes"]:::sourceNode awkA["FILTER 1: awk
print first column IP
FIELD EXTRACTION"]:::filterNode sortA["FILTER 2: sort
group identical IPs
LEXICOGRAPHIC SORT"]:::filterNode uniqA["FILTER 3: uniq -c
merge adjacent and count
FREQUENCY TABLE"]:::countNode sortB["FILTER 4: sort -rn
sort by count descending
NUMERIC REVERSE"]:::filterNode headA["FILTER 5: head -n 5
keep top 5 entries
TRUNCATION"]:::filterNode resultA["TOP 5 CLIENT IPs
FINAL RESULT
ranked list"]:::sinkNode fileA -- "stdin
READ 8 LINES" --> awkA awkA -- "stdout
8 IP TOKENS" --> sortA sortA -- "stdout
sorted tokens" --> uniqA uniqA -- "stdout
IP count pairs" --> sortB sortB -- "stdout
descending ranks" --> headA headA -- "stdout
5 lines" --> resultA ``` ## 4.2 Anatomy of a Single Filter Process (Block Diagram) Every filter is a tiny virtual machine with three streams. The kernel mediates all I/O. ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef kern fill:#263238,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#eceff1 classDef proc fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#e65100 classDef io fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#01579b kernelA["UNIX KERNEL
process scheduler & I/O multiplexer"]:::kern procA["FILTER PROCESS
awk or grep or sort
user-space program"]:::proc stdinA["fd 0: STDIN
reads from previous
pipe or file"]:::io stdoutA["fd 1: STDOUT
writes to next
pipe or file"]:::io stderrA["fd 2: STDERR
errors to terminal
or 2> redirect"]:::io stdinA -- "pipe read" --> procA procA -- "pipe write" --> stdoutA procA -- "diagnostic" --> stderrA kernelA -. "schedules & buffers" .- procA ``` ## 4.3 The "Top-K" Pattern as a Reusable Block The same 5-stage structure is the **template** for almost every "most-frequent" query. Memorize it. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef stage1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1 classDef stage2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20 classDef stage3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#f57f17 classDef stage4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315,color:#bf360c classDef stage5 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,color:#880e4f s1["STAGE 1
EXTRACT
awk or cut"]:::stage1 s2["STAGE 2
SORT
sort"]:::stage2 s3["STAGE 3
COUNT
uniq -c"]:::stage3 s4["STAGE 4
RANK
sort -rn"]:::stage4 s5["STAGE 5
TRUNCATE
head -n K"]:::stage5 s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 ``` ## 4.4 Memory & Streaming Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Viva Favorite — "Why are Unix tools so fast on huge files?"** > > Because each filter holds **only one line in memory** at a time. The kernel maintains a small (typically **64 KB**) **pipe buffer** between stages. Total memory usage is therefore $O(\text{line length})$, not $O(\text{file size})$ — a **streaming** model. ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef buf fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00695c,color:#004d40 classDef pipe fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#bf360c procX["PROCESS X
line in registers"]:::buf pipeBuf["PIPE BUFFER
64 KB kernel queue
holds ~thousands of lines"]:::pipe procY["PROCESS Y
consumes 1 line at a time"]:::buf procX -- "writes 1 line" --> pipeBuf pipeBuf -- "reads 1 line" --> procY ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Explain the Unix philosophy of "small tools connected by pipes" with two advantages relevant to big-data analytics.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Unix philosophy advocates building many **small, single-purpose programs** that read from **stdin**, write to **stdout**, and can be chained via the **pipe** operator to perform complex tasks. **Advantages for big data:** 1. **Composability** — Complex ETL pipelines (extract → sort → count → rank) are built by chaining 2–4 line tools, making them easy to write, test, and reuse. 2. **Streaming / constant memory** — Each tool processes one line at a time, so memory usage is $O(1)$ in file size, allowing analysis of multi-GB logs on modest hardware. 3. *(Bonus)* **Zero-installation** — these tools ship with every Linux distribution, including Hadoop/Spark edge nodes. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage, 3 total.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between `sort` and `uniq`. Why must `sort` precede `uniq` in a typical frequency-count pipeline?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | `sort` | `uniq` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Function | Reorders lines lexicographically or numerically | Removes/collapses **adjacent** duplicate lines | | Key flag | `-n` numeric, `-r` reverse, `-k` key field | `-c` prefix count, `-d` show duplicates only | | Operates on | All lines (global sort) | **Only the immediately previous line** | **Why sort first:** `uniq` compares *line $i$* with *line $i{-}1$*. If duplicate values are scattered throughout the file, `uniq` will **miss** them. `sort` brings all identical lines together, making them adjacent and hence detectable by `uniq`. *(1 mark for tabular distinction, 2 marks for the adjacency explanation.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE rule: *Answer **ONE** full question from the Module-3 internal choice. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.* --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]** #### (a) [7 Marks] Unix Pipeline Analysis on a CSV Dataset Given the file `sales.csv` with header `region,product,units,revenue` and 1 million records, write a **single Unix pipeline** to compute the **total revenue per region** and display the top-3 regions by revenue. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Skip the header row (it would corrupt our sum):** $$ \texttt{tail -n +2 sales.csv} $$ > *Logic:* `tail -n +2` starts output from line 2 onwards, skipping the header. **Step 2 — Extract the region (field 1) and revenue (field 4):** $$ \texttt{tail -n +2 sales.csv} \; \vert \; \texttt{awk -F',' '\{print \$1\",\"\$4\}'} $$ > *Logic:* We rebuild a 2-column stream "region,revenue" so subsequent stages have a clean schema. **Step 3 — Sort by region to group identical regions adjacent:** $$ \texttt{...} \; \vert \; \texttt{sort -t',' -k1,1} $$ **Step 4 — Aggregate (sum) revenue per region using `awk`:** $$ \texttt{...} \; \vert \; \texttt{awk -F',' '\{sum[\$1]+=\$2\} END \{for (r in sum) print r\",\"sum[r]\}'} $$ > *Logic:* Associative array `sum` keyed by region; the `END` block prints the totals. **Step 5 — Sort numerically descending by revenue and keep top 3:** $$ \texttt{...} \; \vert \; \texttt{sort -t',' -k2,2 -rn} \; \vert \; \texttt{head -n 3} $$ **Final pipeline (composed):** ```bash tail -n +2 sales.csv \ | awk -F',' '{print $1","$4}' \ | sort -t',' -k1,1 \ | awk -F',' '{sum[$1]+=$2} END {for (r in sum) print r","sum[r]}' \ | sort -t',' -k2,2 -rn \ | head -n 3 ``` *(Mark split: 1 mark per step × 5 = 5 marks; 2 marks for correct flag explanation and final assembled pipeline.)* #### (b) [7 Marks] The `awk` Pattern-Action Language **Explain the structure of an `awk` program. Using the file `marks.txt` where each line is `name:mark`, write an `awk` command to (i) print the name and grade (A: ≥ 90, B: 75–89, C: 60–74, F: < 60), and (ii) compute the class average.** **Model Solution:** **Structure of an `awk` program:** $$ \texttt{awk 'pattern \{ action \}' file} $$ An `awk` program is a sequence of `pattern { action }` statements. Special patterns `BEGIN` (runs before any input) and `END` (runs after all input) exist. Fields are referenced as `\$1, \$2, \ldots` and the whole line as `\$0`. **(i) Grade assignment using a chain of `if-else`:** ```bash awk -F':' '{ if ($2 >= 90) g = "A"; else if ($2 >= 75) g = "B"; else if ($2 >= 60) g = "C"; else g = "F"; print $1, g; }' marks.txt ``` > *Logic:* `$1` = name, `$2` = mark. The first matching branch assigns grade; `print` emits the result. **(ii) Class average using `END` block:** ```bash awk -F':' '{ total += $2; n++ } END { printf("Average = %.2f over %d students\n", total/n, n) }' marks.txt ``` > *Logic:* `total` accumulates marks, `n` counts records. The `END` block triggers once after EOF and divides. `%.2f` formats the average to two decimals. *(Mark split: 2 marks structure, 2 marks grade logic, 2 marks average, 1 mark for `END` explanation.)* --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### (a) [7 Marks] Streaming and Redirection in Practice **Describe the three standard streams of a Unix process. With an example, explain how `>`, `>>`, `<`, `2>`, and the pipe operator can be combined in a single command to log errors separately and forward clean output to another command.** **Model Solution:** **Three standard streams:** | Stream | FD | Default | Purpose | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- | | stdin | 0 | Keyboard | Program input | | stdout | 1 | Terminal | Program normal output | | stderr | 2 | Terminal | Program error/diagnostic output | **Combined example:** ```bash grep "ERROR" server.log > matched.txt 2> grep-errors.log | wc -l > error-count.txt ``` > *Logic:* > - `grep "ERROR" server.log` — searches the log. > - `> matched.txt` — **redirects stdout** (the matched lines) into `matched.txt`, overwriting. > - `2> grep-errors.log` — **redirects stderr** (e.g., "Permission denied" if the file is missing) to a *separate* log so the error stream does not pollute the data stream. > - `| wc -l` — pipes the matched lines to `wc -l` to count them. (Note: because `>` already captured stdout, the pipe receives a copy.) > - `> error-count.txt` — writes the count to a file. **Another classic pattern — `tee`:** ```bash make build 2>&1 | tee build.log ``` Here `2>&1` **merges stderr into stdout** so both go to the log *and* the terminal — useful for live monitoring with persistent archival. *(Mark split: 2 marks for stream description, 3 marks for operator semantics, 2 marks for the combined example.)* #### (b) [7 Marks] `sed` vs `grep` vs `awk` — Comparative Analysis **Compare `grep`, `sed`, and `awk` in terms of (i) primary purpose, (ii) whether they modify lines or only filter, (iii) ability to compute on fields, and (iv) typical use case. Write a single command to extract from `employees.txt` (format: `id:name:dept:salary`) the average salary of employees in the `IT` department.** **Model Solution:** | Aspect | `grep` | `sed` | `awk` | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | (i) Purpose | Filter lines by regex | Stream-edit (substitute/delete/insert) | Full pattern-action scripting | | (ii) Modifies? | No (passes through unchanged) | **Yes** — in-place text rewriting | Yes — can rewrite or compute | | (iii) Field math | No | Limited (via back-references) | **Yes** — native arithmetic | | (iv) Typical use | Log filtering | Find-and-replace, column swap | Aggregation, reporting, ETL | **Single command for average IT salary:** ```bash awk -F':' '$3 == "IT" { sum += $4; n++ } END { if (n>0) printf("Avg IT salary = %.2f (n=%d)\n", sum/n, n); else print "No IT employees" }' employees.txt ``` > *Logic:* > - `awk -F':'` sets the field separator to colon. > - `pattern $3 == "IT"` matches only IT-department records. > - `action { sum += $4; n++ }` accumulates salary and counts. > - `END { ... }` computes and prints the average. The `if (n>0)` guard prevents division-by-zero. *(Mark split: 2 marks table, 3 marks command logic, 2 marks for the `END` block and edge case.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose 2–4 Marks** > 1. **Forgetting `sort` before `uniq`** — Examiners **deduct 1 mark** per occurrence if your frequency table is wrong because of missing `sort`. > 2. **Lexicographic vs numeric sort** — `sort -k2` sorts `100` *before* `9`. Always use `-n` when sorting counts. This error costs **2 marks** in Part B. > 3. **Using `wc` to count matched lines without `grep -c`** — `grep "x" | wc -l` works but is slower; using `grep -c "x"` is preferred and earns the "efficiency" mark. > 4. **Not skipping the CSV header** — When the dataset has a header row, your `awk` summation includes the word "revenue" as a numeric value, polluting the result. **Always use `tail -n +2`** to skip the header. > 5. **Misuse of `$0`, `$1`, `$NF`** — `$NF` means "Number of Fields" (last field), not "first field". Misusing these loses the field-extraction marks. > 6. **Confusing `>` (overwrite) with `>>` (append)** — Examiners test this directly with a question like "What happens if the command is re-run?" The correct answer is "overwrite for `>`, accumulate for `>>`". --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!TIP] > **Print this section and revise 1 hour before the exam.** - **Unix Philosophy** = small tools + text streams + pipes. - **Three standard streams**: stdin (fd 0), stdout (fd 1), stderr (fd 2). - **Redirection operators** — `>` overwrite, `>>` append, `<` input, `2>` error, `|` pipe, `tee` branch. - **Inspection tools** — `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `less`, `more`. - **Measurement** — `wc -l -w -c -m`. - **Sorting** — `sort -n -r -k -t`; **always sort before** `uniq`. - **Uniqueness** — `uniq -c -d -u`; only collapses **adjacent** duplicates. - **Column ops** — `cut -d -f`, `paste`, `join -1 -2 -t`. - **Character ops** — `tr 'a-z' 'A-Z'`, `tr -d`, `tr -s`. - **Pattern tools** — `grep -E -v -i -c -n` (POSIX/Extended regex). - **Stream editor** — `sed 's/pat/rep/g'`, `-i` in-place, `-E` extended. - **Field-aware scripting** — `awk -F 'pattern{action}'`, `BEGIN`, `END`, `printf`. - **The "Top-K" template**: `extract | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -n K`. - **Streamlining & parallelism** — `xargs -n -P` and `find -exec`. - **Hadoop Streaming link** — `mapper | sort | reducer` is *literally* a Unix pipeline. - **Memory model** — Constant memory per filter, kernel-buffered pipe (~**64 KB**). - **Limitations** — Single-node, no fault tolerance, weak types, fragile CSV parsing. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Analyzing the Data with Unix Tools**

topics.title: Analyzing the Data with Hadoop (Map and Reduce)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analyzing the Data with Hadoop (Map and Reduce)

# 📘 Module 3 — Analyzing the Data with Hadoop (Map and Reduce)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MapReduce** is a distributed data-processing programming model and software framework, introduced by Google in 2004 (Dean & Ghemawat), and adopted by Apache Hadoop as its primary computational engine. It enables the parallel processing of massive, unstructured/semi-structured datasets across large clusters of commodity hardware by abstracting computation into two principal user-defined functions: the **`map()`** function and the **`reduce()`** function, with an implicit shuffle-and-sort phase between them.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **"Analyzing the Data with Hadoop (Map and Reduce)"** refers to the end-to-end analytical pipeline in which raw, voluminous input data (stored in **HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System**) is decomposed into logical splits, transformed in parallel by *Mapper* tasks, redistributed (shuffled) and sorted across the cluster, and finally aggregated by *Reducer* tasks to produce the analytical result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Entities You Must Know for KTU Exams**
> - **Job** — The complete unit of work submitted by the client.
> - **JobTracker / ResourceManager (YARN)** — The master daemon that coordinates and assigns tasks.
> - **TaskTracker / NodeManager (YARN)** — The slave daemon that executes map/reduce tasks on each worker node.
> - **Mapper Task** — Invokes the user-defined `map()` function on each key-value pair.
> - **Reducer Task** — Invokes the user-defined `reduce()` function on each grouped key-value set.
> - **Combiner** — A localized, mini-reducer that runs on the mapper node to pre-aggregate output.
> - **Partitioner** — Decides which reducer a key-value pair is routed to.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The National Election Count

Imagine you are tasked with counting the **total number of votes received by each political party** across **all 10,000 polling booths** in a country.

| Step | Real World (Election) | Hadoop MapReduce |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Divide ballots into 10,000 boxes (one per booth) | **HDFS** splits file into logical **InputSplits** (one per block) |
| 2 | Send each box to a separate counting center (parallel) | Each **Mapper task** runs on the node that holds the split (**Data Locality**) |
| 3 | Each center counts votes booth-wise | `map()` emits intermediate `(party, 1)` pairs |
| 4 | Boxes are shipped to a few regional hubs, grouped by party | **Shuffle & Sort** phase — groups all keys |
| 5 | Each regional hub tallies one party's total | `reduce()` sums counts for each party |
| 6 | Final national tally published | Output written back to **HDFS** |

> **In essence:** *Map* = "do the same small job on your own piece of data" (parallel/distributed), and *Reduce* = "combine all the small answers into one big answer" (aggregation).

---

### 1.3 Standard Hadoop Metrics & Defaults (Bold for Emphasis)

| Parameter | **Default Value** | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| HDFS Block Size | **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x), **256 MB** (Hadoop 3.x) | Determines split boundaries |
| Replication Factor | **3** | Fault tolerance (1 original + 2 replicas) |
| Number of Mappers | **Equal to number of InputSplits** | Determined by `InputFormat` |
| Number of Reducers | **1 by default**, configurable via `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)` | User decision |
| Task Slot (Map) | **2** per TaskTracker (Hadoop 1.x) | Concurrency limit |
| Container Size (YARN) | **1 GB** default | Resource allocation unit |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The MapReduce Programming Model — Stepwise Logic

The complete MapReduce execution consists of **four sequential phases**:

#### **Phase 1 — Input Splitting & Record Reading**
- The input file on HDFS is divided into **InputSplits** (typically one per HDFS block).
- Each split is processed by a single **Mapper**.
- A **`RecordReader`** converts the byte-oriented split into logical `(K1, V1)` records.
  - For text input: `K1 = byte offset (LongWritable)`, `V1 = line of text (Text)`.

#### **Phase 2 — Mapping (User-Defined `map()`)**
- Signature: `map(K1 key, V1 value, Context context)`
- The mapper emits a list of intermediate key-value pairs: `List<(K2, V2)>`.
- **Output of map is NOT written to HDFS** — it is written to the **local disk** of the mapper node as a circular buffer that spills to disk when 80% full.
- Output is partitioned by the **Partitioner** (`HashPartitioner` by default: `hash(K2) mod numReducers`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why local disk and not HDFS?** Writing to HDFS would incur triple replication overhead for temporary data — wasteful.

#### **Phase 3 — Shuffle & Sort (Framework-Handled)**
- **Shuffle**: Intermediate output from all mappers is transferred across the network to the reducer nodes. The key routing is decided by the Partitioner.
- **Sort**: Within each reducer, all key-value pairs are grouped by key and sorted (default: ascending). This is essentially the *group-by-key* operation of SQL.
- **Merge**: If multiple map outputs exist, they are merged using a circular merge sort.

#### **Phase 4 — Reducing (User-Defined `reduce()`)**
- Signature: `reduce(K2 key, Iterable<V2> values, Context context)`
- For each unique `K2`, the reducer iterates over all `V2` values and emits a final list: `List<(K3, V3)>`.
- Output is written to HDFS (typically under `/user/<username>/output/part-r-00000`).

---

### 2.2 The Two Pillars — `map()` and `reduce()` in Detail

#### **A. The Mapper Class**

```java
public static class MyMapper 
       extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context) 
           throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        // user logic — emit (K2, V2) pairs
        context.write(new Text("word"), new IntWritable(1));
    }
}
```

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| `Mapper<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` | Generic base class with 4 type parameters |
| `setup()` | Called once before processing — for initialization |
| `map()` | Called **once per record** (K1, V1) |
| `cleanup()` | Called once after all records — for resource release |
| `run()` | Override to control iteration order |

#### **B. The Reducer Class**

```java
public static class MyReducer 
       extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {
    public void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context) 
           throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable val : values) sum += val.get();
        context.write(key, new IntWritable(sum));
    }
}
```

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` | Generic base class |
| `setup()` | Called once before any reduce — for global initialization |
| `reduce()` | Called **once per unique key** with iterable values |
| `cleanup()` | Called once after all keys processed |

---

### 2.3 The Supporting Cast — Combiner, Partitioner, Counter

#### **Combiner (Local Reducer)**
- Runs **on the mapper node**, after the map output is sorted.
- Performs **local aggregation** to **reduce network traffic**.
- **Contract**: Combiner input/output types MUST match reducer input/output types.
- **Example**: Word count — instead of sending 1000 `(hello, 1)` pairs, send `(hello, 1000)`.

#### **Partitioner**
- Decides which reducer receives which key.
- Default: `HashPartitioner` → `partition = (key.hashCode() & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % numReducers`.
- Custom partitioners allow **data skew handling** and **range partitioning**.

#### **Counters**
- Lightweight global counters defined by the framework (`MAP_INPUT_RECORDS`, `REDUCE_INPUT_GROUPS`) or by the user via `context.getCounter("Group", "Name").increment(1)`.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| **Split Size Calculation** | $splitSize = \max(minSize, \min(maxSize, blockSize))$ | `minSize` = `mapreduce.input.fileinputformat.split.minsize`; `maxSize` = `mapreduce.input.fileinputformat.split.maxsize` |
| **Number of Mappers** | $M = \left\lceil \dfrac{fileSize}{splitSize} \right\rceil$ | One mapper per split |
| **Total HDFS Storage Used** | $Storage = fileSize \times replicationFactor$ | Default replication = **3** |
| **Data Locality Preference** | $Locality \in \{Node, Rack, Off\text{-}switch\}$ | Hadoop prefers node-local → rack-local → off-switch |
| **Combiner Constraint** | $Type_{combiner\_in} = Type_{combiner\_out} = Type_{reducer\_in}$ | Mandatory type compatibility |
| **Partitioner Function (Default)** | $p(k) = \left( hash(k) \;\&\; 2^{31}-1 \right) \bmod R$ | $R$ = number of reducers |
| **Reducer Input Groups** | $G = \left\vert \text{Unique } K2 \right\vert$ | Equals `REDUCE_INPUT_GROUPS` counter |
| **Speculative Execution** | $T_{slow} > \alpha \cdot \overline{T}$ (typical threshold) | Framework launches backup task for stragglers |
| **Map Task Output Records** | $\sum_{i=1}^{M} \vert Output_i \vert$ | = `MAP_OUTPUT_RECORDS` counter |
| **Sort Phase Complexity (in-reducer)** | $O(N \log N)$ per reducer | $N$ = total values received |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** MapReduce powers search engine indexing (the original Google use case), log analytics, ETL pipelines, recommendation preprocessing, and is the conceptual ancestor of Apache Spark's transformations and actions. The `map` → `shuffle` → `reduce` paradigm maps directly to **SQL's `GROUP BY` aggregation** and is the foundation of all modern distributed dataflow engines.

---

### 2.5 Data Types in Hadoop (Writable Framework)

Hadoop uses its own serialization mechanism — **`org.apache.hadoop.io.Writable`** — instead of Java's `Serializable`, for compactness and speed.

| **Java Type** | **Hadoop Writable** | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| `String` | `Text` | Text fields |
| `long` | `LongWritable` | Offsets, counts |
| `int` | `IntWritable` | Small numbers |
| `double` | `DoubleWritable` | Floating-point |
| `boolean` | `BooleanWritable` | Flags |
| `null` | `NullWritable` | Constant/empty key or value |
| Custom class | Implements `Writable<T>` | User-defined |

> For keys, use `WritableComparable<T>` (adds `compareTo()`) so that the framework can sort them.

---
