## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — The Word Count Example

**Problem Statement:** Given a text file stored in HDFS, count the frequency of every word.

#### **Input Data** (assume 1 HDFS block of 128 MB containing two lines):
```
Hello world hello Hadoop
Hadoop world big data
```

#### **STEP 1 — Input Split & Record Reading**
- The block is assigned to a single mapper (1 split = 1 mapper).
- `TextInputFormat` reads line by line.

| Record # | `K1` (Offset) | `V1` (Line) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | `Hello world hello Hadoop` |
| 2 | 25 | `Hadoop world big data` |

#### **STEP 2 — Mapping Phase**
The `map()` function tokenizes each line by whitespace and emits `(word, 1)`:

$$\text{map}(K1=0,\; V1=\text{"Hello world hello Hadoop"}) \rightarrow$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{"Hello"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"world"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"hello"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"Hadoop"},\; 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\text{map}(K1=25,\; V1=\text{"Hadoop world big data"}) \rightarrow$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{"Hadoop"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"world"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"big"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"data"},\; 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total emitted: 8 key-value pairs** written to the mapper's local disk (in-memory buffer first, then spilled/sorted into partitions).

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Sensitivity:** By default, MapReduce treats `"Hello"` and `"hello"` as **different keys**. To make it case-insensitive, the mapper must call `.toLowerCase()` before emitting.

#### **STEP 3 — Combiner (Optional Optimization)**

Assuming one combiner runs locally on the mapper output (sorted by key):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{"Hello"},\; 1) \rightarrow (\text{"Hello"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"Hadoop"},\; 1), (\text{"Hadoop"},\; 1) \rightarrow (\text{"Hadoop"},\; 2) \\
&(\text{"big"},\; 1) \rightarrow (\text{"big"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"data"},\; 1) \rightarrow (\text{"data"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"hello"},\; 1) \rightarrow (\text{"hello"},\; 1) \\
&(\text{"world"},\; 1), (\text{"world"},\; 1) \rightarrow (\text{"world"},\; 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Network transfer reduced from 8 pairs to 6 pairs** — a **25% reduction**.

#### **STEP 4 — Partitioning**

Assuming 2 reducers and the default `HashPartitioner`:

$$
p(k) = \left( \text{hash}(k) \;\&\; 2^{31}-1 \right) \bmod 2
$$

| Key | Hash (illustrative) | Partition |
|---|---|---|
| Hello | 71035123 | Reducer 0 or 1 |
| Hadoop | 124507098 | Reducer 0 or 1 |
| world | 113318802 | Reducer 0 or 1 |
| big | 98237 | Reducer 0 or 1 |
| hello | 99162302 | Reducer 0 or 1 |
| data | 99099022 | Reducer 0 or 1 |

> For exam purposes, the **key point** is: each unique word is sent to **exactly one** reducer.

#### **STEP 5 — Shuffle & Sort at Reducer**

Within each reducer, all incoming key-value pairs are **grouped by key** and **sorted**:

**Reducer 0 receives (e.g.):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{"Hadoop"},\; [2]) \\
&(\text{"big"},\; [1]) \\
&(\text{"data"},\; [1])
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reducer 1 receives (e.g.):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
&(\text{"Hello"},\; [1]) \\
&(\text{"hello"},\; [1]) \\
&(\text{"world"},\; [2])
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **STEP 6 — Reducing Phase**

For each unique key, sum the iterable values:

$$
\text{reduce}(K2=\text{"Hadoop"},\; V2=[2]) \rightarrow (\text{"Hadoop"},\; 2)
$$

$$
\text{reduce}(K2=\text{"world"},\; V2=[2]) \rightarrow (\text{"world"},\; 2)
$$

#### **STEP 7 — Final Output (written to HDFS)**

**`part-r-00000`:**
```
big    1
data   1
Hadoop 2
```

**`part-r-00001`:**
```
Hello  1
hello  1
world  2
```

#### **Summary Equation for the Word Count Job**

$$
\text{count}(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{R} \sum_{j \in V_i^w} 1
$$

Where $R$ = number of reducers and $V_i^w$ = iterable values for word $w$ arriving at reducer $i$.

> The **total number of unique words** in the output equals the **`REDUCE_INPUT_GROUPS`** counter in Hadoop.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Number of Mappers and Reducers

| **Scenario** | **Input File Size** | **Block Size** | **Split Size** | **Mappers (M)** | **Reducers (R)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Default (block = split) | 512 MB | 128 MB | 128 MB | $\lceil 512/128 \rceil = 4$ | 1 |
| Small file (under-split) | 50 MB | 128 MB | 128 MB | 1 (whole file = 1 split) | 1 |
| User-defined large split | 1 GB | 128 MB | 256 MB | $\lceil 1024/256 \rceil = 4$ | 1 |
| Multi-line JSON (whole-file split) | 1 GB | 128 MB | 1 GB (`isSplitable=false`) | 1 | 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the input is a **compressed file**, Hadoop treats it as a **non-splittable** whole — meaning the entire file (even 1 GB) becomes **one InputSplit** and **one Mapper**. This is why compression formats like **bzip2** (splittable) are preferred over **gzip** (non-splittable) in Hadoop.

---

### 3.3 Data Locality — The Distance Cost Model

The Hadoop scheduler prefers tasks to run where their data resides. The cost of running a map task at different locations is often modeled as:

$$
C_{\text{locality}} = \begin{cases}
C_{\text{node}} = 0 & \text{(data on same node — best)} \\ 
C_{\text{rack}} = 1 & \text{(data on same rack — good)} \\ 
C_{\text{off-switch}} = 2 & \text{(data on different rack — costly)}
\end{cases}
$$

> Hadoop's **delay scheduling** algorithm in YARN intentionally waits for a node-local slot, trading off queue time for better network locality.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete MapReduce Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HDFS Input File\n512 MB Text] --> B[InputSplit 1\n128 MB]
    A --> C[InputSplit 2\n128 MB]
    A --> D[InputSplit 3\n128 MB]
    A --> E[InputSplit 4\n128 MB]

    B --> M1[Mapper 1\nNode A]
    C --> M2[Mapper 2\nNode B]
    D --> M3[Mapper 3\nNode C]
    E --> M4[Mapper 4\nNode D]

    M1 --> P1[Partitioner + Sort\nLocal Disk]
    M2 --> P1
    M3 --> P2[Partitioner + Sort\nLocal Disk]
    M4 --> P2

    P1 --> S{Shuffle\nNetwork Transfer}
    P2 --> S

    S --> R1[Reducer 0\nNode A]
    S --> R2[Reducer 1\nNode B]

    R1 --> O1[Output Part r-00000\nHDFS]
    R2 --> O2[Output Part r-00001\nHDFS]

    O1 --> F[Final Result\nStored in HDFS]
    O2 --> F
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The data flows from left to right — HDFS → Splits → Mappers (parallel) → Local Sort → Shuffle (network) → Reducers (parallel) → HDFS output. The framework handles everything except the `map()` and `reduce()` boxes (those are user-written).

---

### 4.2 YARN-Based Resource Negotiation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Application\nDriver Program] -->|submitJob\nApplicationMaster\nrequest| RM[ResourceManager\nMaster Daemon]

    RM -->|allocate container| NM1[NodeManager\nNode 1]
    RM -->|allocate container| NM2[NodeManager\nNode 2]
    RM -->|allocate container| NM3[NodeManager\nNode 3]

    RM -->|launch| AM[ApplicationMaster\nper job]

    AM -->|negotiate resources| RM
    AM -->|launch MapTask| NM1
    AM -->|launch MapTask| NM2
    AM -->|launch ReduceTask| NM3

    NM1 --> H1[HDFS DataNode]
    NM2 --> H2[HDFS DataNode]
    NM3 --> H3[HDFS DataNode]
```

> **Key YARN Components:**
> - **ResourceManager (RM):** Global arbiter of cluster resources.
> - **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent managing containers.
> - **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-job negotiator — replaces the old JobTracker.

---

### 4.3 Combiner vs Reducer — Optimization Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Without[Without Combiner]
        A1[Mapper 1] -->|8 pairs| NET1[Network Shuffle]
        A2[Mapper 2] -->|8 pairs| NET1
        NET1 --> RR1[Reducer]
    end

    subgraph With[With Combiner]
        B1[Mapper 1 + Local Combiner] -->|3 pairs\npre-aggregated| NET2[Network Shuffle]
        B2[Mapper 2 + Local Combiner] -->|3 pairs\npre-aggregated| NET2
        NET2 --> RR2[Reducer]
    end
```

> **Quantitative gain:** Network traffic $\downarrow$ from 16 pairs to 6 pairs = **62.5% reduction**. This is the principal value of the Combiner in Hadoop.

---

### 4.4 Partitioning & Data Skew — The Skew Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mapper 1] -->|key=A 1000 times| P0[Partition 0\nReducer 0]
    M2[Mapper 2] -->|key=B 1000 times| P0
    M3[Mapper 3] -->|key=C 50 times| P1[Partition 1\nReducer 1]
    M4[Mapper 4] -->|key=D 50 times| P1

    P0 --> HOT[Hot Spot\n2000 records]
    P1 --> COLD[Cold Spot\n100 records]
```

> **Custom Partitioner Solution:** When the domain knowledge suggests that key `A` is extremely frequent, write a custom partitioner that uses **range partitioning** or **salting** (prepending a random prefix to the key) to distribute load evenly.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Define the MapReduce programming model. List its two primary functions and their input-output signatures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** MapReduce is a distributed data-processing model introduced by Google for parallel computation on large datasets, implemented in Hadoop as a framework with two user-defined functions.

> **Functions & Signatures (2 marks):**
> 1. `map(K1, V1) → List<(K2, V2)>` — transforms each input record into intermediate key-value pairs.
> 2. `reduce(K2, List<V2>) → List<(K3, V3)>` — aggregates all intermediate values associated with the same key.

> The framework automatically handles partitioning, shuffling, and sorting between these two functions.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**What is a Combiner in MapReduce? Why is it used? State the type-compatibility constraint.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Combiner is a user-defined function that runs on the mapper node **after** the map output is sorted, performing local aggregation before the data is sent over the network.

> **Purpose (1 mark):** It minimizes **network bandwidth consumption** by pre-aggregating intermediate data (e.g., summing counts locally before sending them to reducers).

> **Constraint (1 mark):** The Combiner's input key-value types must be **identical** to the Reducer's input types (i.e., `Combiner<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` must match `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>`), because the Combiner output is fed directly to the Reducer.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the complete execution flow of a MapReduce job with a neat block diagram covering InputSplit, Mapping, Combiner, Shuffle & Sort, and Reducing phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following input text stored in HDFS:
```
Hadoop is fast
Hadoop is scalable
Big data is valuable
```
Assuming **2 reducers** and using the default `HashPartitioner`, show the complete step-by-step execution of a **Word Count** MapReduce job — including mapper output, partitioner output, and final reducer output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Execution Flow (7 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Input Splitting (1 mark):** The HDFS input file is divided into fixed-size blocks (default 128 MB in Hadoop 2.x). Each block is treated as one **InputSplit**. Number of mappers = number of splits.

> **Step 2 — Record Reading (1 mark):** A `RecordReader` (e.g., `LineRecordReader` for `TextInputFormat`) converts the byte stream into `(K1, V1)` pairs — typically `(byte_offset, line_text)`.

> **Step 3 — Mapping (1 mark):** Each mapper invokes the user-defined `map(K1, V1, context)` function for every record. The output `List<(K2, V2)>` is buffered in memory and periodically spilled to local disk. The default buffer size is 100 MB, with an 80% spill threshold.

> **Step 4 — Combiner (Optional) (1 mark):** Runs locally on the sorted spill files to perform mini-aggregation, reducing the volume of data shipped to reducers.

> **Step 5 — Partitioning (1 mark):** The `Partitioner` assigns each `(K2, V2)` to a reducer partition using `p(k) = (hash(k) & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % numReducers`.

> **Step 6 — Shuffle & Sort (1 mark):** Reducers fetch their assigned partitions from all mapper nodes over HTTP. All values for the same key are grouped and sorted (merge sort across multiple map outputs).

> **Step 7 — Reducing & Output (1 mark):** The `reduce(K2, Iterable<V2>, context)` function is called once per unique key. The final output `(K3, V3)` is written to HDFS in the output directory (e.g., `/user/output/part-r-00000`).

> **Diagram** (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid flow).

---

**(b) Word Count Step-by-Step (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mapper Output (3 marks):**

| Mapper | Input Line (V1) | Emitted (K2, V2) Pairs |
|---|---|---|
| M1 | `Hadoop is fast` | (Hadoop, 1), (is, 1), (fast, 1) |
| M1 | `Hadoop is scalable` | (Hadoop, 1), (is, 1), (scalable, 1) |
| M2 | `Big data is valuable` | (Big, 1), (data, 1), (is, 1), (valuable, 1) |

(Assuming 2 splits for the 3 lines, but typically 1 mapper per block — for the exam, we show logical mappers.)

**Step 2 — Partitioner Output (2 marks):**

Using `p(k) = hash(k) mod 2`, suppose partitioning yields (illustrative — actual hash varies, but exam only checks the principle):

**Partition 0 (Reducer 0):**
$$
(\text{Hadoop}, 1),\; (\text{Hadoop}, 1),\; (\text{is}, 1),\; (\text{is}, 1),\; (\text{is}, 1),\; (\text{fast}, 1)
$$

**Partition 1 (Reducer 1):**
$$
(\text{is}, 1),\; (\text{scalable}, 1),\; (\text{Big}, 1),\; (\text{data}, 1),\; (\text{valuable}, 1)
$$

(For academic simplicity, show grouped by key after sort.)

**Step 3 — Reducer Output (2 marks):**

**`part-r-00000`:**
```
Hadoop    2
fast      1
is        3
```

**`part-r-00001`:**
```
Big       1
data      1
is        1
scalable  1
valuable  1
```

> Note: The key `"is"` is split between both reducers — Hadoop does **not** guarantee locality of similar keys to the same reducer. Each reducer independently sums what it receives.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Mapper**, **Combiner**, **Partitioner**, and **Reducer** in Hadoop MapReduce. Prepare a comparison table covering **function, execution location, number of instances, input/output types, and invocation timing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2 GB log file is stored in HDFS with a default block size of 128 MB and a replication factor of 3. Calculate:
- (i) The number of HDFS blocks.
- (ii) The total HDFS storage consumed.
- (iii) The default number of Mappers (assuming no combiner and one line per split-boundary consideration).
- (iv) The split size if `mapreduce.input.fileinputformat.split.minsize = 0` and `mapreduce.input.fileinputformat.split.maxsize = Long.MAX_VALUE`.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Mapper** | **Combiner** | **Partitioner** | **Reducer** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Function (1 mark)** | Transforms input records into intermediate `(K, V)` pairs | Local pre-aggregation of map output | Routes each key to a specific reducer | Final aggregation per unique key |
| **Execution Location (1 mark)** | DataNode (node holding the input split) | Same node as the Mapper (local) | Mapper node (during output write) | NodeManager assigned by YARN |
| **Number of Instances (1 mark)** | 1 per InputSplit | 1 per Mapper (optional) | 1 globally (called per record) | 1 per partition (configured) |
| **Input Type (1 mark)** | `Mapper<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` — `map(K1, V1)` | `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, ?, ?>` — same as Reducer | `(K2, V2)`, int partition number | `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` — `reduce(K2, List<V2>)` |
| **Output Type (1 mark)** | `List<(K2, V2)>` written to local disk | `List<(K2, V2)>` (same type) | `int partitionId` | `List<(K3, V3)>` written to HDFS |
| **Invocation Timing (1 mark)** | Once per input record (K1, V1) | Once per map-output spill (after local sort) | Once per emitted `(K2, V2)` from map | Once per unique `K2` |
| **User-defined? (1 mark)** | Yes (optional override) | Optional, but recommended | Yes (custom possible; default `HashPartitioner`) | Yes (mandatory for the job to produce output) |

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

> **Given:**
> - File size $S = 2 \; \text{GB} = 2048 \; \text{MB}$
> - Block size $B = 128 \; \text{MB}$
> - Replication factor $R = 3$

**(i) Number of HDFS Blocks (2 marks):**
$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{S}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2048}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 16 \rceil = 16 \text{ blocks}
$$

**(ii) Total HDFS Storage Consumed (2 marks):**
$$
\text{Storage} = S \times R = 2048 \; \text{MB} \times 3 = 6144 \; \text{MB} = 6 \; \text{GB}
$$

**(iii) Default Number of Mappers (2 marks):**
Since by default the split size equals the block size, and the file is exactly divisible by the block size:
$$
M = N_{\text{blocks}} = 16 \text{ mappers}
$$

**(iv) Split Size with the given config (1 mark):**
$$
\text{splitSize} = \max(\text{minSize}, \min(\text{maxSize}, \text{blockSize}))
$$
$$
\text{splitSize} = \max(0, \min(\text{Long.MAX\_VALUE}, 128 \; \text{MB})) = 128 \; \text{MB}
$$

> The split size remains **128 MB** (default), so the number of mappers remains **16**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Combiner with Reducer:** Many students write Combiner code that does NOT match the Reducer input/output types. **Always state the type-equality constraint explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting that `reduce()` is called per unique key, not per value:** Iterating over `Iterable<V2>` is the correct mental model — not "for each value, run a reduce."
>
> 3. **Wrong split size formula:** Students often write `splitSize = fileSize / numMappers` (the inverse). The correct formula is $splitSize = \max(\text{minSize}, \min(\text{maxSize}, \text{blockSize}))$.
>
> 4. **Ignoring data locality:** When asked about Hadoop's design, **always** mention that mappers are scheduled on nodes where the data block resides — this is a 1-mark "design principle" question waiting to happen.
>
> 5. **Not knowing YARN vs MapReduce 1.x:** KTU 2024 expects familiarity with YARN (ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster). Stating "JobTracker" without mentioning the YARN equivalent loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Hadoop version confusion:** Hadoop 2.x has 128 MB blocks; Hadoop 3.x has 256 MB. Mention the version if the question is ambiguous.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- **MapReduce** = `map()` + `reduce()` + framework-managed **shuffle/sort**.
- **Mapper** runs on each **InputSplit**; number of mappers = number of splits.
- **Reducer** runs on each **partition**; number of reducers is set by `job.setNumReduceTasks(n)`.
- **Combiner** = local pre-aggregation on the mapper node → reduces network traffic.
- **Partitioner** = `hash(key) mod R` (default `HashPartitioner`).
- **Default HDFS block size**: **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x), **256 MB** (Hadoop 3.x).
- **Default replication factor**: **3**.
- **Default map output buffer**: **100 MB** with 80% spill threshold.
- **Writable types** are used instead of Java's `Serializable` for compactness.
- **Data Locality** hierarchy: `Node-local > Rack-local > Off-switch`.
- **Shuffle** = network transfer; **Sort** = merge sort of map outputs.
- **Speculative execution** = framework runs a backup task for stragglers.
- **YARN architecture** = `ResourceManager` (master) + `NodeManager` (slave) + `ApplicationMaster` (per job).
- **Compressed files** (especially gzip) are **non-splittable** → reduces parallelism.
- **Counter** `MAP_INPUT_RECORDS` and `REDUCE_INPUT_GROUPS` are exam favorites.
- **Split size formula**: $\text{splitSize} = \max(\text{minSize}, \min(\text{maxSize}, \text{blockSize}))$.
- **Word Count** is the **canonical KTU example** — know it inside-out, including the output format of `part-r-NNNNN` files.
- **`Context.write(key, value)`** is the standard emission method in both `map()` and `reduce()`.
- **Setup/Map/Cleanup** lifecycle methods in Mapper and Reducer are common 2-mark theory questions.
- **Why local disk, not HDFS, for map output?** → Avoids triple replication overhead on intermediate data.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Topic: Analyzing the Data with Hadoop (Map and Reduce)**
> *Next recommended topic:* **Joining Data in MapReduce / MapReduce Algorithms (Sorting, Searching, Indexing, TF-IDF)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java MapReduce
Scaling Out
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scaling Out

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling Out (Horizontal Scaling)** is the architectural strategy of expanding the computational and storage capacity of a distributed system by **adding more commodity nodes (machines)** to the existing cluster, rather than upgrading the resources of a single node. It is the foundational design principle upon which all modern Big Data ecosystems (Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL databases) are built.

In the context of the **PECPT755 – Big Data Analytics** syllabus, *Scaling Out* specifically refers to the methodology used by frameworks like **Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)** and **MapReduce** to handle petabyte-scale datasets by partitioning both data and computation across hundreds or thousands of low-cost, independent machines working in parallel.

### 1.1.1 Formal Comparison: Scaling Up vs. Scaling Out

> [!NOTE]
> **Scaling Up (Vertical Scaling)** increases power by adding more CPU, RAM, or storage to a *single* machine.
> **Scaling Out (Horizontal Scaling)** increases power by adding *more* machines to the distributed cluster.

| Property | Scaling Up (Vertical) | Scaling Out (Horizontal) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | Bigger machine | More machines |
| **Hardware** | High-end servers (mainframes) | Commodity hardware |
| **Cost Curve** | Exponential (non-linear) | Linear / near-linear |
| **Failure Domain** | Single point of failure (SPOF) | Naturally fault-tolerant |
| **Theoretical Limit** | Physical hardware ceiling | Theoretically infinite |
| **Typical Use Case** | RDBMS (Oracle, PostgreSQL) | Big Data (HDFS, Cassandra, MongoDB) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen:

- **Scaling Up (Vertical):** You have one giant, ultra-powerful chef with 16 arms, infinite burner space, and a memory of every recipe. If the chef gets sick (fails), the entire kitchen shuts down. Also, you cannot simply "buy" a chef with more arms past a biological limit.
- **Scaling Out (Horizontal):** You have 100 normal chefs, each working on a small portion of the orders. If one chef calls in sick, the other 99 keep cooking (fault tolerance). If orders double, you simply hire 100 more chefs (linear cost). The recipes are split so each chef does a small, manageable task.

**Scaling Out is the "100 normal chefs" strategy.** It is the cornerstone philosophy that allows Google, Facebook, and Amazon to process billions of records every day on cheap, failure-prone hardware.

## 1.3 Key Design Principles in Scaling Out

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Horizontal Scaling in Big Data:**
> 1. **Data Partitioning (Sharding):** Splitting a massive dataset into smaller chunks distributed across nodes.
> 2. **Replication:** Maintaining multiple copies of each chunk to survive hardware failure.
> 3. **Computation Co-location:** Moving the *computation* to where the *data* resides, rather than the reverse (the core HDFS/MapReduce philosophy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Scaling Out

A distributed system that scales out operates on a strict set of logical steps. Let us break down the *why* and *how* behind each step.

### Step 1: Data Ingestion & Chunking
- **Why:** A single 1 PB file cannot fit on a single disk, let alone be processed by one CPU.
- **How:** The file is broken into fixed-size **blocks** (default **128 MB** in HDFS, a standard KTU-asked value). Each block is treated as an independent unit.

### Step 2: Distributed Placement (Rack Awareness)
- **Why:** To survive the failure of an entire rack (a physical group of machines that share a switch and power supply).
- **How:** The **NameNode** (HDFS Master) uses a **Rack Awareness Algorithm** to place the first replica on a *local* node, the second on a *different* node in the *same rack*, and the third on a *different rack* entirely. This is the formula:
  $$\text{Replica Placement Policy} = \begin{cases} R_1: \text{Local Node} \\ R_2: \text{Different Node, Same Rack} \\ R_3: \text{Different Rack} \end{cases}$$

### Step 3: Parallel Computation Push-Down
- **Why:** Network bandwidth is the most precious and bottleneck-prone resource in a data center. Moving terabytes of data over the network is prohibitively expensive.
- **How:** The **JobTracker / Application Master** sends the code (the *Map* task) to the specific **DataNode** that physically holds the block. The *data stays put*; only the logic moves. This is often called the **"Mobile Code over Mobile Data"** principle.

### Step 4: Aggregation & Shuffle
- **Why:** After parallel processing, the partial results from hundreds of nodes must be combined into a single, coherent answer.
- **How:** Intermediate key-value pairs are *shuffled* (sorted and partitioned) across the network to reducers, which aggregate them into the final output.

## 2.2 The Replication Factor & Fault Tolerance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Replication Factor (RF)** is the number of copies of each data block maintained in the cluster. The KTU default for HDFS is **RF = 3**.

The relationship between **cluster storage efficiency** and the **replication factor** is a classic KTU problem. Let us derive it.

### 2.2.1 Storage Overhead Formula
If a dataset has logical size $D$ and the replication factor is $R$, the **physical storage** $P$ required is:
$$P = D \times R$$

The **Storage Overhead Ratio** $\eta$ is the fraction of disk space that is *usable unique data* versus copies:
$$\eta = \frac{D}{P} = \frac{D}{D \cdot R} = \frac{1}{R}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for PECPT755 Module 3 — Scaling Out**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Storage Overhead** | $P = D \times R$ | Total physical disk consumed |
| 2 | **Usable Efficiency** | $\eta = 1 / R$ | Useful data vs. redundant copies |
| 3 | **HDFS Block Size** | Default = **128 MB** | Unit of distribution |
| 4 | **Default Replication** | $R = \mathbf{3}$ | Default fault tolerance level |
| 5 | **Max Cluster Size (theoretical)** | $N_{max} = \infty$ | Linear horizontal growth |
| 6 | **Amdahl's Law Bound** | $S = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ | Theoretical speedup limit |
| 7 | **Block Count** | $B = \lceil D / B_s \rceil$ | Number of blocks for dataset of size $D$ |

### 2.3.1 The CRITICAL Rule for the Block Count Formula

> [!WARNING]
> **Always use the Ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$** because the *last* block of a file is almost always smaller than the full block size $B_s$, but it still occupies a full block of physical storage on disk. KTU examiners specifically look for students who apply the ceiling function correctly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Scaling Out is the *de facto* architecture for:

- **Search Engines:** Google's **GFS (Google File System)** and its successor **Colossus**, both pure scaling-out designs.
- **Social Networks:** Facebook's **Haystack** (photo storage) and **TAO** (graph cache).
- **Streaming:** Netflix's **Kafka** clusters scale out by adding more brokers.
- **Databases:** **Cassandra**, **MongoDB Sharded Clusters**, **Amazon DynamoDB**.
- **Cloud Data Warehouses:** **Snowflake**, **BigQuery**, **Redshift Spectrum** all rely on horizontally scalable storage layers (often S3) decoupled from compute.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Blocks in a HDFS File

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 3-Mark Question):**  
> *A file of size 900 MB is stored in HDFS. If the default block size is 128 MB and the replication factor is 3, calculate: (a) the number of blocks created, (b) the total physical storage consumed, and (c) the usable efficiency ratio.*

Let us perform the derivation step-by-step. Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly.

### Step A: Identifying the Given Variables

Let us define the variables from the problem statement:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 900 \text{ MB} \quad &\text{(Logical file size)} \\
B_s &= 128 \text{ MB} \quad &\text{(Block size)} \\
R &= 3 \quad &\text{(Replication factor)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step B: Calculating the Number of Blocks

The number of blocks $B$ is computed by dividing the file size by the block size, rounded *up* to the nearest integer using the **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$:

$$B = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B_s} \right\rceil$$

Substituting the values:

$$B = \left\lceil \frac{900 \text{ MB}}{128 \text{ MB}} \right\rceil$$

Performing the division:

$$B = \lceil 7.03125 \rceil$$

Applying the ceiling function, we round up to the next whole integer:

$$B = 8 \text{ blocks}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The file is split into 7 full blocks of 128 MB plus 1 partial block of 900 - (7 × 128) = 900 - 896 = 4 MB. This last 4 MB still occupies a full 128 MB slot on the disk.

### Step C: Calculating the Total Physical Storage

The total physical storage $P$ consumed on the cluster is the logical size multiplied by the replication factor:

$$P = D \times R$$

Substituting the known values:

$$P = 900 \text{ MB} \times 3$$

Multiplying through:

$$P = 2700 \text{ MB}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Even though the user uploaded a 900 MB file, the cluster must provision 2700 MB of raw disk. The extra 1800 MB is the redundancy cost paid for fault tolerance.

### Step D: Calculating the Usable Efficiency Ratio

The usable efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of unique logical data to the physical storage:

$$\eta = \frac{D}{P}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\eta = \frac{900 \text{ MB}}{2700 \text{ MB}}$$

Simplifying the fraction:

$$\eta = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

Converting to a percentage:

$$\eta \approx 33.33\%$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** With a replication factor of 3, only **one-third** of the cluster's raw disk is used for *unique* data; the remaining **two-thirds** is redundant copies. This is the engineering *cost* of achieving high availability.

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Law and the Theoretical Speedup Limit

> **Problem Statement (KTU 14-Mark Derivative):**  
> *In a MapReduce job, 80% of the computation is embarrassingly parallel (the Map phase), and 20% is inherently sequential (the Reduce aggregation phase). If the cluster scales out from 1 to 8 nodes, calculate the speedup. What is the theoretical maximum speedup as $N \to \infty$?*

### Step A: Amdahl's Law Formula

Amdahl's Law gives the maximum achievable speedup $S$ of a task when only a fraction $p$ can be parallelized over $N$ processors:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$$

### Step B: Substituting the Given Values

From the problem:
- Parallel fraction: $p = 0.80$
- Sequential fraction: $1 - p = 0.20$
- Number of nodes: $N = 8$

Plugging these into Amdahl's formula:

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \frac{0.80}{8}}$$

### Step C: Simplifying the Sequential Term

The sequential component is:

$$1 - 0.80 = 0.20$$

### Step D: Simplifying the Parallel Term

The parallel component is:

$$\frac{0.80}{8} = 0.10$$

### Step E: Adding the Denominator Terms

$$(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N} = 0.20 + 0.10 = 0.30$$

### Step F: Final Speedup Calculation

$$S(8) = \frac{1}{0.30} = 3.333\overline{3}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Despite 8x more hardware, we only get a **3.33x** speedup because of the irreducible sequential 20%. The system is **bounded by the sequential bottleneck.**

### Step G: The Theoretical Limit as N → ∞

As the number of nodes approaches infinity, the parallel term $\frac{p}{N} \to 0$, leaving only the sequential term:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$

Substituting $p = 0.80$:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.80} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** No matter how many nodes we add, the maximum speedup is **5x** because 20% of the job is permanently sequential. This is the *fundamental ceiling* of scaling out for this workload.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: HDFS Scaling Out Architecture with Rack Awareness

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how a single logical file is broken into blocks, replicated, and placed across two physical racks in a data center — the canonical KTU 14-mark visual question.

```mermaid
graph TB
    Client["Client Application"]
    NN["NameNode Master Metadata Service"]
    DN1["DataNode Rack1 Node1 Local R1"]
    DN2["DataNode Rack1 Node2 Same Rack R2"]
    DN3["DataNode Rack2 Node3 Different Rack R3"]
    DN4["DataNode Rack2 Node4 Free Slot"]

    Client -- "Write Request File F" --> NN
    NN -- "Block 1 placement instructions" --> DN1
    NN -- "Block 1 replica 2" --> DN2
    NN -- "Block 1 replica 3" --> DN3
    DN1 -- "Heartbeat and Block Report" --> NN
    DN2 -- "Heartbeat and Block Report" --> NN
    DN3 -- "Heartbeat and Block Report" --> NN
    DN4 -. "Reserved for future scaling" .-> NN
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **NameNode** (NN) is the *metadata brain* — it never stores the actual data, only the *map* of which block lives on which DataNode.
- **Block 1** has three replicas: $R_1$ on Rack 1 (local), $R_2$ on Rack 1 (different node, same rack), $R_3$ on Rack 2 (different rack).
- **DN4** is an empty node — this is the *elasticity* of scaling out. We can add DN4 later to rebalance the cluster and add more capacity without downtime.

## 4.2 Diagram: Scaling Up vs. Scaling Out — A Block-Level Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SU["Scaling Up Vertical Approach"]
        SU1["Server Cores: 4"]
        SU2["Server RAM: 16 GB"]
        SU3["Server Disk: 1 TB"]
        SU4["Server Cores: 32"]
        SU5["Server RAM: 256 GB"]
        SU6["Server Disk: 16 TB"]
        SU1 --> SU4
        SU2 --> SU5
        SU3 --> SU6
    end

    subgraph SO["Scaling Out Horizontal Approach"]
        SO1["Node 1 4 Core 16 GB 1 TB"]
        SO2["Node 2 4 Core 16 GB 1 TB"]
        SO3["Node 3 4 Core 16 GB 1 TB"]
        SO4["Node N 4 Core 16 GB 1 TB"]
        SO1 --- SO2
        SO2 --- SO3
        SO3 --- SO4
    end
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left** cluster (Scaling Up) shows one box growing in size — you are *replacing* a small server with a bigger one, which requires downtime and a non-linear cost jump.
- The **right** cluster (Scaling Out) shows a row of identical, *cheap* boxes connected by a network — adding Node N is a simple plug-and-play operation that adds linear capacity.

## 4.3 Diagram: MapReduce "Mobile Code over Mobile Data" Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    InputFile["Input File 1 TB 8 Blocks of 128 MB"]
    M1["Map Task 1 on Node A reading Block 1"]
    M2["Map Task 2 on Node B reading Block 2"]
    M3["Map Task 3 on Node C reading Block 3"]
    Shuffle["Shuffle and Sort Phase across network"]
    R1["Reducer 1 on Node D"]
    R2["Reducer 2 on Node E"]
    Output["Final Output in HDFS"]

    InputFile --> M1
    InputFile --> M2
    InputFile --> M3
    M1 --> Shuffle
    M2 --> Shuffle
    M3 --> Shuffle
    Shuffle --> R1
    Shuffle --> R2
    R1 --> Output
    R2 --> Output
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Map tasks** (M1, M2, M3) execute *locally* on whichever node physically holds the input block. **No data crosses the network** during the map phase.
- The **Shuffle phase** is the *only* point where significant network traffic occurs — intermediate key-value pairs are redistributed to reducers.
- The **Reducers** (R1, R2) aggregate the partial results and write the final answer back to HDFS, which triggers another round of replication.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> In Scaling Out questions, students consistently lose marks in three ways:
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in block count calculations — writing $900/128 = 7.03$ instead of $\lceil 7.03 \rceil = 8$.
> 2. **Confusing "Scaling Up" with "Scaling Out"** in definition questions — KTU strictly expects the keywords *"horizontal"*, *"commodity nodes"*, and *"adding more machines"*.
> 3. **Missing the rack-awareness policy** in 14-mark architecture questions — the *order* of replica placement (local, same rack, different rack) is a frequently asked 3-mark sub-part.

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Scaling Out. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Model Answer:**  
**Scaling Out** (also called *Horizontal Scaling*) is the process of increasing the computational and storage capacity of a distributed system by **adding more commodity hardware nodes** to the cluster, rather than upgrading the resources of a single existing node. In Big Data ecosystems like HDFS and MapReduce, scaling out is the default growth strategy because it provides **linear cost scalability**, **fault tolerance** (no single point of failure), and a **theoretically infinite** capacity ceiling, making it suitable for handling petabyte-scale datasets on inexpensive, failure-prone machines.

### Question 2: What is the HDFS default block size and default replication factor? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Model Answer:**  
The **HDFS default block size is 128 MB**, and the **default replication factor is 3**. Each file uploaded to HDFS is split into 128 MB chunks (the final block may be smaller), and each chunk is stored on three different DataNodes following the rack-awareness policy: the first replica on the local node, the second on a different node in the same rack, and the third on a node in a different rack.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks [Module 3 — Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a)** Explain the architectural difference between **Scaling Up** and **Scaling Out** with a neat diagram. Why is Scaling Out the preferred strategy for Big Data workloads? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A Hadoop cluster stores a file of size **2.4 GB**. The HDFS block size is **128 MB** and the replication factor is **3**. Calculate:  
    (i) The number of blocks created.  
    (ii) The total physical storage consumed by the file.  
    (iii) The storage overhead ratio. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Conceptual Difference:**
- **Scaling Up (Vertical):** Improves a single machine's capacity by adding more CPU, RAM, or disk. Bottlenecked by hardware ceilings and cost-inefficient beyond a point.
- **Scaling Out (Horizontal):** Adds more independent commodity nodes to a distributed cluster. Capacity grows linearly with cost; failures are isolated to single nodes.

**2. Why Scaling Out for Big Data?**
- (i) **Cost:** Commodity hardware is exponentially cheaper than high-end servers.
- (ii) **Fault Tolerance:** Failure of one node does not crash the system; data is replicated.
- (iii) **Elasticity:** New nodes can be added online without downtime.
- (iv) **Theoretical Capacity:** Practically unlimited horizontal growth.
- (v) **Parallelism:** Computation is naturally distributed, enabling true parallel processing of large datasets.

**3. Diagram** — Refer to **Section 4.2** above for the Scaling Up vs. Scaling Out block diagram.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $D = 2.4 \text{ GB} = 2400 \text{ MB}$, $B_s = 128 \text{ MB}$, $R = 3$.

**(i) Number of Blocks:**
$$B = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B_s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2400}{128} \right\rceil = \lceil 18.75 \rceil = 19 \text{ blocks}$$

**Verification of last block size:** 18 full blocks × 128 MB = 2304 MB. Remaining: 2400 - 2304 = 96 MB (the 19th partial block).

**(ii) Total Physical Storage:**
$$P = D \times R = 2400 \text{ MB} \times 3 = 7200 \text{ MB} = 7.2 \text{ GB}$$

**(iii) Storage Overhead Ratio:**
$$\eta = \frac{D}{P} = \frac{2400}{7200} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 = 33.33\%$$

This means **33.33%** of the cluster disk holds unique data, while the remaining **66.67%** is redundant replicas used for fault tolerance.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks [Module 3 — Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDFS Rack Awareness replica placement policy**. Why is this policy critical for a Scaling Out architecture? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A MapReduce job has **75% parallelizable code** and **25% sequential code**. Calculate the speedup achieved when the cluster is scaled out to **4 nodes**, and the **theoretical maximum speedup** as $N \to \infty$. Comment on the result. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. The Policy (3 Replica Placement):**
- **Replica 1 ($R_1$):** Placed on the **local DataNode** (the node receiving the write from the client).
- **Replica 2 ($R_2$):** Placed on a **different DataNode within the same rack** as $R_1$.
- **Replica 3 ($R_3$):** Placed on a **DataNode in a different rack** entirely.

**2. Why This Policy is Critical for Scaling Out:**
- (i) **Intra-rack protection:** If one node in a rack fails, the second replica (same rack) survives.
- (ii) **Inter-rack protection:** If the entire rack loses power or its top-of-rack switch fails, the third replica in another rack survives.
- (iii) **Network locality optimization:** Since $R_1$ and $R_2$ share a rack, intra-rack reads (which are faster and cheaper than inter-rack reads) are preferred for the most frequent access patterns.
- (iv) **Write performance trade-off:** Writing to two racks (instead of three) reduces inter-rack network traffic, which is the bottleneck in a Scaling Out architecture.

**3. Diagram** — Refer to **Section 4.1** above for the Rack Awareness placement diagram.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $p = 0.75$ (parallel fraction), $1 - p = 0.25$ (sequential fraction), $N = 4$.

**Amdahl's Law:**
$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}$$

**Speedup at N = 4:**

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.75) + \frac{0.75}{4}}$$

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.1875}$$

$$S(4) = \frac{1}{0.4375} \approx 2.286$$

**Theoretical Maximum Speedup ($N \to \infty$):**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - p} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4$$

**Comment:** Even with 4 nodes, we only get a **~2.29x speedup**, far below the ideal 4x. Theoretically, no amount of hardware can exceed **4x speedup** because 25% of the job is permanently sequential (e.g., driver setup, final file write, metadata updates). To get higher speedup, the engineer must **reduce the sequential fraction** through algorithmic optimization (e.g., using a Combiner, in-memory aggregation, or redesigning the Reduce phase to be parallelizable).

---

## 6. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Scaling Out**

- **Definition:** Scaling Out = **horizontal scaling** = adding more commodity nodes to a distributed system. It is the *opposite* of Scaling Up (vertical).
- **HDFS Default Block Size:** **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x and later; older Hadoop 1.x used 64 MB).
- **HDFS Default Replication Factor:** **3** copies per block.
- **Rack Awareness Rule:** $R_1$ local, $R_2$ same rack different node, $R_3$ different rack.
- **Storage Formula:** $P = D \times R$. Efficiency $\eta = 1/R$.
- **Block Count Formula:** $B = \lceil D / B_s \rceil$ — **always use the ceiling function**.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$. The theoretical max is $S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1-p}$ as $N \to \infty$.
- **Mobile Code over Mobile Data:** Map tasks run *on the node holding the data* — never move the data, move the logic.
- **Key Engineering Trade-off:** Scaling out trades *strong consistency* and *low-latency transactions* (typical of vertically scaled RDBMS) for *massive parallelism*, *linear cost scaling*, and *fault tolerance*.
- **CAP Theorem Connection:** Distributed systems that scale out (HDFS, Cassandra) generally prioritize **Availability (A) and Partition Tolerance (P)** over strict **Consistency (C)** — a fundamental design choice KTU may ask you to justify.
- **Real-World Examples:** Google GFS/Colossus, Hadoop HDFS, Apache Cassandra, MongoDB Sharded Clusters, Amazon DynamoDB, Snowflake, Apache Kafka.
- **Common Mistake:** Do *not* confuse *scaling out a database* (sharding/replication) with *scaling out a compute cluster* (adding worker nodes). Both follow the same principle, but the engineering details differ.

> **End of PECPT755 Module 3 — Scaling Out Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hadoop Streaming
topics.content: > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hadoop Streaming # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hadoop Streaming** is a utility bundled with the Hadoop distribution that enables developers to create and execute **MapReduce jobs** using *any executable program* (script or binary) as the mapper and/or reducer. It achieves this by leveraging the standard **UNIX streaming model**, where the MapReduce framework reads input line-by-line from **Standard Input (STDIN)** and expects the output to be written to **Standard Output (STDOUT)**. This utility was introduced by the Apache Hadoop project to overcome the historical language limitation of Hadoop MapReduce, which originally forced users to write their logic in **Java** only. In the 2024 Scheme syllabus context, Hadoop Streaming is positioned as a **language-agnostic bridge** that decouples the *business logic* (the actual `map()` and `reduce()` functions) from the *execution engine* (the YARN-managed JVM processes of Hadoop). ### Key Technical Characteristics - **Utility Name:** `hadoop-streaming-X.X.X.jar` (typically located in the `$HADOOP_HOME/share/hadoop/tools/lib/` directory). - **Communication Model:** **Process-based pipes** using UNIX file descriptors `0` (STDIN), `1` (STDOUT), and `2` (STDERR). - **Data Format on the Wire:** Each line is treated as a single record, where the first tab-separated (`\t`) token is the **key** and the rest of the line is the **value**. - **Default TextInputFormat:** Operates on **plain text**; binary data requires custom `InputFormat` implementations. - **Supported Languages:** Python, Perl, Ruby, Bash shell scripts, C++, R, Go — any program that can be launched as a separate process. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Translator at a Multilingual Conference" Imagine an international business conference where delegates speak **10 different languages** (English, French, Japanese, Hindi, etc.). The conference organizer originally only hired an **English-Japanese interpreter** (the original Java-based MapReduce API). Only delegates who could speak English or Japanese could present their papers. **Hadoop Streaming** is like hiring a **Universal Conference Assistant** who provides every delegate with: 1. A **microphone** (STDIN) — they speak into it in *whatever language they want*. 2. A **writing pad and projector** (STDOUT) — they write their key results here in a *universally agreed tabular format* (key \t value). 3. A **session chair** (the Hadoop framework) — who manages the schedule, seating, and routing of papers between speakers (mappers and reducers). The session chair **does not need to understand the language** of the speech. As long as the speaker follows the protocol — speak into the mic, write on the pad in the agreed format — the conference runs smoothly. This is exactly how Hadoop Streaming works: the YARN framework does not understand Python, C++, or Bash; it just feeds lines into the script's STDIN and reads lines from its STDOUT. > [!NOTE] > **Why the Name "Streaming"?** > The name is borrowed from the UNIX philosophy of *stream processing* — a chain of small programs (`cat`, `grep`, `awk`, `sort`, `uniq`) connected by pipes (`|`) where data flows as a continuous stream of bytes. Hadoop Streaming replicates this elegant, time-tested paradigm at a distributed cluster scale. --- ## 1.3 Syllabus Highlights & Key Terminology | Term | One-Line Meaning | |---|---| | **STDIN** | Standard Input — file descriptor 0 — where the framework feeds data into your script. | | **STDOUT** | Standard Output — file descriptor 1 — where your script must emit key-value pairs. | | **STDERR** | Standard Error — file descriptor 2 — used for logging/debugging (not consumed by the framework). | | **Mapper Executable** | The user-supplied program that performs the `map()` function logic. | | **Reducer Executable** | The user-supplied program that performs the `reduce()` function logic. | | **GenericOptionsParser** | A Hadoop utility class that parses standard Hadoop command-line arguments (e.g., `-D`, `-conf`, `-fs`) before passing remaining args to the application. | | **Tool Interface** | A Hadoop Java interface (extends `Configurable`) that allows MR jobs to share a common `Configuration` and respond to generic options. | | **ToolRunner** | A helper class that invokes `Tool.run()` after running `GenericOptionsParser`. | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How Hadoop Streaming Actually Works The streaming utility launches the user-supplied mapper and reducer executables as **child processes** spawned by the JVM-based `MapTask` and `ReduceTask` containers. Communication is purely through **pipes**, which are kernel-managed in-memory buffers. ### Step-by-Step Execution Flow 1. **Job Submission:** The user submits a streaming job using the `hadoop jar hadoop-streaming.jar` command, supplying input/output paths, mapper executable, and reducer executable. 2. **Resource Negotiation:** YARN's **ResourceManager** allocates a container on a node, which then launches the `ApplicationMaster` (AM). 3. **Map Task Initialization:** For each input split, the AM launches a `MapTask` JVM. The `MapTask` instantiates the streaming **mapper wrapper** (`HadoopStreamingMapper`), which spawns the user-provided mapper executable as a **child process** via `ProcessBuilder`. 4. **Stream Pumping:** The `PipeMapRed` and `StreamInputFormat` classes feed each line of the input split into the **STDIN** of the mapper child process. A dedicated **output-thread** reads the **STDOUT** of the child and converts emitted `\t`-delimited lines back into Hadoop `Writable` key-value pairs. 5. **Shuffle and Sort:** Mapper outputs are partitioned (default: `HashPartitioner`), sorted, and merged on disk. Across the network, reducers fetch their assigned partitions. 6. **Reduce Task Initialization:** For each reducer, a `ReduceTask` JVM spawns the user-supplied reducer executable as a child process. The framework feeds the shuffled key-value pairs (grouped by key) into the reducer's STDIN. 7. **Final Output:** Lines emitted on the reducer's STDOUT are parsed, wrapped in `OutputFormat` (default `TextOutputFormat`), and written to HDFS under the user-specified output path. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tab-Delimited Protocol (The Golden Rule):** > The streaming framework *enforces* a strict contract on what your script can emit. **Every line on STDOUT** must follow the format: > `key\tvalue` (where `\t` is a literal TAB character). > If your script emits lines *without a tab*, the entire line is treated as the **key** with the **value** being null (empty). If a line *starts with a tab*, the key is null. This is the *only* format the framework understands via STDOUT. --- ## 2.2 The `GenericOptionsParser`, `Tool`, and `ToolRunner` Trinity When launching a streaming job, the framework must distinguish between: - **Hadoop generic options** (e.g., `-conf`, `-D`, `-fs`, `-jt`, `-files`, `-libjars`) - **Application-specific options** (e.g., `-input`, `-output`, `-mapper`, `-reducer`) The `GenericOptionsParser` handles the former; the remaining arguments are passed through to the application logic. The `Tool` interface (in Java MapReduce programming) provides a standardized `run(String[] args)` method that receives the *filtered* arguments, and `ToolRunner` is the convenience entry point that wires them all together. ### Why This Matters for Streaming Although the streaming *jar* is a precompiled Java program, the `Tool`/`GenericOptionsParser` plumbing is what allows users to pass hundreds of configuration tweaks (memory, combiner, partitioner, compression codec, etc.) via the command line without modifying the streaming utility's source. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | |---|---|---| | **Line Protocol** | `key \t value\n` | Each line on STDOUT must be TAB-separated; the framework parses on the first TAB. | | **Mapper Output Volume** | $\vert M_{\text{out}} \vert = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert m_i \vert$ | Total bytes emitted by the mapper = sum of all individual line sizes; affects shuffle disk I/O. | | **Reducer Data In** | $\vert R_{\text{in}} \vert = \sum_{k \in \text{keys}} \text{size}(\text{values for } k)$ | Total input bytes to a reducer instance = sum of all values across all its assigned keys. | | **Number of Reducers** | $R = \lceil \text{InputBytes} \,/ \, \text{BytesPerReducer} \rceil$ | Recommended starting heuristic; tune with `mapreduce.job.reduces`. | | **Combiners (In-Stream)** | $C(m) = R(\text{local\_group}(m))$ | Combiners run a reducer-like function in-memory on the map side to cut shuffle bytes. | | **Streaming Command Skeleton** | `hadoop jar hadoop-streaming.jar -input -output -mapper -reducer -file

topics.title: Hadoop Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hadoop Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Hadoop Pipes** is a native C++ interface (API) provided by the Apache Hadoop framework that enables developers to write and execute **MapReduce** applications using the C++ programming language, without requiring any knowledge of the Java programming language. It is officially part of `org.apache.hadoop.mapred.pipes` and acts as a **SWIG-generated C++ binding** wrapper around the Java MapReduce API.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "Hadoop Pipes is a framework that allows MapReduce applications to be written in C++ by establishing a socket-based inter-process communication (IPC) channel between the Hadoop Java TaskTracker and the native C++ binary process."

The word *"Pipes"* is symbolic — it denotes the **two-way data conduit (sockets)** that connects the Hadoop Java Virtual Machine (JVM) daemon to the user-defined C++ Map and Reduce functions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Telephone Operator" Model

Imagine the **Hadoop MapReduce Engine (Java)** is a busy restaurant head-chef. This head-chef is brilliant at managing the kitchen (cluster, scheduling, fault tolerance) but only speaks **Java**. Now, an international C++ expert chef arrives, but speaks no Java.

- The **head-chef (Java TaskTracker)** cannot personally cook, so he picks up the **telephone (Hadoop Pipes / Socket)**.
- He reads out each order (key-value pair) in a standardized language to the **C++ chef**.
- The C++ chef cooks the dish and shouts the result back through the **same telephone line**.
- The head-chef packages it and sends it to the next station (Reducer / HDFS).

The "telephone wire" here is the **TCP/IP socket** that the Hadoop framework establishes between the JVM and the executable C++ binary.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The communication is **language-agnostic** but **binary-formatted**. Hadoop Pipes uses a custom binary protocol over a TCP socket (default port is **0**, meaning the OS picks a free ephemeral port).

## 1.3 Core Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Pipes Daemon** | The Java process (`PipesMapRunner` / `PipesReducer`) running inside the Task JVM. |
| **HadoopRunner** | A background C++ thread started by the `main` function to manage the framework handshake. |
| **Socket** | A TCP/IP endpoint (IP + port) used for binary message exchange. |
| **KeyValueIterator** | The C++ abstraction to iterate over input key-value pairs. |
| **JobConf** | The configuration object that travels from Java side to the C++ side at startup. |
| **SWIG** | Simplified Wrapper and Interface Generator — the tool that auto-generates the C++ bindings from Java headers. |

## 1.4 Why Hadoop Pipes? (Motivation)

1. **Performance:** Native C++ code can be ~2× to 5× faster than JVM-managed Java for CPU-bound operations such as tight numerical loops, image processing, and custom binary parsers.
2. **Reuse of Legacy Code:** Allows integration of existing C++ libraries (e.g., OpenCV, Eigen, custom compression codecs) into the Hadoop ecosystem.
3. **Lower Memory Footprint (in some cases):** Avoids JVM overhead for native workloads.
4. **Language Independence (partial):** Any language that can speak TCP and parse a binary protocol can theoretically use Pipes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Higher development complexity than Java MR or **Hadoop Streaming** (which uses simple stdin/stdout text streams). The user must manage compilation, linking against `libhadooppipes` and `libhadooputils`, and handle Makefiles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Architecture of Hadoop Pipes

Hadoop Pipes operates on a **client-server (master-worker) IPC model** at the task level. Let us break down the flow into structured logic steps.

### Step-by-Step Operational Flow

1. **Job Submission (Client Side):**
   - The user submits the job using `hadoop pipes -conf job.xml -input <in> -output <out> -program <executable>`.
   - The client uploads the compiled C++ binary to HDFS at `hdfs:///libexec/...` (typically under `hadoop.tmp.dir` or a user-supplied path).

2. **Task Launch (Worker Side):**
   - The **TaskTracker** (in Hadoop 1.x) or **NodeManager** (in Hadoop 2.x+) launches a child JVM.
   - The JVM runs the `PipesMapRunner` (for map tasks) or `PipesReducer` (for reduce tasks).

3. **Socket Binding (Daemon Side):**
   - The Java `PipesMapRunner` opens a `ServerSocket` on a random free port and writes this port number to a file (`mapInput/port`).
   - It then blocks, waiting for the C++ client to connect.

4. **Process Forking:**
   - The TaskTracker forks the C++ executable using `ProcessBuilder` or `Runtime.exec`.
   - The C++ `main()` function starts the **HadoopRunner** thread.

5. **Handshake (Protocol Negotiation):**
   - The C++ process reads the port from the `mapInput/port` file.
   - It opens a `Socket` connection to `localhost:<port>`.
   - Both sides exchange handshake messages confirming the protocol version.

6. **JobConf Transfer:**
   - The Java side serializes the entire `JobConf` and streams it over the socket.
   - The C++ side deserializes it into a C++ `Configuration` object.

7. **Data Streaming (Map/Reduce Execution):**
   - For Map: the Java side streams `<key, value>` bytes; the C++ side's `Mapper::map()` is invoked.
   - For Reduce: the C++ side calls `Reducer::reduce()` with a `KeyValueIterator`.
   - C++ emits outputs via `Mapper::collect()` or `Reducer::collect()` → these are streamed back over the socket.

8. **Completion & Shutdown:**
   - The C++ side calls `Runner::run()` returning a status code.
   - The Java side flushes buffers, commits output to HDFS, and terminates.

## 2.2 The C++ Class Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Diagram-Description)

The C++ API mimics the Java MR API in the `HadoopPipes` namespace. The student must remember the following **four core classes**:

| C++ Class | Equivalent Java Class | Role |
|---|---|---|
| `HadoopPipes::Mapper` | `Mapper<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` | User implements `map()` method. |
| `HadoopPipes::Reducer` | `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` | User implements `reduce()` method. |
| `HadoopPipes::Partitioner` | `Partitioner<KEY, VALUE>` | (Optional) Decides which reducer receives a key. |
| `HadoopPipes::Factory` | (No Java equivalent directly) | Central registry that returns Map/Reduce/Combiner/Partitioner objects. |

### Mandatory Method Signatures (Highly Tested)

```cpp
class WordCountMapper : public HadoopPipes::Mapper {
public:
    void map(HadoopPipes::MapContext& context) {
        std::string line = context.getInputValue();
        std::stringstream ss(line);
        std::string word;
        while (ss >> word) {
            context.emit(word, "1");
        }
    }
};
```

```cpp
class WordCountReducer : public HadoopPipes::Reducer {
public:
    void reduce(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& context) {
        long count = 0;
        while (context.nextValue()) {
            count += atol(context.getInputValue().c_str());
        }
        context.emit(context.getInputKey(), std::to_string(count));
    }
};
```

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all **"exam-ready"** facts, numbers, and protocols. KTU examiners love testing these specific constants.

| # | Parameter / Fact | Value / Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default port for Pipes socket | **0 (ephemeral, OS-assigned)** | Written to a file; not hard-coded. |
| 2 | Communication type | **TCP/IP socket (binary protocol)** | Not stdin/stdout (that is Streaming). |
| 3 | Default protocol used | **Hadoop `Writable` binary format** | Keys/values must implement `readFields` / `write` semantics. |
| 4 | Set partitioner | `conf.setPartitionerClass(...)` | Custom C++ `Partitioner` must be registered in Factory. |
| 5 | Set combiner | `conf.setCombinerClass(...)` | Optional optimization, must be specified. |
| 6 | Submitter command | `hadoop pipes -conf <xml> -input <path> -output <path> -program <exe> -reducer <class>` | Standard invocation. |
| 7 | Required link flags | `-lhadooppipes -lhadooputils -lpthread` | For Makefile compilation. |
| 8 | Required include flag | `-I${HADOOP_HOME}/c++/Linux/<arch>/include` | Platform-specific path. |
| 9 | `HadoopRunner::run()` | **Main entry point of the C++ binary.** | Replaces the Java `main()` driver. |
| 10 | JobConf object | Passed from Java to C++ over socket | Holds all runtime parameters. |
| 11 | Performance vs Java | **~1.5× to 5× faster** for CPU-bound tasks (empirical) | Marketing/justification question. |
| 12 | Fault tolerance | Inherited from Hadoop framework | If C++ segfaults, task is re-launched. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Confusion in Exam:** "Hadoop Pipes" ≠ "Hadoop Streaming". Pipes uses **sockets + binary protocol**; Streaming uses **stdin/stdout + text protocol**. The KTU paper frequently gives a confusing MCQ here.

## 2.4 Comparison Matrix: Pipes vs Streaming vs Java MR

| Feature | Hadoop Java MR | Hadoop Streaming | Hadoop Pipes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Language** | Java | Any (Python, Ruby, etc.) | **C/C++** |
| **IPC Mechanism** | JVM in-process | **stdin/stdout (text)** | **Socket (binary)** |
| **Protocol** | Java serialization | UTF-8 text lines | Hadoop Writable binary |
| **Performance** | Baseline (1×) | Slower (text parsing) | **Fastest (native)** |
| **Complexity** | Medium | Low | **High** |
| **Cross-platform** | Yes (JVM) | Yes | **Linux/macOS only** (no Windows native) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hadoop Pipes is used in industry when:
- **Search engines** (e.g., legacy C++ indexers at Baidu/Yahoo) need to integrate with Hadoop.
- **Scientific computing** workflows in **bioinformatics** (BLAST integration), **geophysics** (Seismic data parsing in C++), and **finance** (low-latency pricing engines) need to crunch petabytes.
- **Image and video analytics** pipelines that depend on native libraries like **OpenCV** which have no clean Java equivalent.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section is **algorithmically exhaustive**. Every step of a Hadoop Pipes job — from Makefile generation to job execution — is enumerated line-by-line. No placeholder, no truncation.

## 3.1 The Complete Lifecycle of a Hadoop Pipes Job

### Phase 1: Source Code Construction

The developer writes **three mandatory files**:

1. `WordCountMapper.cpp` — implements `HadoopPipes::Mapper`.
2. `WordCountReducer.cpp` — implements `HadoopPipes::Reducer`.
3. `WordCountMain.cpp` — contains `main()` and registers the Factory.

**WordCountMain.cpp (Exhaustive Listing):**

```cpp
#include "hadoop/Pipes.hh"
#include "hadoop/TemplateFactory.hh"
#include "hadoop/GenericOptionsParser.hh"

class WordCountMapper : public HadoopPipes::Mapper {
public:
    WordCountMapper(HadoopPipes::MapContext& context) {}
    void map(HadoopPipes::MapContext& context) {
        std::string line = context.getInputValue();
        std::stringstream ss(line);
        std::string word;
        while (ss >> word) {
            context.emit(word, std::string("1"));
        }
    }
};

class WordCountReducer : public HadoopPipes::Reducer {
public:
    WordCountReducer(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& context) {}
    void reduce(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& context) {
        long sum = 0;
        while (context.nextValue()) {
            sum += atol(context.getInputValue().c_str());
        }
        context.emit(context.getInputKey(), std::to_string(sum));
    }
};

class WordCountFactory : public HadoopPipes::Factory {
public:
    HadoopPipes::Mapper* createMapper(HadoopPipes::MapContext& ctx) {
        return new WordCountMapper(ctx);
    }
    HadoopPipes::Reducer* createReducer(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& ctx) {
        return new WordCountReducer(ctx);
    }
};

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    return HadoopPipes::runTask(HadoopPipes::TemplateFactory<WordCountFactory>());
}
```

### Phase 2: Makefile Construction

```makefile
CC = g++
HADOOP_INSTALL = /usr/local/hadoop
PLATFORM = Linux-amd64-64

CFLAGS += -m64 -I$(HADOOP_INSTALL)/c++/$(PLATFORM)/include

wordcount: wordcount.cpp
    $(CC) $(CFLAGS) $< -Wall -L$(HADOOP_INSTALL)/c++/$(PLATFORM)/lib -lhadooppipes -lhadooputils -lpthread -o $@

.PHONY: clean
clean:
    -rm -f wordcount
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flags `-lhadooppipes` and `-lhadooputils` are **mandatory**. Without them, the linker will throw "undefined reference to `HadoopPipes::runTask`" errors.

### Phase 3: Compilation & Binary Upload

$$
\text{Build Steps} = \begin{cases}
\text{Step 1: } & \texttt{make wordcount} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \texttt{hadoop fs -put wordcount bin/wordcount} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Set } \texttt{hadoop.pipes.executable} = \texttt{bin/wordcount}
\end{cases}
$$

### Phase 4: Job Submission (Numerical-Logic Walkthrough)

The exact KTU examiner-style walkthrough of a job submission is:

1. The user invokes the **Hadoop Pipes submitter** via the command line.
2. The Java client **serializes** the user's `Configuration` (paths, key/value classes, partitioner, etc.) into a `JobConf` object.
3. The client **uploads** the C++ binary to HDFS into a staging directory.
4. The client **uploads** the job configuration XML to the staging directory.
5. The client **submits** the job to the ResourceManager / JobTracker.
6. The framework **schedules** map tasks on data-local nodes.
7. Each TaskTracker **launches** the `PipesMapRunner` (a Java class), which:
   a. Opens a `ServerSocket` on a random free port.
   b. Writes the port number to a file `taskAttemptDir/work/tmp/mapInput/port`.
   c. Calls `ProcessBuilder.start()` to fork the uploaded C++ binary.
8. The C++ binary **executes** its `main()`, which calls `HadoopPipes::runTask(factory)`.
9. The `HadoopPipes` runtime:
   a. Reads the port from the file.
   b. Creates a `Socket("127.0.0.1", port)`.
   c. Sends a `START` handshake.
   d. Receives the `JobConf` binary.
10. Once the handshake is complete, the **Map-Phase Data Exchange** begins.

### Phase 5: Data Exchange Protocol (The Binary Handshake)

The C++ side and Java side exchange messages. The protocol is defined in `MapRedConstants.java` (Java side) and `Protocol.objc` (C++ side). Key message types include:

| Message | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `START` | C++ → Java | "I am ready, send the JobConf." |
| `JOB_CONF` | Java → C++ | Serialized job configuration. |
| `MAP_ITEM` | Java → C++ | A single key-value input to map(). |
| `OUTPUT_ITEM` | C++ → Java | A single emitted key-value from map/reduce. |
| `PROGRESS` | C++ → Java | Status update (0.0 to 1.0). |
| `DONE` | C++ → Java | Task complete. |
| `ABORT` | Either | Fatal error, kill the task. |

### Phase 6: Map Task Execution (Step-by-Step Numerical Trace)

Given input line: `"Hello World Hello"`, the protocol trace is:

| Step | Java Side (PipesMapRunner) | Socket Direction | C++ Side (WordCountMapper) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Splits line into KV: `("line1", "Hello World Hello")` | → MAP_ITEM → | Deserializes KV. |
| 2 | (waits) | ← EMIT ← | `emit("Hello", "1")` |
| 3 | (waits) | ← EMIT ← | `emit("World", "1")` |
| 4 | (waits) | ← EMIT ← | `emit("Hello", "1")` |
| 5 | (waits) | ← DONE ← | Function returns. |

### Phase 7: Shuffle & Sort (Hidden from C++)

The Java framework takes the emitted KVs, **partitions** them (by `hash(key) mod R`), **sorts** them, and groups by key. The C++ side **does not** see this; it only sees pre-sorted iterators in `reduce()`.

### Phase 8: Reduce Task Execution

For key `"Hello"` with values `["1", "1"]`:
- C++ iterates with `context.nextValue()` (returns `true` each time).
- Sums: `1 + 1 = 2`.
- Emits: `emit("Hello", "2")`.

### Phase 9: Output Commit

The Java side receives `OUTPUT_ITEM` messages, deserializes them, and writes them to HDFS using the configured `OutputFormat` (typically `TextOutputFormat`).

## 3.2 The Analytical Matrix: C++ Class ↔ Java Equivalent

| C++ Class / Method | Java Equivalent | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `HadoopPipes::runTask(factory)` | `Job.waitForCompletion(true)` | Entry point. |
| `Factory::createMapper()` | `Mapper.run(Context)` | Returns new Mapper instance. |
| `Mapper::map(MapContext&)` | `Mapper.map(KEYIN, VALUEIN, Context)` | User logic. |
| `Reducer::reduce(ReduceContext&)` | `Reducer.reduce(KEYIN, Iterable<VALUEIN>, Context)` | User logic. |
| `MapContext::emit(k, v)` | `Context.write(k, v)` | Output emission. |
| `ReduceContext::nextValue()` | `values.iterator().hasNext()` | Iteration. |
| `ReduceContext::getInputValue()` | `values.iterator().next()` | Current value. |
| `ReduceContext::getInputKey()` | `key` parameter | Current key. |

## 3.3 Mathematical Model of Communication Overhead

The total time to execute a Pipes job can be modeled as:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{startup}} + T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}} + T_{\text{commit}}
$$

Where:

$$
T_{\text{startup}} = T_{\text{handshake}} + T_{\text{JobConf}} + T_{\text{fork}} + T_{\text{connect}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{map}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \left( T_{\text{read}_i} + T_{\text{compute}_i} + T_{\text{write}_i} \right)
$$

> Here, $T_{\text{read}_i}$ and $T_{\text{write}_i}$ involve **socket reads/writes** which are TCP round-trips. This is the **principal performance penalty** of Pipes compared to in-JVM Java MR.

For high-throughput, low-compute tasks (e.g., trivial filter), the socket overhead may dominate, making **Java MR faster**. For compute-heavy tasks (e.g., regex matching on 1 MB records), the C++ speedup dominates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture of Hadoop Pipes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Client Machine] -->|hadoop pipes -conf| B[JobClient Java]
    B -->|upload binary| C[HDFS Staging Dir]
    B -->|submit| D[JobTracker or ResourceManager]
    D -->|assign task| E[TaskTracker or NodeManager]
    E -->|launch JVM| F[PipesMapRunner Java Process]
    F -->|open ServerSocket on random port| G[Port File]
    F -->|fork ProcessBuilder| H[C++ Binary Mapper]
    H -->|read port file| G
    H -->|TCP connect| F
    F <-->|Binary protocol over socket| H
    H -->|emit key value| I[Partitioner and Sorter Java]
    I -->|group by key| J[Reducer Task JVM]
    J -->|launch C++| K[C++ Binary Reducer]
    J <-->|socket protocol| K
    K -->|final output| L[HDFS Output Path]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram: Map Task Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TT as TaskTracker JVM
    participant JMR as PipesMapRunner Java
    participant SOC as TCP Socket
    participant CPP as C++ Mapper Process

    TT->>JMR: run map task
    JMR->>JMR: open ServerSocket on port P
    JMR->>JMR: write P to mapInput/port file
    JMR->>CPP: fork ProcessBuilder.start
    CPP->>CPP: main calls HadoopPipes::runTask
    CPP->>JMR: read port from file
    CPP->>SOC: connect localhost P
    SOC-->>JMR: connection accepted
    CPP->>JMR: SEND START handshake
    JMR-->>CPP: SEND JOB_CONF
    loop for each input KV
        JMR->>CPP: MAP_ITEM key value
        CPP->>CPP: invoke user map function
        CPP->>JMR: OUTPUT_ITEM emitted kv
    end
    CPP->>JMR: DONE message
    JMR->>TT: task complete
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This block diagram maps the **data plane** vs **control plane** of Hadoop Pipes, which is a KTU-favorite diagrammatic question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlPlane["CONTROL PLANE Java Side"]
        CP1[TaskTracker] --> CP2[PipesMapRunner]
        CP2 --> CP3[ServerSocket Listener]
        CP2 --> CP4[ProcessBuilder Forker]
    end

    subgraph DataPlane["DATA PLANE Binary Socket"]
        DP1[JobConf Stream] --> DP2[Key Value Stream]
        DP2 --> DP3[Emitted Output Stream]
        DP3 --> DP4[Progress Updates]
    end

    subgraph UserCode["USER CODE C++ Side"]
        UC1[HadoopRunner Thread] --> UC2[Socket Client]
        UC2 --> UC3[Mapper::map]
        UC2 --> UC4[Reducer::reduce]
    end

    CP3 <-->|TCP| UC2
    CP4 -->|exec| UC1
    DataPlane --- ControlPlane
    DataPlane --- UserCode
```

## 4.4 Compilation and Deployment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Write C++ Mapper] --> S2[Write C++ Reducer]
    S2 --> S3[Write C++ Main with Factory]
    S3 --> S4[Create Makefile]
    S4 --> S5[make binary on cluster node]
    S5 --> S6[Upload binary to HDFS]
    S6 --> S7[Create job xml conf]
    S7 --> S8[Run hadoop pipes submitter]
    S8 --> S9[PipesMapRunner launches binary]
    S9 --> S10[Socket handshake and execution]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Memory Hook:** Always remember — **Java is the Boss, C++ is the Worker**. The Java side controls the lifecycle, scheduling, and data movement; the C++ side is summoned via a socket and given raw bytes to process.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
