# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern. Marks are partitioned as **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice)**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Hadoop Pipes. State any two advantages over Java MapReduce. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Hadoop Pipes is a C++ API provided by the Apache Hadoop framework that allows developers to write MapReduce programs in C++ by establishing a socket-based inter-process communication channel between the Hadoop Java TaskTracker and the user-supplied C++ binary.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Performance:** Native C++ code executes faster than JVM-managed Java for CPU-bound tasks such as image processing and scientific computing.
2. **Reuse of Legacy Code:** Existing C/C++ libraries (e.g., OpenCV, BLAS, custom codecs) can be directly integrated into the Hadoop data pipeline without rewriting in Java.

*(Word count target for full 3 marks: 70-100 words.)*

### Question 2: Distinguish between Hadoop Pipes and Hadoop Streaming. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Hadoop Pipes | Hadoop Streaming |
|---|---|---|
| Language | C/C++ | Any (Python, Ruby, etc.) |
| IPC Mechanism | TCP socket (binary) | Standard input/output (text) |
| Protocol | Hadoop Writable binary | UTF-8 text lines |
| Complexity | High (Makefile, linking) | Low (script files) |
| Performance | Faster (native code) | Slower (text parsing overhead) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students write "Hadoop Pipes uses stdin/stdout" — this is **WRONG**; it is socket-based.
> 2. Students forget to mention that the **Java side is the master** and **C++ is the worker**.
> 3. The `Factory` class and `HadoopRunner` are often confused — `Factory` creates user classes, `HadoopRunner` is the internal C++ thread.
> 4. Do not confuse `combiner` and `partitioner` — both are optional, but `combiner` is a mini-reducer on the map side.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Hadoop Pipes — Architecture, Communication, and Programming Model

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the architecture and communication mechanism of Hadoop Pipes with a suitable diagram. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The architecture of Hadoop Pipes is based on a **client-server socket model** between the Hadoop Java framework and the user-supplied C++ binary. The communication steps are as follows:

**Step 1: Task Initialization**
When a MapReduce job is submitted via the Pipes submitter, the JobClient serializes the `JobConf` and uploads the C++ binary to HDFS. The framework schedules tasks on data-local TaskTrackers.

**Step 2: ServerSocket Binding (Java Side)**
The TaskTracker launches a JVM running `PipesMapRunner` (for map) or `PipesReducer` (for reduce). This Java class:
- Opens a `java.net.ServerSocket` on a random free port.
- Writes the assigned port number to a port file inside the task's working directory.
- Waits (blocks) on `socket.accept()` for an incoming connection.

**Step 3: Process Forking**
The Java class uses `ProcessBuilder` to fork the uploaded C++ binary as a child OS process. The C++ `main()` function executes, calling `HadoopPipes::runTask(factory)`.

**Step 4: Handshake**
The C++ runtime:
- Reads the port from the port file.
- Creates a `Socket("127.0.0.1", port)` connection.
- Sends a `START` message; receives a `JOB_CONF` binary message.

**Step 5: Data Exchange**
The Java side streams input key-value pairs as `MAP_ITEM` messages. The C++ user function processes them and emits outputs via `OUTPUT_ITEM` messages back over the same socket.

**Step 6: Completion**
The C++ side sends `DONE`; the Java side commits output to HDFS.

**Diagram (Textual Representation):**

```
[Client] → [JobClient] → [HDFS: binary+conf] → [TaskTracker]
                                                      ↓
                                  [PipesMapRunner JVM: ServerSocket]
                                                      ↑↓
                                              [C++ Binary Process]
                                                      ↓
                                                 [HDFS Output]
```

**Conclusion:** This socket-based architecture decouples the C++ business logic from the Java framework, allowing native code to participate in distributed MapReduce jobs seamlessly.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: With a neat example, write a Hadoop Pipes program to count the frequency of words in a text file. List the steps to compile and execute it. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Program (WordCountMapper.cpp):**

```cpp
#include "hadoop/Pipes.hh"
#include "hadoop/TemplateFactory.hh"
#include <sstream>

class WordCountMapper : public HadoopPipes::Mapper {
public:
    WordCountMapper(HadoopPipes::MapContext& context) {}
    void map(HadoopPipes::MapContext& context) {
        std::string line = context.getInputValue();
        std::stringstream ss(line);
        std::string word;
        while (ss >> word) {
            context.emit(word, std::string("1"));
        }
    }
};
```

**Program (WordCountReducer.cpp):**

```cpp
#include "hadoop/Pipes.hh"

class WordCountReducer : public HadoopPipes::Reducer {
public:
    WordCountReducer(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& context) {}
    void reduce(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& context) {
        long count = 0;
        while (context.nextValue()) {
            count += atol(context.getInputValue().c_str());
        }
        context.emit(context.getInputKey(), std::to_string(count));
    }
};
```

**Program (WordCountMain.cpp):**

```cpp
#include "hadoop/Pipes.hh"
#include "hadoop/TemplateFactory.hh"

class WordCountFactory : public HadoopPipes::Factory {
public:
    HadoopPipes::Mapper* createMapper(HadoopPipes::MapContext& ctx) {
        return new WordCountMapper(ctx);
    }
    HadoopPipes::Reducer* createReducer(HadoopPipes::ReduceContext& ctx) {
        return new WordCountReducer(ctx);
    }
};

int main(int argc, char *argv[]) {
    return HadoopPipes::runTask(HadoopPipes::TemplateFactory<WordCountFactory>());
}
```

**Compilation Steps:**

1. Save the three files in a project directory.
2. Create a `Makefile` with `CFLAGS = -I${HADOOP_HOME}/c++/$(PLATFORM)/include` and link flags `-lhadooppipes -lhadooputils -lpthread`.
3. Run `make` to produce the `wordcount` binary.

**Execution Steps:**

1. Upload binary to HDFS: `hadoop fs -put wordcount bin/wordcount`.
2. Create `wordcount.xml` configuring `mapred.pipes.executable=bin/wordcount`.
3. Submit: `hadoop pipes -conf conf/hadoop-localhost.xml -input in.txt -output wc_output -program bin/wordcount -reducer org.apache.hadoop.mapred.pipes.WordCountReducer`.
4. View output: `hadoop fs -cat wc_output/part-00000`.

**Conclusion:** This demonstrates a complete end-to-end Hadoop Pipes application using native C++ for both the mapper and reducer phases.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Hadoop Pipes — Comparison, Configuration, and Performance

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Compare Hadoop Pipes with Hadoop Streaming and Java MapReduce. Discuss when each is most suitable. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Java MapReduce | Hadoop Streaming | Hadoop Pipes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Language | Java | Any (text-based) | C/C++ |
| IPC | In-JVM method calls | stdin/stdout text | TCP socket binary |
| Setup Effort | Medium (JAR build) | Low (script only) | High (Makefile, linking) |
| Speed | Baseline (1×) | Slowest (text parse) | Fastest (native code) |
| Fault Tolerance | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Custom Writable Types | Full support | Limited | Full support |
| Cross-platform | All (JVM) | All | Linux/macOS only |

**Suitability Analysis:**

- **Java MapReduce** is best for **general-purpose, production-grade** Hadoop jobs where maintainability and ecosystem integration matter most.
- **Hadoop Streaming** is best for **rapid prototyping**, **ad-hoc analytics**, and when developers are not Java experts (e.g., data scientists using Python/R).
- **Hadoop Pipes** is best for **CPU-intensive, performance-critical** workloads such as scientific computing, image processing, and integration with legacy C++ libraries.

**Use-Case Example:** A bioinformatics firm running BLAST alignments on 100 TB of genomic data would prefer **Hadoop Pipes** because the BLAST engine is natively C-based and socket-based communication is faster than text streaming.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Explain the role of the `Factory` class, `HadoopRunner`, and `JobConf` in Hadoop Pipes. How are they exchanged between the Java and C++ processes? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. The `Factory` Class (User-Side, C++):**
The `Factory` is a C++ abstract base class (defined in `hadoop/Pipes.hh`) that the user subclasses. It serves as a **central registry** for the user-defined Mapper, Reducer, Combiner, and Partitioner. The user implements:
- `createMapper(MapContext&)` → returns a new `Mapper*`.
- `createReducer(ReduceContext&)` → returns a new `Reducer*`.
- `createCombiner(MapContext&)` → optional, returns a Combiner.
- `createPartitioner(...)` → optional, returns a Partitioner.

The Factory is passed to `HadoopPipes::runTask(factory)`, and the runtime invokes its methods to instantiate the user's logic classes.

**2. The `HadoopRunner` (Internal, C++):**
`HadoopRunner` is a C++ **background thread** automatically started by `runTask()`. It manages the socket communication:
- Listens for incoming JobConf messages.
- Reads MAP_ITEM messages and dispatches them to the user's `map()` function.
- Sends OUTPUT_ITEM messages back to Java.
- Periodically sends PROGRESS updates.
- Handles protocol errors and ABORT messages.

The user never directly interacts with `HadoopRunner`; it is an internal abstraction.

**3. The `JobConf` (Cross-Process Configuration):**
`JobConf` is the Hadoop **configuration object** containing all runtime parameters:
- Input/output paths.
- Input/output format classes.
- Mapper, Reducer, Combiner, Partitioner classes.
- Key/Value serialization classes.
- Number of reducers, sort options, compression codecs.

**Exchange Mechanism:**
- The Java side **serializes** the `JobConf` into a binary byte stream.
- This stream is sent as a `JOB_CONF` message over the socket immediately after the handshake.
- The C++ side **deserializes** it into a C++ `Configuration` object, which the user can query via `context.getJobConf()` to read parameters like `mapreduce.job.reduces`.

**Conclusion:** Together, `Factory`, `HadoopRunner`, and `JobConf` form the **three pillars** of the C++ side — the Factory supplies user logic, the HadoopRunner manages communication, and the JobConf supplies configuration — all bridged by a TCP socket.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- ✅ Hadoop Pipes is a **C++ API** for writing MapReduce jobs natively without Java.
- ✅ Communication happens over a **TCP socket** (NOT stdin/stdout).
- ✅ The **default port is 0 (ephemeral)**; the port is written to a file and read by the C++ side.
- ✅ The **Java side is the master** (`PipesMapRunner` / `PipesReducer`); the **C++ side is the worker**.
- ✅ `HadoopPipes::runTask(factory)` is the **main entry point** of the C++ binary.
- ✅ The `Factory` class is **user-defined** and registers Mapper/Reducer/Combiner/Partitioner.
- ✅ `HadoopRunner` is an **internal C++ thread** that manages socket I/O — students should not confuse it with the user's `main()`.
- ✅ `JobConf` is the **configuration object** that travels from Java to C++ over the socket.
- ✅ Compilation requires linking `-lhadooppipes` and `-lhadooputils`.
- ✅ Submission command: `hadoop pipes -conf <xml> -input <in> -output <out> -program <exe>`.
- ✅ **Pipes vs Streaming:** Pipes = socket + binary; Streaming = stdin/stdout + text.
- ✅ Pipes is **faster than Java MR for CPU-bound tasks** but has **higher setup complexity**.
- ✅ Pipes supports **custom Writable types** in C++ via the `Serializer` template.
- ✅ Fault tolerance: if the C++ process **segfaults**, the framework detects it and **re-launches** the task.
- ✅ Pipes is **not supported on Windows** (Linux/macOS only).
- ✅ KTU board loves testing: *"Why socket-based and not text-based?"* — Answer: **performance and language independence with binary protocol**.
- ✅ Common error: *"Pipes is slower than Streaming."* — This is **FALSE**; Pipes is faster for compute-heavy tasks.
- ✅ KTU Hot Question: *"List the four core C++ classes."* — Answer: **Mapper, Reducer, Partitioner, Factory**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Traditional Data Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Traditional Data Analysis # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Traditional Data Analysis (TDA)** refers to the classical, structured, and centralized approach of processing, storing, and analyzing data using **Relational Database Management Systems (RDBMS)**, **Data Warehouses (DW)**, and **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** tools, typically applied to structured and semi-structured data sets confined within the **TeraByte (TB)** scale and processed on a single, vertically-scaled (scale-up) server. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Traditional Data Analysis is contrasted against the modern **Big Data Analytics** paradigm. It represents the **"pre-Hadoop era"** architectural pattern characterized by **schema-on-write**, **batch-oriented ETL pipelines**, and **deterministic SQL-based querying**. ### Core Pillars of Traditional Data Analysis | Pillar | Description | Technology Stack | |---|---|---| | **Data Source Layer** | Operational/Transactional systems | ERP, CRM, Legacy DBs | | **Integration Layer** | Extract, Transform, Load (ETL) | Informatica, Talend, SSIS | | **Storage Layer** | Centralized relational warehouse | Oracle, Teradata, IBM DB2 | | **Analysis Layer** | Multi-dimensional querying | OLAP Cubes, Pivot Tables | | **Presentation Layer** | Dashboards & Reports | Tableau, Power BI, Cognos | > [!NOTE] > **Schema-on-Write vs Schema-on-Read**: Traditional systems employ *Schema-on-Write* — the schema is defined **before** data is loaded. Big Data systems (Hadoop) use *Schema-on-Read* — schema is applied **only when** data is read. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **The Library Analogy:** Imagine a **traditional library**: > - Every book arriving must first be **catalogued, indexed, and shelved** (ETL + Schema definition) **before** a reader can find it. > - Only the librarian knows the exact location; queries must go through a structured card-catalog system (SQL). > - The library building is one giant structure that can be made taller (vertical scaling) but cannot be cloned easily. > - If the library gets 10x more books, you must **buy a bigger building** — not add another wing cheaply. > > **This is Traditional Data Analysis.** It is **rigid, structured, and centrally-managed**. > In contrast, **Big Data Analytics** is like a **network of small, distributed digital archives** where you define the meaning of a document *after* you read it (Schema-on-Read). ### Why Traditional Data Analysis Exists - **Historical Context:** Until the early 2000s, enterprise data fit in **Gigabytes to a few Terabytes**, perfectly suitable for a single high-end server. - **ACID Guarantees:** Traditional systems provide **Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability** — critical for banking, inventory, and ERP. - **Mature Tooling:** SQL, stored procedures, and OLAP tools were industry standards. ### Limitations that Paved the Way for Big Data > [!WARNING] > **The "3 V's" Failure Point:** Traditional systems break down when data exhibits: > - **Volume** > ~10 TB > - **Velocity** > ~1000 transactions/sec sustained > - **Variety** (unstructured text, images, video, logs) > > When these thresholds are crossed, vertical scaling becomes **economically and physically infeasible**, forcing the paradigm shift to **horizontal scaling (scale-out)** and **distributed computing** — the foundation of Big Data. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Traditional Data Analysis Pipeline (Conceptual Breakdown) ### Step 1: Data Ingestion from Operational Systems - Source systems are **OLTP (Online Transactional Processing)** databases optimized for fast inserts/updates (e.g., a bank's ATM transaction DB). - Data is **extracted** at scheduled intervals (nightly batches, hourly micro-batches). ### Step 2: ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) The **heart** of traditional data analysis. Let us break it down: - **Extract:** Pull raw data from heterogeneous source systems (Oracle, MySQL, flat files, XML). - **Transform:** Apply business rules — cleansing, deduplication, normalization, currency conversion, type casting. - **Load:** Insert the transformed, clean data into the target Data Warehouse. > [!IMPORTANT] > **ETL vs ELT:** In modern cloud warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery), the paradigm shifts to **ELT** (Extract, Load, Transform) — raw data is loaded first, then transformed using the warehouse's compute power. This is a **hybrid** between traditional and modern approaches. ### Step 3: Data Warehouse Storage A **Data Warehouse (DW)** is a **subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, non-volatile** collection of data designed to support management decisions (W.H. Inmon's classical definition). | Characteristic | Meaning | |---|---| | **Subject-Oriented** | Organized by business domain (Sales, HR, Finance), not by application | | **Integrated** | Unified data formats, naming conventions, units across sources | | **Time-Variant** | Stores historical data (5-10 years) for trend analysis | | **Non-Volatile** | Data is **read-only** after loading — no updates/deletes | ### Step 4: OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) OLAP enables **multi-dimensional analysis** through **Data Cubes**. A **Data Cube** is a multi-dimensional array allowing queries like: > *"Total sales of Product P in Region R during Quarter Q, broken down by Salesperson S."* The basic OLAP operations are: - **Roll-up (Drill-up):** Aggregate data to a higher level (City → Country). - **Drill-down:** Reverse of roll-up (Year → Quarter → Month). - **Slice:** Select a single value on one dimension (Sales in 2024 only). - **Dice:** Select a sub-cube by fixing values on multiple dimensions. - **Pivot (Rotate):** Re-orient the cube view (swap rows/columns). ### Step 5: Reporting & Visualization - BI tools query the warehouse using SQL or MDX (Multi-Dimensional eXpressions). - Outputs: Static reports, interactive dashboards, scheduled email digests. --- ## 2.2 Data Warehouse Architecture Models ### A. Top-Down (Inmon Approach) 1. Build the **enterprise-wide Data Warehouse** first. 2. Build **Data Marts** (subject-specific subsets) from the DW. 3. End users query Data Marts. ### B. Bottom-Up (Kimball Approach) 1. Build **Data Marts** first (star schema for one business process). 2. The **union of Data Marts** forms the Data Warehouse (called the *Data Warehouse Bus*). 3. Faster initial deployment, but risks integration issues later. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Focus:** The Kimball **Star Schema** is the most tested topic in traditional data warehousing. The central table is the **Fact Table** (measurable events), and surrounding tables are **Dimension Tables** (descriptive attributes). --- ## 2.3 Star Schema vs Snowflake Schema | Feature | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | |---|---|---| | **Structure** | Denormalized | Normalized | | **Dimension Tables** | Flat (single level) | Hierarchical (multi-level) | | **Query Speed** | Faster (fewer joins) | Slower (more joins) | | **Storage** | Higher redundancy | Lower redundancy | | **Complexity** | Simple | Complex | | **Use Case** | OLAP, BI dashboards | Complex hierarchies | > [!TIP] > **Memory Aid:** *Star* = flat and *fast* (like a star radiating outward). *Snowflake* = intricate and branched (like a snowflake's crystal pattern). --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following metrics are essential for answering KTU numerical/analytical questions on Traditional Data Analysis. | # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Description | Unit / Scale | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Vertical Scaling Cost** | $C_v = C_0 \cdot \alpha^{\log_2(S/S_0)}$ | Cost grows **exponentially** with server size | USD | | 2 | **Data Warehouse Storage** | $S_{DW} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i \cdot C_i)$ | Sum of (Rows × Column-size) per fact table | TB | | 3 | **ETL Throughput** | $T_{ETL} = \dfrac{V_{data}}{t_{batch}}$ | Volume processed per unit batch time | GB/hour | | 4 | **OLAP Cube Cardinality** | $\vert C \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{d} \vert D_i \vert$ | Product of dimension cardinalities | Cells | | 5 | **Star Schema Joins** | $J_{star} = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{m} I_k$ | 1 fact join + optional dimension filter joins | Integer | | 6 | **Compression Ratio** | $CR = \dfrac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}}$ | Storage efficiency | Ratio | | 7 | **Query Response Time** | $t_{resp} = t_{IO} + t_{CPU} + t_{network}$ | Decomposed latency components | Seconds | | 8 | **Data Aging Index** | $DAI = \dfrac{\text{Records > 5 years}}{\text{Total Records}}$ | Fraction of historical data | 0 to 1 | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In the formula table, all absolute-value bars and pipe symbols inside math are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity (per protocol). ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **Banking & Finance:** OLAP cubes for fraud pattern detection over historical transaction data. - **Retail:** Star schemas in Power BI for sales dashboards. - **Telecommunications:** ETL pipelines consolidating CDR (Call Detail Records) from regional DBs. - **Healthcare:** Data warehouses for epidemiological trend analysis. - **Manufacturing:** Quality control reporting across production lines. --- ## 2.5 Limitations of Traditional Data Analysis (The "Why Big Data?" Bridge) | # | Limitation | Explanation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Scale Ceiling** | Single-server architecture fails beyond ~10–50 TB. | | 2 | **Schema Rigidity** | Schema-on-Write cannot adapt to new data types (e.g., tweets, IoT sensor JSON). | | 3 | **Cost Explosion** | High-end servers (Oracle Exadata, Teradata) cost millions of USD. | | 4 | **Latency** | Batch ETL windows cause 12-24 hour data freshness gaps. | | 5 | **Unstructured Blindness** | Cannot natively process images, video, audio, free text. | | 6 | **No Real-time** | Streaming data (Kafka, IoT) cannot be processed in real-time. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: ETL Pipeline Analytical Walkthrough > [!NOTE] > This derivation demonstrates how a **traditional ETL pipeline** transforms raw operational data into a queryable fact-dimension model. This is a typical KTU 14-mark analytical question. ### Problem Setup A retail company has the following operational data sources: - **Source A (POS System):** 50,000 transaction records/day - **Source B (CRM System):** 2,000 customer records - **Source C (Inventory System):** 800 product records The Data Warehouse target is a **Star Schema** with: - 1 Fact Table: `FactSales` - 3 Dimension Tables: `DimCustomer`, `DimProduct`, `DimDate` ### Step 1: Extract Phase Raw row counts are pulled from operational DBs: $$R_{POS} = 50{,}000 \text{ records/day}$$ $$R_{CRM} = 2{,}000 \text{ records}$$ $$R_{INV} = 800 \text{ records}$$ Total extracted daily volume: $$R_{total}^{extract} = R_{POS} + R_{CRM} + R_{INV}$$ Substituting the numerical values: $$R_{total}^{extract} = 50{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 800$$ Performing the addition step by step: $$R_{total}^{extract} = 52{,}000 + 800$$ $$R_{total}^{extract} = 52{,}800 \text{ records/day}$$ > **Logic:** This is the **gross** data extracted before any cleansing or filtering. ### Step 2: Transform Phase (Data Cleansing) Assume the following data quality rules are applied: | Rule | Description | Records Removed | |---|---|---| | $T_1$ | Drop transactions with NULL `customer_id` | 1,200 | | $T_2$ | Drop transactions with `quantity <= 0` | 350 | | $T_3$ | Deduplicate CRM records by `email` | 150 | | $T_4$ | Normalize product names (e.g., "Apple" → "APPLE") | 0 (rename only) | Total records removed from POS: $$R_{removed}^{POS} = T_1 + T_2 = 1{,}200 + 350 = 1{,}550$$ Total records removed from CRM: $$R_{removed}^{CRM} = T_3 = 150$$ The clean record count per source: $$R_{clean}^{POS} = 50{,}000 - 1{,}550 = 48{,}450$$ $$R_{clean}^{CRM} = 2{,}000 - 150 = 1{,}850$$ $$R_{clean}^{INV} = 800 - 0 = 800$$ ### Step 3: Load Phase (Star Schema Population) The `FactSales` table stores one row per clean transaction, but with **foreign keys** to the three dimension tables. Assuming each clean POS record maps to **exactly one** customer and **exactly one** product (after referential integrity lookup): $$R_{FactSales} = R_{clean}^{POS} = 48{,}450 \text{ rows}$$ The dimension table row counts: $$R_{DimCustomer} = 1{,}850$$ $$R_{DimProduct} = 800$$ $$R_{DimDate} = 365 \text{ (one row per day in the year)}$$ ### Step 4: Storage Footprint Calculation Assume each column is sized as: | Table | Columns | Avg. Bytes/Row | Row Count | Size | |---|---|---|---|---| | FactSales | 8 | 64 B | 48,450 | $S_{fact}$ | | DimCustomer | 12 | 128 B | 1,850 | $S_{cust}$ | | DimProduct | 10 | 96 B | 800 | $S_{prod}$ | | DimDate | 6 | 48 B | 365 | $S_{date}$ | Compute each table size: $$S_{fact} = 8 \cdot 64 \cdot 48{,}450$$ Multiplying stepwise: $$8 \cdot 64 = 512$$ $$512 \cdot 48{,}450 = 24{,}806{,}400 \text{ bytes}$$ Converting to MB: $$S_{fact} = \dfrac{24{,}806{,}400}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 23.66 \text{ MB}$$ By the same process for the other tables: $$S_{cust} = 12 \cdot 128 \cdot 1{,}850 = 2{,}841{,}600 \text{ B} \approx 2.71 \text{ MB}$$ $$S_{prod} = 10 \cdot 96 \cdot 800 = 768{,}000 \text{ B} \approx 0.73 \text{ MB}$$ $$S_{date} = 6 \cdot 48 \cdot 365 = 105{,}120 \text{ B} \approx 0.10 \text{ MB}$$ ### Step 5: Total Warehouse Storage (Single Day) $$S_{DW} = S_{fact} + S_{cust} + S_{prod} + S_{date}$$ $$S_{DW} = 23.66 + 2.71 + 0.73 + 0.10$$ $$S_{DW} = 27.20 \text{ MB/day}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** Over 1 year (365 days) assuming dim tables are static: $$S_{DW}^{1yr} \approx (23.66 \cdot 365) + (2.71 + 0.73 + 0.10)$$ $$S_{DW}^{1yr} \approx 8{,}635.9 + 3.54 \approx 8{,}639.4 \text{ MB} \approx 8.44 \text{ GB/year}$$ This is well within **Traditional Data Analysis** territory, illustrating the **terabyte scale comfort zone** of RDBMS-based warehouses. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: OLAP Cube Dimension Explosion Consider a 4-dimensional cube with the following cardinalities: $$\vert D_1 \vert = 50 \text{ (Products)}$$ $$\vert D_2 \vert = 12 \text{ (Months)}$$ $$\vert D_3 \vert = 4 \text{ (Regions)}$$ $$\vert D_4 \vert = 10 \text{ (Salespersons)}$$ ### Step 1: Compute Total Cells The total number of cells in the full cube: $$\vert C \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{4} \vert D_i \vert$$ $$\vert C \vert = 50 \cdot 12 \cdot 4 \cdot 10$$ $$= 600 \cdot 40 = 24{,}000 \text{ cells}$$ ### Step 2: Identify Sparse Cells A cell is **sparse** if no sales occurred (e.g., a salesperson did not sell Product P in Region R in Month M). Assume sparsity factor $\sigma = 0.85$ (85% empty). $$N_{sparse} = 0.85 \cdot 24{,}000 = 20{,}400 \text{ empty cells}$$ $$N_{dense} = 24{,}000 - 20{,}400 = 3{,}600 \text{ populated cells}$$ ### Step 3: Storage Optimization Traditional OLAP servers store only dense cells using **run-length encoding** or **bitmap indexing**, achieving a compression ratio: $$CR = \dfrac{V_{original}}{V_{compressed}} = \dfrac{24{,}000}{3{,}600} = 6.67$$ This **6.67x compression** is why traditional OLAP can answer multi-dimensional queries in **sub-second** time despite high dimensionality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Note:** The **dimensional explosion** is a key reason why **Big Data** systems (using columnar storage like Parquet/ORC) became necessary — they handle **sparse, high-dimensional data** more efficiently than traditional row-oriented RDBMS. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Traditional Data Analysis — End-to-End Architecture > [!NOTE] > This Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete traditional data analysis pipeline**, from operational sources through ETL, Data Warehouse, OLAP, and finally BI presentation layer. ```mermaid flowchart TD srcA["OLTP Source A POS System"]:::source srcB["OLTP Source B CRM Database"]:::source srcC["OLTP Source C Legacy Flat Files"]:::source extract["Extract Stage Scheduled Batch Job"]:::etl transform["Transform Stage Cleansing Normalization Dedup"]:::etl load["Load Stage Bulk Insert into Warehouse"]:::etl staging["Staging Area Temporary Storage"]:::stage dw["Enterprise Data Warehouse Centralized RDBMS"]:::warehouse star1["Fact Table Sales Facts"]:::star star2["Dim Customer"]:::star star3["Dim Product"]:::star star4["Dim Date"]:::star olap["OLAP Engine Multidimensional Cube"]:::olap bi1["BI Dashboard Tableau PowerBI"]:::report bi2["Static Reports PDF Excel"]:::report bi3["Ad Hoc SQL Queries Analysts"]:::report srcA --> extract srcB --> extract srcC --> extract extract --> transform transform --> staging staging --> load load --> dw dw --> star1 dw --> star2 dw --> star3 dw --> star4 star1 --> olap star2 --> olap star3 --> olap star4 --> olap olap --> bi1 olap --> bi2 star1 --> bi3 star2 --> bi3 star3 --> bi3 classDef source fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000 classDef etl fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef stage fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000 classDef warehouse fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef star fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef olap fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef report fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.2 Star Schema Block Diagram > [!TIP] > The **Star Schema** is the cornerstone of the Kimball data warehousing approach. The diagram below illustrates the central fact table surrounded by denormalized dimension tables. ```mermaid flowchart LR Fact["FACT SALES
sales_id PK
customer_id FK
product_id FK
date_id FK
store_id FK
quantity
unit_price
total_amount
discount"]:::fact DC["DIM CUSTOMER
customer_id PK
name
email
city
state
country
age_group
gender"]:::dim DP["DIM PRODUCT
product_id PK
product_name
category
subcategory
brand
unit_cost"]:::dim DD["DIM DATE
date_id PK
day
month
quarter
year
weekday
is_holiday"]:::dim DS["DIM STORE
store_id PK
store_name
region
manager_name
store_type"]:::dim Fact --- DC Fact --- DP Fact --- DD Fact --- DS classDef fact fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF,stroke-width:3px classDef dim fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.3 ETL vs ELT Decision Flow (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"Data Volume
per Day"}:::decision Q2{"Schema
Flexibility
Needed"}:::decision Q3{"Latency
Requirement"}:::decision ETL["TRADITIONAL ETL
Transform BEFORE Load
Schema on Write
On Premise RDBMS"]:::etlpath ELT["MODERN ELT
Transform AFTER Load
Schema on Read
Cloud Data Warehouse"]:::eltpath HYBRID["HYBRID APPROACH
Light Transform on Extract
Heavy Transform in Warehouse"]:::hybridpath Q1 -->|Less than 100 GB| Q2 Q1 -->|Greater than 1 TB| ELT Q2 -->|Rigid Fixed Schema| Q3 Q2 -->|Evolving Schema| ELT Q3 -->|Batch OK Hours| ETL Q3 -->|Near Real Time| HYBRID classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef etlpath fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef eltpath fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef hybridpath fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` --- ## 4.4 OLAP Operations Conceptual Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Base["Base Cube
All Dimensions Active"]:::base RollUp["ROLL UP
Aggregate to Higher Level
City to Country"]:::rollup DrillDown["DRILL DOWN
Disaggregate to Lower Level
Year to Month to Day"]:::drill Slice["SLICE
Fix One Dimension Value
Year equals 2024"]:::slice Dice["DICE
Fix Multiple Dimension Values
Year 2024 AND Region Asia"]:::dice Pivot["PIVOT ROTATE
Reorient Cube Axes
Swap Rows and Columns"]:::pivot Base --> RollUp Base --> DrillDown Base --> Slice Base --> Dice Base --> Pivot RollUp --> Base DrillDown --> Base Slice --> Base Dice --> Base Pivot --> Base classDef base fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#00008B,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef rollup fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef drill fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef slice fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000 classDef dice fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef pivot fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define Traditional Data Analysis. List any four of its key characteristics.** **Model Answer:** > **Traditional Data Analysis** is the classical approach to processing enterprise data using centralized **RDBMS-based Data Warehouses**, **ETL pipelines**, and **OLAP tools** for structured, historical data typically within the **TeraByte** scale. **Four Key Characteristics:** 1. **Centralized Architecture:** Single (or few) high-end server with vertical scaling. 2. **Schema-on-Write:** Data schema is rigidly defined *before* loading. 3. **Batch-Oriented ETL:** Data is processed in scheduled batches (nightly/hourly). 4. **ACID Compliance:** Strong consistency, atomicity, isolation, durability guarantees. 5. **Structured Data Focus:** Optimized for tabular, relational data. 6. **High Cost per TB:** Storage and compute on proprietary hardware is expensive. *(Any 4 well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]` **Distinguish between Star Schema and Snowflake Schema with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Star Schema | Snowflake Schema | |---|---|---| | **Normalization** | Denormalized (flat) | Normalized (hierarchical) | | **Joins** | Fewer (faster query) | More (slower query) | | **Storage** | More redundant | Less redundant | | **Complexity** | Simple design | Complex design | | **Example** | `DimProduct` has columns `category_name` directly | `DimProduct` references `DimCategory` which references `DimSuperCategory` | **Example:** In a **Star Schema** retail DW, `FactSales` joins directly to `DimProduct` which holds `category_name` as a single column. In the equivalent **Snowflake Schema**, `DimProduct` joins to `DimCategory` to get the category name — adding one more join per query. *(A clean table + 2-3 lines of explanation = full 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions have an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4 Pattern]` **(a)** Explain the **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) process** in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **staging area** in the ETL pipeline. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast the **Inmon (Top-Down)** and **Kimball (Bottom-Up)** data warehouse design approaches. Which approach is more suitable for a mid-sized retail company with 5 business units, and why? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The **ETL process** is the foundational data integration pipeline in Traditional Data Analysis. It consists of three sequential phases: **1. Extract Phase:** - Data is pulled from heterogeneous source systems: OLTP databases (Oracle, MySQL), ERP systems (SAP), flat files (CSV), XML/JSON feeds, and APIs. - Extraction modes: - *Full Extraction:* Entire source table pulled (high bandwidth, simple logic). - *Incremental Extraction:* Only changed/new records since last run (delta detection via timestamps or CDC — Change Data Capture). - Example: Pulling daily sales from 50 regional POS systems at midnight. **2. Transform Phase:** - **Data Cleansing:** Remove nulls, fix typos, handle duplicates. - **Standardization:** Unify date formats (DD-MM-YYYY → YYYY-MM-DD), currency (USD/EUR → INR), units. - **Business Rule Application:** Calculate derived columns (`profit = revenue - cost`), apply hierarchies. - **Data Enrichment:** Join with reference data (e.g., adding city population to sales data). **3. Load Phase:** - Bulk-insert transformed data into the target Data Warehouse using: - *Bulk Load Utilities* (e.g., Oracle SQL*Loader, Teradata FastLoad). - *Partitioned Inserts* (load by date for performance). - Loading strategies: - *Full Refresh:* Truncate and reload entire table. - *Incremental Load:* Append new/changed records only. **Role of the Staging Area:** The **staging area** is an **intermediate, transient storage** (typically a separate database schema or file system) that sits between the source systems and the Data Warehouse. Its roles are: | Role | Description | |---|---| | **Buffering** | Decouples source extraction from warehouse load — protects source from heavy load. | | **Data Quality Checkpoint** | Validation, profiling, and exception handling before warehouse load. | | **Restartability** | If the load fails, ETL can resume from staging without re-extracting from sources. | | **Audit & Lineage** | Stores raw + transformed data with timestamps for traceability and debugging. | | **Parallelism** | Multiple ETL developers can work on different sources in parallel. | **Block Diagram (Recap):** Refer to **Section 4.1** for the full ETL architecture diagram. *(7 marks: 3 marks for ETL explanation, 2 marks for staging area, 2 marks for diagram.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Inmon's Top-Down Approach (3NF Corporate Information Factory):** 1. First, build an **enterprise-wide, normalized (3NF) Data Warehouse** integrating data from all subject areas. 2. Then, derive **subject-specific Data Marts** (Sales DW, Finance DW) from the central DW. 3. End users query the Data Marts. **Characteristics:** - **Pros:** Single source of truth, strong data consistency, integrated view. - **Cons:** High initial cost, long time-to-value (years), requires deep upfront understanding of enterprise data. **Kimball's Bottom-Up Approach (Dimensional Data Warehouse Bus):** 1. First, build **individual Data Marts** for each business process using **dimensional models (Star Schema)**. 2. The **union of conformed Data Marts** (sharing common dimensions like DimDate, DimCustomer) forms the **Data Warehouse Bus Architecture**. 3. End users start getting value from the very first Data Mart. **Characteristics:** - **Pros:** Fast initial ROI, incremental delivery, business-driven, simpler to scope. - **Cons:** Risk of inconsistent definitions across data marts if not governed properly. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Inmon (Top-Down) | Kimball (Bottom-Up) | |---|---|---| | **Starting Point** | Enterprise DW | Data Marts | | **Schema Style** | 3NF (Normalized) | Star Schema (Dimensional) | | **Time to Value** | Long (1-2 years) | Short (weeks-months) | | **Data Consistency** | Very High (centralized) | Moderate (depends on governance) | | **Initial Cost** | Very High | Moderate | | **Best For** | Large, stable enterprises | Agile, evolving businesses | **Recommendation for Mid-Sized Retail Company:** > **Kimball's Bottom-Up approach is more suitable** because: > 1. The company has only **5 business units** — manageable scope. > 2. **Faster time-to-value** is critical for a mid-sized firm with limited capital. > 3. The **Star Schema** aligns naturally with retail (sales facts + product/store/date dimensions). > 4. The company can **iterate incrementally** — start with a Sales Data Mart, then add Inventory, then HR. > 5. Inmon's approach would over-engineer the solution and delay business benefits. However, the company should enforce a **Data Governance Council** with **conformed dimensions** (shared `DimDate`, `DimProduct` across all 5 business units) to prevent the typical Kimball pitfall of dimension drift. *(7 marks: 2 marks each for both approaches explained, 1 mark for comparison, 2 marks for justified recommendation.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 4 Pattern — Alternative]` **(a)** Discuss the **OLAP architecture** in detail. Explain the five basic OLAP operations (Roll-up, Drill-down, Slice, Dice, Pivot) with a suitable 3D example involving **Product, Time, and Geography** dimensions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Star Schema** design for a hospital management data warehouse. Identify the **grain**, the **measures** in the fact table, and at least **four dimensions** with their attributes. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) Architecture:** OLAP is the analytical query engine layered on top of a Data Warehouse. It enables analysts to perform **multi-dimensional analysis** interactively. **Architectural Components:** 1. **OLAP Server:** The engine (MOLAP, ROLAP, or HOLAP — explained below). 2. **OLAP Cube:** A pre-aggregated multi-dimensional data structure. 3. **MDX / SQL Interface:** Query language for multi-dimensional data. 4. **Client Tools:** Excel pivot tables, Tableau, Power BI connect to the OLAP server. **Types of OLAP:** | Type | Full Form | Storage | Speed | |---|---|---|---| | **MOLAP** | Multidimensional OLAP | Pre-computed cube on disk | Fastest (pre-aggregated) | | **ROLAP** | Relational OLAP | Star schema in RDBMS | Slower (SQL aggregation on the fly) | | **HOLAP** | Hybrid OLAP | Mixed (cube + RDBMS) | Balanced | **Five OLAP Operations — 3D Example:** Consider a cube with dimensions: **Product (P)**, **Time (T)**, **Geography (G)**. The measure is **Sales Revenue (₹)**. **1. Roll-Up (Drill-Up):** - Aggregate data to a higher level of hierarchy. - Example: `Sales by City → Sales by State → Sales by Country`. - In SQL: `SELECT Country, SUM(Sales) FROM Cube GROUP BY Country`. **2. Drill-Down:** - The reverse of roll-up. Disaggregate to finer detail. - Example: `Sales by Year → Sales by Quarter → Sales by Month → Sales by Day`. - In SQL: `SELECT Month, SUM(Sales) FROM Cube GROUP BY Month`. **3. Slice:** - Select a single value on **one** dimension, fixing it. - Example: *Slice the cube at* `Time = "Q1 2024"` — produces a 2D plane of Product × Geography. - Result: A 2D table showing Product sales across regions, but **only for Q1 2024**. **4. Dice:** - Select a sub-cube by fixing values on **two or more** dimensions. - Example: *Dice where* `Time = "Q1 2024"` **AND** `Geography = "Asia"`. - Result: A 1D sub-cube showing Product-wise sales in Asia during Q1 2024. **5. Pivot (Rotate):** - Reorient the axes of the cube to provide an alternative view. - Example: Originally, rows = Products, columns = Time. After pivot: rows = Time, columns = Products. - Purpose: Aesthetics, cross-tabulation, and ease of reading. **Summary Table:** | Operation | Direction | Dimensions Affected | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Roll-Up | Up (coarser) | Reduces granularity on 1 dim | City → Country | | Drill-Down | Down (finer) | Increases granularity on 1 dim | Year → Month | | Slice | Filter (1 dim) | Fixes 1 dimension | Q1 2024 only | | Dice | Filter (≥2 dim) | Fixes multiple dimensions | Q1 2024 + Asia | | Pivot | Reorient | None (visual only) | Swap rows/columns | *(7 marks: 2 marks for OLAP architecture, 5 marks for the 5 operations with examples.)* --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Star Schema for Hospital Management Data Warehouse:** **Grain Definition:** > The **grain** of the fact table is **one row per patient treatment episode** (i.e., one hospital visit / one billable service line item). This is the most atomic level at which a medical event is recorded. **Fact Table: `FactTreatment`** | Column | Type | Description | |---|---|---| | `treatment_id` | PK | Unique surrogate key | | `patient_id` | FK | → DimPatient | | `doctor_id` | FK | → DimDoctor | | `department_id` | FK | → DimDepartment | | `date_id` | FK | → DimDate | | `diagnosis_id` | FK | → DimDiagnosis | | `treatment_duration_minutes` | Measure | Duration in minutes | | `treatment_cost` | Measure | Cost in ₹ | | `medication_cost` | Measure | Cost of prescribed drugs in ₹ | | `lab_test_cost` | Measure | Cost of lab tests in ₹ | | `total_bill_amount` | Measure | Total bill in ₹ | | `insurance_claimed_amount` | Measure | Claimed amount in ₹ | **Dimension Tables (4+ required):** **1. DimPatient:** - `patient_id` (PK), `patient_name`, `age`, `gender`, `blood_group`, `city`, `state`, `pincode`, `insurance_provider`, `chronic_conditions`, `registration_date`. **2. DimDoctor:** - `doctor_id` (PK), `doctor_name`, `specialization`, `department_id` (FK), `qualification`, `years_of_experience`, `consultation_fee`, `rating`. **3. DimDepartment:** - `department_id` (PK), `department_name`, `head_of_department`, `floor_number`, `bed_count`, `is_emergency_24x7`. **4. DimDate:** - `date_id` (PK), `full_date`, `day`, `month`, `quarter`, `year`, `weekday`, `is_weekend`, `is_holiday`, `fiscal_quarter`. **5. DimDiagnosis (Optional 5th):** - `diagnosis_id` (PK), `icd_10_code`, `diagnosis_name`, `category`, `severity`, `is_chronic`. **Star Schema Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** for the generic star schema block diagram. The hospital-specific adaptation has the same topology: `FactTreatment` at the center, surrounded by `DimPatient`, `DimDoctor`, `DimDepartment`, `DimDate`, and `DimDiagnosis`. **Analytical Queries Enabled by this Schema:** 1. *Total revenue per department per quarter.* → `SUM(total_bill_amount) GROUP BY DimDepartment, DimDate.Quarter`. 2. *Average treatment cost by diagnosis category.* → `AVG(total_bill_amount) GROUP BY DimDiagnosis.category`. 3. *Top 10 doctors by patient volume in cardiology.* → `COUNT GROUP BY DimDoctor, filter DimDepartment = Cardiology`. *(7 marks: 1 mark for grain, 3 marks for fact table + 4 dimensions with attributes, 2 marks for star schema diagram and queries, 1 mark for clarity.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Traditional Data Analysis Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing OLTP and OLAP:** Many students interchange these terms. **OLTP = transactional** (insert/update/delete heavy, normalized, current data). **OLAP = analytical** (read-heavy, denormalized, historical data). Mixing them up = **−2 marks**. > > 2. **Forgetting to State the Grain:** In star schema design questions, **not explicitly stating the grain** of the fact table is a **fatal flaw** — it is the first thing a KTU examiner checks. Always start with: *"The grain is one row per ..."* > > 3. **Mixing up Star and Snowflake in Diagrams:** In a Star Schema, dimension tables do **not** join to each other — they only join to the fact table. Drawing joins between dimensions = **incorrect schema** = **−1 to −2 marks**. > > 4. **Skipping the Staging Area in ETL Diagrams:** A common oversight. The staging area is a **mandatory** component of any ETL block diagram. Omission = **−1 mark**. > > 5. **Not Mentioning ACID:** When defining Traditional Data Analysis, **always** mention **ACID compliance** as a defining trait — it is the key contrast with Big Data's eventual consistency. > > 6. **Confusing ETL with ELT:** ETL = Transform **before** Load. ELT = Transform **after** Load. Reversing these = **−2 marks**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Traditional Data Analysis (Module 4)** ### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize - [ ] **Traditional Data Analysis** = Centralized RDBMS + Data Warehouse + OLAP + ETL. - [ ] **Three Pillars:** Data Warehouse, OLAP, BI/Reporting. - [ ] **W.H. Inmon's 4 DW characteristics:** Subject-Oriented, Integrated, Time-Variant, Non-Volatile. - [ ] **Schema-on-Write** (traditional) vs **Schema-on-Read** (big data). - [ ] **ETL** = Extract, Transform, Load (in that order). - [ ] **ELT** = Extract, Load, Transform (cloud modern variant). - [ ] **Staging Area** = intermediate, transient buffer between source and warehouse. - [ ] **ACID** = Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. ### ✅ Architecture to Remember - [ ] **Inmon (Top-Down):** Enterprise DW (3NF) → Data Marts. - [ ] **Kimball (Bottom-Up):** Data Marts (Star Schema) → DW Bus. - [ ] **Star Schema:** Fact Table (center) + Dimension Tables (flat, denormalized). - [ ] **Snowflake Schema:** Normalized dimensions (multiple hierarchy levels). ### ✅ OLAP Operations - [ ] **Roll-Up:** Aggregate (City → Country). - [ ] **Drill-Down:** Disaggregate (Year → Month). - [ ] **Slice:** Fix 1 dimension. - [ ] **Dice:** Fix 2+ dimensions. - [ ] **Pivot (Rotate):** Reorient axes. ### ✅ OLAP Types - [ ] **MOLAP:** Pre-computed cubes (fastest). - [ ] **ROLAP:** SQL on star schema (most flexible). - [ ] **HOLAP:** Hybrid (balanced). ### ✅ Limitations Leading to Big Data - [ ] Single-server scale ceiling. - [ ] Cannot handle unstructured data (text, image, video). - [ ] Batch latency (12-24 hr). - [ ] Vertical scaling cost explosion. - [ ] Schema rigidity. ### ✅ KTU Board Buzzwords (Use in Answers) > *"Schema-on-Write"*, *"Subject-Oriented"*, *"Time-Variant"*, *"OLAP Cube"*, *"Star Schema"*, *"Conformed Dimensions"*, *"Data Mart"*, *"Slowly Changing Dimensions"*, *"Grain of the Fact Table"*, *"ACID Transactions"*. ### ✅ One-Line Takeaway > **Traditional Data Analysis = Structured, Centralized, RDBMS-based, ACID-compliant, schema-on-write processing suited for tera-scale structured data — the architectural predecessor that gave way to Big Data when Volume, Velocity, and Variety exceeded its single-server comfort zone.**

topics.title: Big Data Analytic Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Big Data Analytic Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Big Data Analytic Methods — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Big Data Analytic Methods** refer to the systematic collection of statistical, mathematical, computational, and machine-learning algorithms used to extract actionable intelligence, hidden patterns, predictive insights, and prescriptive knowledge from massive, high-velocity, high-variety datasets that exceed the storage and processing capability of traditional database systems.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** framework, big data analytics is the convergence of three pillars:

- **Data Engineering** — distributed storage (HDFS), distributed processing (MapReduce/Spark), and NoSQL data models.
- **Analytics Engineering** — statistical inference, machine learning, deep learning, and visualization.
- **Operational Engineering** — streaming, real-time decisioning, and MLOps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECPT755 / Module 4):** The KTU 2024 syllabus frames analytic methods as the *modeling layer* that sits on top of the Hadoop/Spark stack, transforming processed big data into *decisions*.

The five canonical families of Big Data Analytic Methods recognised by KTU are:

1. **Descriptive Analytics** — *What happened?* (aggregation, dashboards, clustering).
2. **Diagnostic Analytics** — *Why did it happen?* (root-cause, anomaly detection, drill-down).
3. **Predictive Analytics** — *What will happen?* (regression, classification, time-series).
4. **Prescriptive Analytics** — *What should we do?* (optimization, reinforcement learning).
5. **Cognitive/Discovery Analytics** — *What don't we know?* (graph mining, topic modelling, association mining).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Emergency Department**
> Imagine a hospital receiving 10,000 patient records per hour from IoT wearables, lab machines, and admission desks. A doctor cannot read each record manually. Instead, a *Big Data Analytic Method* acts like a **triage team**:
> - The *clustering triage* groups similar patients.
> - The *classification model* labels each incoming patient as "Critical / Moderate / Stable".
> - The *regression model* predicts how long a patient will stay.
> - The *anomaly detector* flags unusual vitals (e.g., heart rate = 220).
> - The *recommender* suggests treatment pathways.
>
> Just as a triage system converts raw patient streams into prioritized care, **Big Data Analytic Methods convert raw terabytes into prioritized decisions**.

## 1.3 The V's of Big Data and Why They Demand Special Methods

The 5V model dictates *why* traditional RDBMS-era statistics (e.g., a single-machine R script) fail:

- **Volume** — datasets in **Petabytes (10¹⁵ bytes)** and **Exabytes (10¹⁸ bytes)**.
- **Velocity** — data generated at **> 100 GB/sec** in streaming sources.
- **Variety** — structured, semi-structured, unstructured.
- **Veracity** — noise, bias, missing values.
- **Value** — extracting business-defining insight from noise.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** *"V³ + Veracity → Value"* — the analytics methods are the bridge from Veracity to Value.

| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | $1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$ | Information-theoretic entropy |

| Shannon unit | 1 bit | Base-2 information unit | Entropy measurement |
| Hash bucket size | $m$ | Power of 2 (e.g., 1024) | Bloom filters, hashing |

# Big Data Analytic Methods — Section 1 Summary

- **Definition:** A library of algorithms (statistical, ML, optimization) that operate on petabyte-scale data in distributed environments to produce actionable insight.
- **Five families:** Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive, Cognitive.
- **Why they exist:** Traditional single-node statistics cannot scale to the 5V characteristics of big data.
- **Core constant:** $e \approx 2.71828$ for logarithmic loss; **Shannon entropy** measured in *bits*.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Operational Pillars of Big Data Analytic Methods

### Pillar 1 — Supervised Learning (Labelled Data)

The model learns a function $f : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ from labelled pairs $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i \in \mathcal{X}$ is the feature vector and $y_i \in \mathcal{Y}$ is the ground-truth label.

**Sub-categories:**

- **Classification** — discrete $\mathcal{Y}$ (e.g., spam / not-spam).
- **Regression** — continuous $\mathcal{Y}$ (e.g., house price).

**Why it works:** The **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** principle — minimize the expected loss over the training set.

$$\hat{f} = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}\bigl(f(x_i), y_i\bigr)$$

**The "How":** Gradient descent over a convex (or non-convex) loss surface.

### Pillar 2 — Unsupervised Learning (Unlabelled Data)

The model finds latent structure in $\{x_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ without labels.

**Sub-categories:**

- **Clustering** — partition into $k$ groups (K-Means, DBSCAN).
- **Dimensionality Reduction** — project to lower-dimensional manifold (PCA, t-SNE).
- **Association Mining** — co-occurrence rules (Apriori, FP-Growth).

### Pillar 3 — Semi-Supervised & Self-Supervised Learning

A small labelled set is augmented with a large unlabelled set. Critical in big data because labelling is expensive (e.g., medical imaging).

### Pillar 4 — Reinforcement Learning (RL)

An **agent** interacts with an **environment**, receiving *rewards* $r_t$ and learning a **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ that maximizes cumulative return:

$$G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} r_{t+k+1}, \quad 0 \le \gamma \le 1$$

## 2.2 The KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula set** for Module 4. Every formula here is fair game for a 14-mark derivation or a 3-mark definition question.

| # | Method | Core Formula | Use / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Shannon Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Information content in bits; impurity in decision trees |
| 2 | **Gini Impurity** | $\text{Gini} = 1 - \sum_{i} p_i^2$ | CART decision-tree split criterion |
| 3 | **Information Gain** | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | ID3/C4.5 attribute selection |
| 4 | **K-Means Objective** | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ | Minimize within-cluster SSE |
| 5 | **Naive Bayes Posterior** | $P(C \vert X) \propto P(C) \prod_{i} P(x_i \vert C)$ | MAP classification |
| 6 | **Linear Regression** | $\hat{y} = w^{T}x + b$ | Minimize MSE |
| 7 | **MSE / SSE** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Regression loss |
| 8 | **Logistic (Sigmoid)** | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Probability squash |
| 9 | **Cross-Entropy Loss** | $\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{n}\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_i$ | Classification loss |
| 10 | **SVM Decision Function** | $f(x) = \text{sign}\bigl(w^{T}x + b\bigr)$ | Maximum-margin classifier |
| 11 | **Support / Confidence** | $\text{Conf}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{support}(A \cup B)}{\text{support}(A)}$ | Association rule strength |
| 12 | **Lift** | $\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{Conf}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{support}(B)}$ | Rule interestingness |
| 13 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(A, B) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\lVert A \rVert \cdot \lVert B \rVert}$ | Text/document similarity |
| 14 | **TF-IDF** | $\text{tfidf}(t, d) = \text{tf}(t,d) \cdot \log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}$ | Term importance in corpus |
| 15 | **PCA Reconstruction** | $X \approx X_{recon} = U_k \Sigma_k V_k^{T}$ | Top-$k$ singular components |
| 16 | **PageRank** | $PR(p_i) = \frac{1-d}{N} + d \sum_{p_j \in \mathcal{M}(p_i)} \frac{PR(p_j)}{L(p_j)}$ | Graph node importance, $d \approx 0.85$ |
| 17 | **DBSCAN ε-neighbourhood** | $N_{\epsilon}(p) = \{q \in D \mid \text{dist}(p, q) \le \epsilon\}$ | Density-based cluster |
| 18 | **ARIMA $(p, d, q)$** | $\Phi(B)(1-B)^d y_t = \Theta(B)\varepsilon_t$ | Time-series forecasting |
| 19 | **Z-Score (Anomaly)** | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Outlier detection |
| 20 | **Softmax** | $\sigma(z_i) = \frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class probability |

## 2.3 The Algorithmic Map: From Raw Data to Insight

The KTU 2024 syllabus conceptualises the analytic pipeline as a five-stage funnel:

**Stage 1 — Data Ingestion.** Kafka / Flume / Sqoop push data into HDFS / S3 / GCS.

**Stage 2 — Data Preparation.** Spark / Hive / Pig perform ETL, schema inference, type casting, and null handling.

**Stage 3 — Feature Engineering.** TF-IDF, embeddings (Word2Vec, BERT), one-hot encoding, scaling.

**Stage 4 — Model Training.** MLlib (Spark), Mahout, FlinkML, TensorFlowOnSpark.

**Stage 5 — Evaluation & Deployment.** Confusion matrix, ROC-AUC, RMSE, MLOps pipelines.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Methods in Engineering

- **Healthcare:** Predicting sepsis 6 hours before onset using RNNs on streaming ICU vitals.
- **Smart Grid:** K-Means clustering of consumption profiles for dynamic tariff design.
- **Transportation:** Graph analytics (PageRank) for optimizing road-network centrality in Kochi.
- **Cybersecurity:** Isolation Forest + autoencoders for zero-day intrusion detection.
- **E-Commerce:** Apriori/FP-Growth for cross-selling recommendations.
- **Finance:** ARIMA + GARCH for stock-volatility forecasting.
- **Agriculture:** PCA + Random Forest on satellite imagery for crop-yield prediction.

## 2.5 Decision-Tree Entropy — Worked Logic Walk-Through

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" of Entropy:** Imagine a coin. A fair coin has maximum uncertainty (1 bit). A biased coin has less. Entropy quantifies *exactly* how much uncertainty the data carries. Decision trees split on the attribute that **reduces entropy the most** — that reduction is the *Information Gain*.

For a binary classification dataset $S$ with proportions $p$ and $1-p$:

$$H(S) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p)$$

- $H = 0$ when the set is *pure* (all one class).
- $H = 1$ when the set is *perfectly mixed* (50-50).

The tree algorithm picks the split that maximizes $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$, where the conditional entropy is the weighted average of the child entropies.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **most frequently tested 14-mark questions** on KTU Module 4. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — K-Means Convergence and the Lloyd Algorithm

**Goal:** Show that the K-Means objective is non-increasing across iterations.

**Objective Function:**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

where $w_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates the cluster assignment of $x_i$, and $\sum_j w_{ij} = 1$ for all $i$.

### Step 1 — Assignment Step (Cluster Membership Fixed)

Given current centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \ldots, \mu_k$, assign each point to the *nearest* centroid:

$$w_{ij}^{*} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j = \arg\min_{l} \lVert x_i - \mu_l \rVert^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Why this is optimal:** For a fixed $\mu_j$, $J$ is minimized by picking the closest centroid — any other assignment strictly increases the distance term.

### Step 2 — Update Step (Cluster Membership Fixed)

Given the new assignment $w_{ij}$, minimize $J$ with respect to each $\mu_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mu_j} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}(x_i - \mu_j) = 0$$

Solving:

$$\mu_j^{*} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij} x_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_{ij}}$$

**Interpretation:** The new centroid is the *mean* of the points assigned to it.

### Step 3 — Monotonicity

Both steps strictly *decrease* (or leave unchanged) the value of $J$. Since $J \ge 0$ and the cluster space is finite, the algorithm is guaranteed to **converge in a finite number of iterations** (typically fewer than 50 for $k \le 100$).

### Step 4 — Complexity

- Per iteration: $O(n \cdot k \cdot d)$ where $d$ = dimensionality.
- MapReduce parallelization: each mapper computes partial sums; reducer aggregates.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Naive Bayes Classifier (MAP Derivation)

**Goal:** Derive the classification rule from Bayes' theorem under the conditional independence assumption.

### Step 1 — Bayes' Rule

$$P(C \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert C) P(C)}{P(X)}$$

Since $P(X)$ is constant across classes, the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) decision is:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{c} P(C = c) \, P(X \vert C = c)$$

### Step 2 — Conditional Independence Assumption

For a feature vector $X = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$:

$$P(X \vert C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C)$$

This is the **"naive"** assumption — features are independent given the class.

### Step 3 — Final Rule

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{c} P(C = c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C = c)$$

Taking the log (monotonic) for numerical stability:

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_{c} \left[ \log P(C = c) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \vert C = c) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Laplace (Additive) Smoothing

To handle zero-probability features:

$$P(x_i \vert c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + \alpha}{\text{count}(c) + \alpha \cdot \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is vocabulary size and $\alpha$ is the smoothing hyperparameter (typically $\alpha = 1$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Regression via Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form solution for $\mathbf{w}$ in $\hat{y} = X\mathbf{w}$.

### Step 1 — Loss Definition

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2n} \lVert y - X\mathbf{w} \rVert^2 = \frac{1}{2n}(y - X\mathbf{w})^{T}(y - X\mathbf{w})$$

### Step 2 — Gradient Computation

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{n} X^{T}(y - X\mathbf{w}) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve

$$X^{T}y = X^{T}X\mathbf{w}$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}^{*} = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y}$$

**Condition:** $X^{T}X$ must be invertible. If not, use **ridge regression**:

$$\mathbf{w}^{*} = (X^{T}X + \lambda I)^{-1} X^{T} y, \quad \lambda > 0$$

### Step 4 — Distributed Computation (MapReduce)

- **Map:** Each mapper computes $X_i^{T}X_i$ and $X_i^{T}y_i$ on a data shard.
- **Reduce:** Sum partial results; compute the global inverse.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Logistic Regression Gradient

The logistic model:

$$P(y = 1 \vert x; w) = \sigma(w^{T}x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-w^{T}x}}$$

### Step 1 — Cross-Entropy Loss

$$\mathcal{L}(w) = -\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - \hat{y}_i) \right]$$

### Step 2 — Gradient

Using $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$:

$$\nabla_w \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\hat{y}_i - y_i) x_i$$

### Step 3 — Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Update

$$w \leftarrow w - \eta \nabla_w \mathcal{L}, \quad \eta = \text{learning rate}$$

Hadoop/Spark implementations use **mini-batch SGD** to parallelize over partitions.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Principal Component Analysis (PCA)

**Goal:** Find the orthogonal directions of maximum variance.

### Step 1 — Covariance Matrix

$$C = \frac{1}{n-1} X^{T}X \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$$

### Step 2 — Eigendecomposition

$$C v_i = \lambda_i v_i, \quad v_i^{T}v_j = \delta_{ij}$$

### Step 3 — Projection

$$z_i = V_k^{T} x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{k}, \quad k \ll d$$

### Step 4 — Reconstruction Error (Energy Bound)

$$\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i} \ge 0.95$$

Choose $k$ such that 95% variance is retained.

---

> **The "Pick the Right Method" Matrix** is the most-asked 14-mark KTU design question. Memorise it.

| Problem | Method | Loss / Objective | Scalable to Big Data? |

| Predict continuous value | Linear Regression | MSE | Yes (Spark MLlib) |
| Binary classification | Logistic Regression | Cross-entropy | Yes (SGD) |
| Multi-class classification | Softmax Regression | Categorical cross-entropy | Yes |

| Anomaly detection | Autoencoder | Reconstruction MSE | Yes (TensorFlowOnSpark) |
| Image classification | CNN | Cross-entropy | Yes (GPU + Spark) |
| Sequence prediction | RNN / LSTM | Cross-entropy | Yes (GPU + Spark) |
| Recommendation | ALS (Matrix Factorization) | RMSE | Yes (Spark MLlib) |

**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning with one real-world example each.**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |

| Labels | Requires labelled $(x, y)$ pairs | No labels — only $\{x_i\}$ |
| Goal | Learn $f: X \to Y$ | Find latent structure |
| Example | Email spam detection (spam / not-spam) | Customer segmentation via K-Means |
| Loss | Prediction error (MSE, cross-entropy) | Reconstruction / clustering objective |

**(1 mark) Definition of each + (1 mark) tabular comparison + (1 mark) examples.**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Define Information Gain. Why is it used in ID3?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**(1 mark)** Information Gain is the *reduction* in entropy $H(S)$ achieved by partitioning the dataset $S$ on attribute $A$.

**(1 mark)** The first term $H(S)$ is the entropy *before* the split; the second is the *weighted average entropy* after the split.

**(1 mark)** ID3 chooses the attribute with the **highest Information Gain** at each node, because a high IG means the split produces purer (less-impure) child subsets — yielding shorter, more accurate trees.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm. Derive the K-Means objective function and show that each iteration is non-increasing.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm Overview (2 marks)**

K-Means partitions $n$ data points into $k$ clusters by minimizing within-cluster sum of squared errors (SSE). It is iterative: alternate between *assignment* and *update*.

**Step 2 — Objective Function (2 marks)**

$$J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{k} w_{ij} \, \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$$

where $w_{ij} = 1$ iff $x_i$ is assigned to cluster $j$, else $0$.

**Step 3 — Non-Increase Proof (3 marks)**

- *Assignment step:* For each $x_i$, choosing the closest $\mu_j$ minimizes $\lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ over all $j$ — so $J$ does not increase.
- *Update step:* Setting $\partial J / \partial \mu_j = 0$ yields $\mu_j^{*} = \frac{\sum_i w_{ij} x_i}{\sum_i w_{ij}}$, the mean of the assigned points — this *minimizes* $J$ for the fixed assignment.
- Since $J \ge 0$ and finite, the algorithm converges in finite iterations.

#### (b) [7 marks] Discuss the limitations of K-Means and explain how K-Means++ improves centroid initialization.

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations of K-Means (4 marks):**

1. **Sensitivity to initialization** — different seeds yield different SSE.
2. **Assumes spherical, equal-sized clusters** — fails on elongated or non-convex shapes.
3. **Requires $k$ upfront** — no automatic cluster-count selection.
4. **Sensitive to outliers** — one outlier can pull a centroid drastically.
5. **Euclidean-only** — not suited for categorical or mixed data.
6. **Local minima** — not guaranteed to reach global optimum.

**K-Means++ Initialization (3 marks):**

- Choose the first centroid uniformly at random.
- For each subsequent centroid, pick $x_i$ with probability $\propto D(x_i)^2$, where $D(x_i)$ is the distance to the nearest already-chosen centroid.
- This spreads initial centroids far apart, reducing SSE by **a factor of $O(\log k)$** compared to random initialization (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the Naive Bayes classifier. Derive the MAP decision rule and discuss Laplace smoothing.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem (1 mark)**

$$P(C \vert X) = \frac{P(X \vert C) P(C)}{P(X)}$$

**Step 2 — MAP Decision (2 marks)**

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_c P(C = c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \vert C = c)$$

The log-form (numerically stable):

$$\hat{C} = \arg\max_c \left[ \log P(C) + \sum_i \log P(x_i \vert C) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Conditional Independence Assumption (2 marks)**

Naive Bayes assumes $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ are conditionally independent given $C$. This is rarely true in practice, but the classifier still works surprisingly well because the *ranking* of class probabilities often remains correct.

**Step 4 — Laplace Smoothing (2 marks)**

To avoid $P(x_i \vert c) = 0$:

$$P(x_i \vert c) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, c) + 1}{\text{count}(c) + \vert V \vert}$$

where $\vert V \vert$ is vocabulary size, and the +1 is the smoothing hyperparameter $\alpha$.

#### (b) [7 marks] Compare Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, and Support Vector Machine. State one engineering use case where each is the most appropriate.

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Naive Bayes | Decision Tree | SVM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assumption | Feature independence | Information gain / Gini | Maximum margin hyperplane |
| Training speed | Very fast | Fast | Slow (quadratic) |
| Handles high-dim | Yes | Moderate | Yes (kernel) |
| Interpretability | Medium | High (rule-based) | Low (kernel) |
| Non-linear boundaries | Weak | Strong | Strong (kernel trick) |
| Engineering use case | Real-time spam filter (text) | Credit-risk scoring (banking) | Image classification (medical imaging) |

**(4 marks)** Tabular comparison; **(3 marks)** justified use-case selection.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing "support" with "confidence"** in Apriori derivations. Support is a *frequency*; confidence is a *conditional probability*. Many students swap the denominator.
> 2. **Forgetting the Laplacian smoothing** in Naive Bayes numericals — if a word has zero count in training, $P(\text{word} \vert c) = 0$ and the entire product vanishes.
> 3. **Mixing up SSE (Sum of Squared Errors) and SSR (Sum of Squares due to Regression)** in regression — KTU marks them as separate, and the formula differs by 1 in denominator.
> 4. **Ignoring the convergence proof** in K-Means — the *non-increase* property is worth 2-3 marks; stating the algorithm *converges* without proof loses marks.
> 5. **Writing `|x|` instead of `\lvert x \rvert`** in LaTeX answer sheets — the pipe character breaks Markdown rendering and examiners cannot award full marks for an ambiguous formula.
> 6. **Mis-stating the "naive" assumption** as "features are independent" instead of "features are independent **given the class**". The conditional clause is mandatory.
> 7. **Confusing PCA variance ratio with reconstruction error** — they are *complements*; total = 1 only when $k = d$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

- [ ] Big Data Analytic Methods span **5 families**: Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive, Cognitive.
- [ ] **Supervised vs. Unsupervised** — labelled vs. unlabelled. The fundamental distinction.
- [ ] **K-Means** minimizes $J = \sum_i \sum_j w_{ij} \lVert x_i - \mu_j \rVert^2$ via Lloyd's algorithm.
- [ ] **K-Means++** spreads initial centroids proportionally to $D^2$.
- [ ] **Naive Bayes** uses MAP under conditional independence + Laplace smoothing.
- [ ] **Logistic regression** = sigmoid + cross-entropy + SGD.
- [ ] **Linear regression** has closed form $\mathbf{w} = (X^{T}X)^{-1} X^{T} y$.
- [ ] **Ridge regression** adds $\lambda I$ for numerical stability.
- [ ] **Decision trees** use Information Gain (ID3) or Gini (CART) as split criteria.
- [ ] **Entropy $H(S) \in [0, \log_2 k]$** — 0 = pure, max = uniform.
- [ ] **SVM** finds the maximum-margin hyperplane; kernel trick handles non-linearity.
- [ ] **Apriori** uses support-confidence-lift; FP-Growth is the scalable successor.
- [ ] **PCA** projects onto top-$k$ eigenvectors; retain ≥ 95% variance.
- [ ] **PageRank** with damping $d \approx 0.85$ is the canonical graph-importance method.
- [ ] **DBSCAN** is density-based; handles arbitrary shapes and noise.
- [ ] **ARIMA $(p, d, q)$** = autoregression + differencing + moving average.
- [ ] **TF-IDF** = term-frequency $\times$ inverse-document-frequency; backbone of text analytics.
- [ ] **Cosine similarity** $\in [-1, 1]$ for vector comparison.
- [ ] **Mini-batch SGD** is the standard parallel training strategy in Spark MLlib.
- [ ] **CRISP-DM** is the *de facto* six-phase methodology.
- [ ] **MapReduce** = mappers compute partial stats; reducer aggregates; works for all analytic methods.
- [ ] **No absolute value `|x|`** in LaTeX — always use `\lvert x \rvert`.
- [ ] **No `end` / `graph` / `subgraph` as Mermaid node IDs** — prefix with letters.
- [ ] **Shannon entropy** is measured in **bits** (base-2 logarithm).
- [ ] The natural log base $e \approx 2.71828$ is used for log-loss; base-2 for entropy.
- [ ] Big data methods are **horizontally scalable** — parallelism is the key design principle.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Big Data Analytic Methods**

topics.title: Architecture for Big Data Analysis - Real-Time vs. Offline Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT755 - BIG DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture for Big Data Analysis - Real-Time vs. Offline Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big Data Analysis Architecture** is a structured, layered blueprint of software components, data pipelines, and processing engines that govern the ingestion, transformation, storage, and querying of massive datasets. It is broadly classified into two paradigms: **Offline (Batch) Analysis** and **Real-Time (Stream) Analysis**, each optimized for distinct latency, throughput, and consistency requirements.

According to the **KTU 2024 PECPT755 Module 4 syllabus**, the term *"Architecture for Big Data Analysis"* refers to the end-to-end design pattern that answers three critical engineering questions:

1. **How is data ingested?** (Batch files vs. event streams)
2. **How is data processed?** (Scheduled jobs vs. continuous operators)
3. **How are results delivered?** (Hours-old reports vs. sub-second dashboards)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 4 explicitly mandates the study of *Lambda Architecture*, *Kappa Architecture*, and the comparative study of **real-time versus offline analysis** — focusing on latency, fault-tolerance, and reprocessing capabilities.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

To make this immediately intuitive, let us compare a Big Data architecture to a **professional restaurant kitchen**:

| Architecture Type | Restaurant Analogy | Core Trait |
|---|---|---|
| **Offline (Batch) Analysis** | A *Catering Service* that prepares hundreds of meals hours before the event. Everything is cooked in bulk, frozen, and reheated. | High throughput, high latency |
| **Real-Time (Stream) Analysis** | A *Live Teppanyaki Counter* where the chef cooks each dish the moment the customer orders it. | Low latency, event-driven |
| **Hybrid (Lambda)** | A restaurant that **pre-prepares** the rice and sauces in bulk **AND** stir-fries fresh ingredients live on the counter. | Combines both strengths |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Batch = Catering, Stream = Live Counter, Lambda = Best of Both Worlds.*

---

## 1.3 Defining the Two Core Paradigms

### A. Offline (Batch) Analysis
A **non-continuous**, scheduled data processing model where large volumes of data are collected over a period (hours/days) and processed as a single, atomic job. The system answers: *"What happened yesterday?"*

### B. Real-Time (Stream) Analysis
A **continuous**, event-driven processing model where each data record is processed within **milliseconds to seconds** of its arrival. The system answers: *"What is happening right now?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metrics Vocabulary (Memorize These):**
> - **Latency:** The time gap between data generation and insight generation. Measured in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Throughput:** The volume of data processed per unit time. Measured in **records/second** or **MB/s**.
> - **Window:** A logical time-bound slice of an infinite stream (e.g., tumbling, sliding, session).
> - **Exactly-Once Semantics (EOS):** A delivery guarantee ensuring each record affects the final state **exactly one time** — never zero, never twice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Components of Any Big Data Architecture

Every architecture, whether batch, real-time, or hybrid, consists of three layers. We will analyze each.

### Layer 1: The Ingestion Layer
This is the **front door** of the architecture. It defines *how* raw data enters the system.

- **Batch Ingestion:** Uses tools like **Apache Sqoop** (RDBMS → HDFS) or **Apache Flume** (log files → HDFS). Data lands as immutable files in a distributed file system like **HDFS**.
- **Stream Ingestion:** Uses tools like **Apache Kafka**, **Amazon Kinesis**, or **Apache Pulsar**. Data flows as an unbounded sequence of events partitioned into *topics*.

### Layer 2: The Processing Layer
This is the **brain** of the architecture. It defines *how* raw data is transformed into insight.

- **Batch Processing Engine:** Examples include **Apache Hadoop MapReduce** and **Apache Spark (Batch Mode)**. The driver program reads the *entire* dataset, applies a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) of transformations, and writes the result.
