### Layer 5: Query & Scripting Layer (Hive, Pig, Impala)

#### 2.5.1 Apache Hive

Hive provides **SQL-like querying (HQL)** over HDFS-resident data. Internally, HQL is compiled to **MapReduce, Tez, or Spark** jobs.

**Execution Pipeline:**

$$
\text{HQL} \xrightarrow{\text{Parser}} \text{AST} \xrightarrow{\text{Semantic Analysis}} \text{Logical Plan} \xrightarrow{\text{Optimizer}} \text{Physical Plan} \xrightarrow{\text{Execution Engine}} \text{Result}
$$

#### 2.5.2 Apache Pig

Pig Latin is a **dataflow language** for ETL-style transformations.

**Pig Latin Operators:**

- **LOAD, STORE:** I/O.
- **FOREACH ... GENERATE:** Projection / transformation.
- **FILTER, DISTINCT, SAMPLE:** Row selection.
- **JOIN, COGROUP, GROUP:** Set operations.
- **ORDER, LIMIT, UNION:** Output shaping.
- **Custom UDFs:** User-Defined Functions in Java/Python/JavaScript.

### Layer 6: NoSQL Database Layer

| Database | Type | Data Model | Strength | Use Case |
|----------|------|------------|----------|----------|
| **HBase** | Wide-column store | Sorted map of maps | Strong consistency, random reads | Time-series, sparse data |
| **MongoDB** | Document store | BSON/JSON documents | Schema flexibility, rich queries | Catalogs, content mgmt |
| **Cassandra** | Wide-column store | Partition-sorted rows | Write-heavy, no single point of failure | IoT, messaging, telemetry |

**CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer):**

A distributed data store can simultaneously satisfy **only two** of the three properties:

$$
\text{Consistency (C)} \quad \bigwedge \quad \text{Availability (A)} \quad \bigwedge \quad \text{Partition Tolerance (P)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, **P is non-negotiable** for distributed systems, so designers choose between **CP** (HBase, MongoDB with majority writes) and **AP** (Cassandra, DynamoDB).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Cheat Sheet — Big Data Mining Tools (Module 4)**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | HDFS Block Count | $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil \vert F \vert / B \rceil$ | $B$ default = **128 MB** |
| 2 | HDFS Replication Overhead | $\text{Overhead} = (R - 1) \times 100$% | $R$ = replication factor |
| 3 | HDFS Physical Storage | $S_{\text{phys}} = \vert F \vert \times R$ | MB / GB / TB |
| 4 | NameNode Heartbeat | $T_{\text{hb}} = 3$ s (default) | Failure detection latency |
| 5 | MapReduce Mappers | $M = \min(N_{\text{blocks}}, N_{\text{containers}})$ | Limited by smallest |
| 6 | MapReduce Reducers | $R_{\text{count}} = \lfloor 0.95 \times N_{\text{nodes}} \times v_{\text{max}} \rceil$ | Hadoop heuristic |
| 7 | Spark RDD Partitions | $P_{\text{default}} = N_{\text{cores}} \times 2\text{ to }4$ | Tuning parameter |
| 8 | Speedup (Amdahl) | $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + p / N}$ | $p$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = nodes |
| 9 | Shuffle Read/Write | $V_{\text{shuffle}} = \sum_{i} \text{size}(\text{partition}_i)$ | Network-bound cost |
| 10 | Cost per TB (HDFS) | $C = (C_{\text{disk}} \times R) + C_{\text{replication\_BW}}$ | Trade-off metric |
| 11 | Throughput (HDFS) | $\Theta = \frac{\vert F \vert}{T_{\text{read}}}$ | MB/s |
| 12 | CAP Constraint | $C + A + P \leq 2$ | Pick any two |
| 13 | Logistic (Sigmoid) | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Range $(0, 1)$ |
| 14 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$ | Mahout SGD objective |
| 15 | Cosine Similarity | $\text{sim}(u, v) = \frac{u \cdot v}{\Vert u \Vert_2 \cdot \Vert v \Vert_2}$ | Collaborative filtering |
| 16 | k-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \min_{\mu_j \in C} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$ | Mahout/MLlib clustering |
| 17 | FP-Growth Support | $\text{sup}(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{N_{\text{total\_transactions}}}$ | Frequent itemset mining |
| 18 | Information Gain | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in V(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Weka/RF decision tree split |
| 19 | TF-IDF Weight | $w_{t,d} = \text{tf}(t, d) \times \log\left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ | Text mining (MLlib) |
| 20 | Hadoop Latency | $T_{\text{MR}} = T_{\text{read}} + T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}} + T_{\text{write}}$ | E2E job time |

> **Engineering Field Utility:**
> - **Telecom & IoT:** Cassandra + Spark Streaming for ingesting billions of sensor events daily.
> - **Banking & Finance:** HBase + Mahout for real-time fraud detection on transactional streams.
> - **Healthcare & Genomics:** Hadoop + Spark MLlib for genomic sequence alignment and patient outcome prediction.
> - **E-Commerce:** MongoDB + Spark ALS for hyper-personalized recommendation engines.
> - **Social Media Analytics:** Hive + Pig + MLlib for sentiment mining over petabytes of posts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Estimating HDFS Block Count and Cluster Storage

### Problem Setup

> A logistics company ingests **daily log files** of total size $\vert F \vert = 8.5 \text{ GB} = 8704 \text{ MB}$ into HDFS. The block size is $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ with replication factor $R = 3$.
> **Determine:** (a) Number of blocks. (b) Total physical storage consumed. (c) Storage overhead percentage.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert file size to common unit.**

$$
\vert F \vert = 8.5 \text{ GB} \times 1024 \text{ MB/GB} = 8704 \text{ MB}
$$

We convert to MB because $B$ is given in MB, ensuring unit consistency.

**Step 2 — Apply the ceiling division formula for block count.**

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{\vert F \vert}{B} \right\rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{8704}{128} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Compute exact division and check remainder.**

$$
8704 \div 128 = 68.0
$$

The division is exact, so:

$$
N_{\text{blocks}} = 68
$$

**Step 4 — Apply replication to find physical storage.**

$$
S_{\text{phys}} = \vert F \vert \times R = 8704 \times 3 = 26112 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to GB for readability.**

$$
S_{\text{phys}} = 26112 \div 1024 = 25.5 \text{ GB}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute storage overhead percentage.**

$$
\text{Overhead} = (R - 1) \times 100 = (3 - 1) \times 100 = 200\%
$$

> **Final Answer:** 68 blocks; 25.5 GB physical storage; 200% overhead.

## 3.2 Derivation: Amdahl's Speedup for a Parallel Mining Job

### Problem Setup

> A Spark job running on a 10-node cluster has a **parallel fraction** $p = 0.95$ (95% parallelizable code; 5% is sequential — e.g., driver setup, result aggregation). The cluster is upgraded to $N = 20$ nodes.
> **Compute:** (a) Original speedup at 10 nodes. (b) New speedup at 20 nodes. (c) Efficiency of each.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - p) + \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute speedup at $N = 10$ nodes.**

$$
S(10) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{10}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.095} = \frac{1}{0.145}
$$

$$
S(10) \approx 6.90
$$

**Step 3 — Compute speedup at $N = 20$ nodes.**

$$
S(20) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.95}{20}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.0475} = \frac{1}{0.0975}
$$

$$
S(20) \approx 10.26
$$

**Step 4 — Compute efficiency (Speedup / Nodes).**

For $N = 10$:

$$
E(10) = \frac{6.90}{10} = 0.690 = 69.0\%
$$

For $N = 20$:

$$
E(20) = \frac{10.26}{20} = 0.513 = 51.3\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** Doubling the cluster size from 10 → 20 nodes improves speedup by only ~48.7%, while efficiency drops from 69% → 51.3%. This quantifies the **diminishing returns** of parallel scaling due to the 5% sequential bottleneck.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Big Data Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Matrix for Big Data Mining Tool Selection**

| Criterion | HDFS | MapReduce | Spark | Hive | HBase | Mahout | MLlib | Weka | RapidMiner |
|-----------|------|-----------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------|------|------------|
| **Data Volume** | Petabyte | Petabyte | Petabyte | Petabyte | Petabyte | Terabyte–PB | Terabyte–PB | Megabyte–GB | Megabyte–GB |
| **Latency** | High | High | Low (in-memory) | High | Low (random read) | Medium | Low | Low | Low |
| **Ease of Use** | Low | Low (Java) | Medium (Scala/Py) | High (SQL) | Medium | Medium | High (Scala/Py) | High (GUI) | High (GUI) |
| **Mining Algorithms** | N/A | Limited | Extensive | N/A | N/A | Good (clustering, CF) | Excellent (full ML) | Excellent (academic) | Excellent (full stack) |
| **Real-time** | No | No | Yes (Streaming) | No (Tez helps) | Yes | No | Yes | No | Limited |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Yes | Yes | Yes (RDD lineage) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **Hardware Cost** | Commodity | Commodity | RAM-heavy | Commodity | Commodity | Commodity | RAM-heavy | Single-machine | Single-machine |
| **Best Use Case** | Raw lake storage | Batch ETL | Iterative ML, stream | Ad-hoc SQL | Random-access serving | Distributed ML | Distributed ML | Teaching/research | Enterprise analytics |

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: K-Means Convergence in Mahout

### Algorithm Walk-Through

**Step 1 — Initialization.** Choose $k$ initial centroids $\{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$ from data points $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N\}$.

**Step 2 — Assignment.** For each point $x_i$, assign it to the nearest centroid:

$$
a_i = \arg\min_{j \in \{1, \dots, k\}} \Vert x_i - c_j \Vert^2
$$

**Step 3 — Update.** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all assigned points:

$$
c_j = \frac{1}{\vert S_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in S_j} x_i
$$

Where $S_j = \{ x_i : a_i = j \}$.

**Step 4 — Convergence Check.** Compute the objective function $J$:

$$
J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Vert x_i - c_{a_i} \Vert^2
$$

Iterate Steps 2–3 until $\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon$.

**Mahout Parallelization:** Each mapper processes a partition of $X$ and emits $(c_j, \text{partial sum}, \text{count})$. A combiner locally aggregates these. A single reducer computes the new centroids:

$$
c_j^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_{\text{partials}} \text{partial\_sum}_j}{\sum_{\text{partials}} \text{count}_j}
$$

## 3.5 Comparative Analysis: Hive vs. Pig vs. Spark SQL

| Aspect | Hive | Pig Latin | Spark SQL |
|--------|------|-----------|-----------|
| **Language Style** | Declarative SQL-like HQL | Procedural dataflow | Declarative + DataFrame API |
| **Best For** | Analysts familiar with SQL | Step-by-step ETL pipelines | Iterative ML + ad-hoc queries |
| **Execution Engine** | MapReduce / Tez / Spark | MapReduce / Tez | Spark DAG |
| **Latency** | Seconds to minutes | Seconds to minutes | Sub-second to seconds |
| **UDF Support** | Java, Python | Java, Python, JS, Ruby | Scala, Java, Python, R |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-read | Schema-on-read (optional) | Schema-on-read with DataFrames |
| **Optimization** | Cost-based optimizer (Calcite) | Rule-based optimizer | Catalyst optimizer (advanced) |

flowchart LR

    Client -->|Read Request| DN3[DataNode 3]
    DN1 -.Replication.-> DN2
    DN2 -.Replication.-> DN3

```

    S3 --> S4[Output - Driver Result]
    S1 -.Partition 1.-> T1[Task]
    S1 -.Partition 2.-> T2[Task]
    S1 -.Partition N.-> TN[Task]
    S2 -.Partition 1.-> T3[Task]

```

flowchart LR

flowchart TB

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of Apache Hive in the Big Data ecosystem. List any four HQL operations supported by Hive.**

**Model Answer:**

Apache Hive is a **data warehousing infrastructure** built on top of Hadoop that provides **SQL-like querying** (HQL — Hive Query Language) over data stored in HDFS or compatible file systems. It bridges the gap between traditional RDBMS analysts and the Hadoop ecosystem by translating declarative HQL queries into **MapReduce, Tez, or Spark** jobs for distributed execution.

**Key Roles:**

1. **Schema-on-Read:** Applies table-like structure at query time, not at storage time — enabling flexible analysis of raw, semi-structured data.
2. **Batch Querying:** Suited for large-scale, latency-tolerant analytical workloads.
3. **MetaStore:** Central repository of table definitions, partitions, and column types backed by a relational DB.
4. **Extensibility:** Supports UDFs, UDAFs, and UDTs in Java/Python.

**Four HQL Operations:**

- `CREATE TABLE` — Define a schema mapping to HDFS files.
- `LOAD DATA INPATH` — Move or reference HDFS data into a Hive table.
- `SELECT ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ... ORDER BY` — Standard SQL analytics.
- `INSERT OVERWRITE DIRECTORY` — Persist query results to HDFS.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between HBase and Cassandra as NoSQL data stores. Mention their CAP positions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HBase | Cassandra |
|--------|-------|-----------|
| **Data Model** | Wide-column store; sorted map keyed by row | Wide-column store; partition-sorted rows |
| **Origin** | Inspired by Google BigTable | Inspired by Amazon Dynamo + BigTable |
| **CAP Position** | **CP** — strong consistency, partition tolerance | **AP** — high availability, partition tolerance |
| **Consistency Model** | Strictly consistent (single-master) | Eventually consistent (tunable) |
| **Write Path** | Writes go to HDFS Write-Ahead Log | Writes go to commit log + memtable |
| **Best For** | Random real-time reads, sparse data | Write-heavy IoT, multi-DC replication |
| **Query Language** | Java API, Phoenix SQL | CQL (Cassandra Query Language) |

**Conclusion:** HBase is preferred when **strong consistency** and low-latency point reads are paramount; Cassandra is preferred when **continuous availability** and write-throughput across geographies are critical.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat architecture diagram, explain the **HDFS read/write process**. Describe the role of the NameNode, DataNode, and the replication mechanism. How does HDFS handle DataNode failure?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **MapReduce and Apache Spark** as Big Data processing engines. Discuss the concept of **RDD** and **lazy evaluation** in Spark.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**1. HDFS Architecture — Components:**

- **NameNode (Master):** Stores metadata — filename, directory tree, block-to-DataNode mapping, replication factor. Runs in RAM for fast access; metadata persisted via **FsImage** and **EditLog**.
- **DataNode (Slave):** Stores actual data blocks. Sends heartbeats to NameNode every 3 seconds; sends **BlockReports** periodically (default every 6 hours).
- **Secondary NameNode:** Checkpoints FsImage periodically; does **not** act as a hot standby.

**2. Write Process (Step-by-Step):**

1. Client calls `create()` on the **DistributedFileSystem** object, which contacts the NameNode.
2. NameNode checks file permissions, existing files, and assigns block IDs.
3. NameNode returns a list of DataNodes forming the **replication pipeline** (e.g., DN1 → DN2 → DN3).
4. Client streams data in **packet-sized chunks** (default 64 KB) along the pipeline.
5. Each DataNode stores the packet locally and forwards it downstream.
6. Acknowledgments are pipelined back to the client.
7. On completion, the client informs the NameNode, which updates its metadata.

**3. Read Process:**

1. Client calls `open()`; NameNode returns block locations sorted by proximity.
2. Client connects to the nearest DataNode holding the first block and streams data.
3. For subsequent blocks, the client may read from different DataNodes.
4. Block-level checksums verify data integrity.

**4. Replication Mechanism:**

- Default **replication factor $R = 3$**.
- Replicas are placed using the **Rack-Aware Policy**: one replica on the local node, one on a different rack, one on the same remote rack.
- The NameNode continuously monitors replication; under-replicated blocks are scheduled for re-replication.

**5. DataNode Failure Handling:**

- If heartbeats lapse for >10 minutes (default timeout), the NameNode declares the DataNode **dead**.
- All blocks previously hosted by the dead node become **under-replicated**.
- The NameNode instructs surviving DataNodes to **re-replicate** these blocks to restore $R = 3$.
- The dead node's data is not lost because replicas exist on other nodes.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**1. MapReduce vs Spark — Detailed Comparison:**

| Aspect | MapReduce | Apache Spark |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Execution Model** | Two-phase: Map → Shuffle → Reduce | DAG of stages with arbitrary transformations |
| **Data Sharing** | Disk-based between jobs (HDFS read/write) | In-memory RDDs across iterations |
| **Iteration Overhead** | High (disk I/O each iteration) | Low (RDDs cached in memory) |
| **Latency** | High (sub-minute to minutes) | Low (sub-second to seconds) |
| **Ease of Use** | Verbose Java API | Concise Scala, Python, R, Java APIs |
| **Streaming** | Not native (use Storm/Flink) | Native via Spark Streaming & Structured Streaming |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Re-execute failed tasks (disk-based) | Recompute lost partitions via RDD lineage |
| **Resource Manager** | YARN | YARN, Mesos, Kubernetes, Standalone |
| **Best For** | Linear batch ETL, one-pass jobs | Iterative ML, graph processing, streaming |

**2. RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset):**

- An **RDD** is an immutable, partitioned collection of records that can be operated on in parallel.
- Properties: `partitions`, `dependencies` (lineage), `compute` function, `partitioner`, `preferred locations`.
- RDDs are **lazily evaluated** — transformations are recorded in a DAG but not executed until an **action** (e.g., `count()`, `collect()`) is triggered.

**3. Lazy Evaluation:**

- **Transformations** (`map`, `filter`, `reduceByKey`) build the lineage DAG.
- **Actions** (`collect`, `saveAsTextFile`) trigger actual job execution.
- Benefits: **optimization** (pipelining of narrow transformations into single stages), **reduced I/O**, and **fault tolerance** (lost partitions can be recomputed from lineage).

**4. Why Spark is Faster:**

For an iterative ML algorithm (e.g., k-Means with 100 iterations), MapReduce writes 100× to HDFS; Spark keeps the working set in memory, achieving **10×–100× speedup**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A (a):**
> Students often **draw only a 2-tier diagram** (NameNode + DataNode) and forget the **Secondary NameNode**. The Secondary NameNode is a **checkpoint node**, not a hot standby. Confusing this with HDFS HA (High Availability) loses 1 mark. Also, students frequently omit the **rack-aware placement strategy** in the replication explanation — this is a **3-mark item** in the marking scheme.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A (b):**
> A common mistake is to say "RDDs store data in memory" without mentioning **lineage-based recomputation** for fault tolerance. Examiners expect the answer: "RDDs are fault-tolerant because their lineage (DAG of transformations) allows Spark to recompute lost partitions from preceding RDDs."

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **CAP theorem** with a diagram. Compare the CAP positioning of **HBase, MongoDB, Cassandra, and Redis**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Apache Mahout** as a scalable machine learning library. Describe any two algorithms supported by Mahout with their mathematical formulations.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**1. CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):**

A distributed data store can simultaneously provide **only two** of the following three guarantees:

- **Consistency (C):** Every read returns the most recent write (linearizability).
- **Availability (A):** Every request receives a (non-error) response, even in the face of node failures.
- **Partition Tolerance (P):** The system continues to operate despite arbitrary network message losses between nodes.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$
C + A + P \leq 2
$$

**Pictorial Representation (Venn Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C[C - Consistency] 
    A[A - Availability]
    P[P - Partition Tolerance]
    C --- CA[CA - Impossible in Distributed Systems]
    C --- CP[CP - HBase, MongoDB, Redis, BigTable]
    A --- AP[AP - Cassandra, DynamoDB, CouchDB]
    P --- CA
    P --- CP
    P --- AP
```

**2. CAP Positioning of Big Data Stores:**

| Database | CAP Choice | Trade-off Made | Justification |
|----------|------------|----------------|---------------|
| **HBase** | CP | Sacrifices availability during partition | Single-master architecture with strong consistency via HDFS |
| **MongoDB** | CP (default with majority write concern) | Sacrifices availability during partition | Primary-secondary with majority write concern |
| **Cassandra** | AP | Sacrifices consistency (eventual) | Masterless ring; tunable consistency per query |
| **Redis** | CP (in clustered mode) | Sacrifices availability during failover | Asynchronous replication; master-replica |

> **Key Insight:** In real distributed deployments, **P is non-negotiable** (network failures are inevitable), so the design choice is effectively **CP vs. AP**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**1. Apache Mahout — Overview:**

Apache Mahout is a **distributed machine learning library** built on top of Hadoop and Spark. It provides scalable implementations of algorithms for **clustering, classification, collaborative filtering, and frequent pattern mining** that can handle datasets too large for single-machine tools like Weka or scikit-learn.

**Mahout Algorithm Taxonomy:**

- **Clustering:** k-Means, Fuzzy k-Means, Canopy, Streaming k-Means, Dirichlet Process Clustering, Spectral.
- **Classification:** Naive Bayes, Hidden Markov Models, Logistic Regression (SGD), Random Forest, Multinomial Naive Bayes.
- **Collaborative Filtering:** Item-based, user-based, matrix factorization (SVD).
- **Frequent Pattern Mining:** Parallel FP-Growth.
- **Dimensionality Reduction:** Stochastic SVD, PCA.

**2. Algorithm 1: k-Means Clustering (Mathematical Formulation)**

**Objective Function:**

$$
J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \min_{c_j \in C} \Vert x_i - c_j \Vert^2
$$

Where $C = \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_k\}$ are the cluster centroids.

**Update Equations:**

**Assignment Step:**

$$
a_i = \arg\min_{j \in \{1, \dots, k\}} \Vert x_i - c_j \Vert^2
$$

**Update Step:**

$$
c_j = \frac{1}{\vert S_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in S_j} x_i, \quad \text{where } S_j = \{x_i : a_i = j\}
$$

**Convergence Criterion:**

$$
\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \vert < \epsilon
$$

**Mahout Implementation:** k-Means is implemented as a MapReduce job where mappers compute partial sums, combiners aggregate locally, and the reducer recomputes centroids.

**3. Algorithm 2: Naive Bayes Classification (Mathematical Formulation)**

**Bayes' Theorem:**

$$
P(C_k \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(x)}
$$

**Naive Independence Assumption:**

$$
P(x \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

**Final Classification Rule:**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{C_k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)
$$

**Mahout Implementation:** Uses **Hadoop MapReduce** to compute term/class counts in parallel. The classifier supports both **complement** and **standard** Naive Bayes variants for discrete and continuous features.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (a):**
> A frequent error is **misclassifying Redis as AP**. In its clustered configuration with master-replica failover, Redis is **CP** — it rejects writes during partition if no majority is reachable. Also, students forget that **MongoDB's CAP position is tunable** based on write concern (`w: majority` is CP, `w: 1` leans AP). Examiners reward nuance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B (b):**
> Students often write Mahout's algorithm equations without stating the **Naive Bayes independence assumption** explicitly, or fail to mention the **MapReduce parallelization strategy** in k-Means. Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for the parallel/distributed angle — omitting it costs heavy marks.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Big Data Mining Tools (Module 4)**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Big Data mining tools span **6 layers**: Storage → Resource Mgmt → Processing → Query/Scripting → Mining/ML → Visualization.
- ☐ **HDFS** = NameNode (metadata) + DataNodes (blocks, $R=3$ default, 128 MB block size).
- ☐ **MapReduce** = two-phase paradigm; disk-based; fault-tolerant via task re-execution.
- ☐ **Spark** = in-memory RDD-based DAG; lineage recomputation for fault tolerance; lazy evaluation.
- ☐ **YARN** = ResourceManager (global) + NodeManager (per-node) + ApplicationMaster (per-job).
- ☐ **CAP Theorem**: A distributed system picks **2 of 3**; P is non-negotiable in practice.
- ☐ **HBase = CP**, **Cassandra = AP**, **MongoDB = CP (default)**, **Redis = CP (clustered)**.

**Tools & Their Sweet Spots:**
- ☐ **HDFS** → Cheap, fault-tolerant, write-once-read-many storage.
- ☐ **MapReduce** → Linear batch ETL pipelines.
- ☐ **Spark** → Iterative ML, graph, streaming, low-latency analytics.
- ☐ **Hive** → SQL-on-Hadoop for analysts; MapReduce/Tez/Spark engine.
- ☐ **Pig Latin** → Procedural dataflow ETL.
- ☐ **HBase** → Random real-time reads on sparse, wide-column data.
- ☐ **Cassandra** → Write-heavy, multi-DC, masterless deployments.
- ☐ **MongoDB** → Document-oriented, schema-flexible applications.
- ☐ **Mahout** → Distributed ML on Hadoop/Spark.
- ☐ **MLlib** → Spark's full ML library (classification, clustering, CF, FPM, feature).
- ☐ **Weka** → GUI-driven, single-machine, academic ML suite.
- ☐ **RapidMiner** → Visual, code-optional enterprise data science platform.
- ☐ **Tableau / Power BI / Kibana** → BI and operational dashboards.

**High-Yield Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $N_{\text{blocks}} = \lceil \vert F \vert / B \rceil$; $S_{\text{phys}} = \vert F \vert \times R$.
- ☐ Amdahl: $S(N) = \frac{1}{(1-p) + p/N}$.
- ☐ HDFS heartbeat = 3 s; NameNode timeout = 10 min.
- ☐ Spark partitions default ≈ $2 \times$ to $4 \times$ total cores.
- ☐ Hadoop MapReduce mappers = number of input blocks; reducers via heuristic.
- ☐ Cross-entropy loss for Mahout SGD logistic regression.
- ☐ Cosine similarity for collaborative filtering in MLlib ALS.
- ☐ k-Means objective and update rules.
- ☐ Bayes' theorem with naive independence assumption for Naive Bayes.

**Exam-Smart Traps to Avoid:**
- ☐ Don't confuse **Secondary NameNode** with **Standby NameNode** in HDFS HA.
- ☐ Don't state "RDDs are stored in memory" without **lineage** for fault tolerance.
- ☐ Don't claim **CAP = all three are achievable** — only two of three.
- ☐ Don't label Cassandra as CP or HBase as AP — remember the inverses.
- ☐ For Mahout, always include the **distributed/MapReduce** angle, not just the math.

**Key Vocabulary for Theory Answers:**
- ☐ Schema-on-Read, Schema-on-Write.
- ☐ Rack-aware placement, rack failure domain.
- ☐ Lineage, DAG, narrow vs. wide transformation, shuffle boundary.
- ☐ In-memory computation, lazy evaluation, action vs. transformation.
- ☐ MapReduce, Map-Only job, Reducer-Only job, Combiner.
- ☐ Master-slave vs. masterless architecture.
- ☐ Eventually consistent, strongly consistent, tunable consistency.
- ☐ Cosine similarity, Pearson correlation, FP-growth, support, confidence, lift.

**Suggested KTU Study Order for This Module:**
1. Master **HDFS architecture and replication** (highest weightage).
2. Internalize **MapReduce and Spark** comparison (DAG vs. two-phase).
3. Memorize **CAP positioning** of every NoSQL store.
4. Practice **Mahout/MLlib algorithm derivations** (k-Means, Naive Bayes, ALS, FP-Growth).
5. Compare **Hive vs. Pig vs. Spark SQL** in tabular form.
6. Review **Weka and RapidMiner** as single-machine GUI alternatives for academic context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — Remember the Big Data Tool Stack:**
> **"H-Map-Spark-Yarn-Hive-Pig-Mahout-MLlib" → "Hot Meals, Spicy, Yummy, Highly Palatable, Memorably Luscious"**
> = **HDFS, MapReduce, Spark, YARN, Hive, Pig, Mahout, MLlib** — the **Big Eight** of KTU Module 4.
